| Unidirectional communication | Most apps need bidirectional (HTTP, TCP) | Cannot implement feedback |

**Why Not Used Practically (4 marks for explanation):**

1. **No Reliability:** Any bit error or lost frame in the channel is **permanently lost** — there is no retransmission.
2. **No Synchronization:** Sender has no idea whether the receiver is actually ready or overwhelmed.
3. **No Flow Regulation:** A fast sender can **drown** a slow receiver once the infinite buffer assumption breaks.
4. **No Congestion Management:** Network congestion is **invisible** to the sender.

**Conclusion:** The Simplex Protocol is **pedagogical scaffolding** — it introduces the concept of framing and encapsulation in the data link layer but is **incapable of supporting real-world error-prone, feedback-dependent communication**.

---

### **Part B – Question B (14 Marks)** — *Numerical & Analytical Focus*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** A Simplex Protocol is used over a **5000 km** link with a bandwidth of **4 Mbps** and a propagation speed of **$2.5 \times 10^8$ m/s**. The frame size is **2000 bytes**. Calculate the **transmission time, propagation time, total transit time, and link utilization**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the bandwidth is **doubled** to **8 Mbps**, recalculate the **link utilization** and comment on the improvement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Given Data Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Distance ($d$) | 5000 km $= 5 \times 10^6$ m |
| Bandwidth ($B$) | 4 Mbps $= 4 \times 10^6$ bps |
| Propagation Speed ($v$) | $2.5 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Frame Size ($L$) | 2000 bytes $= 16{,}000$ bits |

**Step 1 — Transmission Time (2 marks):**

$$
T_t = \dfrac{L}{B} = \dfrac{16{,}000 \text{ bits}}{4 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Propagation Time (2 marks):**

$$
T_p = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}}{2.5 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Transit Time (1 mark):**

$$
T_{transit} = T_t + T_p = 4 + 20 = 24 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Link Utilization (1 mark):**

$$
U = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + T_p} = \dfrac{4}{4 + 20} = \dfrac{4}{24} = \dfrac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667 = 16.67\%
$$

---

**(b) Recalculation with Doubled Bandwidth (7 marks):**

**New Parameter:** $B_{new} = 8 \text{ Mbps} = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}$

**Step 1 — New Transmission Time (2 marks):**

$$
T_t^{new} = \dfrac{16{,}000}{8 \times 10^6} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Propagation Time Unchanged (1 mark):**

$$
T_p = 20 \text{ ms} \quad (\text{independent of bandwidth})
$$

**Step 3 — New Link Utilization (2 marks):**

$$
U_{new} = \dfrac{T_t^{new}}{T_t^{new} + T_p} = \dfrac{2}{2 + 20} = \dfrac{2}{22} = \dfrac{1}{11} \approx 0.0909 = 9.09\%
$$

**Step 4 — Analytical Comment (2 marks):**

> **Comment:** Surprisingly, **doubling the bandwidth reduced the link utilization from 16.67% to 9.09%**. This counterintuitive result occurs because the **propagation time $T_p$ dominates** on long-haul links. The Simplex Protocol fails to utilize the increased bandwidth effectively because it has **no pipelining or windowing mechanism** to keep the channel saturated. In contrast, **Go-Back-N** or **Selective Repeat ARQ** would allow multiple frames to be "in flight," drastically improving utilization. This limitation is the **key motivator** for the evolution from Simplex to Sliding Window Protocols.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams:**

- **Definition:** Simplex Protocol = **unidirectional, error-free, flow-control-free** data link layer protocol. **(Must state all three absences in definition questions.)**

- **Four Pillars of Assumptions:** Always-ready network layers; infinite receiver buffer; error-free channel; one-way data flow. **(Always enumerate all four in 7+ mark questions.)**

- **Sender's Core Action:** Fetch packet → encapsulate → send → **do NOT wait for ACK** → loop.

- **Receiver's Core Action:** Wait for frame → decapsulate → deliver to network layer → **do NOT send ACK** → loop.

- **Critical Formula (HIGH-YIELD):**

$$
U_{simplex} = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + T_p} = \dfrac{\dfrac{L}{B}}{\dfrac{L}{B} + \dfrac{d}{v}}
$$

- **Critical Distinction (Exam Trap):**
  - Simplex: $U = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + T_p}$
  - Stop-and-Wait: $U = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}$ (**extra $T_p$** for ACK round-trip)

- **Numerical Pattern:** Always convert **bytes to bits** ($1 \text{ byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$) and **km to m** before plugging into formulas. Propagation speed is **$2 \times 10^8$ m/s** in fiber/copper, **$3 \times 10^8$ m/s** in free space.

- **No Sequence Number Strictly Needed:** Since there is no ACK and no retransmission, the receiver cannot detect or recover lost frames — **sequence number is academic** in pure Simplex.

- **Why Simplex Cannot Be Used Practically:** It cannot handle **bit errors, lost frames, receiver overflow, or feedback**. The infinite buffer assumption **violates physical memory constraints**.

- **Evolution Pathway:** Simplex → **Stop-and-Wait** (adds ACK) → **Go-Back-N** (adds pipelining) → **Selective Repeat** (adds selective retransmit). The Simplex Protocol is the **conceptual starting point** of this evolution.

- **KTU 2024 Examiner's Favourite:** Expect a **comparison question** with Stop-and-Wait ARQ, asking to **highlight the addition of ACK/NAK, timeouts, and flow control** in moving from Simplex to Stop-and-Wait.

- **Memory Aid (Mnemonic):** **"SAFE"** — **S**implex = **A**lways one-way, **F**low-control-free, **E**rror-free assumption.

- **Most-Likely 14-Mark Question Pattern:** Either (i) **FSM diagrams + assumptions**, or (ii) **numerical on link utilization** with a follow-up **comment on bandwidth scaling**. Practice both!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol for an Error-Free Channel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol for an Error-Free Channel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Simplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol for an Error-Free Channel

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Simplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol** is a fundamental **Data Link Layer flow control mechanism** designed for a unidirectional communication channel that is assumed to be completely error-free. In this protocol, the sender transmits a single data frame to the receiver and then enters a *waiting state*, suspending all further transmissions until an Acknowledgment (ACK) is received from the receiver, confirming the successful receipt of the frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — OECPT613, Module 2):**
> The protocol is studied under the assumption of:
> 1. **Simplex Channel:** Data flows in only one direction (sender → receiver).
> 2. **Error-Free Channel:** No frames are lost, corrupted, duplicated, or reordered.
> 3. **Infinite Receiver Buffer:** The receiver can always accept incoming frames.
> 4. **Dedicated Return Path:** A reverse channel exists purely for ACKs (often neglected in pure simplex, but mathematically modeled).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Registered Letter" Model

Imagine a postman in a quiet town who must deliver a stack of 50 important letters. The rule is: **he can drop only one letter in the mailbox, then must stand there and wait** until the receiver comes out, waves a green flag (the ACK), and confirms, *"I got it."* Only then does the postman walk back, take the next letter, and repeat.

- The **letter** = the Data Frame.
- The **green flag** = the Acknowledgment (ACK).
- The **waiting at the mailbox** = the *Stop* phase of the protocol.
- The **error-free town** = no letters are lost or stolen.

This is precisely how the Stop-and-Wait protocol operates in the data link layer.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

The following quantities govern the performance of the protocol (all measured in **seconds**):

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Frame Transmission Time | $T_f$ | Time taken by the sender to push all bits of a frame onto the medium. |
| Propagation Time | $T_p$ | Time for a single bit to traverse the physical medium from sender to receiver. |
| Acknowledgment Time | $T_a$ | Time taken to transmit the ACK frame back to the sender. |
| Total Cycle Time | $T_t$ | Time elapsed from the start of one frame transmission to the start of the next. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Channel Utilization ($U$)** is the single most important performance metric for this protocol. It represents the fraction of total channel capacity that is used for *useful data* transmission, as opposed to idle waiting time. **A low utilization value indicates a poorly performing link.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

The protocol operates as a strict **synchronous handshake** sequence. Let us deconstruct its operation step-by-step:

**Step 1: Frame Preparation & Transmission**
- The sender encapsulates a packet from the Network Layer into a Data Link Layer frame.
- The frame is transmitted bit-by-bit onto the medium. This phase consumes exactly $T_f$ seconds.
- *Why?* Because the sender must physically push the entire frame onto the wire, and the transmission time is governed by the formula $T_f = L / R$, where $L$ is the frame length in bits and $R$ is the channel bandwidth in bits per second.

**Step 2: Propagation to the Receiver**
- The first bit of the frame begins its journey across the medium. The last bit finishes arriving at the receiver after $T_p$ seconds.
- *Why?* The bit travels at the signal propagation speed of the medium (typically $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper/fiber).
- $T_p = \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Propagation Speed}}$

**Step 3: Instantaneous Processing**
- The receiver instantly processes the frame (negligible processing time, assumed zero) and generates an ACK.
- Because the channel is error-free, the ACK is always correctly received.

**Step 4: ACK Propagation Back**
- The ACK frame travels back to the sender, consuming $T_a$ transmission time and $T_p$ propagation time.

**Step 5: The "Stop" — Sender Waits**
- The sender remains completely idle from the moment it finishes sending the frame until it receives the ACK.
- This idle time is the **fundamental inefficiency** of the Stop-and-Wait protocol.

**Step 6: Resumption**
- Upon receiving the ACK, the sender immediately picks up the next packet and repeats from Step 1.

### 2.2 The Concept of "a" — The Bandwidth-Delay Product

The single most critical dimensionless parameter in all of data communications is:

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_f}$$

This ratio compares the **propagation delay** to the **transmission time**.

- If $a < 1$: The frame is "long" relative to the channel's delay. The channel is busy most of the time. *Utilization is high.*
- If $a > 1$: The frame is "short" relative to the delay. The sender finishes transmitting long before the first bit even arrives at the receiver. *Utilization is very poor.*
- If $a = 1$: A perfect balance (rare in practice).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Real-World Example:** For a geostationary satellite link, $T_p \approx 270$ ms. If we transmit a small 1 KB frame at 1 Mbps, $T_f = 8$ ms. Thus $a = 33.75$, meaning the channel is idle **97%** of the time! This is why Stop-and-Wait is unsuitable for high-latency, high-bandwidth links.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula Name | Equation | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Transmission Time | $T_f = L / R$ | $L$ = Frame length, $R$ = Bandwidth | seconds (s) |
| Propagation Time | $T_p = d / v$ | $d$ = Distance, $v$ = Signal speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s) | seconds (s) |
| Normalized Delay | $a = T_p / T_f$ | Bandwidth-Delay ratio | dimensionless |
| Total Cycle Time | $T_t = T_f + 2T_p + T_a$ | Time for one complete frame-ACK handshake | seconds (s) |
| **Channel Utilization (General)** | $U = \dfrac{T_f}{T_f + 2T_p + T_a}$ | Fraction of time channel carries useful data | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **Channel Utilization (Simplified)** | $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | When $T_a \approx 0$ (negligible ACK) | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = U \times R$ | Effective data rate delivered to receiver | bits/second (bps) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Tip:** In most KTU numerical problems, the acknowledgment time $T_a$ is considered **negligible** ($T_a \approx 0$) because ACK frames are tiny (e.g., 48 bits) compared to data frames. Always check the problem statement to confirm this assumption. If $T_a$ is given, you **must** include it in the denominator.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Although the pure Stop-and-Wait protocol is rarely deployed in modern high-speed networks (TCP, for example, uses a sliding window variant), the *concept* forms the bedrock of:
- **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** serial communication.
- **XMODEM file transfer protocol** (a 1970s classic).
- **Hardware handshaking** in embedded systems (RTS/CTS lines).
- **Bluetooth's L2CAP layer** in some acknowledgment modes.
- **Foundation for understanding ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest)** algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Channel Utilization ($U$)

**Objective:** To mathematically derive the fraction of time the channel is used for transmitting actual data, given an error-free simplex environment.

**Given Assumptions:**
- The channel is error-free (no retransmissions).
- The ACK is instantaneous or infinitesimally small ($T_a \to 0$).
- The sender and receiver are perfectly synchronized.

**Step 1: Define the Time for One Complete Cycle**

A single "cycle" begins the moment the sender starts transmitting a frame and ends the moment the sender starts transmitting the *next* frame. This total elapsed time is the sum of all distinct time intervals.

$$
T_t = T_{f} + T_{p,\text{forward}} + T_{a} + T_{p,\text{return}}
$$

Since the medium is symmetric (same distance, same propagation speed) and $T_a$ is negligible:

$$
T_t = T_f + 2T_p + 0
$$

$$
T_t = T_f + 2T_p
$$

**Step 2: Apply the Definition of Channel Utilization**

Utilization is defined as the ratio of "useful time" (time spent transmitting payload data) to the "total time" (the entire cycle duration).

$$
U = \frac{\text{Time spent transmitting useful data}}{\text{Total time for one cycle}}
$$

$$
U = \frac{T_f}{T_t}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the Total Cycle Time**

$$
U = \frac{T_f}{T_f + 2T_p}
$$

**Step 4: Normalize using the parameter $a$**

To make the expression independent of absolute time units, we divide the numerator and the denominator by $T_f$:

$$
U = \frac{\frac{T_f}{T_f}}{\frac{T_f + 2T_p}{T_f}} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{2T_p}{T_f}}
$$

Recalling the definition $a = \frac{T_p}{T_f}$:

$$
\boxed{U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}}
$$

**Step 5: Verification through Limit Cases**

*Case A: $a \to 0$ (Short link, long frame)*
The propagation delay is negligible. The channel is always busy with the next frame as soon as the ACK arrives.
Result: $U = \frac{1}{1+0} = 1$ (100% utilization). *Physically sensible.*

*Case B: $a \to \infty$ (Long link, short frame)*
The frame is so small that the sender finishes almost instantly, but the link is "frozen" waiting for propagation.
Result: $U = \frac{1}{1+\infty} \to 0$ (0% utilization). *Physically sensible.*

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A channel has a bandwidth of **4 Mbps** and a one-way propagation delay of **20 ms**. The data frames are **2000 bits** long, and ACK frames are **negligible** in size.
**(i)** Calculate the value of the parameter $a$.
**(ii)** Calculate the channel utilization $U$ using the Stop-and-Wait protocol.
**(iii)** Calculate the effective throughput.

**Solution:**

**Part (i): Calculate $a$**

First, compute the transmission time $T_f$:

$$
T_f = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{2000 \text{ bits}}{4 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 0.5 \text{ ms}
$$

Now compute $a$:

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_f} = \frac{20 \text{ ms}}{0.5 \text{ ms}} = 40
$$

**Part (ii): Calculate Channel Utilization $U$**

Using the derived formula $U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}$:

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2(40)} = \frac{1}{81} \approx 0.01234
$$

To express as a percentage:

$$
U_{\%} = 1.234\%
$$

**Part (iii): Calculate Effective Throughput**

$$
\text{Throughput} = U \times R = \frac{1}{81} \times 4 \text{ Mbps} = \frac{4}{81} \text{ Mbps} \approx 49.38 \text{ kbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even though the link is rated for 4 Mbps, the Stop-and-Wait protocol only manages to deliver about 49.38 kbps of useful data — a massive **98.77%** waste of bandwidth! This is the central reason why sliding window protocols (Module 2 continuation) were invented.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The diagram below illustrates the message exchange between the sender, the forward channel, the receiver, and the reverse acknowledgment channel.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[SENDER STATION] -- Frame 1, F0 --> FC[FORWARD CHANNEL]
    FC -- Bits propagate at 2x10^8 m/s --> R[RECEIVER STATION]
    R -- Generates ACK 0 --> RC[REVERSE CHANNEL]
    RC -- ACK propagates back --> S
    S -- Waits for ACK, then Frame 1, F1 --> FC
    subgraph SENDER_LOGIC [SENDER STATE MACHINE]
        direction TB
        StateA[State: Ready] -- Send Frame --> StateB[State: Waiting for ACK]
        StateB -- ACK Received --> StateA
    end
    subgraph RECEIVER_LOGIC [RECEIVER STATE MACHINE]
        direction TB
        StateX[State: Idle] -- Frame Arrives --> StateY[State: Process and Send ACK]
        StateY -- ACK Dispatched --> StateX
    end
```

### 4.2 Sender and Receiver State Transition Diagram

This state diagram precisely captures the algorithmic flow of the protocol. Every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters to satisfy the Mermaid compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> senderReady
    senderReady: SENDER READY
    waitingAck: WAITING FOR ACK
    senderReady --> waitingAck : Send Frame F0
    waitingAck --> senderReady : Receive ACK 0
    waitingAck --> waitingAck : Timeout Reached
    
    [*] --> receiverIdle
    receiverIdle: RECEIVER IDLE
    processingFrame: PROCESSING FRAME
    sendingAck: SENDING ACK
    receiverIdle --> processingFrame : Frame F0 Arrives
    processingFrame --> sendingAck : Frame Complete
    sendingAck --> receiverIdle : ACK 0 Dispatched
```

### 4.3 Timing Diagram (The Heart of Stop-and-Wait)

The following sequence diagram captures the temporal sequence of events. Notice the long idle "wait" periods on both the sender and the channel.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SENDER as SENDER
    participant MEDIUM as CHANNEL MEDIUM
    participant RECEIVER as RECEIVER
    SENDER->>SENDER: T0: Pick up Packet 0
    SENDER->>MEDIUM: T0 to T0+Tf: Transmit Frame 0
    Note over MEDIUM: T0+Tf to T0+Tf+Tp: Last bit in transit
    MEDIUM->>RECEIVER: T0+Tf+Tp: Frame 0 fully received
    RECEIVER->>RECEIVER: Process Frame 0 (negligible)
    RECEIVER->>MEDIUM: Send ACK 0 (negligible time)
    Note over MEDIUM: ACK 0 in transit (Tp)
    MEDIUM->>SENDER: T0+Tf+2Tp: ACK 0 arrives
    SENDER->>SENDER: T0+Tf+2Tp: Resumes activity
    SENDER->>MEDIUM: Transmit Frame 1
    Note over SENDER: IDLE from T0+Tf to T0+Tf+2Tp
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define the term "Stop-and-Wait Protocol" and state the two key assumptions made when analyzing its simplest form (the error-free case).*

**Model Answer:**
The Stop-and-Wait Protocol is a Data Link Layer flow control mechanism in which the sender transmits a single data frame and then halts (waits) until it receives an acknowledgment (ACK) from the receiver confirming the successful delivery of that frame, before sending the next frame.

**Two Key Assumptions (Error-Free Case):**
1. **Error-Free Channel:** It is assumed that no frame is lost, corrupted, or duplicated during transmission. Hence, no retransmission mechanism is required.
2. **Unidirectional Simplex Data Flow:** Data flows only from sender to receiver, while the reverse channel is used exclusively for acknowledgment signals (or is assumed absent/ideal).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*What is the "Bandwidth-Delay Product" and how does it influence the channel utilization of a Stop-and-Wait system?*

**Model Answer:**
The **Bandwidth-Delay Product** is mathematically expressed as the product of the link bandwidth ($R$) and the one-way propagation delay ($T_p$). It represents the volume of data (in bits) that can "fit" inside the wire at any given instant.

$$
\text{BDP} = R \times T_p
$$

**Influence on Utilization:**
It directly governs the value of the parameter $a = T_p / T_f$. As the BDP increases, the channel becomes "longer" relative to the frame size. A high BDP means the sender is forced to wait for a long time (2 × $T_p$) for the ACK, while the channel remains idle. Consequently, the channel utilization $U = \frac{1}{1+2a}$ plummets. Stop-and-Wait performs poorly (low utilization) on links with high Bandwidth-Delay Products (e.g., satellite links).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** Students frequently compute only $T_p$ instead of $2T_p$ (round-trip delay) in the denominator. Always remember it is a *handshake* — the signal must travel *forward and back*.
> 2. **Mixing up units:** $T_f$ derived from Mbps and kbps must match. Convert all times to either milliseconds (ms) or microseconds (μs) *before* computing ratios.
> 3. **Neglecting $T_a$:** If a problem explicitly states the ACK size and bandwidth, $T_a$ **must** be included in the formula. Neglecting it leads to a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Confusing Utilization with Throughput:** Utilization is dimensionless (a ratio). Throughput is measured in bps/kbps/Mbps. Mark these clearly as distinct answers.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Path 1**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the channel utilization $U$ of a Simplex Stop-and-Wait protocol for an error-free channel. Clearly define every variable in your derivation and state the assumptions used.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider a geostationary satellite link connecting a ground station in Kerala to a ground station in Germany. The one-way propagation delay is **270 ms**. The channel bandwidth is **1.536 Mbps** (a standard T1 line). The data link layer uses a frame size of **1024 bytes** and Stop-and-Wait flow control. Compute the parameter $a$, the channel utilization $U$ (in %), and the effective throughput in kbps. Neglect the ACK transmission time.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Detailed Derivation**

**Assumptions:**
1. The communication channel is error-free.
2. Data transmission is one-way (simplex).
3. The ACK frame is infinitesimally small ($T_a \approx 0$).
4. The processing time at the receiver is negligible.

**Step 1: Define the time components.**
Let $T_f$ be the time taken by the sender to transmit one data frame of length $L$ bits over a link of bandwidth $R$ bps.

$$
T_f = \frac{L}{R} \text{ seconds}
$$

Let $T_p$ be the time taken for a signal bit to travel across the physical medium of distance $d$ meters.

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} \text{ seconds}
$$

where $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s is the signal propagation speed in copper or fiber.

**Step 2: Determine the total cycle time.**
The total cycle time $T_t$ includes: transmitting the frame, propagation to receiver, and propagation of ACK back.

$$
T_t = T_f + 2T_p
$$

**Step 3: Express utilization.**
Channel utilization $U$ is the fraction of the cycle time used for transmitting actual data.

$$
U = \frac{T_f}{T_t} = \frac{T_f}{T_f + 2T_p}
$$

**Step 4: Introduce the parameter $a$.**
Dividing numerator and denominator by $T_f$, and defining $a = T_p / T_f$:

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2 \cdot \frac{T_p}{T_f}} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

This completes the derivation. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Numerical Solution**

**Step 1: Compute $T_f$.**
Frame size $L = 1024$ bytes $= 1024 \times 8 = 8192$ bits.
Bandwidth $R = 1.536 \times 10^6$ bps.

$$
T_f = \frac{8192}{1.536 \times 10^6} = 5.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5.333 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the parameter $a$.**

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_f} = \frac{270 \text{ ms}}{5.333 \text{ ms}} = 50.625
$$

**Step 3: Compute Channel Utilization $U$.**

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2(50.625)} = \frac{1}{102.25} \approx 0.00978
$$

In percentage: **$U \approx 0.978\%$**

**Step 4: Compute Effective Throughput.**

$$
\text{Throughput} = U \times R = 0.00978 \times 1.536 \text{ Mbps} \approx 0.01502 \text{ Mbps}
$$

Converting to kbps: **$\text{Throughput} \approx 15.02 \text{ kbps}$**

> **Final Answer Summary:** $a = 50.625$, $U \approx 0.978\%$, Throughput $\approx 15.02$ kbps. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Path 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Draw a detailed timing diagram for the Simplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol showing the transmission of three consecutive frames (Frame 0, Frame 1, Frame 2) and their corresponding acknowledgments. Label the axes (Sender, Receiver, Time) and clearly indicate the propagation delay and idle time on the diagram.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 1000-km long cable link connects two routers. The signal propagation speed in the cable is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. The link bandwidth is **1 Gbps**. If the Stop-and-Wait protocol is used with a frame size of **1500 bytes**:
   (i) Calculate the propagation time $T_p$.
   (ii) Calculate the transmission time $T_f$.
   (iii) What percentage of the link's capacity is wasted due to the waiting time of the protocol?

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) [7 Marks] Timing Diagram**

Below is a conceptual timing diagram for the transmission of three frames. The X-axis represents elapsed time. The Y-axis represents the state of the Sender, the Channel, and the Receiver.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SENDER as SENDER
    participant CHANNEL as CHANNEL
    participant RECEIVER as RECEIVER
    Note over SENDER,RECEIVER: TIME AXIS: 0 -- Tf -- Tf+Tp -- Tf+2Tp -- 2Tf+2Tp -- 2Tf+3Tp -- 2Tf+4Tp
    SENDER->>CHANNEL: 0 to Tf: Transmit Frame 0
    Note right of SENDER: SENDER IDLE (Waiting for ACK)
    CHANNEL->>RECEIVER: Tf to Tf+Tp: Frame 0 in transit
    RECEIVER->>CHANNEL: Tf+Tp: Send ACK 0
    CHANNEL->>SENDER: Tf+Tp to Tf+2Tp: ACK 0 in transit
    SENDER->>CHANNEL: Tf+2Tp to 2Tf+2Tp: Transmit Frame 1
    CHANNEL->>RECEIVER: 2Tf+2Tp to 2Tf+3Tp: Frame 1 in transit
    RECEIVER->>CHANNEL: 2Tf+3Tp: Send ACK 1
    CHANNEL->>SENDER: 2Tf+3Tp to 2Tf+4Tp: ACK 1 in transit
    SENDER->>CHANNEL: 2Tf+4Tp to 3Tf+4Tp: Transmit Frame 2
    Note over SENDER,RECEIVER: Pattern repeats for Frame 2
```

**Key Labels for the Diagram:**
- **X-axis:** Time (in seconds or ms).
- **Y-axis (Top Row — Sender):** Active only during $T_f$ for each frame; completely idle during $2T_p$ waiting period.
- **Y-axis (Middle Row — Channel):** Carries the frame forward for $T_p$, then carries the ACK backward for $T_p$.
- **Y-axis (Bottom Row — Receiver):** Active only when a frame arrives and an ACK is dispatched.

**(7 Marks awarded for correctness, labels, and inclusion of idle time and propagation delay.)**

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Numerical Solution**

**Step 1: Calculate Propagation Time $T_p$.**

Given: Distance $d = 1000 \text{ km} = 10^6$ m, Velocity $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Transmission Time $T_f$.**

Given: Frame size $L = 1500$ bytes $= 12000$ bits, Bandwidth $R = 10^9$ bps.

$$
T_f = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12000}{10^9} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 12 \text{ μs} = 0.012 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Parameter $a$.**

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_f} = \frac{5 \text{ ms}}{0.012 \text{ ms}} \approx 416.67
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Channel Utilization $U$.**

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(416.67)} = \frac{1}{834.33} \approx 0.001198
$$

In percentage: **$U \approx 0.12\%$**

**Step 5: Calculate Wasted Capacity.**

$$
\text{Wasted \%} = (1 - U) \times 100\% = (1 - 0.001198) \times 100\% \approx 99.88\%
$$

> **Final Answer Summary:** $T_p = 5$ ms, $T_f = 12$ μs, Wasted Capacity $\approx$ **99.88%**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Simplex Stop-and-Wait** is a Data Link Layer protocol for error-free, unidirectional channels.
- [ ] The sender transmits **one frame**, then **stops and waits** for an ACK.
- [ ] The **total cycle time** is $T_t = T_f + 2T_p + T_a$ (round-trip propagation is $2T_p$).
- [ ] The critical dimensionless parameter is $a = T_p / T_f$ (propagation-to-transmission ratio).
- [ ] **Channel Utilization:** $U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}$ (when $T_a \approx 0$).
- [ ] **Throughput:** $\text{Throughput} = U \times R$ (measured in bps).
- [ ] Stop-and-Wait is **inefficient for high BDP** links (e.g., satellites, long fiber, high bandwidth).
- [ ] If $a$ is **small**, utilization is **high**. If $a$ is **large**, utilization is **low**.
- [ ] The "Stop" phase is the *idle period* where the channel is wasted — this is the protocol's primary drawback.
- [ ] This protocol is the **conceptual foundation** for Sliding Window protocols (Go-Back-N ARQ, Selective Repeat ARQ).
- [ ] Always distinguish between **Utilization** (dimensionless ratio) and **Throughput** (bits per second).
- [ ] In KTU board problems, always verify whether to **include or neglect** $T_a$ based on the problem statement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol for Noisy Channel, Sliding Window Protocols: One-Bit Sliding Window Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol for Noisy Channel, Sliding Window Protocols: One-Bit Sliding Window Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 The Problem of a Noisy Channel

In a **noise-free ideal channel**, every transmitted frame arrives correctly at the receiver, and the Stop-and-Wait protocol works perfectly — the sender transmits one frame, waits for an Acknowledgment (ACK), then sends the next. However, **real-world channels are noisy**: bits get corrupted due to electrical interference, signal attenuation, or thermal noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Noisy Channel** is a communication medium in which transmitted frames have a non-zero probability of being damaged (bit errors) or lost entirely. The protocol must therefore incorporate **error detection, retransmission, and duplicate detection** mechanisms to guarantee reliable delivery.

### 1.2 Why Simple Stop-and-Wait Fails on a Noisy Channel

In the **basic Simplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol (noise-free case)**, the receiver sends a positive ACK assuming the frame is always correct. On a noisy channel, this breaks down catastrophically because:

1. A **frame may be damaged in transit** — receiver discards it, but sender never knows.
2. An **ACK may be lost or damaged** — sender waits forever (deadlock).
3. A **NAK may itself get lost** — sender never retransmits.
4. **Duplicate frames** can arise if a delayed ACK causes the sender to retransmit a frame that the receiver actually got.

### 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Repetitive Postman"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine sending a registered letter through the postal service. The postman needs a signed receipt (ACK) before sending the next letter. If the letter is **lost**, the postman must resend it. If the **receipt is lost**, the postman resends the letter — but you, having already received the original, must **return the duplicate** by saying, "I already got this one!" The mechanism of **numbering letters 1, 2, 3…** and rejecting any letter that does not match the expected next number is exactly how the Noisy-Channel Stop-and-Wait and One-Bit Sliding Window protocols operate.

### 1.4 The Simplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol for Noisy Channel — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **Simplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol for a Noisy Channel** is a Data Link Layer protocol in which the sender transmits one frame, then waits for a control response from the receiver. The receiver checks the frame using an error-detection code (e.g., CRC). If the frame is correct and bears the **expected sequence number**, the receiver delivers it to the network layer and sends an **ACK** containing the **next expected sequence number**. If the frame is damaged or carries the **wrong sequence number**, the receiver discards it and sends a **NAK** (or a duplicate ACK) to trigger retransmission. Sequence numbers alternate between **0 and 1** to resolve ambiguity caused by lost or delayed acknowledgments.

### 1.5 The One-Bit Sliding Window Protocol — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **One-Bit Sliding Window Protocol** is a bidirectional (full-duplex) Data Link Layer protocol in which both sender and receiver maintain a **window of size 1**. Each side alternates between sending a data frame and waiting for an acknowledgment. Every outgoing frame contains a **sequence number (0 or 1)**, and every acknowledgment contains an **acknowledgment number (the sequence number of the next expected frame)**. This unifies the data and ACK channels into a single bidirectional frame exchange, achieving reliability over a noisy channel while using minimal buffering — only **one frame in transit in each direction at a time**.

### 1.6 Key Metrics Introduced

- **Propagation Time ($T_p$)** — Time for one bit to travel from sender to receiver.
- **Transmission Time ($T_t$ or $T_{frame}$)** — Time to push all bits of a frame onto the medium.
- **Channel Utilization ($U$)** — Fraction of channel capacity used for useful data.
- **Sequence Number Space** — Range of distinct sequence numbers (here: $\{0, 1\}$).
- **Maximum Window Size ($W_{max}$)** — Number of unacknowledged frames allowed (here: $1$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Logic of Simplex Stop-and-Wait on a Noisy Channel

The protocol is a **four-event state machine** at both sender and receiver. The steps below are exhaustive and aligned with the Forouzan's *Data Communications and Networking* treatment as commonly adopted in KTU modules.

### Sender-Side Algorithm (Logical Flow)

1. **Initialize** sequence number $N_S = 0$.
2. Fetch a **data packet** from the Network Layer.
3. Construct a **frame** = `[Data | SeqNum = $N_S$ | CRC]` and transmit it.
4. **Start a timer** with timeout duration $T_{out}$.
5. **Wait** for one of three events:
   - **(a) ACK received with next-expected number = $N_S \oplus 1$:** Stop timer, invert $N_S \leftarrow 1 - N_S$, go to Step 2.
   - **(b) NAK received OR duplicate ACK received:** Stop timer, go to Step 3 (retransmit same frame).
   - **(c) Timer expires:** Go to Step 3 (retransmit — assumes frame or ACK was lost).
6. Repeat indefinitely.

### Receiver-Side Algorithm (Logical Flow)

1. **Initialize** expected sequence number $N_R = 0$.
2. **Wait** for an incoming frame.
3. On frame arrival:
   - Run **CRC check** on the frame.
   - If **CRC fails** (frame damaged) → Send **NAK**, go to Step 2.
   - If **CRC passes AND SeqNum == $N_R$** → Deliver `Data` to Network Layer, send **ACK** with number $1 - N_R$, set $N_R \leftarrow 1 - N_R$, go to Step 2.
   - If **CRC passes AND SeqNum $\neq N_R$** (duplicate frame) → Discard frame, send **ACK** with number $1 - N_R$ (i.e., the number the sender currently expects), go to Step 2.

### Why Sequence Numbers are Mandatory (The "Why" behind Step 3)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Reason:** If the sender's ACK (or NAK) is lost, the sender will retransmit the *same* frame after the timeout. The receiver must be able to recognize that this is a **retransmission**, not a new frame, so it does not deliver the same packet twice to the Network Layer. A 1-bit sequence number (0 or 1) is the minimum information required to distinguish "new" from "old."

## 2.2 Operational Logic of the One-Bit Sliding Window Protocol

The One-Bit Sliding Window Protocol is essentially a **bidirectional, merged** version of the noisy-channel stop-and-wait. The two simplex channels (data forward, ACK reverse) are fused into a **single full-duplex channel** carrying both data and acknowledgments inside the same frame structure.

### Conceptual Model

- Both stations have **two roles simultaneously**: a sender (window for outgoing data) and a receiver (window for incoming data).
- A single frame carries:
  - `seq` — sequence number of the **data portion** (0 or 1).
  - `ack` — acknowledgment number of the **next expected data frame** (0 or 1).
  - `packet` — payload from the Network Layer (may be empty in a pure ACK frame).
  - `checksum` — error detection code.

### Sender Station Logic (in each role)

- Maintains `next_frame_to_send` ($N_S$) and `frame_expected` ($N_R$).
- When allowed to send:
  - If data is available from Network Layer → construct frame with `seq = $N_S$` and `ack = $N_R$`.
  - If no data is available → construct a **pure ACK frame** with empty packet, `seq = $N_S$` (still alternates!), and `ack = $N_R$`.
  - Save a copy of the frame (for possible retransmission) and send it.
- Start timer; on ACK/NAK or timeout → retransmit if no new ACK arrives.

### Receiver Station Logic

- On frame arrival:
  - If `seq == $N_R$` and CRC OK → deliver data, slide $N_R \leftarrow 1 - N_R$, set `ack = $N_R$`.
  - If `seq == $1 - N_R$` and CRC OK → this is a retransmission; do **not** deliver duplicate; send ACK with `ack = $N_R$` again.
  - If CRC fails → ignore (sender will retransmit on timeout).
- If data to send → piggyback ACK on data frame; else send standalone ACK frame.

## 2.3 Piggybacking — A Critical Optimization

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piggybacking** is the technique of including an acknowledgment inside an outgoing data frame (in the `ack` field) instead of sending a separate, dedicated ACK frame. This **halves the number of control frames** on the channel. The cost is a small added delay — the sender may have to wait up to a brief timeout for a data frame to attach the ACK to.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Parameter / Quantity** | **Formula** | **Meaning / Use** |
|---|---|---|
| Transmission time of a frame | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = frame length (bits), $R$ = link bandwidth (bps) |
| Propagation time | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = link length (m), $v$ = signal propagation speed (m/s) |
| Parameter $a$ | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$ | Ratio of propagation to transmission time |
| Channel utilization (Stop-and-Wait, noise-free) | $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | Fraction of time the sender is busy transmitting useful data |
| Cycle time (noisy, with retransmissions) | $T_{cycle} = T_t + T_p + T_{ack} + T_p + n \cdot T_{retransmit}$ | Total wall-clock time for one successful frame delivery |
| Mean number of transmissions | $E[N] = \dfrac{1}{1 - p}$ | Geometric mean; $p$ = frame error probability |
| Throughput on noisy channel | $S = \dfrac{L}{T_t \cdot E[N]}$ | Effective useful bits per second delivered |
| Maximum window size (Go-Back-N bound) | $W \le 2^k - 1$ | $k$ = sequence number bits; for $k=1$, $W_{max}=1$ |
| One-Bit Window — required seq bits | $k = 1$ | Window size $W = 1 \Rightarrow 1$ bit suffices to distinguish 2 frames |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Cheat-Sheet Reminder:** For a 1-bit sliding window, the protocol **must alternate** sequence numbers on every frame (data or pure-ACK), because the receiver has only two valid states — "expecting 0" or "expecting 1." Sending two consecutive frames with the same `seq` would break duplicate detection.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stop-and-Wait Noisy Channel** — Used in legacy half-duplex radio links, satellite command channels, and simple IoT sensor protocols (e.g., LoRaWAN class-A confirmed uplinks).
- **One-Bit Sliding Window** — Conceptually identical to **HDLC Asynchronous Balanced Mode (ABM)** and the **PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol)** when configured with no extended sequence numbers. Forms the pedagogical foundation for understanding larger-window protocols like **Go-Back-N ARQ** and **Selective Repeat ARQ**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Channel Utilization for Stop-and-Wait on a Noisy Channel

### Setting Up the Problem

We want to find the **channel utilization** $U$ — the fraction of total time the channel carries *useful* data — for a single successful frame delivery, accounting for the fact that **a frame may have to be transmitted $N$ times** before it gets through.

### Defining the Variables

Let:
- $T_t$ = transmission time of one data frame $= L / R$
- $T_p$ = one-way propagation time
- $T_{ack}$ = transmission time of the ACK frame
- $p$ = probability that a single transmission (data or ACK) is received in error

### Step 1: Compute the cycle time for ONE attempt

If the first transmission succeeds:

$$
T_{cycle,1} = T_t + T_p + T_{ack} + T_p
$$

Here, $T_t$ is the data transmission, $2T_p$ accounts for round-trip propagation, and $T_{ack}$ is the ACK transmission time.

### Step 2: Model the number of attempts as a Geometric Random Variable

The probability that the first transmission is successful (data arrives intact AND ACK arrives intact) is:

$$
P_1 = (1 - p_d)(1 - p_a) = (1 - p)^2
$$

where $p_d$ is the data-frame error probability and $p_a$ is the ACK-frame error probability. We assume symmetry: $p_d = p_a = p$ for simplicity, as is standard in KTU derivations.

So the probability that exactly $N = n$ attempts are needed is:

$$
P(N = n) = (1 - P_1)^{n-1} \cdot P_1
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Expected Number of Transmissions $E[N]$

This is the well-known mean of the geometric distribution:

$$
E[N] = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n \cdot P_1 \cdot (1 - P_1)^{n-1}
$$

Using the standard result $\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} n x^{n-1} = \dfrac{1}{(1-x)^2}$ with $x = 1 - P_1$:

$$
E[N] = \dfrac{1}{P_1} = \dfrac{1}{(1-p)^2}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Expected Cycle Time

$$
E[T_{cycle}] = E[N] \cdot T_{cycle,1} = \dfrac{T_t + 2T_p + T_{ack}}{(1-p)^2}
$$

### Step 5: Compute the Channel Utilization

Utilization is "useful transmission time" over "total elapsed time":

$$
U = \dfrac{T_t}{E[T_{cycle}]} = \dfrac{T_t \, (1-p)^2}{T_t + 2T_p + T_{ack}}
$$

### Step 6: Express in Terms of the Standard Parameter $a$

Defining $a = T_p / T_t$ and assuming $T_{ack} \ll T_t$ (negligible ACK time, as is common in KTU textbook problems):

$$
U = \dfrac{(1-p)^2}{1 + 2a}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the KTU exam-standard formula.** When $p = 0$ (noise-free), it reduces to the classical $U = 1/(1+2a)$. When $a \to 0$ (very short link), $U \to (1-p)^2$, showing that even on a perfect short link, noise caps utilization below 1.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Noisy Stop-and-Wait vs. One-Bit Sliding Window

| **Aspect** | **Noisy Simplex Stop-and-Wait** | **One-Bit Sliding Window** |
|---|---|---|
| Direction of communication | Simplex (one-way data, return ACK) | Full-duplex (bidirectional) |
| Window size | 1 (data side) | 1 (each direction) |
| Sequence number bits | 1 | 1 |
| ACK mechanism | Separate control frame | Piggybacked on data frame |
| Piggybacking supported | No (simplex) | Yes (with bounded delay) |
| Handles lost data frame | Yes (via timeout) | Yes (via timeout) |
| Handles lost ACK | Yes (via seq num) | Yes (via seq num) |
| Handles premature timeout | Yes (via seq num duplicate) | Yes (via seq num duplicate) |
| Maximum throughput at high $a$ | $\approx \dfrac{1}{2a}$ | $\approx \dfrac{1}{2a}$ |
| Buffer at receiver | 1 frame | 1 frame |
| Used in real systems | Legacy half-duplex, IoT | HDLC ABM, PPP (pedagogical) |

## 3.3 Derivation: Why One Bit is Sufficient for Window Size 1

A window of size $W = 1$ means the sender can transmit one frame and must wait for its acknowledgment. The receiver can be in one of exactly two states:

1. **Expecting frame with sequence number 0** (i.e., the last correctly received frame had seq 1).
2. **Expecting frame with sequence number 1** (i.e., the last correctly received frame had seq 0).

To uniquely encode 2 states, we need $\lceil \log_2(2) \rceil = 1$ bit. Therefore, **1-bit sequence numbers are optimal** — neither sufficient (since 0 bits cannot distinguish) nor wasteful (2 bits would be redundant).

$$
k_{min} = \lceil \log_2(W + 1) \rceil = \lceil \log_2(2) \rceil = 1 \text{ bit}
$$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A 1 Mbps link connects two stations 500 m apart. Frame size = 1000 bits. Propagation speed $= 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. The probability of a single transmission error is $p = 0.1$. Compute the channel utilization for:
> (a) Noise-free Stop-and-Wait.
> (b) Noisy Stop-and-Wait.
> (c) One-Bit Sliding Window (qualitative answer).

### Step A: Compute $T_t$, $T_p$, and $a$

$$
T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{1000}{10^6} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_p = \dfrac{d}{v} = \dfrac{500}{2 \times 10^8} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 0.0025 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{0.0025}{1} = 0.0025
$$

### Step B: Noise-free Utilization

$$
U_{clean} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 0.005} = \dfrac{1}{1.005} \approx 0.9950 = 99.50\%
$$

### Step C: Noisy Utilization (using the formula derived above)

$$
U_{noisy} = \dfrac{(1-p)^2}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{(0.9)^2}{1.005} = \dfrac{0.81}{1.005} \approx 0.8059 = 80.60\%
$$

### Step D: Interpretation

- The noise-free link is essentially perfectly utilized because propagation is negligible compared to transmission ($a \ll 1$).
- A **10% per-transmission error rate** drops utilization to about **80.6%**, since roughly $1/(1-p)^2 \approx 1.23$ transmissions are needed on average.
- For the One-Bit Sliding Window, the utilization formula is **the same** as in (b), with the added benefit of piggybacking reducing $T_{ack}$ overhead in bidirectional traffic.

## 3.5 Derivation of Timer-Timeout Setting

The timeout must be set long enough to include:

$$
T_{out} \ge T_p + T_{ack} + T_p = 2T_p + T_{ack}
$$

In practice, KTU problems assume $T_{out} = 2T_p + T_{ack}$ (the minimum that covers round-trip). If set shorter → **premature timeout** → unnecessary retransmission. If set much longer → **wasted idle time** after a lost frame. The protocol's robustness depends critically on choosing $T_{out}$ correctly.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Noisy Stop-and-Wait (Successful Case with One Retransmission)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant R as Receiver
    participant T as NetworkLayer
    Note over S,R: Initial State: S expects to send seq=0, R expects seq=0
    S->>R: Frame(seq=0, data=P0, CRC)
    Note right of R: CRC check FAILS (bit error)
    R-->>S: NAK (or duplicate ACK)
    Note over S: Stop timer, do NOT advance seq
    S->>R: Frame(seq=0, data=P0, CRC)
    Note right of R: CRC check OK, seq==0 expected
    R->>T: Deliver P0
    R-->>S: ACK(seq=1, ack=1)
    Note over S: Stop timer, advance seq to 1
    S->>R: Frame(seq=1, data=P1, CRC)
    Note right of R: CRC check OK, seq==1 expected
    R->>T: Deliver P1
    R-->>S: ACK(seq=0, ack=0)
    Note over S: Stop timer, advance seq to 0
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Sender Side of Noisy Stop-and-Wait

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Ready
    Ready --> WaitACK: Send frame with seq=Ns, start timer
    WaitACK --> Ready: ACK received with next=Ns XOR 1
    WaitACK --> WaitACK: NAK received, retransmit same frame
    WaitACK --> WaitACK: Timeout, retransmit same frame
    Ready --> Ready: Toggle Ns after successful ACK
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Receiver Side of Noisy Stop-and-Wait

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Expect0
    Expect0 --> Expect1: Frame CRC OK AND seq==0, deliver, send ACK(1)
    Expect1 --> Expect0: Frame CRC OK AND seq==1, deliver, send ACK(0)
    Expect0 --> Expect0: Frame CRC OK AND seq==1 (duplicate), send ACK(0)
    Expect1 --> Expect1: Frame CRC OK AND seq==0 (duplicate), send ACK(1)
    Expect0 --> Expect0: Frame CRC fails, send NAK
    Expect1 --> Expect1: Frame CRC fails, send NAK
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — One-Bit Sliding Window: Full-Duplex Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StationA
        A_NL[Network Layer A]
        A_SEND[Sender Buffer: next_seq=Ns_A]
        A_RECV[Receiver Buffer: frame_expected=Nr_A]
        A_TX[Transmitter]
        A_RX[Receiver Physical]
    end
    subgraph Channel
        CH((Noisy Full-Duplex Link))
    end
    subgraph StationB
        B_RX[Receiver Physical]
        B_RECV[Receiver Buffer: frame_expected=Nr_B]
        B_SEND[Sender Buffer: next_seq=Ns_B]
        B_TX[Transmitter]
        B_NL[Network Layer B]
    end
    A_TX -- "Frame seq=Ns_A, ack=Nr_A" --> CH
    CH --> B_RX
    B_RX --> B_RECV
    B_RECV --> B_NL
    B_RECV -- "On correct seq" --> B_SEND
    B_TX -- "Frame seq=Ns_B, ack=Nr_B" --> CH
    CH --> A_RX
    A_RX --> A_RECV
    A_RECV --> A_NL
    A_RECV -- "On correct seq" --> A_SEND
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — One-Bit Sliding Window: Lost Frame + Lost ACK Recovery

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Station A
    participant B as Station B
    Note over A,B: Both have Ns=0, Nr=0 initially
    A->>B: Frame(seq=0, ack=0, data=P0)
    B->>B: CRC OK, seq==0, deliver P0, set Nr=1
    B-->>A: Frame(seq=0, ack=1, data=Q0)
    Note left of A: ACK frame LOST IN TRANSIT
    A->>A: Timer expires for P0
    A->>B: Frame(seq=0, ack=1, data=P0_retransmit)
    Note right of B: seq==0 BUT Nr is already 1, so this is a DUPLICATE
    B-->>A: Frame(seq=0, ack=1, data=Q0_again)
    Note left of A: Now A receives the new ACK, advances Ns to 1
    Note over A,B: Protocol converges; no duplicate delivery
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The One-Bit Sliding Window is *robust to every combination* of single-frame loss in either direction because the **single sequence-number bit alternates on every transmission**, giving the receiver an unambiguous way to discriminate new data from retransmissions and silent timeouts from genuine losses.

## 4.6 Mermaid Flow Chart — Decision Logic for "What to Send?" in One-Bit Sliding Window

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Station wakes up]) --> q1{Network Layer has<br/>packet to send?}
    q1 -- Yes --> buildData[Build data frame: seq=Ns, ack=Nr, data=packet]
    q1 -- No --> buildAck[Build ACK-only frame: seq=Ns, ack=Nr, data=empty]
    buildData --> saveCopy[Save copy of frame in retransmit buffer]
    buildAck --> noCopy[No need to save ACK-only]
    saveCopy --> send[Transmit frame, start timer]
    noCopy --> send2[Transmit frame]
    send --> wait[Wait for response or timeout]
    send2 --> wait
    wait --> ev{Response received?}
    ev -- Yes --> adv[Advance Ns to 1 XOR Ns]
    ev -- Timeout --> retrans[Retransmit saved frame, restart timer]
    adv --> start
    retrans --> wait
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Q: Why are sequence numbers essential in the Simplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol for a noisy channel? What is the minimum number of sequence number bits required for window size 1, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

Sequence numbers are essential to **detect duplicate frames** that arise when the sender retransmits a frame because the original ACK (or NAK) was lost or the timer expired prematurely. Without sequence numbers, the receiver would not be able to tell whether an arriving frame is a fresh transmission or a retransmission of an already-delivered packet — leading to **duplicate delivery** to the Network Layer.

For a window size of 1, the sender can have at most **one outstanding unacknowledged frame** at any time. The receiver therefore needs to distinguish only between "expecting frame 0" and "expecting frame 1." The minimum number of bits required to encode 2 distinct states is:

$$
k_{min} = \lceil \log_2(2) \rceil = 1 \text{ bit}
$$

Hence **1-bit sequence numbers (0 and 1)** are both necessary and sufficient. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: Differentiate between a NAK and a duplicate ACK in the context of the noisy-channel Stop-and-Wait protocol. Is the NAK strictly necessary?**

**Model Answer:**

A **NAK (Negative Acknowledgment)** is an explicit control frame sent by the receiver to inform the sender that the most recent frame was damaged (CRC failure). A **duplicate ACK** is a *re-send of the previous ACK* triggered when the receiver sees a frame with the correct CRC but a sequence number that does not match the expected one — implying the sender's previous frame was either lost or its ACK was lost.

The NAK is **not strictly necessary** because the sender will eventually retransmit on timer expiry. However, NAKs **reduce latency** by avoiding the wait for timeout, thereby improving channel utilization. Many practical systems (including TCP, conceptually) use duplicate ACKs as an implicit NAK rather than a separate NAK control frame. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students frequently **forget to state the timeout rule** explicitly — losing 2 marks.
> - Do **not** confuse "NAK" with "duplicate ACK" — they are *functionally equivalent* in this protocol but conceptually distinct.
> - In sliding window questions, **always show the piggybacking decision logic** for the case when no data is available — this is worth 2 marks alone.
> - Sequence number analysis (1 bit enough, alternation on *every* frame) is a favorite 2–3 mark sub-question. Do not skip it.

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Simplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol for a Noisy Channel** in detail. Clearly describe the sender and receiver algorithms, the role of sequence numbers, and the handling of damaged frames, lost ACKs, and duplicate frames. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 200 kbps satellite link connects two ground stations. The one-way propagation delay is 250 ms. Frames are 1000 bits long. ACK frames are 100 bits long. The probability that a single transmission (data or ACK) is received in error is $p = 0.05$. Compute the **expected channel utilization** assuming the Noisy Stop-and-Wait protocol is in use. State one limitation of this protocol for high-bandwidth-delay-product links. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Sender Algorithm (3 marks):**
The sender keeps a 1-bit sequence number $N_S \in \{0, 1\}$ initialized to 0. For each packet from the Network Layer:
1. Construct a frame containing `data`, `seq = $N_S$`, and a CRC.
2. Transmit the frame and start a timer of duration $T_{out} \ge 2T_p + T_{ack}$.
3. **Three possible events:**
   - ACK with number $1 - N_S$ received → stop timer, $N_S \leftarrow 1 - N_S$, send next frame.
   - NAK received (or duplicate ACK) → stop timer, retransmit the same frame.
   - Timeout → retransmit the same frame (assumes data or ACK was lost).

**Step 2 — Receiver Algorithm (2 marks):**
The receiver keeps expected sequence number $N_R$ initialized to 0. On every incoming frame:
- If **CRC fails** → send NAK, do not change $N_R$.
- If CRC OK and `seq == $N_R$` → deliver data to Network Layer, send ACK with number $1 - N_R$, set $N_R \leftarrow 1 - N_R$.
- If CRC OK and `seq $\neq N_R$` → frame is a duplicate; discard, send ACK with $1 - N_R$ again.

**Step 3 — Role of Sequence Numbers (1 mark):**
Sequence numbers are essential to **detect and discard duplicate frames** that occur when ACKs are lost or timers expire prematurely. With a window of size 1, exactly **1 bit** is needed to encode $\{0, 1\}$.

**Step 4 — Comprehensive Scenario Coverage (1 mark):**
The protocol correctly handles:
- **Damaged data frame** → NAK triggers retransmission.
- **Lost data frame** → timer expires, sender retransmits.
- **Damaged/lost ACK** → sender times out and retransmits; receiver's seq-num check discards the duplicate.
- **Premature timeout** → receiver detects duplicate via seq num, sends ACK to halt further retransmission.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute $T_t$ for data frames (1 mark):**

$$
T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{1000}{200 \times 10^3} = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T_{ack}$ for ACK frames (1 mark):**

$$
T_{ack} = \dfrac{L_{ack}}{R} = \dfrac{100}{200 \times 10^3} = 0.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T_p$ (given) and parameter $a$ (1 mark):**

$$
T_p = 250 \text{ ms}, \quad a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{250}{5} = 50
$$

**Step 4 — Compute single-attempt cycle time (1 mark):**

$$
T_{cycle,1} = T_t + T_p + T_{ack} + T_p = 5 + 250 + 0.5 + 250 = 505.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute expected number of transmissions (1 mark):**

$$
E[N] = \dfrac{1}{(1-p)^2} = \dfrac{1}{(0.95)^2} = \dfrac{1}{0.9025} \approx 1.108
$$

**Step 6 — Compute expected utilization (1 mark):**

$$
E[U] = \dfrac{T_t}{E[N] \cdot T_{cycle,1}} = \dfrac{5}{1.108 \times 505.5} = \dfrac{5}{560.09} \approx 0.00893 = 0.893\%
$$

**Step 7 — Limitation (1 mark):**
For a high-bandwidth-delay-product link (large $a$ as shown here, $a = 50$), the channel spends **>99% of the time idle** waiting for acknowledgments. The protocol is **inefficient** because the sender can have only one outstanding frame at a time. The remedy is a **larger sliding window** (e.g., Go-Back-N or Selective Repeat ARQ) — but that is beyond the scope of this 1-bit protocol.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]

**Q: (a)** Describe the **One-Bit Sliding Window Protocol** with the help of sender and receiver state machines. Explain **piggybacking** and how the protocol handles lost frames and lost ACKs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 64 kbps link uses the One-Bit Sliding Window Protocol. The one-way propagation delay is 20 ms and the frame size is 256 bits. ACK-only frames are 64 bits. Calculate the **maximum throughput** and the **channel utilization**. If the link error probability is $p = 0.01$, what is the **expected number of transmissions** per successful frame? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Protocol Description (2 marks):**
The One-Bit Sliding Window Protocol is a **full-duplex** Data Link Layer protocol where each station is simultaneously a sender and a receiver. Both stations maintain:
- $N_S$: sequence number of the next data frame to send.
- $N_R$: sequence number of the next data frame expected.

Each frame contains fields: `seq`, `ack`, `packet`, `checksum`. The window size in each direction is 1.

**Step 2 — Sender Logic (2 marks):**
When the station is permitted to send:
- If the Network Layer has a packet, it constructs a **data frame** with `seq = $N_S$`, `ack = $N_R$`, and the packet. It saves a copy for retransmission.
- If no data is available but $N_R$ has changed (meaning an incoming frame was correctly received), it sends a **pure ACK frame** with empty packet, `seq = $N_S$`, and `ack = $N_R$`.
- It starts a timer; on ACK receipt it advances $N_S \leftarrow 1 - N_S$; on timeout it retransmits the saved frame.

**Step 3 — Receiver Logic (1 mark):**
On receiving a frame:
- If `seq == $N_R$` and CRC OK → deliver data, set $N_R \leftarrow 1 - N_R$, send ACK with `ack = $N_R$`.
- If `seq $\neq N_R$` and CRC OK → duplicate; send ACK with the *current* `ack = $N_R$` (do not deliver).
- If CRC fails → ignore; sender will retransmit on timeout.

**Step 4 — Piggybacking (1 mark):**
Piggybacking is the technique of **embedding the acknowledgment** for the opposite direction inside a data frame traveling in one's own direction. This reduces the number of separate control frames on the channel by up to 50%. If no data frame is available within a short wait, a **standalone ACK frame** is sent.

**Step 5 — Error & Loss Handling (1 mark):**
- **Lost data frame:** Sender's timer expires → retransmit with same `seq`. Receiver detects duplicate via `seq $\neq N_R$`, sends ACK, suppresses duplicate delivery.
- **Lost ACK:** Sender's timer expires for the data frame → retransmits. Receiver's duplicate-detection logic (step 3) prevents duplicate delivery and the new ACK is eventually received.
- **Lost in both directions:** The alternating sequence numbers (0,1,0,1,…) prevent the protocol from getting permanently stuck.

#### Model Solution — (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute $T_t$, $T_{ack}$, $T_p$ (1 mark):**

$$
T_t = \dfrac{256}{64000} = 4 \text{ ms}, \quad T_{ack} = \dfrac{64}{64000} = 1 \text{ ms}, \quad T_p = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute parameter $a$ (1 mark):**

$$
a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{20}{4} = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cycle time for one attempt (1 mark):**

$$
T_{cycle,1} = T_t + T_p + T_{ack} + T_p = 4 + 20 + 1 + 20 = 45 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum throughput (clean channel) (1 mark):**

$$
S_{max} = \dfrac{L}{T_{cycle,1}} = \dfrac{256 \text{ bits}}{0.045 \text{ s}} = 5688.89 \text{ bps} \approx 5.69 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Channel utilization (clean channel) (1 mark):**

$$
U = \dfrac{T_t}{T_{cycle,1}} = \dfrac{4}{45} = 0.0889 = 8.89\%
$$

Equivalently: $U = 1/(1+2a) = 1/(1+10) = 1/11 = 0.0909 \approx 9.09\%$ (the small discrepancy is due to ignoring $T_{ack}$ in the textbook formula).

**Step 6 — Expected transmissions under $p = 0.01$ (1 mark):**

$$
E[N] = \dfrac{1}{(1-p)^2} = \dfrac{1}{(0.99)^2} = \dfrac{1}{0.9801} \approx 1.0203
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
The link is **bandwidth-delay-product-limited** with $a = 5$. Maximum throughput is only $\approx 9\%$ of the raw link capacity (5.69 kbps out of 64 kbps). The expected number of transmissions is only marginally above 1 because the error probability is small. The dominant inefficiency is **not noise** but the **stop-and-wait waiting time**. This motivates higher-window protocols (Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Simplex Stop-and-Wait (Noisy) and One-Bit Sliding Window**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **Noisy channel** requires **error detection + retransmission (ARQ)** plus **sequence numbers** for duplicate suppression.
- [ ] The three failure modes the protocol must handle: **damaged frame, lost frame, lost/damaged ACK** (and combinations thereof).
- [ ] **Sequence number** is the *only* mechanism that disambiguates a retransmission from a new frame.
- [ ] For **window size 1**, exactly **1 bit** of sequence number is both necessary and sufficient: $k = \lceil \log_2(W+1) \rceil = 1$.

### Sender-Side
- [ ] Sender maintains one bit: $N_S \in \{0,1\}$, alternates only on **correct ACK receipt** (not on NAK or timeout).
- [ ] On **NAK, duplicate ACK, or timeout** → retransmit the *same* frame, do **not** toggle $N_S$.
- [ ] Timer must satisfy: $T_{out} \ge 2T_p + T_{ack}$.

### Receiver-Side
- [ ] Receiver maintains $N_R \in \{0,1\}$, the sequence number of the next expected frame.
- [ ] On **CRC fail** → NAK, $N_R$ unchanged.
- [ ] On **CRC OK and seq == $N_R$** → deliver to Network Layer, ACK with $1 - N_R$, toggle $N_R$.
- [ ] On **CRC OK and seq $\neq N_R$** → discard (duplicate), re-send ACK with current $1 - N_R$.

### One-Bit Sliding Window Specifics
- [ ] **Bidirectional (full-duplex)**: each station is both sender and receiver.
- [ ] **Piggybacking**: ACK is included in outgoing data frame, saving one control frame per round-trip.
- [ ] When no data is available, send a **pure ACK frame** with empty payload, but with `seq` field still toggled (so receiver can detect duplicates).
- [ ] Window size = 1 in each direction → 1 sequence-number bit per direction.

### Quantitative Formulas (must memorize)
- [ ] $T_t = L/R$, $T_p = d/v$, $a = T_p/T_t$
- [ ] $U_{\text{clean}} = 1/(1+2a)$
- [ ] $U_{\text{noisy}} = (1-p)^2 / (1+2a)$
- [ ] $E[N] = 1/(1-p)^2$
- [ ] $k_{\text{min}} = \lceil \log_2(W+1) \rceil$

### Real-World Links
- [ ] Noisy Stop-and-Wait → simple IoT/LoRaWAN confirmed uplinks, satellite command channels.
- [ ] One-Bit Sliding Window → pedagogical foundation for **HDLC ABM** and **PPP**; conceptual basis for **TCP sliding window** (extended with much larger window).
- [ ] Both protocols **fail to utilize high-BDP links** — this is why modern systems use larger window ARQ (Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat) or hybrid ARQ.

### Common KTU Exam Pitfalls
- [ ] Forgetting to mention that **timer-expiry retransmission is what recovers lost ACKs** — students often think only NAKs handle errors.
- [ ] Saying "1 bit sequence number is enough" without explaining **why** (i.e., $W=1$ gives 2 states, $\log_2 2 = 1$).
- [ ] Confusing "damaged frame" (receiver sends NAK) with "duplicate frame" (receiver sends ACK) — opposite responses.
- [ ] In piggybacking, forgetting to specify the **fallback** case (pure ACK frame when no data to send).
- [ ] For one-bit sliding window, stating that the **ACK field is also a sequence number** — many students think ACKs are just booleans. The ACK field carries $N_R$, the next expected sequence number from the other side.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protocol Using Go-Back-N
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Protocol Using Go-Back-N**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Go-Back-N Automatic Repeat Request (GBN ARQ)** is a specific sliding-window protocol used in the Data Link Layer of the OSI reference model for reliable, in-sequence delivery of data frames over an unreliable physical channel. It is formally defined as a *continuous transmission* ARQ scheme in which the sender is permitted to transmit up to **N** unacknowledged frames (the **send window size**) before being forced to halt and wait for acknowledgments. The defining operational constraint of Go-Back-N is that the **receiver only accepts frames that arrive in strict numerical sequence**. Any out-of-order or erroneous frame causes the receiver to silently discard the offending frame and every subsequent frame, and the sender is required to *go back* and retransmit that frame and all frames that were sent after it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Go-Back-N is positioned under the broader topic of *Data Link Layer protocols* and *Sliding Window Flow Control*. Students must master the sender–receiver finite state logic, sequence number space, and timer management for the KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Key Protocol Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Constraint |
|---|---|---|
| Send Window Size | $N$ | $1 \le N \le 2^k - 1$ |
| Receive Window Size | $1$ | Always exactly one |
| Sequence Number Bits | $k$ | Positive integer (commonly 3, 4, or 8) |
| Max Sequence Number | $2^k - 1$ | Modulo-$2^k$ arithmetic |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Train Conductor's Roll Call"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** Imagine a school teacher (the *Sender*) reading a story aloud to a class (the *Receiver*), but she hands out a *numbered page* to every student before moving on. She is allowed to keep reading up to **N** pages ahead without stopping (the *sending window*).
>
> If a student raises a hand and says, *"Ma'am, page 7 is missing/torn"* (a *corrupted frame*), she does **not** ask the student about page 8, 9, or 10. She simply says: *"Tear those aside. I will re-read from page 7 onwards."* The teacher then restarts the roll-call from page 7 — this is the **"Go-Back-N"** action. The students (receiver) keep only page 6, the last good one, and throw away 8, 9, 10 because they arrived after the gap.
>
> Meanwhile, the teacher uses a single kitchen timer (the *frame timer*) set when she sends page 7. If the timer rings before a confirmation ("We got page 7 correctly!") arrives, she assumes page 7 was lost in the hallway and re-reads everything from 7 onwards.

### 1.4 Why Go-Back-N Exists

Pure **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** is inefficient on high-bandwidth-delay-product links because the sender idles while waiting for an acknowledgment after every single frame. Go-Back-N *pipelining* solves this by keeping multiple frames in flight, while still providing reliable delivery using a single timer. Compared to its more sophisticated sibling, **Selective Repeat ARQ**, Go-Back-N is *simpler* (only one timer, no per-frame buffering at the receiver) but *less bandwidth-efficient* when the bit error rate is high, because it retransmits frames that may have arrived correctly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Send Window — Operational Rules

The sender maintains a sliding window of sequence numbers. At any instant it may transmit any frame whose sequence number falls in the current window. The window slides forward whenever a *cumulative* acknowledgment (ACK) arrives.

- **Lower edge of window** ($S$): Sequence number of the oldest unacknowledged frame.
- **Upper edge of window** ($S + N - 1$): Highest sequence number the sender is currently permitted to transmit.
- **Window slides** by $k$ positions when an ACK for sequence number $S$ arrives, where $k$ is the number of consecutive frames being acknowledged by the cumulative ACK.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single Timer Rule:** Go-Back-N uses **exactly one timer** for all outstanding frames. The timer is started when the *first* unacknowledged frame in the window is transmitted. It is restarted whenever an ACK arrives that advances the lower edge of the window, OR when it expires (causing retransmission of all unacknowledged frames).

### 2.2 The Receive Window — Operational Rules

The receiver in Go-Back-N is intentionally simple:

1. It expects the **next-in-sequence** frame number, denoted $R$.
2. On receiving a frame with seqnum $= R$:
   - Accept it, deliver data to the network layer.
   - Send a **cumulative ACK** $= R$ (meaning "I have received everything up to and including $R$").
   - Increment $R \pmod{2^k}$.
3. On receiving a frame with seqnum $\ne R$ (i.e., out of order, duplicate, or corrupted):
   - **Silently discard** the frame (do *not* deliver to network layer, do *not* buffer it).
   - Send a **duplicate ACK** $= R - 1 \pmod{2^k}$ to remind the sender of the last correctly received in-sequence frame.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why discard instead of buffer?** Buffering out-of-order frames is the job of **Selective Repeat ARQ**. By discarding, Go-Back-N keeps the receiver memory footprint at exactly **one frame** — a deliberate engineering trade-off favouring simplicity over link efficiency on noisy channels.

### 2.3 Sequence Number Constraint — The Modulo-$2^k$ Bound

Because both sender and receiver use modulo-$2^k$ arithmetic, the sequence space wraps around. To prevent ambiguity between a *new* frame and a *retransmitted* old frame, the maximum send window size is bounded:

$$N_{\max} = 2^k - 1$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse Go-Back-N ($N \le 2^k - 1$) with Selective Repeat ($N \le 2^{k-1}$). For Go-Back-N, the receiver window is 1, so the sender can safely use a window of $2^k - 1$. If the sender were allowed to use $2^k$, the receiver could not distinguish a retransmission from a new transmission after wrap-around.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units / Constraints |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N \le 2^k - 1$ | Maximum send window size | $k$ = sequence number bits |
| 2 | $U = \dfrac{N}{1 + 2a}$ | Link utilization (efficiency), $N < 1 + 2a$ | $0 \le U \le 1$ (dimensionless) |
| 3 | $U = 1$ | Saturation efficiency when $N \ge 1 + 2a$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{\text{Propagation Time}}{\text{Transmission Time}}$ | Bandwidth-Delay product factor | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Frame transmission time | Seconds |
| 6 | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | Propagation delay (one-way) | Seconds |
| 7 | $S_{eff} = U \cdot R = \dfrac{N \cdot L}{(1 + 2a) \cdot T_t}$ | Effective throughput | Frames/sec or bits/sec |
| 8 | $BDP = R \cdot T_p$ | Bandwidth-Delay Product | Bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Latex Table Safety Note:** All vertical bars in this table that would have been `\vert` have been deliberately replaced with the expression form to maintain Markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Go-Back-N (or its modern derivative, *cumulative-ack pipelined ARQ*) is widely used in:

- **Classical HDLC** (High-Level Data Link Control) standard family.
- **PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol)** for legacy dial-up and point-to-point links.
- **Wi-Fi (802.11)**'s historical Stop-and-Wait variant and several **Bluetooth** baseband modes.
- **Satellite link MACs** where the round-trip-time is large and Stop-and-Wait would be disastrous.

The principle of *one timer + cumulative ACK + sender retransmits from the gap* remains a foundational pattern taught in every networking curriculum worldwide.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Link Utilization $U$ for Go-Back-N

We start by defining the fundamental timing parameters on a point-to-point link.

**Step 1 — Frame transmission time.** The time to push an entire frame of $L$ bits onto the medium at line rate $R$ bits/sec:

$$T_t = \frac{L}{R} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 2 — One-way propagation time.** The time for one bit to traverse the physical medium of length $d$ metres at signal speed $s$ m/s:

$$T_p = \frac{d}{s} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Define the bandwidth-delay factor.** The standard networking parameter $a$ is the ratio:

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t}$$

This represents how many "frame-times" of idle wait the sender experiences per transmission if it used Stop-and-Wait.

**Step 4 — Establish the Stop-and-Wait baseline (for comparison).** In Stop-and-Wait, the sender transmits one frame ($T_t$), waits for the ACK to come back ($T_p$ on the forward path + $T_t^{ACK}$ for the ACK itself + $T_p$ on the reverse path). The cycle time $T_{cycle}^{SW}$ is:

$$T_{cycle}^{SW} = T_t + 2 T_p + T_t^{ACK}$$

Assuming $T_t^{ACK} \ll T_t$ (negligible ACK frame transmission time):

$$T_{cycle}^{SW} \approx T_t + 2 T_p = T_t (1 + 2a)$$

Stop-and-Wait utilization is therefore:

$$U_{SW} = \frac{T_t}{T_t (1 + 2a)} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}$$

**Step 5 — Generalize to Go-Back-N (no errors, ideal channel).** With Go-Back-N pipelining, the sender keeps transmitting $N$ frames back-to-back. The total time to launch $N$ frames is $N \cdot T_t$. After the ACK for the first frame returns, the pipeline is fully drained, and a new batch begins. The cycle time per "wave" of $N$ frames is:

$$T_{cycle}^{GBN} = N \cdot T_t + 2 T_p = T_t (N + 2a)$$

Therefore, link utilization (fraction of time the sender is actually pushing useful bits onto the wire) is:

$$U_{GBN} = \frac{N \cdot T_t}{T_t (N + 2a)} = \frac{N}{N + 2a}$$

**Step 6 — Express $U$ using the conventional $1 + 2a$ form.** Dividing numerator and denominator by $N$:

$$U_{GBN} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{2a}{N}} \quad \text{(valid for any } N \text{)}$$

Multiplying both numerator and denominator by $\dfrac{N}{1}$:

$$U_{GBN} = \frac{N}{1 + 2a} \cdot \frac{1}{N \cdot \frac{1}{N}} \quad \text{(intermediate — simplified below)}$$

A cleaner form is obtained by cross-multiplying, and noting that for very large $N$ the bound becomes the textbook formula:

$$U_{GBN} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{N}{1 + 2a} & \text{if } N < 1 + 2a \quad \text{(window-size-limited)} \\[6pt] 1 & \text{if } N \ge 1 + 2a \quad \text{(fully saturated)} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does $U$ saturate at 1?** When the window is wide enough to keep the pipeline continuously full — i.e., the sender can always launch a new frame the moment the previous ACK frees a slot — the channel is 100% utilized. Any further increase in $N$ yields no benefit.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Behavioural Comparison Across ARQ Schemes

| Property | Stop-and-Wait | **Go-Back-N** | Selective Repeat |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max send window $N$ | $1$ | $2^k - 1$ | $2^{k-1}$ |
| Receive window | $1$ | $1$ | $\le 2^{k-1}$ |
| Number of timers | $1$ (per frame) | $1$ (single, shared) | $N$ (one per frame) |
| Out-of-order buffering at receiver | No | No | Yes |
| Retransmission scope on one error | $1$ frame | All $N$ unACKed | Only the lost frame |
| Best-case efficiency | $\dfrac{1}{1+2a}$ | $\dfrac{N}{1+2a}$ (cap $1$) | $\dfrac{N}{1+2a}$ (cap $1$) |
| Worst-case efficiency (high BER) | $\dfrac{1}{1+2a} \cdot (1-P_f)$ | $\dfrac{N}{1+2a} \cdot (1-P_f) \cdot P_{reach}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+2a}$ (per frame) |
| Receiver complexity | Very low | Low | High |

Here $P_f$ is the per-frame bit error probability and $P_{reach}$ is the probability that all $N$ frames escape error.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style Problem)

**Problem:** A channel has $R = 1 \text{ Mbps}$ bandwidth, $T_p = 20 \text{ ms}$ one-way propagation delay, and frame length $L = 1000 \text{ bits}$. The sequence number field has $k = 3$ bits. Use Go-Back-N ARQ.

**Step 1 — Compute $T_t$:**

$$T_t = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bits/sec}} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $a$:**

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{20 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 20$$

**Step 3 — Compute max send window:**

$$N_{\max} = 2^k - 1 = 2^3 - 1 = 7$$

**Step 4 — Compute utilization.** Since $N = 7$ but $1 + 2a = 41$, the window is *bottlenecked*, so:

$$U_{GBN} = \frac{N}{1 + 2a} = \frac{7}{41} \approx 0.1707 \text{ or } 17.07\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute effective throughput:**

$$S_{eff} = U \cdot R = 0.1707 \times 10^6 \approx 1.707 \times 10^5 \text{ bps} \approx 170.7 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 6 — Effective frames per second:**

$$\text{Frames/sec} = \frac{S_{eff}}{L} = \frac{1.707 \times 10^5}{1000} \approx 170.7 \text{ frames/sec}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Even with pipelining of 7 frames, the link is only $\sim 17\%$ utilized because the high propagation delay dominates. To saturate this link the sender window would need to be $N \ge 41$, which would require $k \ge 6$ bits in the sequence number field — a classic KTU design trade-off.
