# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**Explain the functions of LCP and NCP in PPP.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Link Control Protocol (LCP):** Responsible for **establishing, configuring, maintaining, and terminating** the point-to-point link. It negotiates parameters such as maximum receive unit (MRU), authentication protocol (PAP/CHAP), magic number, error detection, and compression. LCP operates in the **Link Establishment** and **Link Termination** phases.

> **Network Control Protocol (NCP):** A family of protocols, one per network-layer protocol. It negotiates network-layer-specific parameters. For IPv4, the relevant NCP is **IPCP** (IP Control Protocol), used to assign/negotiate IP addresses, DNS addresses, and IP compression options. NCP operates in the **Network-Layer** phase, after LCP has set up the link.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2]`
**Define the channel allocation problem and list the five key assumptions used to model it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Channel Allocation Problem:** The problem of deciding *how to share a finite-capacity broadcast channel* among multiple competing stations so as to minimize collisions, maximize throughput, ensure fairness, and bound latency.

> **Five Key Assumptions:**
> 1. **Station Model** — $N$ independent stations, each generating frames at an average rate $\lambda$ frames/sec, with frames of fixed length $L$ bits.
> 2. **Single Channel Assumption** — All stations share one common broadcast channel of capacity $C$ bps.
> 3. **Collision Assumption** — Concurrent transmissions interfere and are garbled.
> 4. **Time Assumption** — Either *continuous* (transmit anytime) or *slotted* (transmit at slot boundaries).
> 5. **Carrier Sense Assumption** — Stations either *can* or *cannot* sense the channel before transmission.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **HDLC frame format**. Discuss the role of **bit stuffing** in HDLC with an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **three sub-protocols** of PPP and describe the **PPP frame format** with a diagram. Compare PPP with SLIP. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**HDLC Frame Format Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["Flag 01111110"] --> AD["Address 8 or 16 bits"]
    AD --> CT["Control 8 or 16 bits"]
    CT --> IF["Information Variable I-frames only"]
    IF --> FC["FCS 16 or 32 bits CRC"]
    FC --> F2["Flag 01111110"]
```

**Explanation of each field:**

1. **Flag (1 byte):** Both opening and closing delimiter — `01111110`. Used for synchronization.
2. **Address (1 or 2 bytes):** Identifies the secondary station. Always present; in unbalanced mode only one secondary, in extended addressing can have multiple.
3. **Control (1 or 2 bytes):** Identifies the frame type:
   - `0xxx xxxx` (bit 0 = 0) → **Information frame (I-frame)**
   - `10xx xxxx` (bits 1,0 = 10) → **Supervisory frame (S-frame)**
   - `11xx xxxx` (bits 1,0 = 11) → **Unnumbered frame (U-frame)**
4. **Information (variable):** User payload; only present in I-frames.
5. **FCS (Frame Check Sequence):** CRC error-detection field (16-bit CCITT or 32-bit IEEE polynomial).
6. **Flag:** Closing delimiter (same as opening).

**Bit Stuffing — Example:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Whenever the sender encounters **five consecutive `1` bits** in the data (excluding flags), it automatically inserts a `0` bit. The receiver removes any `0` that follows five `1`s.

**Example:** Original data (between flags): `0110111111100111111010`
- After 5 consecutive `1`s, insert a `0`:
  - `0110111111` → `0110111110 1`
  - `11111` → `11111 0`... (after stuffing) → `111110`

**Stuffed output:** `011011111011100111110010`
- Receiver removes the `0` after every sequence of five `1`s → recovers the original.

**Purpose:** Prevents accidental occurrence of the flag pattern `01111110` inside the payload, ensuring unambiguous frame delimitation.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**The Three Sub-Protocols of PPP:**

1. **LCP (Link Control Protocol):** Establishes, configures, tests, and terminates the data link. Functions: link establishment/configuration, error detection negotiation, authentication setup (PAP/CHAP), frame size negotiation.

2. **NCP (Network Control Protocol):** A family of protocols, one per network layer. For IPv4 → **IPCP**, for IPv6 → **IPV6CP**, etc. Handles IP address assignment, DNS negotiation, header compression.

3. **Multilink PPP (MP):** Combines multiple PPP physical links into one logical link (load balancing, increased bandwidth).

**PPP Frame Format Diagram (already shown in Section 4.2):** The frame contains Flag, Address (`0xFF`), Control (`0x03`), Protocol (2 bytes identifying the payload type), Payload, FCS, and Flag.

**PPP vs SLIP — Comparison Table:**

| Feature | SLIP | PPP |
|---|---|---|
| Standardization | De facto (RFC 1055) | De jure (RFC 1661, 1662) |
| Protocols Supported | IP only | Multiple (IP, IPX, AppleTalk, etc.) |
| Error Detection | None | CRC (FCS) |
| Authentication | None | PAP, CHAP |
| IP Address Assignment | Manual only | Dynamic (via NCP/IPCP) |
| Compression | None | Optional |
| Byte/Bit Stuffing | END byte | Byte stuffing `0x7D` |
| Link Testing/Diagnostics | None | LCP echo-request/reply |
| Status | Obsolete | Active standard |

> **Conclusion:** PPP is a **superset** of SLIP functionality and has completely replaced SLIP in modern deployments (dial-up, DSL, serial point-to-point).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** What is the **channel allocation problem**? Compare **static and dynamic channel allocation** with suitable examples. Why is dynamic allocation preferred in LANs? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the throughput expression for **Pure ALOHA** and **Slotted ALOHA**. Show that the maximum throughput of slotted ALOHA is **double** that of pure ALOHA. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Definition:** The channel allocation problem is the design challenge of *sharing a finite-capacity broadcast channel* among multiple stations in a way that minimizes collisions, maximizes throughput, ensures fairness, and bounds delay.

**Static Channel Allocation:**

- **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM):** The bandwidth $B$ is split into $N$ non-overlapping sub-bands. Each station transmits on its own sub-band continuously.
  - **Example:** Classical FM radio broadcasting.
- **Time Division Multiplexing (TDM):** Time is divided into slots; each station transmits in its assigned slot.
  - **Example:** Early T1/E1 digital telephony.

**Dynamic Channel Allocation:**

- Channel is not pre-assigned; stations *contend* for it.
- **Examples:** ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, Token Ring.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Static (FDM/TDM) | Dynamic (ALOHA/CSMA) |
|---|---|---|
| Channel assignment | Pre-determined, permanent | On-demand, contention-based |
| Performance under low load | Wastes bandwidth | Excellent |
| Performance under high load | Predictable but inefficient | Degrades due to collisions |
| Complexity | Low | Higher (requires sensing/backoff) |
| Burst tolerance | Poor | Excellent |
| LAN suitability | Inefficient for bursty LAN traffic | Preferred for LANs |

**Why Dynamic is Preferred in LANs:**

LAN traffic is **bursty** — most stations are idle most of the time, but when active they generate short bursts at high rate. Static allocation would dedicate capacity to idle stations, wasting up to $(N-1)/N$ of the channel. Dynamic allocation lets idle stations' capacity be used by active ones, yielding much higher aggregate throughput — exactly the property LANs require.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

> **Note:** The full derivation is provided in Section 3.1 and 3.2. We present the final results here.

**Pure ALOHA:**

$$\boxed{S_{\text{pure}} = G \cdot e^{-2G}}$$

Maximum occurs at $G = 0.5$:

$$S_{\max}^{\text{pure}} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.1839 \approx 18.4\%$$

**Slotted ALOHA:**

$$\boxed{S_{\text{slotted}} = G \cdot e^{-G}}$$

Maximum occurs at $G = 1$:

$$S_{\max}^{\text{slotted}} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679 \approx 36.8\%$$

**Ratio Proof (Slotted = 2× Pure):**

$$\frac{S_{\max}^{\text{slotted}}}{S_{\max}^{\text{pure}}} = \frac{1/e}{1/(2e)} = \frac{2e}{e} = 2$$

$$\boxed{\therefore \; S_{\max}^{\text{slotted}} = 2 \cdot S_{\max}^{\text{pure}}}$$

**Intuitive Explanation:** In pure ALOHA, a frame's vulnerable period is $2T$ (it can be hit by frames from the previous $T$ and the next $T$). In slotted ALOHA, transmission can start **only at slot boundaries**, so a frame can collide only with frames in the **current slot** (and only from stations that began transmitting *in the same slot*). This halves the vulnerable period, doubling the success probability.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways Students Lose Marks (Data Link & MAC Protocols):**
> 1. **Confusing the *throughput formula* between Pure and Slotted ALOHA.** The exponent is **$2G$ for Pure** and **$G$ for Slotted**. Mix this up and you lose all 7 marks in part (b).
> 2. **Forgetting to state the vulnerable period** when deriving throughput. Examiners award marks for the *assumption* "vulnerable period = $2T$" explicitly.
> 3. **Writing the address field as `0xAA` in PPP** (incorrect — it is always **`0xFF`** to denote "all stations").
> 4. **Confusing LCP with NCP.** LCP = Link Control Protocol (manages the link); NCP = Network Control Protocol (manages the network-layer protocol running on the link).
> 5. **Skipping the bit-stuffing example.** Just defining bit stuffing is not enough — KTU requires a **worked numerical example** showing insertion and removal of the `0` bit.
> 6. **Not specifying the optimal $G$ value** when calculating maximum throughput. Always state $G = 0.5$ for Pure ALOHA, $G = 1$ for Slotted ALOHA.
> 7. **Calling CSMA/CD a "collision avoidance" protocol** — it is *collision detection* (WiFi's 802.11 uses CSMA/CA, which is *collision avoidance*). Examiners will deduct marks for this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

### Data Link Protocols
- ✅ **HDLC** is bit-oriented, uses **`01111110`** flag bytes, and applies **bit stuffing** (insert `0` after five `1`s).
- ✅ **SLIP** is byte-oriented, uses **`0xC0`** END byte, and supports **IP only** (no error detection, no authentication).
- ✅ **PPP** uses the same flag as HDLC (`0x7E`), has fixed **Address = `0xFF`**, **Control = `0x03`**, and a **2-byte Protocol field**.
- ✅ **Three PPP sub-protocols:** **LCP** (link setup), **NCP/IPCP** (IP configuration), **Multilink PPP** (link bundling).
- ✅ **PPP is the standard; SLIP is obsolete.**

### Channel Allocation Problem
- ✅ **Five Assumptions:** Station Model, Single Channel, Collision, Time (Continuous/Slotted), Carrier Sense (Yes/No).
- ✅ **Static = FDM/TDM** (predictable, wastes capacity under bursty traffic).
- ✅ **Dynamic = ALOHA / Slotted ALOHA / CSMA / CSMA/CD** (efficient for bursty traffic).

### ALOHA Family — Must-Memorize Formulas
- ✅ **Pure ALOHA:** $S = G e^{-2G}$, $S_{\max} = 1/(2e) \approx 18.4\%$, vulnerable period $= 2T$.
- ✅ **Slotted ALOHA:** $S = G e^{-G}$, $S_{\max} = 1/e \approx 36.8\%$, vulnerable period $= T$.
- ✅ **Slotted achieves exactly 2× the throughput of Pure ALOHA.**

### CSMA Family
- ✅ **1-persistent:** Transmit immediately if idle — aggressive, more collisions.
- ✅ **Non-persistent:** Wait random time — less greedy, fewer collisions.
- ✅ **p-persistent:** For slotted channels — transmit with probability $p$.
- ✅ **CSMA/CD:** Adds **collision detection** + **jam signal** + **Binary Exponential Backoff** (foundation of Ethernet 802.3).
- ✅ **CSMA/CA** (not in syllabus detail, but recognize it): Used in WiFi 802.11.

### Examiner's Mnemonic
> 🎯 **"B-B-P-S-C"** → **B**it stuffing (HDLC), **B**yte stuffing (PPP), **P**ure ALOHA ($1/2e$), **S**lotted ALOHA ($1/e$), **C**SMA/CD (Ethernet).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple Access Protocols: ALOHA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multiple Access Protocols: ALOHA**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Access Protocols** are a set of rules that determine how multiple users/stations share a common broadcast communication medium (such as a radio channel, satellite link, or wired bus) without (or with minimal) collision, ensuring orderly access to the shared channel.

**ALOHA** is one of the earliest and simplest **random access (contention-based) Multiple Access Protocols**, originally developed at the University of Hawaii in the early 1970s for use with radio communication. In ALOHA, any station that has a frame to transmit simply transmits it over the shared channel. If the frame collides with another transmission, the station waits a random amount of time and retransmits.

There are two principal versions:
1. **Pure ALOHA (Unslotted ALOHA)**
2. **Slotted ALOHA**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – "A Group Conversation in a Dark Room"**

Imagine a group of people in a completely dark room. They cannot see each other. Whenever someone wants to speak, they just start talking out loud. If two people talk at the same time, they collide — neither can be understood. They both stop, wait a random amount of time, and try again.

- **Pure ALOHA** is exactly this: any user can transmit **at any time** without coordination.
- **Slotted ALOHA** is like giving each person a "slot" (a small time window). Now, a person can only start talking at the **beginning of a slot**. This reduces the chance of collision and improves efficiency.

### 1.3 Key Terminology and Physical Metrics

- **Frame (Packet):** A unit of data transmitted by a station.
- **Frame Transmission Time (T):** The time required to transmit one standard frame. Also called the **frame time**.
- **Channel Load / Offered Load (G):** The average number of transmission attempts (including new frames and retransmissions) per frame time T.
- **Throughput (S):** The average number of successfully transmitted frames per frame time T.
- **Vulnerable Time:** The interval during which a frame is susceptible to collision with another frame.
- **Propagation Delay (tₚ):** The time for a signal to travel from sender to receiver (assumed negligible in the ALOHA model for derivation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Metric:** Throughput efficiency of ALOHA is measured as **S/G**, the ratio of useful throughput to the offered load.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pure ALOHA – Operational Logic

Pure ALOHA allows each station to transmit a frame **as soon as it has data ready**, with no time synchronization between stations.

**Step-by-step logic:**
- A station with a ready frame transmits it on the shared channel immediately.
- The station waits for an **Acknowledgment (ACK)** from the receiver.
- If no ACK is received within a timeout period, the station assumes a **collision** has occurred.
- The station waits for a **random backoff time** (to avoid repeated collisions).
- The frame is **retransmitted** after the backoff period.
- The process repeats until the frame is successfully acknowledged.

**The Crucial Concept – Vulnerable Time:**
Consider a frame F that station A starts transmitting at time `t`. Another frame F' that overlaps with F at any point during F's transmission will cause a collision. Therefore, F is vulnerable to collision for a period that begins **one frame time before** its own transmission and ends **one frame time after** its start.

- Vulnerable time for Pure ALOHA = **2T**

### 2.2 Slotted ALOHA – Operational Logic

Slotted ALOHA was proposed by Roberts in 1975 to improve the efficiency of Pure ALOHA. It introduces **time slotting** using a **global clock or synchronization beacon**.

**Step-by-step logic:**
- Time is divided into equal **slots of duration T** (one frame transmission time).
- A station may begin transmission **only at the boundary of a slot**.
- If two stations transmit in the same slot, they collide entirely (the slots align perfectly).
- After collision, each station waits a random number of slots before retransmission.

**The Crucial Concept – Vulnerable Time:**
Since transmissions are aligned to slot boundaries, a frame can only collide with frames transmitted in the **same slot** or the **adjacent slot (if we consider partial overlap)**. With perfect slot alignment, a frame is vulnerable to collision only during its own slot.

- Vulnerable time for Slotted ALOHA = **T**

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas use a Poisson arrival model for transmission attempts. The probability of `k` attempts in a given interval of duration `t` (where average attempts = `λt`) is:
> `P(k) = (λt)^k · e^(-λt) / k!`

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------------------|------------|--------------|
| 1 | Frame transmission time | $T$ | seconds |
| 2 | Offered load (Pure & Slotted) | $G$ | attempts / frame time |
| 3 | Throughput (Pure ALOHA) | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | frames / frame time |
| 4 | Max Throughput (Pure ALOHA) | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ | ~18.4% |
| 5 | Optimal Load (Pure ALOHA) | $G = 0.5$ | attempts / frame time |
| 6 | Throughput (Slotted ALOHA) | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | frames / frame time |
| 7 | Max Throughput (Slotted ALOHA) | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ | ~36.8% |
| 8 | Optimal Load (Slotted ALOHA) | $G = 1$ | attempt / frame time |
| 9 | Vulnerable Time (Pure ALOHA) | $V = 2T$ | seconds |
| 10 | Vulnerable Time (Slotted ALOHA) | $V = T$ | seconds |
| 11 | Channel Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{S}{G}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** ALOHA forms the conceptual basis for **Ethernet's CSMA/CD**, **Wi-Fi's CSMA/CA**, **RFID tag anti-collision algorithms (ISO 18000-63)**, **satellite uplink protocols (VSAT)**, and **Aloha-type reservation systems** in cellular IoT (e.g., **NB-IoT PRACH**). Pure ALOHA is still used in **very low-duty-cycle sensor networks** and **passive RFID systems** for its simplicity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Pure ALOHA Throughput

**Assumption:** Transmission attempts follow a **Poisson distribution** with mean rate `λ` attempts per second. Frame time is `T` seconds.

**Step 1 – Define Offered Load G**
The average number of transmission attempts per frame time `T` is:
$$G = \lambda \cdot T$$

**Step 2 – Probability of Zero Other Transmissions in Vulnerable Period**
The vulnerable period for a frame in Pure ALOHA spans `2T` seconds (one frame time before and one frame time after the start of transmission). The average number of attempts in this `2T` period is:
$$\text{Mean attempts in } 2T = \lambda \cdot 2T = 2G$$

The probability that **no other station transmits** during this vulnerable period (i.e., zero competing attempts) using the Poisson distribution is:
$$P(\text{success}) = P(k = 0) = \frac{(2G)^{0} \cdot e^{-2G}}{0!} = e^{-2G}$$

**Step 3 – Derive the Throughput S**
Throughput is the offered load multiplied by the probability of success:
$$S = G \cdot P(\text{success}) = G \cdot e^{-2G}$$

This is the **Pure ALOHA throughput equation**.

**Step 4 – Find the Maximum Throughput**
Differentiate `S` with respect to `G` and set to zero:
$$\frac{dS}{dG} = \frac{d}{dG}\left(G \cdot e^{-2G}\right)$$

Using the product rule `d(uv)/dx = u'v + uv'`:
$$\frac{dS}{dG} = (1) \cdot e^{-2G} + G \cdot (-2) \cdot e^{-2G} = e^{-2G} (1 - 2G)$$

Set `dS/dG = 0`:
$$e^{-2G} (1 - 2G) = 0$$

Since `e^{-2G} > 0` for all real `G`, we must have:
$$1 - 2G = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad G = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 5 – Substitute Optimal G into Throughput Equation**
$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{-2 \cdot (1/2)} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.1839$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA is `1/(2e) ≈ 0.184`, i.e., only **~18.4%** of the channel's capacity is used productively. This occurs when the offered load is `G = 0.5`.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Slotted ALOHA Throughput

**Step 1 – Define Offered Load G**
Same as before:
$$G = \lambda \cdot T$$
where `λ` is the average attempt rate per second.

**Step 2 – Probability of Zero Other Transmissions in Vulnerable Period**
In Slotted ALOHA, a frame is vulnerable **only during its own slot of duration T**. The average number of attempts in one slot is:
$$\text{Mean attempts in } T = \lambda \cdot T = G$$

The probability that no other station transmits in the same slot:
$$P(\text{success}) = P(k = 0) = \frac{G^{0} \cdot e^{-G}}{0!} = e^{-G}$$

**Step 3 – Derive the Throughput S**
$$S = G \cdot P(\text{success}) = G \cdot e^{-G}$$

This is the **Slotted ALOHA throughput equation**.

**Step 4 – Find the Maximum Throughput**
Differentiate `S` with respect to `G`:
$$\frac{dS}{dG} = \frac{d}{dG}\left(G \cdot e^{-G}\right)$$

Apply the product rule:
$$\frac{dS}{dG} = (1) \cdot e^{-G} + G \cdot (-1) \cdot e^{-G} = e^{-G} (1 - G)$$

Set `dS/dG = 0`:
$$e^{-G} (1 - G) = 0$$

Since `e^{-G} > 0`:
$$1 - G = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad G = 1$$

**Step 5 – Substitute Optimal G into Throughput Equation**
$$S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The maximum throughput of Slotted ALOHA is `1/e ≈ 0.368`, i.e., **~36.8%** of channel capacity. This is **exactly double** Pure ALOHA, achieved by halving the vulnerable period from `2T` to `T`.

---

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix – Pure ALOHA vs. Slotted ALOHA

| Property | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA |
|----------|------------|---------------|
| Time synchronization | Not required | Required (global clock) |
| Transmission rule | Anytime | Only at slot boundaries |
| Vulnerable period | $2T$ | $T$ |
| Throughput equation | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ |
| Max throughput | $1/(2e) \approx 18.4\%$ | $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ |
| Optimal load G | $0.5$ | $1$ |
| Implementation complexity | Very low | Moderate (synchronization) |
| Performance at high load | Poor (degrades quickly) | Better (doubles pure ALOHA) |
| Used in | Simple RFID, legacy radio | Satellite uplink, NB-IoT RACH |

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A Slotted ALOHA system has a frame time `T = 20 ms` and an average of `200 frames/second` arriving (including new and retransmitted). Calculate the throughput and the channel efficiency.

**Solution:**

Given:
- Frame time `T = 20 ms = 0.02 s`
- Total attempts per second = 200

The offered load per frame time:
$$G = \text{attempts/sec} \times T = 200 \times 0.02 = 4$$

Throughput of Slotted ALOHA:
$$S = G \cdot e^{-G} = 4 \cdot e^{-4} = 4 \cdot 0.01832 = 0.0733 \text{ frames per frame time}$$

Successful frames per second:
$$S_{per\_sec} = \frac{S}{T} = \frac{0.0733}{0.02} \approx 3.66 \text{ frames/second}$$

Channel efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{S}{G} = \frac{0.0733}{4} = 0.0183 \approx 1.83\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Although the load is 4 frames per frame time, the channel is **severely overloaded** (`G >> 1`). The system is in the "overload" region where the throughput curve has already crossed its peak (at `G = 1`) and is falling. The probability of success per slot is only `e^{-4} ≈ 1.83%`, hence very low efficiency. **Lesson for KTU:** Always operate near `G = 1` (Slotted) or `G = 0.5` (Pure) for maximum efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pure ALOHA – Vulnerable Time Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the vulnerable time concept in Pure ALOHA. Frame F1 (transmitted by Station A) is vulnerable to collision for a period of `2T` — one frame time before its start and one frame time after. Any frame F2, F3, F4, F5 that overlaps this vulnerable window will cause a collision.

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef vulnerable fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef safe fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef colliding fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#d4ac0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef main fill:#d6e9ff,stroke:#2980b9,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    A0["Previous slot 0 to T"]:::safe
    A1["Start F1 at time t"]:::main
    A2["End F1 at time t + T"]:::main
    A3["Vulnerable window: t - T to t + T"]:::vulnerable
    A4["F2 collides (starts before F1)"]:::colliding
    A5["F3 collides (overlaps)"]:::colliding
    A6["F4 collides (starts during F1)"]:::colliding
    A7["F5 collides (ends after F1)"]:::colliding
    A8["Safe: starts after t + T"]:::safe

    A0 --> A3
    A3 --> A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A8
    A3 --> A4
    A3 --> A5
    A3 --> A6
    A3 --> A7
```

### 4.2 Slotted ALOHA – Slot-Aligned Transmission

The following diagram shows the slot structure in Slotted ALOHA. Each slot is exactly one frame time T. All stations must begin transmission at slot boundaries. A collision occurs only when two or more stations pick the same slot.

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef slotEmpty fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
    classDef slotSuccess fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef slotCollide fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef slotIdle fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#757575,color:#000

    S1["Slot 1: IDLE"]:::slotIdle
    S2["Slot 2: Station A transmits SUCCESS"]:::slotSuccess
    S3["Slot 3: IDLE"]:::slotIdle
    S4["Slot 4: B and C collide COLLISION"]:::slotCollide
    S5["Slot 5: Station D transmits SUCCESS"]:::slotSuccess
    S6["Slot 6: IDLE"]:::slotIdle
    S7["Slot 7: E, F, G collide COLLISION"]:::slotCollide
    S8["Slot 8: Station A retransmits SUCCESS"]:::slotSuccess

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
```

### 4.3 Throughput vs. Load Curve (S vs. G)

This functional block diagram maps the throughput behavior of Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA as a function of the offered load G. It highlights the critical operating points and the regions of operation.

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef curve fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef peak fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef low fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#000

    Title["Throughput S versus Load G"]:::curve
    Reg1["Region 1: G small - S rises linearly with G"]:::low
    PeakPure["PURE ALOHA PEAK: G = 0.5, S = 1 by 2e = 0.184"]:::peak
    PeakSlot["SLOTTED ALOHA PEAK: G = 1, S = 1 by e = 0.368"]:::peak
    Reg2["Region 2: Beyond peak - S falls toward 0 as collisions dominate"]:::low
    Axis["X axis: G attempts per frame time | Y axis: S frames per frame time"]:::curve

    Title --> Reg1 --> PeakPure --> PeakSlot --> Reg2
    Title --> Axis
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ALOHA System

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef station fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef channel fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef receiver fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    ST1["Station 1: Frame ready buffer"]:::station
    ST2["Station 2: Frame ready buffer"]:::station
    ST3["Station N: Frame ready buffer"]:::station
    TXM["Transmit decision logic - Pure: anytime, Slotted: at slot boundary"]:::control
    CH["Shared Broadcast Channel - Bus, Radio, or Satellite link"]:::channel
    REC["Receiver: Detects collision and sends ACK or NACK"]:::receiver
    ACK["ACK feedback path to sender"]:::control
    BACK["Random Backoff Timer - Wait random k slots then retry"]:::control

    ST1 --> TXM
    ST2 --> TXM
    ST3 --> TXM
    TXM --> CH
    CH --> REC
    REC --> ACK
    ACK --> ST1
    ACK --> ST2
    ACK --> ST3
    REC --> BACK
    BACK --> TXM
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define ALOHA. Differentiate between Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **ALOHA** is a random-access multiple access protocol that allows multiple stations to share a common broadcast channel without prior coordination, recovering from collisions via random retransmission. **(1 mark)**
- **Pure ALOHA:** No time synchronization, any station can transmit at any time, vulnerable time = `2T`, max throughput = `1/(2e) ≈ 18.4%`. **(1 mark)**
- **Slotted ALOHA:** Time is divided into slots of duration T, stations transmit only at slot boundaries, vulnerable time = `T`, max throughput = `1/e ≈ 36.8%`. **(1 mark)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is meant by "vulnerable time" in ALOHA protocols? Compare vulnerable time for Pure and Slotted ALOHA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Vulnerable time** is the interval during which an ongoing frame transmission is susceptible to collision with other frames. **(1 mark)**
- In **Pure ALOHA**, since stations transmit independently, a frame collides with any other frame that starts within ±T of its own start → vulnerable time = `2T`. **(1 mark)**
- In **Slotted ALOHA**, transmissions are aligned to slot boundaries, so a frame only collides with frames in the same slot → vulnerable time = `T`. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question – Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Q. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (a) Explain the working of Pure ALOHA protocol with a suitable diagram. Derive the expression for throughput and find the maximum throughput. (7 marks)**
> **(b) Explain Slotted ALOHA protocol with diagram. Derive the throughput and show that its maximum throughput is twice that of Pure ALOHA. (7 marks)**

#### **Question A – Part (a) Solution (7 marks):**

**Working of Pure ALOHA (3 marks):**
- In Pure ALOHA, all stations share a common broadcast channel. Any station with a ready frame transmits it immediately on the channel. **(0.5 mark)**
- The receiver checks for errors using CRC. If the frame is error-free, an ACK is sent back; if errors are detected (due to collision), no ACK is sent. **(0.5 mark)**
- The transmitting station waits for an ACK timeout. If no ACK arrives, it assumes a collision occurred. **(0.5 mark)**
- The station waits a **random backoff time** (exponentially increasing in many implementations) and retransmits. **(0.5 mark)**
- A new frame can be transmitted at any time, completely asynchronous. **(0.5 mark)**
- See Mermaid diagram 4.4 for block architecture. **(0.5 mark)**

**Derivation of Throughput (3 marks):**
- Let G = mean number of transmission attempts per frame time. **(0.5 mark)**
- Vulnerable time = 2T → mean attempts in 2T = 2G. **(0.5 mark)**
- Probability of zero other attempts: `P(success) = e^{-2G}`. **(0.5 mark)**
- Throughput: `S = G · e^{-2G}`. **(0.5 mark)**
- `dS/dG = e^{-2G}(1 − 2G) = 0` → `G = 1/2`. **(0.5 mark)**
- `S_max = (1/2) · e^{-1} = 1/(2e) ≈ 0.184`. **(0.5 mark)**

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA is approximately **18.4%**, occurring at `G = 0.5`. The system becomes unstable for `G > 0.5` as throughput drops.

---

#### **Question A – Part (b) Solution (7 marks):**

**Working of Slotted ALOHA (2.5 marks):**
- A central clock or beacon divides time into discrete **slots of duration T** (one frame time). **(0.5 mark)**
- A station is allowed to begin transmission **only at the start of a slot**. **(0.5 mark)**
- If two (or more) stations transmit in the same slot, the slot experiences a **full collision** (the entire frame is destroyed). **(0.5 mark)**
- Each colliding station waits a **random number of slots** (often with binary exponential backoff) and retransmits. **(0.5 mark)**
- Receiver sends ACK on success; absence of ACK implies collision. **(0.5 mark)**

**Derivation of Throughput (3 marks):**
- Let G = mean attempts per frame time. **(0.5 mark)**
- Vulnerable time = T → mean attempts in T = G. **(0.5 mark)**
- `P(success) = e^{-G}`. **(0.5 mark)**
- Throughput: `S = G · e^{-G}`. **(0.5 mark)**
- `dS/dG = e^{-G}(1 − G) = 0` → `G = 1`. **(0.5 mark)**
- `S_max = 1 · e^{-1} = 1/e ≈ 0.368`. **(0.5 mark)**

**Comparison and Conclusion (1.5 marks):**
- Ratio: `S_slotted_max / S_pure_max = (1/e) / (1/(2e)) = 2`. **(0.75 mark)**
- Therefore, maximum throughput of Slotted ALOHA is **exactly twice** that of Pure ALOHA. **(0.75 mark)**

---

#### **Question B – Alternative (Internal Choice) (14 Marks)**

> **Q. (a) With a neat diagram, explain the concept of vulnerable time in Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA. (7 marks)**
> **(b) In a Slotted ALOHA system, the frame transmission time is 25 ms. If the total number of transmission attempts (new + retransmitted) is 80 per second, calculate the throughput and the channel efficiency. (7 marks)**

**Question B – Part (a) Solution (7 marks):**
- **Definition of vulnerable time (1 mark):** The interval during which a transmitted frame can be corrupted by another transmission.
- **Pure ALOHA vulnerable time = 2T (3 marks):**
  - Frame F starts at time t, ends at t+T. Any other frame starting in (t−T, t+T) will overlap.
  - Hence vulnerable period spans 2T. Illustrated using a timeline diagram (3 marks for the diagram and explanation).
- **Slotted ALOHA vulnerable time = T (3 marks):**
  - Time divided into equal slots of duration T. Transmission can begin only at slot boundaries.
  - Overlap is possible only within the same slot. Therefore, vulnerable time reduces to T.
  - This 50% reduction doubles the maximum throughput.

**Question B – Part (b) Solution (7 marks):**
- Given: `T = 25 ms = 0.025 s`, attempts/sec = 80. **(0.5 mark)**
- Offered load: `G = 80 × 0.025 = 2`. **(1 mark)**
- Throughput: `S = G · e^{-G} = 2 · e^{-2} = 2 × 0.1353 = 0.2707` frames per frame time. **(2 marks)**
- Successful frames per second: `S / T = 0.2707 / 0.025 = 10.83` frames/sec. **(1.5 marks)**
- Channel efficiency: `η = S / G = 0.2707 / 2 = 0.1354 = 13.54%`. **(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by G:** Many students write only `P(success) = e^{-2G}` and forget the factor `G` in the throughput equation. Remember: `S = G × P(success)`. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Confusing G and S:** `G` is the *offered* load (all attempts), `S` is the *successful* throughput. Never equate them. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Wrong vulnerable time:** Students often write `T` for Pure ALOHA — the correct value is `2T`. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Numerical unit error:** When calculating `frames/sec`, always divide `S` by `T`, not multiply. **(−0.5 mark)**
> 5. **Not stating the optimal G:** Always mention that maximum occurs at `G = 1/2` (Pure) or `G = 1` (Slotted). **(−0.5 mark)**
> 6. **Skipping the differentiation step:** Examiners award partial credit only if the derivative `dS/dG` is explicitly shown. **(−1 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Recall Bullets for KTU Exam Day:**

- **ALOHA** = random-access protocol; stations transmit without coordination; collisions are accepted and resolved via retransmission. ✅
- **Two variants:** Pure ALOHA (no slots) and Slotted ALOHA (time-slotted). ✅
- **Pure ALOHA vulnerable time** = `2T`; **Slotted ALOHA vulnerable time** = `T`. ✅
- **Pure ALOHA throughput:** $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$. ✅
- **Slotted ALOHA throughput:** $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$. ✅
- **Maximum throughput Pure ALOHA** = `1/(2e) ≈ 0.184` (18.4%) at `G = 0.5`. ✅
- **Maximum throughput Slotted ALOHA** = `1/e ≈ 0.368` (36.8%) at `G = 1`. ✅
- **Slotted ALOHA is exactly 2× more efficient** than Pure ALOHA at peak load. ✅
- **Channel efficiency** = `S / G`. ✅
- **Poisson arrival model** is the foundation of all ALOHA derivations — mention this in your answer to score full marks. ✅
- **Backoff strategy** is required after collision; common choices: Uniform random, Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB). ✅
- **Synchronization requirement:** Slotted ALOHA needs a global clock; this adds implementation cost. ✅
- **Real-world use:** RFID tag reading (Pure ALOHA), VSAT satellite uplink, NB-IoT Random Access Channel (Slotted ALOHA). ✅
- **Beyond `G = 0.5` (Pure) or `G = 1` (Slotted):** system becomes unstable — throughput decreases as load increases. ✅
- **KTU 2024 Scheme tip:** Always draw a labeled timeline diagram showing the vulnerable period when explaining ALOHA. It earns easy 2–3 marks. ✅

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carrier Sense Multiple Access Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Carrier Sense Multiple Access Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Carrier Sense Multiple Access Protocols — Core Foundation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carrier Sense Multiple Access (CSMA)** is a **contention-based Media Access Control (MAC) protocol** in which a transmitting station first *listens* to (senses) the shared communication channel for the presence of a **carrier signal** (i.e., ongoing transmission) before initiating its own frame transmission. This "listen-before-talk" strategy is a probabilistic method designed to **reduce, but not eliminate, frame collisions** on a shared multi-access broadcast medium.

In the layered OSI/TCP-IP architecture, CSMA operates at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2) — specifically the MAC sublayer**, governing how multiple independent stations arbitrate access to a single shared physical medium such as coaxial cable (10BASE5/10BASE2), twisted-pair hubs, or radio frequencies in wireless networks.

### 1.2 The Problem CSMA Solves — Why Pure ALOHA Fails

Earlier random-access protocols (Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA) suffer from very low maximum throughput:

$$\text{Throughput}_{Pure\ ALOHA} = G \cdot e^{-2G} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_{max} = 0.184 \text{ (or 18.4\%)}$$

$$\text{Throughput}_{Slotted\ ALOHA} = G \cdot e^{-G} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_{max} = 0.368 \text{ (or 36.8\%)}$$

where $G$ is the offered load (average number of transmission attempts per frame time) and $S$ is the throughput (successful frame transmissions per frame time).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Telephone Party Line:** Imagine a community sharing a single telephone wire (a "party line"). If two people pick up the phone and talk simultaneously, their voices collide into unintelligible noise. Pure ALOHA is like just *talking randomly* without checking — many collisions. CSMA is like first *listening* to the wire: if you hear someone talking, you wait. Most of the time, collisions are avoided. CSMA/CD is like also being able to *hear your own voice collide* and stop immediately. CSMA/CA is like agreeing in advance not to talk over each other.

## 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Definition | Symbol / Unit |
|------|-----------|---------------|
| **Carrier Sense** | Detection of energy on the medium | — |
| **Multiple Access** | Multiple stations share one channel | — |
| **Propagation Delay** | Time for signal to traverse the medium | $\tau$ (seconds) |
| **Transmission Delay** | Time to push entire frame onto medium | $T_t = L/R$ (seconds) |
| **Frame Time** | Time to transmit one frame end-to-end | $T_{fr} = 2\tau + T_t$ |
| **Vulnerable Window** | Time period during which collision is possible | depends on protocol |
| **Persistence** | Probability of transmitting upon finding idle channel | $p$ |

## 1.4 Classification of CSMA Protocols (The Family Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CSMA[CSMA Family]
    CSMA --> P1[1-Persistent CSMA]
    CSMA --> NP[Non-Persistent CSMA]
    CSMA --> PP[p-Persistent CSMA]
    P1 --> CD[CSMA with Collision Detection]
    NP --> CD
    PP --> CD
    CD --> ALOHA[Improves ALOHA performance]
    CSMA --> CA[CSMA with Collision Avoidance]
    CA --> WIFI[Used in 802.11 WiFi]
    style CSMA fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff
    style CD fill:#c00000,color:#fff
    style CA fill:#548235,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 OECPT613 syllabus explicitly requires students to differentiate between **1-persistent, non-persistent, and p-persistent CSMA**, and explain **CSMA/CD and CSMA/CA** with their respective throughput vs. load characteristics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Three CSMA Variants

## 2.1 Operational Logic of "Listen Before Talk"

The common operational core of all CSMA variants is the **Carrier Sense Procedure**:

1. **Sense the channel** — Station measures signal energy on the medium.
2. **If IDLE** — Channel is free; the variant-specific rule determines whether to transmit.
3. **If BUSY** — Channel is occupied; the variant-specific rule determines waiting strategy.
4. **After transmission begins** — Depending on the sub-variant, the station may also *detect* or *avoid* collisions.

The three variants differ only in **steps 2 and 3** — i.e., what to do when the channel is sensed idle or busy.

---

## 2.2 1-Persistent CSMA

### Operational Rules
- **If channel is sensed IDLE** → Transmit *immediately* (with probability $p = 1$).
- **If channel is sensed BUSY** → *Continuously* monitor until it becomes idle, then transmit *immediately* (with persistence = 1).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The "greedy" protocol. The moment a station sees the channel free, it transmits without any hesitation. If two stations were both waiting for the same busy channel to clear, both will start transmitting at the *exact same instant* — causing a collision.

### Why Collisions Still Occur
Because of **propagation delay** $\tau$, a station at the far end of the medium may not yet *see* a frame that was just launched by a station at the near end. If the far station senses the channel during the propagation window and finds it "idle," it begins transmitting, and the two frames collide mid-medium.

The **vulnerable window** for 1-persistent CSMA is the **maximum propagation delay** $\tau$, which is **half** that of pure ALOHA.

### Throughput Formula (Simplified)
$$S = \frac{G \cdot e^{-G(1+2a)}}{G(1+2a) + (1 + e^{-Ga})}$$

where $a = \tau / T_{fr}$ is the ratio of propagation delay to frame transmission time (a key KTU parameter).

Maximum throughput (as $G \to \infty$):

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} \cdot \frac{1}{e}$$

---

## 2.3 Non-Persistent CSMA

### Operational Rules
- **If channel is sensed IDLE** → Transmit immediately.
- **If channel is sensed BUSY** → *Wait a random amount of time* (back-off), then re-sense.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The "polite" protocol. Rather than staring at the channel and jumping on it the instant it clears, the station gives up its immediate ambition and waits a random interval. This reduces the chance that two waiting stations will both pounce the moment the channel goes idle.

### Trade-off
- **Advantage:** Lower collision probability (since colliding stations wait independently).
- **Disadvantage:** Higher channel idle time (wasted bandwidth while stations are in random back-off).

### Throughput Formula

$$S = \frac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1 + 2a) + e^{-aG}}$$

Maximum throughput:

$$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} \cdot \frac{1}{e \cdot (1 + 2a) + 1} \cdot \text{... (more verbose)}$$

For $a \to 0$ (negligible propagation delay), $S_{max} \to 1/e \approx 0.368$ — but this is *better than 1-persistent* for non-zero $a$.

---

## 2.4 p-Persistent CSMA

### Operational Rules (Used in Slotted Channels)
This variant is used for **slotted** channels (e.g., where time is divided into discrete slots of length $\tau$):
- **If channel is sensed IDLE** in current slot → Transmit with probability $p$; defer to next slot with probability $(1-p)$.
- **If channel is sensed BUSY** → Wait until end of current transmission, then apply the idle-slot rule above.
- **If transmission is deferred** → Repeat in next slot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A probabilistic compromise. Instead of "always transmit" (1-persistent) or "always wait" (non-persistent), transmit with moderate probability $p$, giving other waiting stations a fair chance. Used in **slotted** systems like early radio packet networks.

### Limiting Cases
- $p = 1$ → Reduces to **1-persistent CSMA** (greedy).
- $p \to 0$ → Approaches an extremely cautious, almost never-transmitting behaviour (overly conservative).

---

## 2.5 CSMA with Collision Detection (CSMA/CD)

### The Key Innovation
> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **CSMA/CD**, a station *monitors the medium while transmitting*. If it detects another signal on the channel that exceeds expected energy (indicating interference), it realizes a collision is occurring and **immediately aborts transmission**, transmits a **jam signal** to alert all other stations, and then waits a random back-off time before retrying.

### Operational Steps
1. **Sense channel.** If idle, start transmitting; if busy, wait until idle.
2. **Transmit and listen simultaneously.**
3. **If collision detected** → Stop transmitting, send **jam signal** (32-bit pattern), then execute **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)**.
4. **Backoff and retry.**

### Why CSMA/CD Is Superior
By aborting the transmission *as soon as* a collision is detected, the wasted channel time is limited to the time it takes to detect the collision — namely the **maximum propagation delay** $\tau$, not the full frame transmission time $T_{fr}$.

### Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) Algorithm
After the $n$-th collision (where $n \leq 16$ for IEEE 802.3 Ethernet), the station waits a random number of slot times:

$$k = \text{rand}(0, 2^{\min(n,10)} - 1)$$

where each slot time is the **slot time** $= 2\tau = 51.2\ \mu s$ in classic 10 Mbps Ethernet.

| Attempt ($n$) | Range of $k$ | Max Wait |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0–1 | 1 slot |
| 2 | 0–3 | 3 slots |
| 3 | 0–7 | 7 slots |
| 4 | 0–15 | 15 slots |
| ... | ... | ... |
| 10 | 0–1023 | 1023 slots |
| 16 | give up | drop frame |

### Throughput of CSMA/CD (Classic Ethernet)
The simplified throughput of IEEE 802.3 CSMA/CD is:

$$S = \frac{1}{1 + 6.4 \cdot a}$$

where $a = \tau / T_{fr}$ and the constant 6.4 reflects the standard Ethernet slot accounting (512-bit slot time ÷ 64-byte minimum frame). For a 64-byte frame on 10 Mbps Ethernet with 2500 m max segment length, $a \approx 0.01$, giving $S \approx 0.94$ — far superior to ALOHA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** CSMA/CD is the **MAC protocol of classic IEEE 802.3 Ethernet** (10BASE5, 10BASE2, 10BASE-T). It was retired from full-duplex switched Ethernet because collisions cannot occur in full-duplex point-to-point links, but it remains historically and pedagogically essential.

---

## 2.6 CSMA with Collision Avoidance (CSMA/CA)

### Why Avoidance Instead of Detection?
> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **wireless networks**, a station **cannot simultaneously transmit and listen on the same frequency** because its own transmission signal dwarfs any received signal. Therefore, **CSMA/CD is physically impossible in radio networks**. Instead, **CSMA/CA** is used — it tries to *prevent* collisions through careful coordination.

### Operational Steps (IEEE 802.11 DCF Mode)
1. **Sense channel.** If idle for a **DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space)** duration, transmit.
2. **If channel busy** → Start a **backoff timer** counting down from a random value chosen in $[0, CW]$ where $CW$ is the **Contention Window**.
3. **Timer counts down only when channel is idle** (frozen when busy).
4. **When timer reaches 0**, transmit the frame.
5. **Receiver sends ACK** after a **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space)** if frame received correctly.
6. **If no ACK received** → Increase $CW$ (doubling, like BEB) and retry.

### Optional RTS/CTS Handshake
To combat the **hidden terminal problem**, IEEE 802.11 optionally uses:
- **RTS (Request To Send)** — sender announces intent.
- **CTS (Clear To Send)** — receiver grants permission; all stations hearing CTS defer.

### Key Timing Relationships (Critical for KTU)

$$SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS$$

| Parameter | Typical 802.11b Value | Meaning |
|-----------|----------------------|---------|
| SIFS | 10 µs | Short Inter-Frame Space |
| PIFS | 30 µs | PCF Inter-Frame Space |
| DIFS | 50 µs | DCF Inter-Frame Space |
| Slot Time | 20 µs | Backoff slot unit |
| EIFS | 364 µs | Extended IFS (used after error) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** CSMA/CA is the foundation of **IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi**, **IEEE 802.15.4 (ZigBee)**, and most modern wireless MAC protocols. Understanding it is essential for IoT and wireless network design.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for Exam Calculations** — All required formulas in one consolidated table. *Note: vertical bars use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.*

| Protocol | Throughput $S$ | Max Throughput $S_{max}$ | Vulnerable Window |
|----------|---------------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| Pure ALOHA | $G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ | $2 \cdot T_{fr}$ |
| Slotted ALOHA | $G \cdot e^{-G}$ | $\dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ | $T_{fr}$ |
| 1-Persistent CSMA | $\dfrac{G e^{-G(1+2a)}}{G(1+2a) + 1 + e^{-aG}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{(1+2a)\cdot e}$ | $T_{fr} + \tau$ |
| Non-Persistent CSMA | $\dfrac{G e^{-aG}}{G(1+2a) + e^{-aG}}$ | $> \dfrac{1}{(1+2a)\cdot e}$ for $a>0$ | reduced |
| CSMA/CD (Ethernet) | $\dfrac{1}{1+6.4a}$ | $\approx 0.94$ for $a=0.01$ | $\tau$ (detected quickly) |

**Key parameter definitions used throughout:**

$$a = \frac{\tau}{T_{fr}} = \frac{\tau \cdot R}{L}$$

where $R$ = channel bit rate (bps), $L$ = frame length (bits), $\tau$ = max one-way propagation delay (seconds).

**Binary Exponential Backoff range:**

$$k \in \big[0,\ 2^{\min(n,\,10)} - 1\big]$$

where $n$ is the collision attempt number ($n = 1, 2, \ldots, 16$).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The parameter $a$ is the *single most important metric* in random-access analysis. It tells you whether a network is *propagation-limited* ($a \gg 1$, e.g., satellite) or *bandwidth-limited* ($a \ll 1$, e.g., LAN). Performance of all CSMA variants degrades as $a$ increases.

## 2.8 Comparative Summary of CSMA Variants

| Feature | 1-Persistent | Non-Persistent | p-Persistent | CSMA/CD | CSMA/CA |
|---------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Listen before transmit | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Detect collision | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Avoid collision | ✗ | partial | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Channel idle time | Low | High | Medium | Lowest | Low |
| Collision rate | High | Low | Medium | Very Low | Very Low |
| Channel type | Continuous | Continuous | Slotted | Continuous | Wireless |
| Used in | legacy | legacy | early radio | Ethernet 802.3 | Wi-Fi 802.11 |

# 3. Analytical Derivations and Worked Examples

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Warning:** When asked to *derive* throughput, students commonly lose marks by skipping intermediate steps. Show every substitution explicitly. Marks are awarded for **method**, not just the final answer.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Computing the Parameter $a$ for a Given Network

### Problem Setup
A 10 Mbps Ethernet LAN uses 64-byte minimum frames. The maximum one-way propagation delay across the network is $\tau = 25.6\ \mu s$. Compute the parameter $a$.

### Solution

$$\text{Frame length in bits:}\quad L = 64\ \text{bytes} \times 8\ \text{bits/byte} = 512\ \text{bits}$$

$$\text{Frame transmission time:}\quad T_{fr} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{512\ \text{bits}}{10 \times 10^6\ \text{bits/s}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{fr} &= \frac{512}{10^7}\ \text{s} \\
       &= 51.2 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} \\
       &= 51.2\ \mu s
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= \frac{\tau}{T_{fr}} \\
  &= \frac{25.6\ \mu s}{51.2\ \mu s} \\
  &= 0.5
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The value $a = 0.5$ means the propagation delay is half the frame transmission time — a significant value. In standard 10BASE5 Ethernet, the *maximum allowable* $a$ is 0.05, which constrains the maximum cable length to **500 m × 2.56 = 1280 m equivalent** (with 4 repeaters, total 2500 m).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Throughput of 1-Persistent CSMA (Sketch)

### Assumption
The offered load $G$ is the average number of transmission attempts per frame time, and propagation delay is constant $\tau$.

### Step 1: Effective Channel Width
The channel appears "busy" for a duration equal to the transmission time plus twice the propagation delay, because a station at the far end only "sees" the channel busy after one propagation delay, and the last bit takes one more propagation delay to leave.

$$\text{Busy period} = T_{fr} + 2\tau = T_{fr}(1 + 2a)$$

### Step 2: Probability of Successful Acquisition
A station wishing to transmit in a given frame time succeeds if no other station transmits in the vulnerable window of size $1 + 2a$ (in frame-time units).

$$P(\text{success}) = e^{-G(1+2a)}$$

The factor $e^{-G(1+2a)}$ arises from the Poisson assumption of the offered load $G$ per frame time.

### Step 3: Throughput
$$S = G \cdot P(\text{success}) = G \cdot e^{-G(1+2a)}$$

### Step 4: Maximum Throughput
Differentiate and set derivative to zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dS}{dG} &= e^{-G(1+2a)} - G(1+2a)e^{-G(1+2a)} = 0 \\
1 - G(1+2a) &= 0 \\
G^{*} &= \frac{1}{1+2a}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting back:

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= G^{*} \cdot e^{-G^{*}(1+2a)} \\
        &= \frac{1}{1+2a} \cdot e^{-1} \\
        &= \frac{1}{e(1+2a)}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\ S_{max,\,1\text{-persistent}} = \frac{1}{e(1+2a)} \ }$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CSMA/CD Throughput Approximation

### Problem Setup
Classic IEEE 802.3 Ethernet: 10 Mbps, max segment length 2500 m (5 × 500 m segments with 4 repeaters), propagation speed $2 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$, minimum frame = 512 bits, slot time = 51.2 µs.

### Step 1: Compute Maximum Propagation Delay

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \frac{d}{v} = \frac{2500\ \text{m}}{2 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}} \\
     &= 12.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} \\
     &= 12.5\ \mu s
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Slot Time

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Slot} &= 2\tau = 25.0\ \mu s \quad \text{(theoretical)} \\
\text{Standard Slot} &= 51.2\ \mu s \quad \text{(IEEE round-up for safety margin)}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Compute $a$ for 64-Byte Frame

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{fr} &= \frac{64 \times 8}{10 \times 10^6} = 51.2\ \mu s \\
a &= \frac{\tau}{T_{fr}} = \frac{12.5}{51.2} \approx 0.244
\end{aligned}$$

For the *minimum* frame size, the standard applies a slot ratio constant of approximately $2\tau / T_{fr} = 0.5$ (this enforces the 512-bit minimum frame rule). In the simplified Ethernet throughput formula:

$$S = \frac{1}{1 + 6.4a}$$

For $a = 0.01$ (typical for 1500-byte maximum frame):

$$\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{1}{1 + 6.4(0.01)} \\
  &= \frac{1}{1.064} \\
  &= 0.940
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\ S \approx 0.94\ \text{(94\% efficiency for maximum-length frames)} \ }$$

For minimum-length frames ($a \approx 0.5$):

$$S = \frac{1}{1 + 6.4(0.5)} = \frac{1}{4.2} \approx 0.238\ (23.8\%\ \text{efficiency})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This shows that Ethernet performance is **highly frame-length dependent**. Long frames approach the theoretical maximum throughput; short frames waste bandwidth to collision-detection overhead. This is why Ethernet frames have a *minimum* size of 64 bytes.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Binary Exponential Backoff

A station has experienced its **3rd consecutive collision** on an Ethernet network. Calculate the range of backoff slot times it may select.

### Solution

For collision attempt $n = 3$:

$$k \in [0,\ 2^{\min(n,\,10)} - 1] = [0,\ 2^3 - 1] = [0,\ 7]$$

So the station picks a random integer in $\{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$.

Each slot time is $51.2\ \mu s$, so the maximum wait is:

$$7 \times 51.2\ \mu s = 358.4\ \mu s$$

After the 10th collision, the range saturates at:

$$k \in [0,\ 2^{10} - 1] = [0,\ 1023]$$

$$\text{Max wait} = 1023 \times 51.2\ \mu s \approx 52.4\ \text{ms}$$

After 16 collisions, the frame is **discarded** and the upper layers are notified.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Plot — Throughput vs Load

The throughput vs. load ($S$ vs. $G$) curves for the major random-access protocols follow a characteristic shape: rise to a maximum, then fall off as $G$ increases (more collisions than successes).

| Protocol | $S_{max}$ | Shape Notes |
|----------|:---:|---------|
| Pure ALOHA | 0.184 | Earliest peak, fastest decline |
| Slotted ALOHA | 0.368 | Double the efficiency of pure ALOHA |
| 1-Persistent CSMA | $\dfrac{1}{e(1+2a)}$ | For $a=0.01$: $S_{max} \approx 0.529$ |
| Non-Persistent CSMA | $> \dfrac{1}{e(1+2a)}$ | For $a=0.01$: $S_{max} \approx 0.815$ |
| CSMA/CD | $\approx 0.94$ (large frames) | Best performance, but with collision detection cost |
| CSMA/CA | $\approx 0.6$–$0.8$ | Trade-off for wireless medium |
| Ideal Channel | 1.0 | Upper bound — no contention |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When a question asks "which is most efficient for given $a$?" — **non-persistent** beats **1-persistent** for typical non-zero $a$, and **CSMA/CD** beats both because it aborts collisions quickly.

## 4.1 State Machine of 1-Persistent CSMA

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Sensing: Frame ready to send
    Sensing --> Transmitting: Channel idle
    Sensing --> Waiting: Channel busy
    Waiting --> Sensing: Channel became idle
    Transmitting --> Collision: Detected other signal
    Transmitting --> Success: No collision detected
    Collision --> Backoff: Abort + Jam signal
    Backoff --> Sensing: After random wait
    Success --> Idle: Frame complete
    note right of Collision
      1-persistent: collisions happen
      when multiple stations wait
      for the same idle event
    end note
```
