# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *Explain in brief the working of CSMA/CD protocol. State one limitation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection (CSMA/CD)** is a contention-based MAC protocol used in classical IEEE 802.3 Ethernet. The procedure is: (1) the station senses the channel; (2) if idle, it begins transmitting; (3) it continues to monitor the channel *while transmitting*; (4) if a collision is detected (signal energy exceeds expected), the station immediately aborts transmission, sends a **jam signal** to inform all other stations, and then waits a random back-off interval using the **Binary Exponential Backoff algorithm** before retrying. 

**Limitation (1 mark):** CSMA/CD requires a station to *simultaneously* transmit and listen — which is **physically impossible in wireless media** because the transmitted signal overwhelms any received signal. Hence, CSMA/CD is restricted to **wired Ethernet** networks. Additionally, the minimum frame size of 64 bytes is required to ensure that the collision window can be fully detected before the frame finishes transmitting.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *Differentiate between 1-persistent CSMA and non-persistent CSMA.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | 1-Persistent CSMA | Non-Persistent CSMA |
|--------|------------------|---------------------|
| **Channel idle** | Transmits immediately (probability = 1) | Transmits immediately |
| **Channel busy** | *Continuously* monitors; transmits the instant channel goes idle | *Randomly waits* for a back-off interval, then re-senses |
| **Collision probability** | High — multiple waiting stations pounce at the same instant | Low — independent random waits |
| **Idle time waste** | Low | Higher |
| **Best suited for** | Light to moderate load | Heavy load |
| **Maximum throughput** | $\dfrac{1}{e(1+2a)}$ | Slightly higher than 1-persistent for non-zero $a$ |

**Conclusion:** Non-persistent CSMA trades channel idle time for fewer collisions, making it more efficient at high loads.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model 2024]**
> (a) Explain the different types of CSMA protocols with suitable diagrams. Compare their throughput performance. **(7 marks)**
> (b) With neat diagrams, explain the working of CSMA/CD. Discuss the Binary Exponential Backoff algorithm with an example. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**1. 1-Persistent CSMA (2 marks)**
- A station that wishes to transmit first senses the channel.
- If the channel is **idle**, it transmits immediately (with persistence 1).
- If the channel is **busy**, it *waits continuously* until the channel becomes idle, and then transmits immediately.
- **Disadvantage:** High collision probability if two or more stations are waiting for the same busy channel — both will transmit the moment it becomes idle.
- Throughput: $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G(1+2a)}}{G(1+2a) + 1 + e^{-aG}}$ and $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{e(1+2a)}$.

**2. Non-Persistent CSMA (2 marks)**
- If the channel is **idle**, the station transmits.
- If the channel is **busy**, the station waits a *random amount of time* and then re-senses.
- **Advantage:** Reduced collision probability because the random back-offs are independent.
- **Disadvantage:** Wasted channel capacity during the back-off interval.
- Throughput: $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1+2a) + e^{-aG}}$ — generally higher than 1-persistent for $a > 0$.

**3. p-Persistent CSMA (2 marks)**
- Designed for **slotted** channels; time is divided into slots of length $\tau$.
- If the channel is **idle** in the current slot, transmit with probability $p$; defer to next slot with probability $1-p$.
- If the channel is **busy**, wait until end of current transmission, then apply the above rule.
- **Limiting cases:** $p=1$ reduces to 1-persistent CSMA; $p \to 0$ is overly conservative.

**4. Comparison (1 mark):**
- Throughput order for typical $a$: **Non-persistent > 1-persistent** for moderate-to-heavy load.
- p-persistent bridges both behaviours through tuning of $p$.

#### Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**1. Working of CSMA/CD (3 marks)**
- Station senses the channel. If idle → start transmitting; if busy → wait until idle.
- Station *monitors* the medium during transmission. A collision is detected by observing signal energy higher than expected.
- On detecting a collision, the station **stops transmitting immediately**, sends a **jam signal** (32-bit pattern) to inform all other stations, and starts the back-off procedure.

**2. Binary Exponential Backoff (3 marks)**
- After the $n$-th collision, the station chooses a random integer $k$ in the range:
$$k \in \big[0,\ 2^{\min(n,\,10)} - 1\big]$$
- The station then waits $k$ slot times (each slot = $51.2\ \mu s$ in 10 Mbps Ethernet) before attempting retransmission.
- If collision recurs, the range doubles (BEB).
- After 16 consecutive collisions, the frame is dropped and the upper layer is notified.

**3. Example (1 mark)**
- After 1st collision: $k \in [0, 1]$.
- After 2nd collision: $k \in [0, 3]$.
- After 3rd collision: $k \in [0, 7]$.
- Maximum backoff time after 3rd collision = $7 \times 51.2\ \mu s = 358.4\ \mu s$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model 2024]**
> (a) What is CSMA/CA? Why is collision detection not used in wireless networks? Explain the working of CSMA/CA with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**
> (b) A 10 Mbps Ethernet network has a maximum propagation delay of 25.6 µs. Frames are 256 bits long. Calculate the parameter $a$ and the maximum throughput assuming CSMA/CD. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**1. CSMA/CA Definition (1 mark)**
CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with **Collision Avoidance**) is a MAC protocol for wireless networks (e.g., IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi) that attempts to **avoid** collisions before they happen, because wireless stations cannot reliably detect collisions during transmission.

**2. Why CSMA/CD Is Not Used in Wireless (2 marks)**
- *Hidden terminal problem:* Station A's signal may not reach station C, but B is between them and interferes.
- *Exposed terminal problem:* A station refrains from transmitting even when it could.
- *Near-far effect:* A strong nearby transmission drowns out a weaker far signal, making detection impossible.
- *Half-duplex radio:* A wireless radio cannot transmit and receive on the same frequency simultaneously — its own signal would saturate the receiver.
- *Signal attenuation:* Wireless signals are highly variable due to fading, multipath, and obstacles.

**3. Working of CSMA/CA (3 marks)**
- Station senses the channel.
- If the channel is **idle for DIFS** (DCF Inter-Frame Space), the station starts a **backoff counter** with a random value in $[0, CW]$.
- The counter decrements *only when the channel is idle*; it is *frozen* if the channel becomes busy.
- When the counter reaches 0, the station transmits.
- The receiver replies with an **ACK** after a **SIFS** (Short Inter-Frame Space) if the frame is received correctly.
- If the ACK is not received within a timeout, the **Contention Window $CW$ is doubled** (similar to BEB) and the process restarts.
- Optional **RTS/CTS handshake** can be used to further reduce the hidden terminal problem.

**4. Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the functional architecture diagram in Section 4.3.

#### Part (b) Model Solution — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Channel bit rate: $R = 10\ \text{Mbps} = 10 \times 10^6\ \text{bits/s}$
- Maximum propagation delay: $\tau = 25.6\ \mu s = 25.6 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}$
- Frame length: $L = 256\ \text{bits}$

**Step 1: Calculate Frame Transmission Time (2 marks)**

$$T_{fr} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{256\ \text{bits}}{10 \times 10^6\ \text{bits/s}}$$

$$T_{fr} = 25.6 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 25.6\ \mu s$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Parameter $a$ (2 marks)**

$$a = \frac{\tau}{T_{fr}} = \frac{25.6\ \mu s}{25.6\ \mu s} = 1.0$$

**Step 3: Apply CSMA/CD Throughput Formula (2 marks)**

$$S = \frac{1}{1 + 6.4a} = \frac{1}{1 + 6.4 \times 1.0} = \frac{1}{7.4}$$

$$S \approx 0.135$$

**Step 4: State the Result (1 mark)**

$$\boxed{\ a = 1.0 \quad ; \quad S_{max} \approx 0.135\ \text{or}\ 13.5\% \ }$$

**Interpretation:** The value $a = 1$ indicates that the propagation delay is *equal* to the frame transmission time — a relatively poor condition for CSMA/CD efficiency. The network achieves only about **13.5% efficiency**, much lower than the typical ~94% achievable for large frames. This shows that **short frames in high-latency networks are inefficient under CSMA/CD**, motivating the use of larger minimum frame sizes (64 bytes in standard Ethernet) and modern full-duplex switched networks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $a$ calculation:** Forgetting that $a = \tau/T_{fr}$, *not* $T_{fr}/\tau$ — reverses the efficiency interpretation.
> 2. **Wrong constant in CSMA/CD formula:** Using $S = 1/(1+2a)$ instead of $S = 1/(1+6.4a)$ — the 6.4 factor comes from the slot time of 512 bits in 10 Mbps Ethernet, not just from $2\tau$.
> 3. **Mixing CSMA/CD with CSMA/CA:** Writing that wireless networks use CSMA/CD — incorrect. Wireless uses CSMA/CA because of half-duplex radios.
> 4. **Skipping BEB derivation:** For marks on the backoff algorithm, write the *formula* for $k$, an *example*, and the *slot time* — all three.
> 5. **Ignoring the unit mismatch:** $\tau$ in microseconds vs. $T_{fr}$ in microseconds — keep them consistent.
> 6. **Forgetting the jam signal:** CSMA/CD explicitly includes a 32-bit jam signal after collision detection — many students omit this.
> 7. **No mention of $CW$ doubling in CSMA/CA:** Students often describe CSMA/CA as "listen and wait" but miss the **Contention Window doubling** on failure, which is essential.
> 8. **Stating vulnerable windows incorrectly:** For CSMA/CD, the relevant window is the *collision detection* window (≈ $\tau$), not the entire frame time.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — CSMA Protocols**

- [x] **Definition:** CSMA = "Listen before talk" to reduce collisions on shared medium.
- [x] **Three persistence variants:**
  - 1-persistent: greedy; transmit immediately on idle; high collision rate.
  - Non-persistent: polite; random back-off on busy; lower collision rate.
  - p-persistent: probabilistic; for slotted channels; tunable.
- [x] **CSMA/CD** (Collision Detection): Listen *while* transmitting; abort on collision; jam signal; BEB; used in classic Ethernet.
- [x] **CSMA/CA** (Collision Avoidance): Used in wireless; uses DIFS/SIFS timing; random back-off with $CW$ doubling; optional RTS/CTS.
- [x] **Key parameter:** $a = \tau/T_{fr}$ — high $a$ degrades all CSMA performance.
- [x] **Throughput order (typical):** CSMA/CD > CSMA/CA > Non-persistent CSMA > p-persistent > 1-persistent > Slotted ALOHA > Pure ALOHA.
- [x] **Throughput formulas (must memorize):**
  - Pure ALOHA: $S = Ge^{-2G}$; $S_{max} = 1/(2e) \approx 0.184$.
  - Slotted ALOHA: $S = Ge^{-G}$; $S_{max} = 1/e \approx 0.368$.
  - 1-persistent CSMA: $S_{max} = 1/(e(1+2a))$.
  - CSMA/CD: $S \approx 1/(1+6.4a)$.
- [x] **Binary Exponential Backoff:** $k \in [0, 2^{\min(n,10)}-1]$; slot time = 51.2 µs; max 16 retries.
- [x] **802.11 Inter-frame spaces:** SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS.
- [x] **Minimum Ethernet frame size:** 64 bytes (ensures collision window is fully detected).
- [x] **Real-world deployment:** CSMA/CD = IEEE 802.3 Ethernet (legacy); CSMA/CA = IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi, 802.15.4 ZigBee.
- [x] **Why CSMA/CD is impossible in wireless:** Half-duplex radio; strong self-signal drowns out collision detection.
- [x] **Hidden terminal problem:** A → B and C → B succeed even though A and C are unaware of each other.
- [x] **Exposed terminal problem:** B → A and C → D could succeed but B fears interfering with C.
- [x] **Engineering insight:** Higher $a$ means propagation-limited (e.g., satellite), lower $a$ means bandwidth-limited (e.g., LAN). CSMA is best when $a$ is small.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collision-Free Protocols, Wireless LANs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Collision-Free Protocols, Wireless LANs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Collision-Free Protocols?

In any shared-medium network, when multiple stations attempt to transmit simultaneously, their **signals overlap and corrupt each other**, producing what we call a **collision**. Pure (unslotted) **ALOHA** and **slotted ALOHA** simply accept collisions and recover through retransmission, while **CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access)** tries to *avoid* collisions by listening before talking — but it cannot prevent them entirely. **Collision-free protocols** go one step further: they use a deterministic or reservation-based scheme that **guarantees that, when two stations transmit, their frames will never collide**, even at the cost of some channel idle time or added control overhead.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> A **Collision-Free MAC Protocol** is a multiple-access control scheme in which the right to use the shared broadcast channel is granted to stations in a deterministic manner, eliminating the possibility of simultaneous transmissions by two or more stations. The guarantee is statistical in CSMA families but **absolute** in collision-free protocols such as the Bit-Map protocol, Binary Countdown, and Token Ring.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Conference Room Whiteboard"

Imagine **eight colleagues in a meeting room** who all want to talk, but only one microphone is shared. Two naive schemes exist:

| Naïve Scheme | Behaviour | Problem |
|---|---|---|
| **ALOHA style** | Anyone talks whenever they want | Two people speak at once → nobody is understood (collision) |
| **CSMA style** | Wait until the room is silent, then speak | The "hidden listener" (e.g., someone behind a pillar) cannot hear a soft speaker, so two people start talking, believing the room is silent |

A **collision-free protocol** is like installing a **whiteboard with eight checkboxes** at the front of the room. Each person who wants to speak **places a tick in their numbered slot** during a "reservation round." After the round, the chairperson scans the boxes from left to right, and the persons whose boxes are ticked speak **strictly one at a time** in numerical order. **No two people ever talk at once**, by construction.

The whiteboard is the *control channel*, the checkboxes are the *contention slots*, and the speaking turns are the *data slots* — the same logic used by the **Bit-Map Protocol**.

### 1.3 Why Collision-Free Protocols Matter for Wireless LANs

Wired Ethernet uses **CSMA/CD (Collision Detection)** — stations can detect a collision *while transmitting* and abort. Wireless stations **cannot** do this reliably because:

- The **near-far problem**: a strong local signal drowns out the weaker collision signal from a distant station.
- The **Hidden Station Problem**: Station A and Station C both communicate with B, but A and C are out of radio range of each other, so they transmit simultaneously and collide **at B**.
- The **Exposed Station Problem**: A station unnecessarily defers because it senses a neighbour's transmission, even though its own transmission would not actually interfere.

These issues make **collision detection impractical in wireless**, forcing the design of **collision-avoidance (CSMA/CA)** and reservation-based mechanisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard reference in KTU syllabus:** The **IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)** family is the dominant Wireless LAN standard, and it uses **CSMA/CA with RTS/CTS** as its core medium-access mechanism. The **IEEE 802.5 (Token Ring)** and **IEEE 802.4 (Token Bus)** were the canonical wired collision-free protocols, while **802.11e/802.11n/ac/ax** extended the wireless collision-avoidance philosophy with QoS and MIMO enhancements.

### 1.4 Classification of Collision-Free Protocols

| Family | How collision is avoided | Example Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| **Reservation-based** | Stations explicitly declare their intent in a contention period | **Bit-Map Protocol**, **Distributed Reservation Protocol** |
| **Token-based (Round-Robin)** | A logical token circulates; only the token holder may transmit | **Token Ring (802.5)**, **Token Bus (802.4)**, **FDDI** |
| **Binary Countdown (Contention-resolution)** | Stations transmit concurrent address bits; highest-numbered wins | **Binary Countdown Protocol** |
| **Wireless Collision-Avoidance** | Carrier sensing **plus** short control frames reserve the medium | **CSMA/CA**, **RTS/CTS**, **DCF/PCF in IEEE 802.11** |

### 1.5 Key Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Exam Recall)

- **DIFS (Distributed Inter-Frame Space) = 34 µs** in IEEE 802.11b/g (a DCF constant).
- **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space) = 10 µs** — used for highest-priority control frames.
- **PIFS = SIFS + SlotTime = 19 µs** — used by the PCF.
- **Slot Time = 9 µs** (802.11g) / **20 µs** (802.11a) / **9 µs** (802.11b).
- **CW_min (Contention Window minimum) = 31** slots; **CW_max = 1023** slots.
- **DCF** = Distributed Coordination Function (CSMA/CA-based).
- **PCF** = Point Coordination Function (poll-based, optional).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bit-Map Protocol (Reservation ALOHA)

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

Assume **N** stations share a single channel, and each station is assigned a fixed number from **0** to **N−1**.

**Step 1 — Contention Period (Contention Slots):**
The channel time is divided into *contention slots*, one per station. During the **k-th slot**, station *k* is allowed to broadcast a single bit called the **"ready bit"**:
- **1** → "I have a frame to send."
- **0** → "I have nothing to send."

Because each station knows its own slot number and the order, **no collisions occur** in the contention period.

**Step 2 — Transmission Period (Data Frames):**
After all N bits of the bit-map have been transmitted, every station knows exactly which stations wish to transmit. The stations then **transmit their data frames in numerical order of station number**.

**Step 3 — Repeat:**
After the last data frame, a new contention round begins, and the process repeats.

### 2.1.2 Channel Efficiency of Bit-Map Protocol

Let:
- **d** = propagation delay (one-way, end-to-end)
- **t_f** = time to transmit a single data frame
- **t_b** = time to transmit a single contention (bit-map) bit

A complete cycle consists of **N contention bits + data frames of stations that had something to say**. In the worst case (every station wants to send), the cycle length is:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = N \cdot t_b + N \cdot (t_f + 2d)
$$

The factor **2d** accounts for the propagation of the last bit of the data frame to the farthest station.

The **channel efficiency** is defined as:

$$
\eta_{\text{bitmap}} = \frac{\text{useful data transmission time per cycle}}{\text{total cycle duration}} = \frac{N \cdot t_f}{N \cdot t_b + N \cdot (t_f + 2d)}
$$

Simplifying (cancel N):

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{bitmap}} = \frac{t_f}{t_f + 2d + t_b}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The contention overhead grows linearly with **N**. For very large N, the bit-map protocol becomes inefficient. It is, however, **fair** (low-numbered stations get priority) and **collision-free by construction**.

### 2.1.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The bit-map protocol's idea survives in modern systems as **TDMA reservation schemes** (e.g., in cellular systems and satellite networks), where each user gets a *mini-slot* to request bandwidth. It also inspired the **Distributed Reservation Protocol** used in some IEEE 802.16 (WiMAX) profiles.

## 2.2 Binary Countdown Protocol

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

1. Every station that wants to send transmits its **binary station address** bit-by-bit, starting from the **most significant bit (MSB)**.
2. All stations transmit **concurrently on the channel** using a wired-OR rule: the logical OR of all transmitted bits is what is heard.
3. After each bit, every station examines the channel:
   - If it transmitted a **0** but the channel carries a **1**, it **loses the contention** and drops out for the rest of the round.
   - Stations that transmitted a 1 (and saw a 1 on the channel) **continue**.
4. The station whose address is *highest* (in binary) survives and gets the channel.

**Why it is collision-free:** Because stations that lose drop out *immediately* and do not transmit further, the surviving station's bits propagate without interference.

### 2.2.2 Property

The winning station's identity is implicitly known to all (highest address) — no extra signalling is required. **O(log N) bits** are needed per arbitration, compared to **N bits** in the bit-map.

## 2.3 Token Passing Protocols (Token Ring, Token Bus)

### 2.3.1 Token Ring (IEEE 802.5)

- Stations are physically or logically arranged in a **ring** (closed loop).
- A small control frame called the **token** circulates continuously.
- A station may **transmit only when it holds the token**.
- After transmitting (or if it has nothing to send), the station **passes the token** to the next station in the ring.
- Token Ring operates at **4 Mbps or 16 Mbps** (classic); later extensions (HSTR, 802.5t) reached 100 Mbps and 1 Gbps.

### 2.3.2 Token Management Issues

- **Token loss** (noise destroys token) → a designated **Active Monitor** detects silence and regenerates a token.
- **Orphaned frame** (token holder crashes mid-transmission) → monitor strips the frame.
- **Duplicate token** → monitor enforces uniqueness via a special claim-token process.
- **Star-shaped rings (MAUs)** → physical star, logical ring.

### 2.3.3 Token Bus (IEEE 802.4)

- Stations are connected to a **linear bus** but form a **logical ring**.
- The token passes from one MAC address to the next-highest address on the bus.
- Used in **MAP (Manufacturing Automation Protocol)** industrial networks.

### 2.3.4 Throughput of Token Ring

Define:
- **T_token** = time to transmit a token (3 bytes in IEEE 802.5: SD, AC, ED = 24 bits)
- **t_f** = average frame transmission time
- **N** = number of stations
- **d** = propagation delay per link
- **L** = total cable length

The maximum token-holding time per station is enforced as **THT (Token Holding Time)**, e.g., **10 ms** in classic 16-Mbps Token Ring.

The **mean cycle time** (token goes around once) is:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = N \cdot T_{\text{token}} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{f,i} + N \cdot d
$$

The throughput is the total useful frame bits transmitted per cycle divided by cycle duration. For a fully loaded ring of N stations with equal-length frames:

$$
\boxed{\;S_{\text{ring}} = \frac{N \cdot t_f}{N \cdot (T_{\text{token}} + d + t_f)}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Token Ring and Bit-Map are the two most-tested classical collision-free protocols. The examiner expects you to draw the timeline, identify propagation delay, and compute efficiency.

## 2.4 Wireless LANs — IEEE 802.11 Architecture

### 2.4.1 Network Components

- **Station (STA)** — end device (laptop, phone).
- **Access Point (AP)** — bridges wireless STAs to a wired Distribution System (DS).
- **Basic Service Set (BSS)** — one AP + its associated stations.
- **Extended Service Set (ESS)** — multiple BSSs interconnected via a DS.
- **Independent BSS (IBSS / Ad-hoc network)** — no AP; STAs talk peer-to-peer.

### 2.4.2 The 802.11 MAC Sub-Layer

The MAC layer is split into two coordination functions:

| Function | Full Name | Mechanism | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DCF** | Distributed Coordination Function | CSMA/CA with binary exponential backoff | Best-effort, contention-based |
| **PCF** | Point Coordination Function | AP polls STAs in round-robin | Real-time, contention-free |

PCF is rarely implemented in practice; DCF dominates. Modern QoS (802.11e) introduces **HCF (Hybrid Coordination Function)** with EDCA and HCCA.

### 2.4.3 The Inter-Frame Spaces (IFS) — The Heartbeat of 802.11

Stations must remain idle for the appropriate IFS before transmitting, and this **ordering** creates a priority hierarchy:

$$
\boxed{\;SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS\;}
$$

| IFS | Typical Value (802.11b) | Used by |
|---|---|---|
| **SIFS** | 10 µs | ACK, CTS, fragments |
| **PIFS** | 19 µs (= SIFS + slot) | PCF (real-time) |
| **DIFS** | 34 µs (= PIFS + slot) | DCF best-effort data |
| **EIFS** | 364 µs | Recovery from erroneous frames |

A station that senses the medium idle for **DIFS**, then waits a random backoff, then transmits — this is the **CSMA/CA** flow.

## 2.5 CSMA/CA and the Hidden-Station Solution — RTS/CTS

### 2.5.1 Plain CSMA/CA (Without RTS/CTS)

1. Station senses the channel. If idle for **DIFS**, it picks a random backoff counter uniformly from **[0, CW]**.
2. The counter decrements while the medium is idle; freezes when the medium becomes busy.
3. When the counter reaches 0, the station transmits.
4. Receiver waits **SIFS**, then sends an **ACK** if the frame's CRC passes.
5. If no ACK arrives within the timeout, the sender assumes collision/interference and **doubles the contention window** (Binary Exponential Backoff), capped at **CW_max = 1023**.

### 2.5.2 RTS/CTS Handshake (IEEE 802.11 Optional Mechanism)

To defeat the **hidden station problem**, the sender first transmits a short **Request-To-Send (RTS)** frame (20 bytes) to the receiver. The receiver replies with **Clear-To-Send (CTS)** (14 bytes) after **SIFS**.

All stations hearing the RTS or CTS **set their NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** for the duration declared in the frame's duration field — this is a virtual carrier-sense mechanism. The hidden station *A* (which cannot hear B) hears B's RTS (or C's CTS), sets its NAV, and refrains from transmitting during the data exchange.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** RTS/CTS adds overhead of ~32 bytes per data frame. It is therefore typically used only when frame sizes exceed the **RTS_Threshold** (configurable, default ~500 bytes). For small frames, plain CSMA/CA suffices.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Constant Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bit-Map efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{t_f}{t_f + 2d + t_b}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| 2 | Token Ring cycle (N stations) | $T_{\text{cyc}} = N(T_{\text{tok}} + d + t_f)$ | seconds |
| 3 | Token-passing throughput | $S = \dfrac{N \cdot t_f}{N \cdot (T_{\text{tok}} + d + t_f)}$ | frames per second equivalent |
| 4 | DIFS | $SIFS + 2 \cdot \text{SlotTime}$ | 34 µs in 802.11b |
| 5 | PIFS | $SIFS + 1 \cdot \text{SlotTime}$ | 19 µs in 802.11b |
| 6 | Contention Window after *i* collisions | $CW_i = \min(\,2^{i+4}-1,\; CW_{\max}\,)$ | $CW_0=15,\;CW_{\max}=1023$ |
| 7 | Backoff slots | $\text{BO} = \text{Rand}[\,0,\; CW_i\,]$ | Integer slots |
| 8 | NAV setting | $NAV = \text{Duration field of RTS/CTS/data}$ | Microseconds |
| 9 | SIFS value (802.11b) | **10 µs** | Fixed per PHY |
| 10 | 802.11b bit rate | 1, 2, 5.5, 11 Mbps | DSSS |
| 11 | 802.11a bit rate | 6 to 54 Mbps | OFDM, 5 GHz |
| 12 | 802.11g bit rate | 1 to 54 Mbps | OFDM, 2.4 GHz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Use:** These formulas directly drive the design of Wi-Fi access points (Atheros, Broadcom, Intel chipsets), 5G NR scheduling, and IoT MACs (IEEE 802.15.4 / ZigBee) that borrow heavily from the CSMA/CA model. The hidden-station problem is also studied in vehicular networks (WAVE/DSRC) and satellite constellations (Starlink scheduling).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Channel Efficiency of Bit-Map Protocol

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A **broadcast channel** is shared by **N = 4** stations. Each station has a frame of length **L = 1000 bits**. The bit rate is **R = 1 Mbps**, so the frame transmission time is:

$$
t_f = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1000 \text{ bits}}{10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

Let **d = 25 µs** (one-way propagation) and the contention-slot duration equal the one-bit transmission time at 1 Mbps:

$$
t_b = \frac{1}{R} = 1 \mu s
$$

Find the **channel efficiency** in the worst case (all 4 stations want to send).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Compute the **2d** round-trip propagation term:

$$
2d = 2 \cdot 25 \mu s = 50 \mu s
$$

**Step 2** — Write the **cycle duration** (in the worst case, all 4 stations transmit):

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = N \cdot t_b + N \cdot (t_f + 2d)
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 4 \cdot (1 \mu s) + 4 \cdot (1 \text{ ms} + 50 \mu s)
$$

**Step 3** — Convert everything to the same unit. Use **microseconds** (1 ms = 1000 µs):

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 4 \mu s + 4 \cdot (1000 \mu s + 50 \mu s) = 4 \mu s + 4 \cdot 1050 \mu s
$$

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = 4 \mu s + 4200 \mu s = 4204 \mu s
$$

**Step 4** — Compute **useful data transmission time** (4 data frames of 1 ms each):

$$
T_{\text{data}} = N \cdot t_f = 4 \cdot 1000 \mu s = 4000 \mu s
$$

**Step 5** — Compute **efficiency**:

$$
\eta = \frac{T_{\text{data}}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{4000 \mu s}{4204 \mu s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\eta \approx 0.9515 = 95.15\%\;}
$$

### 3.1.3 Sanity Check (Limits)

- If **N → ∞** with **t_f, d, t_b fixed**, $T_{\text{cycle}} \sim N(t_f + 2d + t_b)$ and $T_{\text{data}} \sim N t_f$, so $\eta \to \dfrac{t_f}{t_f + 2d + t_b}$ → independent of N at the limit. ✔
- If **d → 0** (very short cable), $\eta \to \dfrac{t_f}{t_f + t_b} \to 1$ as $t_b \ll t_f$. ✔
- If **t_f → 0** (infinitesimal frames), $\eta \to 0$ (overhead dominates). ✔

## 3.2 Derivation — Throughput of Token Ring

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A **16-Mbps IEEE 802.5 Token Ring** has **N = 20 stations** arranged on a **closed loop of total length L = 1000 m**. Propagation speed is **v = 2 \times 10^8 m/s**. The token is **3 bytes = 24 bits**, and the average frame length is **L_f = 4000 bits**. All stations always have a frame ready.

Find the **maximum sustainable throughput**.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Token transmission time:

$$
T_{\text{token}} = \frac{24 \text{ bits}}{16 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 1.5 \mu s
$$

**Step 2** — One-way propagation time around the whole ring:

$$
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{1000 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 5 \mu s
$$

**Step 3** — Frame transmission time:

$$
t_f = \frac{4000 \text{ bits}}{16 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 250 \mu s
$$

**Step 4** — Per-station per-cycle time = token transit + frame transmission + propagation (the *1* here is per station):

$$
T_{\text{station}} = T_{\text{token}} + t_f + T_{\text{prop}} = 1.5 \mu s + 250 \mu s + 5 \mu s = 256.5 \mu s
$$

**Step 5** — Full cycle for **N = 20** stations:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = N \cdot T_{\text{station}} = 20 \cdot 256.5 \mu s = 5130 \mu s
$$

**Step 6** — Useful data bits per cycle:

$$
\text{Data bits} = N \cdot L_f = 20 \cdot 4000 = 80{,}000 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 7** — Throughput in bits per second:

$$
S = \frac{\text{Data bits}}{T_{\text{cycle}}} = \frac{80{,}000 \text{ bits}}{5130 \mu s} = \frac{80{,}000}{5130 \times 10^{-6}} \text{ bps}
$$

$$
S = 15.595 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} \approx 15.6 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 8** — Channel utilization:

$$
\boxed{\;U = \frac{S}{R_{\text{link}}} = \frac{15.6}{16} \approx 97.5\%\;}
$$

### 3.2.3 Interpretation

At 97.5% utilization, Token Ring is *highly* efficient for this configuration. The remaining 2.5% is consumed by the token itself, the bit-time to circulate, and the per-frame propagation. The maximum theoretical efficiency approaches 100% as the frame length grows.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Collision-Free Protocols

| Property | Bit-Map | Binary Countdown | Token Ring | CSMA/CA (802.11) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reservation mechanism** | N-bit contention map | Address-bit contention | Physical/logical token | Carrier sense + RTS/CTS |
| **Collision-free?** | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes | ✔ Yes | ✔ Collisions avoided, not guaranteed |
| **Worst-case access delay** | $2N \cdot t_b$ (one contention cycle) | $\log_2 N$ bit slots | N token rotations (THT) | Bounded by CW_max retries |
| **Overhead per frame** | 1/N contention bit amortized | $\log_2 N$ bits | 1 token = 3 bytes | RTS+CTS+ACK = ~40 bytes |
| **Fairness** | Strict (low-numbered first) | Address-dependent | Strict (token order) | Best-effort with random backoff |
| **Failure mode** | Clock-drift between stations | Clock-drift | Token loss / duplication | Hidden-station residual collision |
| **Scalability** | Poor (O(N) overhead) | Good (O(log N)) | Moderate | Excellent (used up to thousands of nodes) |
| **Used in** | Historical, satellite TDMA | Early studies, theoretical | Old Token Ring LANs | **All modern Wi-Fi networks** |

## 3.4 Derivation — CSMA/CA Backoff in IEEE 802.11

### 3.4.1 Problem Setup

A station is in DCF mode. After its **2nd failed transmission attempt**, what is the range of the random backoff counter? (Standard 802.11b, $CW_{\min}=31$, $CW_{\max}=1023$.)

### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Recall the formula:

$$
CW_i = \min(\, 2^{i+4} - 1,\; CW_{\max}\,)
$$

where **i** is the number of *previous transmission attempts*. For **i = 2** (the station has already failed twice, now starting the **3rd** attempt, but the contention window is computed using **i = current retry count**):

- Convention: **i = 0** at the first attempt, so after 2 failed attempts, **i = 2**.

**Step 2** — Substitute:

$$
CW_2 = \min(\, 2^{2+4} - 1,\; 1023\,) = \min(\, 2^{6} - 1,\; 1023\,) = \min(\,63,\; 1023\,) = 63
$$

**Step 3** — Backoff is uniform in **[0, CW]**:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Backoff} \in \text{Uniform}[0, 63] \text{ slots} = [0, 567] \mu s\;}
$$

(multiplying by 9 µs per slot in 802.11b).

**Step 4** — If the 3rd attempt also fails, **i = 3**:

$$
CW_3 = \min(2^{7} - 1, 1023) = \min(127, 1023) = 127
$$

The window keeps doubling until it hits **1023** at **i = 6** (i.e., $2^{6+4}-1 = 1023$).

> [!NOTE]
> **After CW_max is reached**, IEEE 802.11 retries up to a maximum of **`dot11LongRetryLimit = 4`** (or 7 for short frames) before giving up and dropping the MSDU. The 802.11e amendment modifies this to per-AC retry limits for QoS differentiation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bit-Map Protocol Timeline (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph C[Contention Period - N slots, no collisions]
        C0["Slot 0: Station 0 broadcasts its ready bit"]
        C1["Slot 1: Station 1 broadcasts its ready bit"]
        C2["Slot 2: Station 2 broadcasts its ready bit"]
        C3["Slot 3: Station 3 broadcasts its ready bit"]
    end
    subgraph T[Data Transmission Period - in numerical order]
        D0["Stn 0 sends its data frame"]
        D1["Stn 1 sends its data frame"]
        D3["Stn 3 sends its data frame"]
        D2["Stn 2 sends its data frame"]
    end
    C0 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> D0 --> D1 --> D3 --> D2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Stations whose ready bit was **1** transmit data in increasing order of station number. In the example, all four stations want to send, so all four data frames follow the contention period.

## 4.2 Binary Countdown Arbitration (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Round 1: All wishing stations transmit MSB of their address concurrently"]
    B{"Channel OR-result = 1 ?"}
    C["Station loses, drops out for rest of round"]
    D["Station survives - continues to next bit"]
    E["Round k: only survivors transmit k-th bit"]
    F{"Only one survivor?"}
    G["That station wins and transmits its data frame"]
    A --> B
    B -- "Yes, and I sent 0" --> C
    B -- "I sent 1, sees 1" --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F -- "No" --> E
    F -- "Yes" --> G
```

## 4.3 IEEE 802.11 Architecture Stack (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph App["Upper Layers"]
        A1["Application HTTP, Video, VoIP"]
        A2["Transport TCP UDP"]
    end
    subgraph LLC["Logical Link Control 802.2"]
        L1["LLC SAP Multiplexing"]
    end
    subgraph MAC["MAC Sublayer 802.11"]
        M1["DCF Distributed Coordination Function"]
        M2["PCF Point Coordination Function"]
        M3["MAC Frame Construction"]
        M4["Fragmentation Reassembly"]
        M5["NAV Network Allocation Vector"]
    end
    subgraph PHY["Physical Layer Variants"]
        P1["802.11b DSSS 2.4 GHz"]
        P2["802.11a OFDM 5 GHz"]
        P3["802.11g OFDM 2.4 GHz"]
        P4["802.11n MIMO OFDM"]
        P5["802.11ac MU-MIMO 5 GHz"]
        P6["802.11ax Wi-Fi 6 OFDMA"]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> L1
    L1 --> M1
    L1 --> M2
    M1 --> M3
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M3 --> M5
    M3 --> P1
    M3 --> P2
    M3 --> P3
    M3 --> P4
    M3 --> P5
    M3 --> P6
```

## 4.4 Hidden Station vs Exposed Station (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A((Station A)) -- "Signal X to B" --> B((AP B))
    A -. "Cannot hear C" .-> C((Station C))
    C -- "Signal Y to B" --> B
    D((Station D)) -- "Transmitting to E" --> E((AP E))
    F((Station F)) -. "Senses D, defers" .-> G((Wants to send to H))
    G -- "But F to H would not collide with D to E" --> H((Receiver H))
```

> **Interpretation:**  
> - **Hidden-station case:** A and C cannot sense each other, but both transmit to B → frames collide **at B**.  
> - **Exposed-station case:** F senses D's transmission and unnecessarily defers, even though F→H would not collide with D→E.

## 4.5 CSMA/CA Frame Exchange with RTS/CTS (Mermaid Timing Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant M as Medium
    participant R as Receiver
    participant H as Hidden Station
    Note over S: Wait DIFS, then random backoff
    S->>M: RTS (sets NAV in all hearing STAs)
    M->>R: RTS arrives after SIFS-equivalent propagation
    Note over R: SIFS wait
    R->>M: CTS
    M->>S: CTS arrives
    Note over S: SIFS wait
    S->>M: DATA frame
    M->>R: DATA arrives
    Note over R: SIFS wait
    R->>M: ACK
    Note over H: H heard the CTS, NAV reserves channel, stays silent
    M-->>H: CTS heard
    Note over S: Channel is free again after ACK + DIFS
```

## 4.6 Token Ring Logical Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1((Station 1)) -- "Token" --> S2((Station 2))
    S2 -- "Token or data" --> S3((Station 3))
    S3 -- "Token" --> S4((Station 4))
    S4 -- "Token" --> S5((Station 5))
    S5 -- "Token returns" --> S1
    S2 -- "Has data: transmits, then forwards token" --> S3
    S1 -. "Has no data: forwards token immediately" .-> S2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**"Differentiate between collision and contention in a multiple access protocol. Why is collision detection (CD) unsuitable for wireless media?"**

**Model Answer (≈3-mark depth):**

| Aspect | Collision | Contention |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Two or more stations transmit on a shared channel simultaneously, corrupting each other's frames | Two or more stations *compete* to use the channel; the winner is chosen by the protocol (CSMA, RTS/CTS, etc.) |
| **Outcome** | Frame loss → retransmission required | Either orderly access (CSMA) or arbitration (collision-free) |
| **In CD protocols** | Detected and aborted in flight | Resolved *before* transmission by sensing the carrier |

**Why CD fails in wireless:**

1. **Near-far problem:** A strong local transmit signal from a wireless station completely masks the much weaker collision signal from a distant station. The NIC cannot hear a collision while transmitting.
2. **Hidden station problem:** A and C may both transmit to B, but A cannot even sense C's signal — so A's CD never triggers, yet B receives a corrupted frame.
3. **Cost and complexity:** Implementing full-duplex radio (transmit + listen simultaneously) was historically impractical and expensive for consumer Wi-Fi.
4. **Half-duplex radio convention:** IEEE 802.11 radios are designed as half-duplex; the antenna switches between transmit and receive, not both.

Hence 802.11 uses **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)** — it *tries to prevent* collisions through carrier sensing, random backoff, and RTS/CTS, rather than detect them.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
**"List and briefly explain the four Inter-Frame Spaces (IFS) used in IEEE 802.11 DCF. Why are multiple IFS values needed?"**

**Model Answer (≈3-mark depth):**

| IFS | Full Name | Value (802.11b) | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SIFS** | Short Inter-Frame Space | 10 µs | ACK, CTS, fragmented data, polling responses |
| **PIFS** | PCF Inter-Frame Space | 19 µs | AP polling (PCF) |
| **DIFS** | DCF Inter-Frame Space | 34 µs | Regular data frames (contention-based) |
| **EIFS** | Extended Inter-Frame Space | 364 µs | Recovery from erroneous frame reception |

**Why multiple IFS values are needed:**

The different IFS values establish a **priority hierarchy**. After the medium becomes idle, the station that waited the **shortest** IFS gets to transmit first. Hence:

- An **ACK** (SIFS) jumps in before a regular data frame (DIFS) — critical for collision recovery.
- The **PCF** controller (PIFS) can pre-empt normal traffic for real-time polling.
- A station that just received a corrupted frame defers for **EIFS** to give the real sender time to retransmit — avoiding unfair capture of the channel.

In short, IFS values provide a **hardware-enforced priority mechanism** without needing a separate signalling channel.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — Token Ring and Bit-Map

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question]**
> **(a)** With a clear timeline diagram, explain the operation of the **Bit-Map Protocol**. Derive an expression for its channel efficiency in the worst case. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A Token Ring LAN with 25 stations operates at 16 Mbps. The total ring length is 1 km, the propagation speed is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s, the token is 24 bits long, and the average frame length is 4000 bits. If every station always has a frame to send, compute **(i)** the cycle time, **(ii)** the throughput, and **(iii)** the channel utilization. Comment on the result. (7 marks)

#### ✍️ Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):**  
The Bit-Map Protocol divides time into **two alternating periods**:
- **Contention period** of N bit-slots, where station *k* transmits a 1 in slot *k* if it has a frame.
- **Transmission period** where the willing stations transmit in *numerical order*.

**Step 2 — Timeline (2 marks):**

```
|--- Contention (N bits) ---|---- Stn0 data ----|---- Stn1 data ----| ... 
```

**Step 3 — Derivation (3 marks):**  
Worst-case cycle (all N stations transmit):

$$
T_{\text{cyc}} = N t_b + N (t_f + 2d)
$$

Useful data time:

$$
T_{\text{data}} = N t_f
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\boxed{\;\eta = \frac{N t_f}{N t_b + N(t_f + 2d)} = \frac{t_f}{t_f + t_b + 2d}\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Discussion (1 mark):**  
The bit-map overhead grows linearly with **N**, so the protocol is not scalable to thousands of nodes. However, it is **fair** and **collision-free by construction** because each station transmits in its *own* predetermined slot.

#### ✍️ Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Constants (1 mark):**

$$
T_{\text{token}} = \frac{24 \text{ bits}}{16 \times 10^6} = 1.5 \mu s,\quad
T_{\text{prop}} = \frac{1000}{2\times 10^8} = 5 \mu s
$$

$$
t_f = \frac{4000}{16 \times 10^6} = 250 \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Per-station cycle (1 mark):**

$$
T_{\text{stn}} = T_{\text{token}} + t_f + T_{\text{prop}} = 1.5 + 250 + 5 = 256.5 \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Full cycle for 25 stations (1 mark):**

$$
T_{\text{cyc}} = 25 \times 256.5 = 6412.5 \mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Throughput (2 marks):**

$$
S = \frac{N \cdot L_f}{T_{\text{cyc}}} = \frac{25 \cdot 4000 \text{ bits}}{6412.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}}
$$

$$
S = 15.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} = 15.6 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Utilization (1 mark):**

$$
U = \frac{S}{16 \text{ Mbps}} = 0.975 = 97.5\%
$$

**Step 6 — Comment (1 mark):**  
With long frames and a small ring, Token Ring achieves near-wire-speed utilization (97.5%). Only 2.5% of channel time is spent on the token and propagation. **Conclusion:** Token Ring is highly efficient for heavy, sustained load but suffers high access delay under light load because the token must still circulate to a station that has nothing to send.

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — Wireless LANs and CSMA/CA

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Question]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Hidden Station** and **Exposed Station** problems in wireless LANs. With a sequence diagram, show how **RTS/CTS** in IEEE 802.11 solves the hidden-station problem. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A wireless LAN operates under IEEE 802.11b DCF. A station's first three transmission attempts have failed. Compute the contention window size and the maximum backoff time (in microseconds) for the **4th attempt**. State the formula for binary exponential backoff. Given: SIFS = 10 µs, slot time = 9 µs, CW_min = 31, CW_max = 1023. (7 marks)

#### ✍️ Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Hidden Station (2 marks):**  
Three stations A, B, C. A and C are out of radio range. Both have frames for B. A senses the channel idle (cannot hear C), transmits. C also senses the channel idle (cannot hear A), transmits. **The two frames collide at B** — A and C never know.

**Step 2 — Exposed Station (1.5 marks):**  
B is transmitting to A. C wants to send to D. C senses the medium busy (B's signal) and defers. But C → D would not collide with B → A at receivers A and D respectively. **C unnecessarily waits**, wasting bandwidth.

**Step 3 — RTS/CTS Solution (2.5 marks):**  
Sender transmits **RTS** (20 bytes) after DIFS + backoff. Receiver replies **CTS** (14 bytes) after SIFS. Both RTS and CTS contain a **Duration field** = total NAV reservation. All stations hearing either frame **set their NAV timer** and stay silent for that duration. The hidden station hears the CTS (not the RTS) and defers. The exposed station hears the RTS but the CTS it sets its NAV to does not overlap with its intended transmission (since the receiver of the hidden-station data is different).

**Step 4 — Sequence diagram (1 mark)** — see the Mermaid sequence in SECTION 4.5.

#### ✍️ Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — BEB formula (2 marks):**

$$
CW_i = \min(\, 2^{i+4} - 1,\; CW_{\max}\,)
$$

where **i** is the retry counter (0 for first attempt).

**Step 2 — Compute CW for the 4th attempt (2 marks):**  
The 4th attempt is the retry number **i = 3** (since i = 0 for the 1st, 1 for the 2nd, 2 for the 3rd, **3 for the 4th**).

$$
CW_3 = \min(2^{3+4} - 1,\; 1023) = \min(2^7 - 1,\; 1023) = \min(127,\; 1023) = 127
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum backoff slots (1 mark):**

$$
\text{BO}_{\max} = CW_3 = 127 \text{ slots}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum backoff time in microseconds (2 marks):**

$$
T_{\text{BO, max}} = 127 \text{ slots} \times 9 \mu s/\text{slot} = 1143 \mu s
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Off-by-one error:** Many students use $i = 4$ for the 4th attempt. The correct value is **i = 3**, because the *first* attempt corresponds to $i = 0$. **Read the question carefully** — "after 3 failed attempts" means we are about to start attempt number 4, with retry count **i = 3**.
> 2. **Formula confusion:** Some students write $CW = 2^i$ or $CW = 2^{i-1}$ — the *correct* 802.11 BEB is $2^{i+4} - 1$. The "+4" comes from $CW_0 = 15 = 2^4 - 1$.
> 3. **Forgetting CW_max cap:** At high retry counts the min operation kicks in. Always check whether your computed value exceeds **1023**.
> 4. **Unit slip:** Multiply the *number of slots* by the *slot duration* — do not just leave the answer in slots.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 The 5 Must-Know Concepts:**
> 1. **Bit-Map Protocol** — N-bit contention map + ordered transmission. Efficiency $\eta = \frac{t_f}{t_f + 2d + t_b}$. Fair, collision-free, **not scalable**.
> 2. **Binary Countdown** — Address-bit contention, OR-rule, highest-address wins. **O(log N)** bits, no extra info needed.
> 3. **Token Ring / Token Bus** — Logical or physical ring; **only token holder transmits**. Throughput $\approx$ link rate for heavy load. Failure modes: token loss, duplicate, orphaned frame → **Active Monitor** resolves.
> 4. **IEEE 802.11 DCF** — CSMA/CA + BEB + RTS/CTS (optional). IFS hierarchy: **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS**. NAV is virtual carrier sense.
> 5. **Hidden & Exposed Station** — Hidden: solved by RTS/CTS and NAV. Exposed: only partially solved in 802.11 (still wastes some time).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📌 Key Formulas (Quick-Recall):**
> - Bit-Map efficiency: $\eta = \frac{t_f}{t_f + 2d + t_b}$
> - Token Ring cycle: $T_{\text{cyc}} = N \cdot (T_{\text{tok}} + d + t_f)$
> - 802.11 BEB window: $CW_i = \min(2^{i+4}-1,\; 1023)$
> - DIFS = SIFS + 2 × SlotTime

> [!TIP]
> **🧠 Exam Strategy (per KTU 2024 pattern):**
> - For 3-mark questions: draw the **timeline diagram** and **state the formula**. 1 diagram + 1 formula = usually full marks.
> - For 14-mark questions: **always show units** in every intermediate step; examiners explicitly award a mark for unit consistency.
> - If asked for the *worst case* of a collision-free protocol, mention the *lower-numbered station priority* (Bit-Map) or the *token-holding-time* (Token Ring) — students often lose a mark by ignoring this.
> - For wireless questions, always mention **half-duplex radios** and **near-far problem** as the *fundamental reason* CSMA/CD is not used.

> **Final Word:** Collision-free protocols are the deterministic counterpart of CSMA. They trade **channel utilization under low load** for **absolute collision guarantees**. In modern networks, the philosophy lives on inside every Wi-Fi frame exchange (RTS/CTS/ACK triplet) and every cellular scheduling decision (5G NR's mini-slot reservations).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Link Layer Switching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Link Layer Switching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Link Layer (Layer 2) Switching** is the process of forwarding data frames between network segments by examining the destination **Medium Access Control (MAC) address** contained in the frame header, and selectively delivering the frame only to the appropriate port. The fundamental device that performs this function is the **Bridge** (or, in modern terminology, a **Layer 2 Switch**), which operates strictly at the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) of the **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OECPT613 – Module 2):**
> Data Link Layer switching concerns the construction of forwarding tables, isolation of collision domains, the operation of **Transparent Bridges**, the **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)**, and the architecture of **Virtual Local Area Networks (VLANs)**. All switching decisions are based on the **48-bit MAC address** of the frame.

The Data Link Layer switch is essentially a multi-port bridge with hardware-based forwarding capability, providing:
- **Frame filtering and forwarding** based on MAC addresses
- **Collision domain separation** (each port is a separate collision domain)
- **Selective frame delivery** (no broadcast storms in unicast traffic)
- **Increased aggregate bandwidth** (each segment can operate independently)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Post Office"

Imagine a massive post office that receives thousands of letters (frames) every second. Each letter has the recipient's house number and street (MAC address) printed on it. The postmaster does not read the letter's content; he only glances at the address and drops it into the correct outgoing mailbox (port) that serves that street.

| Post Office Concept | Data Link Layer Switch Concept |
|---|---|
| Letter | Data Frame |
| House number + Street | Destination MAC Address |
| Postmaster | Bridge / Switch |
| Outgoing mailbox | Switch Port |
| City map | Forwarding / MAC Address Table |
| Mis-sorted letter | Frame dropped or flooded |

This analogy captures the essence: the switch never looks at the *content* (which is the job of the Network Layer), it only looks at the *header's address field* and makes a forwarding decision. Critically, if the postmaster does not recognize a new address, he sends a copy of the letter to *every* other mailbox — this is called **flooding**, which is fundamental to bridge learning.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard metrics are essential for KTU 2024 exam answers:
> - **MAC Address Size:** **48 bits** (expressed as 12 hexadecimal digits, e.g., `00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E`)
> - **Bridge / Switch Operating Layer:** **OSI Layer 2 (Data Link Layer)**
> - **Forwarding Table Aging Time:** Typically **300 seconds (5 minutes)** in IEEE 802.1D
> - **STP Hello Time:** **2 seconds**
> - **STP Max Age Timer:** **20 seconds**
> - **STP Forward Delay:** **15 seconds**
> - **Default VLAN (Virtual LAN):** **VLAN 1**

## 1.4 Why Data Link Layer Switching Matters

In a shared Ethernet bus (legacy coaxial cable), all devices compete for the same physical medium, and any two simultaneous transmissions cause a **collision**. By breaking the network into switched segments:
- Each port becomes its own **collision domain** (collisions stay local to the port).
- The total available bandwidth is **multiplied** by the number of switched segments.
- Security is improved because frames are delivered only to intended recipients.
- Network scalability is enhanced (more devices can be added without slowing everyone down).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Hierarchy of Layer 2 Switching

Layer 2 switching evolves from simple bridges to sophisticated managed switches. The hierarchy, in order of increasing capability, is:

1. **Transparent Bridge** — A two-port device that learns MAC addresses and forwards frames.
2. **Multi-port Bridge / Learning Bridge** — A bridge with more than two ports; maintains a forwarding table.
3. **Layer 2 Switch** — A hardware-based multi-port bridge with dedicated ASICs for high-speed forwarding.
4. **Managed Switch** — A switch supporting VLANs, STP, port mirroring, QoS, and SNMP management.
5. **Layer 3 Switch** — Performs both Layer 2 switching and Layer 3 routing (multi-layer switch).

## 2.2 Transparent Bridges and the Learning Process

A **transparent bridge** is "transparent" because the connected network endpoints (hosts) do not need any configuration changes — they are unaware that a bridge is in the network. The bridge has three core functional components:

- **Learning:** Examines the **source MAC address** of every incoming frame and records the port on which it arrived in the forwarding table.
- **Flooding:** When a frame arrives with a **destination MAC** not in the table, the bridge copies it to *every* port except the one it arrived on.
- **Forwarding:** If the destination MAC *is* in the table, the frame is sent *only* to the corresponding port.
- **Filtering:** If the destination MAC is on the same port as the source MAC, the frame is filtered (dropped) — no need to forward.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite Concept:** The bridge **learns from the source** but **acts on the destination** of a frame. This asymmetric observation/decision pattern is a common exam question.

## 2.3 Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) — IEEE 802.1D

In any bridged network with **redundant links** (for fault tolerance), loops can form, leading to the **broadcast storm** problem. The **Spanning Tree Protocol** solves this by logically disabling redundant paths while keeping them in standby.

### 2.3.1 The Five States of a Spanning Tree Port

1. **Disabled** — Administratively shut down.
2. **Blocking** — Receives BPDUs only; does not forward frames. (Prevents loops.)
3. **Listening** — Sends and receives BPDUs; prepares to forward.
4. **Learning** — Builds the MAC address table but still does not forward user frames.
5. **Forwarding** — Full operation; sends/receives user data frames.

### 2.3.2 Bridge Protocol Data Unit (BPDU) and Path Cost

A **BPDU** is the control frame exchanged between bridges to elect the **Root Bridge** and determine the lowest-cost path. The path cost is computed using IEEE-defined port cost values:

| Link Speed | IEEE 802.1D Cost (Short) | IEEE 802.1D Cost (Long) |
|---|---|---|
| 10 Mbps | 100 | 2,000,000 |
| 100 Mbps | 19 | 200,000 |
| 1 Gbps | 4 | 20,000 |
| 10 Gbps | 2 | 2,000 |

## 2.4 Virtual Local Area Networks (VLANs) — IEEE 802.1Q

A **VLAN** is a logical grouping of switch ports into separate broadcast domains, regardless of the physical location of the devices. A single physical switch can host multiple VLANs, each behaving like an independent LAN.

### 2.4.1 The 802.1Q Frame Tag

When a frame traverses a **trunk link** (carrying multiple VLANs), a **4-byte VLAN tag** is inserted into the Ethernet header:

$$\text{TPID} \; (16 \text{ bits}) \quad \vert \quad \text{PCP} \; (3 \text{ bits}) \quad \vert \quad \text{CFI} \; (1 \text{ bit}) \quad \vert \quad \text{VID} \; (12 \text{ bits})$$

Where:
- **TPID** = Tag Protocol Identifier (0x8100 for IEEE 802.1Q)
- **PCP** = Priority Code Point (used for QoS marking)
- **CFI** = Canonical Format Indicator
- **VID** = VLAN Identifier (range 1 to 4094; 0 and 4095 are reserved)

## 2.5 Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) — RFC 826

**ARP** maps a known **IP address (Layer 3)** to an unknown **MAC address (Layer 2)** on a local network. It operates directly at the boundary between Layer 2 and Layer 3 and is therefore a vital "helper protocol" for Data Link Layer switching.

### 2.5.1 ARP Message Types

- **ARP Request:** Broadcast frame (`FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`) sent by the requester: *"Who has IP 192.168.1.5? Tell 192.168.1.10."*
- **ARP Reply:** Unicast frame sent by the owner: *"192.168.1.5 is at MAC 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E."*

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| MAC address size | $48 \text{ bits}$ | Hex format `XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX` |
| Total possible MACs | $2^{48}$ | $\approx 2.8 \times 10^{14}$ unique addresses |
| Broadcast MAC | $\text{FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF}$ | Destination of all 1s |
| Total VLANs (802.1Q) | $2^{12} - 2 = 4094$ | VID 0 and 4095 reserved |
| Bridge table size | $N \text{ entries} \times 48 \text{ bits}$ | $N$ = number of learned MACs |
| STP Hello Time | $2 \text{ s}$ | BPDU interval |
| STP Max Age | $20 \text{ s}$ | Before declaring neighbour dead |
| STP Forward Delay | $15 \text{ s}$ | Listening $\rightarrow$ Learning $\rightarrow$ Forwarding |
| ARP Cache timeout | $15$ to $20 \text{ minutes}$ | OS-dependent |
| Frame transmission time | $T_{tx} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = frame length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) |
| End-to-end delay (store-forward) | $T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{R_i} + n \cdot t_{proc}$ | $n$ = number of switches traversed |
| Maximum VLAN tag overhead | $4 \text{ bytes} = 32 \text{ bits}$ | Inserted after Source MAC field |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why Layer 2 Switching is Used |
|---|---|
| Enterprise LAN design | Cheaper than routers; line-rate forwarding via ASICs |
| Data centre top-of-rack | 10/25/40/100 Gbps Layer 2 fabrics |
| VLAN segmentation | Separates departments, IoT, guest traffic cheaply |
| Spanning Tree | Provides sub-second failover in mesh topologies |
| ARP / Proxy ARP | Required for any IPv4 / Ethernet interoperability |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Building the Bridge Forwarding Table (Learning Algorithm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** A bridge has 4 ports (P1, P2, P3, P4). The following frames arrive in sequence. Build the bridge forwarding table step by step. The forwarding table is initially empty.

| # | Source MAC | Destination MAC | Incoming Port |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01 | BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:01 | P1 |
| 2 | BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:01 | AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01 | P2 |
| 3 | CC:CC:CC:CC:CC:01 | AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01 | P3 |
| 4 | BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:01 | DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:01 | P2 |
| 5 | DD:DD:DD:DD:DD:01 | AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01 | P4 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Process Frame 1:** Source = AA:AA, Dest = BB:BB, Port = P1
The bridge *learns* AA:AA on port P1. Since BB:BB is *unknown*, the frame is **flooded** out P2, P3, P4.

$$\text{Table after Frame 1:} \quad \{ \text{AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01} \rightarrow P1 \}$$

**Step 2 — Process Frame 2:** Source = BB:BB, Dest = AA:AA, Port = P2
The bridge *learns* BB:BB on port P2. AA:AA is *known* on P1, so the frame is **forwarded out P1** (not flooded).

$$\text{Table after Frame 2:} \quad \{ \text{AA:AA:...} \rightarrow P1,\;\; \text{BB:BB:...} \rightarrow P2 \}$$

**Step 3 — Process Frame 3:** Source = CC:CC, Dest = AA:AA, Port = P3
The bridge *learns* CC:CC on port P3. AA:AA is *known* on P1, so the frame is **forwarded out P1** only.

$$\text{Table after Frame 3:} \quad \{ \text{AA:AA:...} \rightarrow P1,\;\; \text{BB:BB:...} \rightarrow P2,\;\; \text{CC:CC:...} \rightarrow P3 \}$$

**Step 4 — Process Frame 4:** Source = BB:BB, Dest = DD:DD, Port = P2
The bridge *refreshes* the BB:BB entry (still on P2). DD:DD is *unknown*, so the frame is **flooded** out P1, P3, P4.

$$\text{Table after Frame 4:} \quad \{ \text{AA:AA} \rightarrow P1,\; \text{BB:BB} \rightarrow P2,\; \text{CC:CC} \rightarrow P3,\; \text{DD:DD} \rightarrow P4 \text{ (just learned)} \}$$

Wait — the source BB:BB is on P2 (incoming), so the destination table update for DD:DD only happens *if DD:DD were the source*. Since DD:DD is the *destination* and is unknown, the frame is **flooded**, and DD:DD has not been learned yet.

$$\text{Table after Frame 4:} \quad \{ \text{AA:AA} \rightarrow P1,\; \text{BB:BB} \rightarrow P2,\; \text{CC:CC} \rightarrow P3 \}$$

**Step 5 — Process Frame 5:** Source = DD:DD, Dest = AA:AA, Port = P4
The bridge *learns* DD:DD on port P4. AA:AA is *known* on P1, so the frame is **forwarded out P1**.

$$\boxed{\text{Final Table:} \quad \{ \text{AA:AA} \rightarrow P1,\; \text{BB:BB} \rightarrow P2,\; \text{CC:CC} \rightarrow P3,\; \text{DD:DD} \rightarrow P4 \}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Spanning Tree — Root Bridge and Designated Port Election

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** Three switches (SW1, SW2, SW3) are connected in a triangle. Bridge IDs (Priority + MAC) are: SW1 = 32768.00:00:00:00:00:01, SW2 = 32768.00:00:00:00:00:02, SW3 = 24576.00:00:00:00:00:03. All link costs are equal (cost = 4). Determine the **Root Bridge**, the **Root Ports**, and the **Designated Ports**.

### Step 1 — Elect the Root Bridge

The Root Bridge is the switch with the **lowest Bridge ID**. Bridge ID = Priority (16 bits) + MAC (48 bits). Since SW3 has priority 24576 (lower than 32768), even with a higher MAC, the lower priority wins.

$$\text{Root Bridge} = SW3 \quad (\text{Priority } 24576 < 32768)$$

### Step 2 — Determine Root Ports for Non-Root Bridges

For each non-root bridge, the Root Port is the port with the **lowest cumulative cost** to the root. Since all direct links have cost 4, both SW1 and SW2 connect directly to SW3 with cost 4.

$$\text{Root Port of SW1} = \text{port to SW3 (cost = 4)}$$

$$\text{Root Port of SW2} = \text{port to SW3 (cost = 4)}$$

### Step 3 — Determine Designated Ports

On each segment (link), the Designated Port is the port of the bridge with the **lowest cost to root**, breaking ties by lowest Bridge ID.

| Segment | Connected Bridges | Winner (Designated Port) | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Link SW1 ↔ SW3 | SW1, SW3 | SW3 | SW3 is the root, so all its ports are Designated |
| Link SW2 ↔ SW3 | SW2, SW3 | SW3 | SW3 is the root, so all its ports are Designated |
| Link SW1 ↔ SW2 | SW1, SW2 | SW1 (lower MAC 00:..:01) | Same cost to root (both 8 via SW3); SW1 has lower Bridge ID |

### Step 4 — Block Redundant Port

The only non-designated, non-root port is SW2's port toward SW1. This port enters the **Blocking** state.

$$\boxed{\text{Root} = SW3, \quad \text{Blocking Port} = SW2 \rightarrow SW1 \text{ link}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: ARP Cache Resolution Delay

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** Host A (IP 10.0.0.1) wants to send an IP packet to Host B (IP 10.0.0.2) on the same LAN. Host A has an empty ARP cache. The link speed is 100 Mbps. Compute the total delay for ARP resolution before the IP packet can be transmitted. ARP Request size = 42 bytes, ARP Reply size = 42 bytes, propagation delay per link = 5 µs. Assume negligible processing delay.

### Step 1 — Frame Transmission Time

$$T_{tx} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{42 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{100 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps}}$$

$$T_{tx} = \frac{336}{10^{8}} = 3.36 \; \mu s$$

### Step 2 — Propagation Delay (one-way)

$$T_{prop} = 5 \; \mu s$$

### Step 3 — Total Delay for ARP Request (A → B)

$$T_{req} = T_{tx} + T_{prop} = 3.36 + 5 = 8.36 \; \mu s$$

### Step 4 — Total Delay for ARP Reply (B → A)

$$T_{rep} = T_{tx} + T_{prop} = 3.36 + 5 = 8.36 \; \mu s$$

### Step 5 — Total ARP Resolution Time

$$T_{ARP} = T_{req} + T_{rep}$$

$$T_{ARP} = 8.36 + 8.36 = 16.72 \; \mu s$$

$$\boxed{T_{ARP} \approx 16.72 \; \mu s \text{ (round-trip ARP exchange before IP forwarding)}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Switching Methods Comparison

| Parameter | Store-and-Forward | Cut-Through | Fragment-Free |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reads full frame? | Yes (entire frame) | No (only first 6 bytes = dest MAC) | First 64 bytes only |
| Checksum verified? | Yes | No | Partial (collision check) |
| Latency | Highest | Lowest | Medium |
| Discards bad frames? | Yes (before forwarding) | No (forwards corrupted frames) | Yes (collision fragments < 64B dropped) |
| Best for | Modern networks, error-prone links | High-speed, low-error backbones | Mixed environments |
| Buffer required? | Yes (full frame) | No (just header) | Partial |

### Latency Comparison Formula

For a switch with internal processing time $t_{proc}$ and frame length $L$ bits at link rate $R$:

$$T_{store\text{-}forward} = \frac{L}{R_{in}} + t_{proc} + \frac{L}{R_{out}}$$

$$T_{cut\text{-}through} = \frac{6 \text{ bytes header}}{R_{in}} + t_{proc} + \frac{L}{R_{out}}$$

$$T_{cut\text{-}through} \ll T_{store\text{-}forward} \quad \text{when } L \text{ is large}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Layer 2 Switch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INP["Incoming Port Array"]
        P1[Port 1]
        P2[Port 2]
        P3[Port 3]
        P4[Port 4]
    end
    subgraph CORE["Switch Core Engine"]
        MACIN[MAC Address Learner]
        FWDT[(Forwarding Table CAM)]
        DEC[Forwarding Decision Unit]
        FLOOD[Flooding Logic]
    end
    subgraph OUTP["Outgoing Port Array"]
        OP1[Out to Segment 1]
        OP2[Out to Segment 2]
        OP3[Out to Segment 3]
        OP4[Out to Segment 4]
    end
    P1 --> MACIN
    P2 --> MACIN
    P3 --> MACIN
    P4 --> MACIN
    MACIN --> FWDT
    FWDT --> DEC
    DEC -->|Known Dest| OP1
    DEC -->|Known Dest| OP2
    DEC -->|Unknown Dest| FLOOD
    FLOOD --> OP1
    FLOOD --> OP2
    FLOOD --> OP3
    FLOOD --> OP4
```

## 4.2 Transparent Bridge Learning and Forwarding Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Frame Arrives on Port Pi] --> B{Source MAC in Table?}
    B -->|No| C[Add Source MAC to Table via Port Pi]
    B -->|Yes| D[Refresh Timestamp for Source MAC]
    C --> E{Destination MAC in Table?}
    D --> E
    E -->|Yes, on same port Pi| F[FILTER: Drop Frame]
    E -->|Yes, on different port Pj| G[FORWARD: Send out Pj only]
    E -->|No, unknown| H[FLOOD: Send out all ports except Pi]
    F --> X[End]
    G --> X
    H --> X
```

## 4.3 Spanning Tree Protocol State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Disabled
    Disabled --> Blocking : Port Enabled
    Blocking --> Listening : Selected as Root or Designated
    Listening --> Learning : Forward Delay 15 s elapsed
    Listening --> Blocking : Lost election
    Learning --> Forwarding : Forward Delay 15 s elapsed
    Learning --> Blocking : Lost election
    Forwarding --> Blocking : Topology change detected
    Forwarding --> [*] : Port Disabled
```

## 4.4 VLAN Segmentation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PHY["Physical Switch with 8 Ports"]
        direction LR
        P1[Port 1 - VLAN 10]
        P2[Port 2 - VLAN 10]
        P3[Port 3 - VLAN 20]
        P4[Port 4 - VLAN 20]
        P5[Port 5 - VLAN 30]
        P6[Port 6 - VLAN 30]
        P7[Port 7 - Trunk]
        P8[Port 8 - Trunk]
    end
    subgraph BD1["Broadcast Domain 1 - VLAN 10"]
        H1[Host A]
        H2[Host B]
    end
    subgraph BD2["Broadcast Domain 2 - VLAN 20"]
        H3[Host C]
        H4[Host D]
    end
    subgraph BD3["Broadcast Domain 3 - VLAN 30"]
        H5[Host E]
        H6[Host F]
    end
    P1 --- H1
    P2 --- H2
    P3 --- H3
    P4 --- H4
    P5 --- H5
    P6 --- H6
    P7 --> T1[Trunk to Switch B]
    P8 --> T2[Trunk to Router]
```

## 4.5 ARP Request and Reply Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Host A (10.0.0.1)
    participant BC as Broadcast Domain
    participant B as Host B (10.0.0.2)
    A->>A: Check ARP cache for 10.0.0.2
    Note over A: Cache miss
    A->>BC: ARP Request (Broadcast FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF)
    Note over BC: Who has 10.0.0.2? Tell 10.0.0.1
    BC->>B: Deliver broadcast to all hosts
    B->>B: Match found: this is my IP
    B->>A: ARP Reply (Unicast to AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01)
    Note over A: Cache updated, IP packet now forwarded
```

## 4.6 Store-and-Forward vs Cut-Through Switch Internal Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SF["Store-and-Forward Switch"]
        A1[Frame In] --> B1[Buffer Full Frame]
        B1 --> C1[FCS Check / CRC Verify]
        C1 -->|Valid| D1[Lookup Forwarding Table]
        C1 -->|Invalid| X1[Drop Frame]
        D1 --> E1[Forward to Egress Port]
    end
    subgraph CT["Cut-Through Switch"]
        A2[Frame In] --> B2[Read First 6 Bytes Header]
        B2 --> C2[Lookup Forwarding Table]
        C2 --> D2[Begin Forwarding Immediately]
        D2 --> E2[Transmit Rest of Frame While Receiving]
    end
```
