# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a transparent bridge. How does it learn the MAC addresses of connected devices?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **transparent bridge** is a Layer 2 device that connects two or more LAN segments and forwards frames between them based on MAC addresses. It is called "transparent" because the connected hosts require no configuration changes and remain unaware of its presence.

**Learning process (2 marks):** The bridge maintains a forwarding table initially empty. For every incoming frame, it examines the **source MAC address** and the **incoming port**, then records this mapping in the table. If the source MAC already exists, the timestamp is refreshed. Over time, the bridge builds a complete picture of which MAC is reachable via which port. Entries that are not refreshed within the **aging time (300 seconds)** are purged.

**Forwarding logic (1 mark):** If the destination MAC is in the table, the frame is forwarded only to the associated port; if the destination is the same port as the source, the frame is filtered; if unknown, the frame is flooded to all ports except the incoming one.

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is the purpose of the Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)? Name the five port states defined in IEEE 802.1D.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Purpose of STP (1.5 marks):** The Spanning Tree Protocol, defined in **IEEE 802.1D**, prevents **bridging loops** and **broadcast storms** in networks with redundant Layer 2 paths. It logically disables redundant links by computing a loop-free tree topology, while keeping them as backups in case the active path fails.

**Five Port States (1.5 marks):**
1. **Disabled** — Administratively shut down.
2. **Blocking** — Receives BPDUs only; no frame forwarding.
3. **Listening** — Sends and receives BPDUs; prepares for transition.
4. **Learning** — Builds the MAC address table; still does not forward user frames.
5. **Forwarding** — Sends and receives both BPDUs and user data frames.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Question A(a) — 7 Marks:**
**With a neat diagram, explain the architecture and operation of a transparent bridge. How does it handle the case of an unknown destination address?**

**Model Solution:**

A **transparent bridge** operates at the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI model and connects two or more LAN segments. Its key architectural components are:

1. **A set of ports** (typically 2 or more) connected to LAN segments.
2. **A forwarding / MAC address table** stored in CAM (Content Addressable Memory).
3. **A frame processor** that performs learning, filtering, forwarding, and flooding.

**Operational steps (5 marks):**

1. **Frame Reception:** A frame arrives on port P1.
2. **Learning:** The bridge reads the **source MAC address** of the frame. If not in the table, it adds a new entry: `{ Source_MAC → P1 }`. If present, the aging timer is reset.
3. **Decision on Destination:** The bridge reads the **destination MAC address**:
   - If destination MAC is **on the same port P1**, the frame is **filtered** (dropped) — no need to forward.
   - If destination MAC is **on a different port Pn**, the frame is **forwarded** out of Pn only.
   - If destination MAC is **unknown**, the bridge **floods** the frame out of *all ports except P1*.

**Handling of Unknown Destination (2 marks):**
When a host on a new segment sends its first frame, the bridge has not yet learned its MAC. The bridge **floods** the frame to all other segments. When the destination host replies, the bridge learns the destination's MAC from the *source* field of the reply, and the MAC is added to the table. From this point onward, subsequent frames to that MAC are forwarded selectively — no flooding required.

**Diagram Reference:** Use the block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

**Question A(b) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the Spanning Tree Protocol in detail. How is the Root Bridge elected? What happens to redundant links in a switched network with three interconnected switches?**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition and Purpose (1.5 marks):**
The **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP)**, defined in **IEEE 802.1D**, is a Layer 2 loop-prevention mechanism. It ensures a loop-free logical topology in bridged networks that contain redundant physical links for fault tolerance. STP operates by exchanging **Bridge Protocol Data Units (BPDUs)** between switches.

**Root Bridge Election (2.5 marks):**
1. Initially, every switch assumes itself to be the root and announces its **Bridge ID** in BPDUs. The Bridge ID is an 8-byte value: 2-byte **Priority** (default 32768, configurable in steps of 4096) + 6-byte **MAC address**.
2. Switches compare incoming BPDUs. The switch with the **lowest Bridge ID** becomes the **Root Bridge**.
3. The **lowest priority wins**; ties are broken by the **lowest MAC address**.

**Path Cost and Designated Ports (1.5 marks):**
- Each port has an administrative cost (e.g., 100 for 10 Mbps, 19 for 100 Mbps, 4 for 1 Gbps).
- For each non-root switch, the port with the lowest cumulative cost to the root becomes the **Root Port**.
- On each segment, the port of the switch with the lowest cost to the root becomes the **Designated Port**. All other ports become **Non-Designated** and enter the **Blocking** state.

**Behavior on Redundant Links with 3 Switches (1.5 marks):**
Suppose Switches A, B, C form a triangle. After STP convergence:
- The lowest-ID switch (say A) becomes the Root.
- A's ports on both links (A-B and A-C) are **Designated Forwarding** ports.
- B and C each elect a **Root Port** toward A.
- On the B-C link, one side (say B) becomes the **Designated Port** while the other (C's port toward B) becomes **Non-Designated** and enters **Blocking**.
- The blocked port still listens for BPDUs; if A fails, B and C will detect the change and re-converge, unblocking the redundant link.

**Total: 7 marks awarded with proper explanation of BPDU exchange, port roles, and re-convergence behavior.**

---

### Question Choice B: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 (Alternative Set)]

**Question B(a) — 7 Marks:**
**What is a VLAN? Explain the IEEE 802.1Q tagging mechanism with the frame format. Why are VLANs necessary in modern enterprise networks?**

**Model Solution:**

**VLAN Definition (1 mark):**
A **Virtual Local Area Network (VLAN)** is a logical grouping of switch ports into a single broadcast domain, independent of physical location. Devices in the same VLAN communicate as if on the same physical LAN, even if they are on different floors or buildings.

**IEEE 802.1Q Tagging Mechanism (3 marks):**
When a frame must cross a **trunk link** (a link carrying multiple VLANs), a **4-byte (32-bit) VLAN tag** is inserted between the **Source MAC** and the **EtherType/Length** fields of the standard Ethernet frame.

The 4-byte tag consists of:
- **TPID (Tag Protocol Identifier):** 16 bits, set to **0x8100** to indicate an 802.1Q tagged frame.
- **PCP (Priority Code Point):** 3 bits, used for QoS marking (8 priority levels, 0–7).
- **DEI (Drop Eligible Indicator):** 1 bit, formerly CFI; indicates frames eligible for dropping under congestion.
- **VID (VLAN Identifier):** 12 bits, identifying the VLAN. Valid range: **1 to 4094**. **VID 0** = priority-only tag; **VID 4095** = reserved.

**Frame Size Implications:** Maximum Ethernet frame grows from 1518 to **1522 bytes** with the 4-byte tag.

**Necessity of VLANs (3 marks):**
1. **Broadcast Control:** Limits broadcast domains, reducing unnecessary traffic.
2. **Security:** Isolates sensitive departments (e.g., Finance, HR) on a separate VLAN.
3. **Cost Efficiency:** Eliminates need for separate physical switches per department.
4. **Flexibility:** Users can be moved logically without re-cabling.
5. **QoS Prioritization:** PCP bits allow traffic prioritization per VLAN.

---

**Question B(b) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP). With a sequence diagram, describe how a host resolves an IPv4 address to a MAC address on a local network. What is ARP spoofing?**

**Model Solution:**

**ARP Definition (1 mark):**
The **Address Resolution Protocol (ARP)**, defined in **RFC 826**, maps a **32-bit IPv4 address (Layer 3)** to a **48-bit MAC address (Layer 2)** on a local broadcast network (e.g., Ethernet).

**ARP Operation Steps (3 marks):**
1. Host A wants to send an IP packet to Host B (10.0.0.2). Host A checks its **ARP cache**.
2. If the cache has no entry for 10.0.0.2, Host A constructs an **ARP Request** frame containing:
   - Sender's IP and MAC.
   - Target's IP (10.0.0.2); Target's MAC field is left as `00:00:00:00:00:00` (unknown).
3. The ARP Request is encapsulated in an Ethernet frame with destination MAC = **FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF** (broadcast).
4. Every host on the LAN receives the broadcast; only the host with IP 10.0.0.2 (Host B) responds.
5. Host B sends an **ARP Reply** (unicast) containing its MAC address.
6. Host A receives the reply, updates its ARP cache, and proceeds to send the IP packet encapsulated in an Ethernet frame with the correct destination MAC.

**Sequence Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the diagram in Section 4.5 of these notes — show ARP Request as broadcast, ARP Reply as unicast.

**ARP Spoofing (1 mark):**
**ARP spoofing (or ARP poisoning)** is a **Layer 2 man-in-the-middle attack** where a malicious host sends **gratuitous ARP replies** associating its own MAC address with the IP of another host (e.g., the default gateway). This causes victim hosts to send traffic to the attacker instead of the real gateway. Defenses include **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)**, **static ARP entries**, and **port security** on managed switches.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Learning vs Forwarding:** Students often incorrectly say "bridge learns destination MAC." It learns from the *source* and acts on the *destination* — examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **STP Priority Default:** Default bridge priority is **32768**, not 0. Writing 0 is a common error.
> 3. **VID Range:** Valid VLAN IDs are **1 to 4094**; 0 and 4095 are reserved — partial marking lost if you write 4096.
> 4. **ARP is Layer 2/3 boundary protocol:** Do not say "ARP is a Layer 3 protocol" — it is fundamentally a **Layer 2** protocol in the KTU context, used to support Layer 3.
> 5. **Cut-Through Latency:** The latency formula uses the **first 6 bytes** (destination MAC), not the entire header (14 bytes).
> 6. **Broadcast MAC:** All 1s in binary is `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` — writing all 0s is wrong (that is the "null" or "unspecified" address).
> 7. **Spanning Tree Port States Order:** Order matters — Disabled → Blocking → Listening → Learning → Forwarding. Reversing any two costs 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Link Layer Switching**

- **Layer 2 device hierarchy:** Hub (Layer 1, no isolation) → Bridge (Layer 2, learns) → Switch (multi-port bridge with ASICs) → Layer 3 Switch (adds routing).
- **MAC address:** **48 bits**, 12 hex digits, unique per NIC. Broadcast MAC = `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`.
- **Transparent bridge operations:** **Learning** (from source), **Filtering** (same port), **Forwarding** (known different port), **Flooding** (unknown dest).
- **Bridge table aging time:** **300 seconds** (5 minutes); entries purged if not refreshed.
- **Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D):** Prevents loops in redundant topologies using BPDUs.
- **Root Bridge election:** Lowest **Bridge ID** (Priority 32768 default + MAC); lowest priority first, then lowest MAC.
- **Five STP port states:** Disabled → Blocking → Listening → Learning → Forwarding.
- **STP timers:** Hello = 2 s, Max Age = 20 s, Forward Delay = 15 s.
- **VLANs (IEEE 802.1Q):** Logical broadcast domains; **12-bit VID** = 4094 usable VLANs; **4-byte tag** with TPID `0x8100`, PCP (3), DEI (1), VID (12).
- **ARP (RFC 826):** Maps IPv4 → MAC using broadcast request and unicast reply; **gratuitous ARP** = unsolicited announcement.
- **Switching modes:** **Store-and-Forward** (full CRC check, highest latency), **Cut-Through** (reads only first 6 bytes, lowest latency, no error check), **Fragment-Free** (first 64 bytes, hybrid).
- **Broadcast domain:** Defined by VLANs / router boundaries. A single Layer 2 switch with all ports in one VLAN = one broadcast domain.
- **Collision domain:** Each switch port is its own collision domain (full-duplex = no collisions).
- **ARP Spoofing defense:** Use **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)**, **port security**, and **static ARP entries** on critical infrastructure.
- **Frame size with 802.1Q tag:** Standard Ethernet max = 1518 bytes; with 802.1Q tag = **1522 bytes** (called "baby giant" frames).
- **Common exam keywords to use:** "transparent," "forwarding table," "CAM," "loop-free topology," "broadcast storm," "trunk link," "access port."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Transport Layer (OSI Layer-4)**, **Design Issues** refer to the fundamental architectural and protocol-level problems that must be resolved before a reliable, process-to-process communication service can be built on top of the best-effort, host-to-host **Network Layer**. According to the KTU 2024 scheme (aligned with Forouzan/Crowcroft reference treatment), the design issues are the set of decisions a transport protocol must make regarding **addressing, connection management, reliability, flow regulation, multiplexing, and recovery**.

The Transport Layer is uniquely positioned because it provides **end-to-end services** while relying on the underlying network which offers only **host-to-host packet delivery**. Bridging this gap is the central design challenge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> *"Design issues of the Transport Layer — addressing, connection establishment & termination, flow control & buffering, multiplexing, crash recovery, and the transport service primitives (Berkeley sockets)."*

## 1.2 The Core List of Design Issues

A canonical transport protocol must address **seven (7)** design issues:

1. **Transport Service Primitives** — The set of operations (verbs) a user process can invoke.
2. **Addressing (TSAP / NSAP / Ports)** — Identifying the correct end application process.
3. **Connection Establishment** — A 3-way handshake to synchronize sequence numbers.
4. **Connection Termination** — Graceful (and abrupt) release of the association.
5. **Flow Control & Buffering** — Preventing a fast sender from drowning a slow receiver.
6. **Multiplexing & Demultiplexing** — Sharing one network address among many sockets.
7. **Crash Recovery** — Restoring state after a host or link failure.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Postal Service"

Imagine the **Internet** is the global postal system:

| Internet Component | Postal Analogy | Design Issue It Raises |
|---|---|---|
| IP Packet | A sealed letter in a sorting bin | Host-to-host delivery only |
| IP Address | Building street address | Building-to-building only |
| **Port Number** | **Apartment / flat number** | **Addressing** |
| TCP Connection | Registered Speed-Post account | **Connection Establishment** |
| ACK | Delivery receipt returned | **Error & Flow Control** |
| 3-Way Handshake | Caller dials, recipient says "Hello", caller says "Hi" | **Synchronization** |
| Crash | Postal van catches fire | **Crash Recovery** |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Picture:** The network is like a courier that promises *"we'll try our best to deliver your letter."* The transport layer's design issues are the rules we invent to turn this fragile promise into a *reliable* conversation between two specific people sitting in two specific rooms.

## 1.4 Why the Transport Layer Exists (and why it is so complex)

The Network Layer (IP) provides a **connectionless, unreliable, best-effort datagram service**. It does not guarantee:
- In-order delivery
- Duplicate suppression
- Loss recovery
- Process-level addressing

All of the above **must** be engineered inside the transport layer — these *are* the design issues.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If a question asks *"Why do we need a transport layer if we already have an IP layer?"* — your one-line answer should be:
> *"Because IP is best-effort and host-to-host; the transport layer upgrades this to reliable, process-to-process, end-to-end communication through specific design decisions (its design issues)."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Design Issues — Exhaustive Breakdown

### Issue 1: Transport Service Primitives
Primitives are the **verbs** an application uses to talk to the transport layer. The reference model uses **five (5) primitives**:

| Primitive | Meaning | Type |
|---|---|---|
| `LISTEN` | Block until a peer tries to connect | Server |
| `CONNECT` | Actively attempt to establish a connection | Client |
| `SEND` | Transmit data over an established connection | Both |
| `RECEIVE` | Block until data arrives | Both |
| `DISCONNECT` | Terminate the connection gracefully | Both |

**Two Flavors of Service Primitives:**

- **OSI Reference Model** — uses 5 primitives (T-CONNECT, T-DATA, T-DISCONNECT, etc.) with **4-phase** confirmations.
- **Berkeley Sockets (de facto standard, used by TCP/UDP)** — uses `socket()`, `bind()`, `listen()`, `accept()`, `connect()`, `send()`, `recv()`, `close()`.

> [!NOTE]
> Berkeley Sockets are what the KTU 2024 module 3 most heavily emphasizes because they are what **real Internet transport protocols** (TCP/UDP) use.

### Issue 2: Addressing — TSAP, NSAP, and Ports
- **NSAP (Network Service Access Point)** — IP address of the host.
- **TSAP (Transport Service Access Point)** — Port number identifying the specific process.
- A complete transport address is the **pair (NSAP, TSAP)** = (IP, Port).
- **Port Number Ranges (IANA registered, 16-bit):**

  | Range | Binary Range | Decimal | Purpose |
  |---|---|---|---|
  | Well-Known | `00000000 00000000` – `00000000 11111111` | **0 – 1023** | Servers (HTTP=80, FTP=21, SSH=22) |
  | Registered | `00000001 00000000` – `00111111 11111111` | **1024 – 49151** | User processes, IANA-registered |
  | Dynamic/Private | `01000000 00000000` – `11111111 11111111` | **49152 – 65535** | Ephemeral client ports |

### Issue 3: Connection Establishment — The 3-Way Handshake
A naive two-way handshake fails because of **delayed duplicates** (the "Phantom Packet Problem"). Solution: use **initial sequence numbers (ISN)** synchronized in **3 messages**.

**Procedure:**
1. **SYN** — Client chooses ISN = `x` and sends a segment.
2. **SYN+ACK** — Server acknowledges `x+1`, picks its own ISN = `y`.
3. **ACK** — Client acknowledges `y+1`. Connection is now **ESTABLISHED**.

### Issue 4: Connection Termination
Two kinds of release:
- **Asymmetric (Abrupt)** — One side closes; TCP actually uses this with `FIN` but pairing it with the *4-way FIN-ACK-ACK handshake* gives a graceful release.
- **Symmetric (Graceful, 3-way army problem)** — Each side independently closes its half when it has no more data; preferred for some protocols.

TCP in practice uses a **4-step graceful termination** (FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK) — the half-close property.

### Issue 5: Flow Control & Buffering
**Flow control** ensures a fast sender does not overrun a slow receiver.

- **Buffering Strategies:**
  * **No Buffering** — Sender blocks; wasteful.
  * **Fixed Buffer per Connection** — Wastes memory; supports max throughput.
  * **Variable-length Buffer per Connection** — Reasonable.
  * **Circular Buffer per Connection (most common)** — Efficient ring; TCP uses this concept.

- **Mechanism in TCP:** Sliding Window protocol using a **receive window (rwnd)** advertised in every ACK.

### Issue 6: Multiplexing & Demiplexing
- **Upward Multiplexing (Many-to-One)** — Multiple transport connections share **one** network connection (saves NSAPs).
- **Downward Multiplexing (One-to-Many)** — One transport connection uses **multiple** network paths (e.g., for throughput or redundancy).

### Issue 7: Crash Recovery
A server may crash and lose memory. Two strategies exist:

| Strategy | Idea | Vulnerability |
|---|---|---|
| **Restart all connections** | Reboot and tell everyone "start over" | May lose committed work |
| **Use TPDUs only after acknowledgment** | Sender keeps segment until ACKed; if crash, peer re-requests | Indefinite loops possible |

The **"Phantom Packets"** problem of the 1970s is what motivated the entire design issue of *connection establishment* with ISNs.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning / Value | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| $N_p$ | Total number of distinct port numbers = $2^{16}$ = **65,536** | Addressing |
| $R_{\text{well}}$ | Well-known port range = $0$ to $2^{10}-1$ = 0 to 1023 | Application layer mapping |
| $R_{\text{reg}}$ | Registered range = $2^{10}$ to $2^{15}+2^{14}-1$ | User apps |
| $R_{\text{dyn}}$ | Dynamic range = $2^{15}+2^{14}$ to $2^{16}-1$ | Ephemeral |
| ISN $x$ | Sender's initial sequence number | 3-way handshake |
| ISN $y$ | Receiver's initial sequence number | 3-way handshake |
| rwnd | Receiver's advertised window (bytes) | Flow control |
| $\text{ISN}_{\text{space}}$ | Full 32-bit sequence space (TCP) = $2^{32}$ = **4,294,967,296** | Crash recovery |
| $T_{\text{conn}} = 3 \cdot RTT$ | Theoretical handshake time | Connection setup |
| $\eta_{\text{multiplex}}$ | Multiplexing efficiency = $\frac{\sum \text{throughputs of shared connections}}{\text{NSAP capacity}}$ | Throughput |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do not confuse:**
> - **NSAP vs TSAP** → NSAP = host (IP), TSAP = process (Port).
> - **Berkeley Sockets vs OSI Primitives** → Sockets are de-facto (TCP/UDP); OSI is the textbook reference.
> - **Multiplexing UP vs DOWN** → "Up" = many-to-one (save IP addresses); "Down" = one-to-many (use multiple paths).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Number of Concurrent Transport Connections

A single client process can open many simultaneous connections. How many *theoretically*?

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{conn, max}} &= 2^{16} \times 2^{16} \\[4pt]
                     &= 2^{32} \\[4pt]
                     &= 4{,}294{,}967{,}296
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The client uses an **ephemeral port** (16 bits) and chooses a **server port** (16 bits). The 2 ports together uniquely identify one connection (a 4-tuple in TCP).

**Row-by-row explanation:**
- Line 1: Client ephemeral port choices × server well-known/registered port choices.
- Line 2: Sum of exponents: $16+16 = 32$.
- Line 3: $2^{32}$ evaluated to a decimal integer.

$$
N_{\text{conn, max}} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \approx 4.29 \times 10^{9} \text{ simultaneous sockets}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: 3-Way Handshake Time Cost

For a network with one-way propagation delay $T_p$ and transmission time $T_t$ (negligible for control segments), Round-Trip Time is:

$$
RTT = 2 \cdot T_p
$$

The 3-way handshake therefore consumes:

$$
T_{\text{handshake}} = 1.5 \cdot RTT = 3 \cdot T_p
$$

*Logic:* SYN travels one $T_p$, SYN+ACK returns one $T_p$, ACK travels one $T_p$.

**Why 3 messages, not 2?**
In a 2-message handshake, a *delayed duplicate* of an old SYN could open a stale connection. The 3rd ACK confirms that the peer **received the freshly chosen ISN**, which a duplicate cannot.

## 3.3 Derivation: TCP Sequence Number Space and Wrap-Around Protection

TCP uses a **32-bit sequence number** that increments by the number of transmitted bytes (not by 1 per segment).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ISN}_{\text{space, max}} &= 2^{32} \text{ unique sequence numbers} \\[4pt]
                               &= 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ values} \\[4pt]
                               &= 4.294 \times 10^{9} \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(before wrap)}
\end{aligned}
$$

At 1 Gbps, wrap-around occurs in:

$$
T_{\text{wrap}} = \frac{2^{32} \times 8 \text{ bits}}{10^{9} \text{ bits/sec}} = \frac{34.36 \times 10^{9}}{10^{9}} \approx 34.36 \text{ seconds}
$$

*Logic:* Divide total bit-capacity by bit-rate. At 1 Gbps the sequence number space re-cycles in ~34 seconds — which is why **PAWS (Protection Against Wrapped Sequence numbers)** and **Timestamps** are part of modern TCP design.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Design Issues to Failure Modes

| # | Design Issue | If Not Solved | Failure Manifestation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | No primitives | Apps cannot invoke transport | Layer-4 unusable |
| 2 | No TSAP / port | Cannot distinguish processes | "Delivery to wrong flat" |
| 3 | No 3-way handshake | Duplicate SYNs cause ghost connections | Phantom-packet attacks |
| 4 | No graceful termination | Half-open sockets; data loss | Resource leaks |
| 5 | No flow control | Receiver buffer overflows | Packet loss, retransmits |
| 6 | No multiplexing | One app per IP | IPv4 address exhaustion (worse) |
| 7 | No crash recovery | Lost TPDUs after reboot | Data inconsistency |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical: Predicting a Client's Ephemeral Port

> A web browser on host `10.0.0.5` opens 5 simultaneous TCP connections to `93.184.216.34:80` (example.com). What is the size of the connection identifier space needed?

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Per connection tuple} &= (\text{srcIP}, \text{srcPort}, \text{dstIP}, \text{dstPort}) \\[2pt]
\text{Unique identifier}  &= 2^{16} \text{ (src ports)} \times 1 \text{ (fixed dst port 80)} \\[2pt]
                            &= 65{,}536 \text{ unique connections possible per srcIP to that server}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **5** connections, the OS picks 5 distinct ephemeral ports from the dynamic range $49152$ to $65535$. Probability of collision when picking at random in a 16,384-port space:

$$
P_{\text{collision, 5 picks}} \approx 1 - e^{-\binom{5}{2}/16384} \approx 1 - e^{-10/16384} \approx 6.1 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Negligible — the OS picks sequentially, not randomly, so collision is in practice zero.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchy of Transport Layer Design Issues

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Transport Layer Design Issues] --> B[1 Service Primitives]
    A --> C[2 Addressing]
    A --> D[3 Connection Establishment]
    A --> E[4 Connection Termination]
    A --> F[5 Flow Control and Buffering]
    A --> G[6 Multiplexing and Demultiplexing]
    A --> H[7 Crash Recovery]

    B --> B1[OSI Primitives]
    B --> B2[Berkeley Sockets]

    C --> C1[NSAP equals IP Address]
    C --> C2[TSAP equals Port Number]
    C --> C3[Transport Address equals IP plus Port]

    D --> D1[3 Way Handshake]
    D --> D2[SYN with ISN x]
    D --> D3[SYN ACK with ISN y]
    D --> D4[Final ACK confirms y plus 1]

    E --> E1[Graceful 4 way FIN]
    E --> E2[Abrupt Reset]

    F --> F1[Fixed Buffer]
    F --> F2[Variable Buffer]
    F --> F3[Circular Ring Buffer]
    F --> F4[Sliding Window rwnd]

    G --> G1[Upward many to one]
    G --> G2[Downward one to many]

    H --> H1[Restart all]
    H --> H2[Ack before commit]
    H --> H3[PAWS Timestamps]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Berkeley Socket Lifecycle (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Server Process] --> stepB[socket call creates endpoint]
    stepB --> stepC[bind assigns TSAP port 80]
    stepC --> stepD[listen puts socket in passive mode]
    stepD --> stepE[accept blocks waiting for client]

    stepF[Client Process] --> stepG[socket call]
    stepG --> stepH[connect active open to server port 80]
    stepH --> stepI[3 Way Handshake]
    stepI --> stepJ[ESTABLISHED state]

    stepJ --> stepK[send and recv full duplex]
    stepK --> stepL[close issues FIN]
    stepL --> stepM[4 Way Handshake Terminates]

    stepE -.completes only when.-> stepI
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — 3-Way Handshake Sequence with ISNs

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server

    Note over C: Chooses ISN x
    C->>S: SYN seq equals x
    Note over S: Chooses ISN y, acks x plus 1
    S->>C: SYN ACK seq equals y ack equals x plus 1
    Note over C: Acks y plus 1
    C->>S: ACK seq equals x plus 1 ack equals y plus 1
    Note over C,S: Connection ESTABLISHED
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Upward vs Downward Multiplexing

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Upward[Upward Multiplexing - many transport conns share one NSAP]
        t1A[TPDU app1] --> n1A[Single NSAP IP]
        t2A[TPDU app2] --> n1A
        t3A[TPDU app3] --> n1A
    end

    subgraph Downward[Downward Multiplexing - one transport conn spans many NSAPs]
        t1B[Single TPDU stream] --> n1B[NSAP path A]
        t1B --> n2B[NSAP path B]
        t1B --> n3B[NSAP path C]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Q:** List any **three** transport service primitives as used in the **Berkeley Sockets** API and state their purpose.

**Model Answer (3 points):**
1. **`socket()`** — Creates a new communication endpoint and returns a file-descriptor-like handle. *(1 mark)*
2. **`bind()`** — Associates the socket with a specific local **IP address and port number (TSAP)**. *(1 mark)*
3. **`listen()` / `accept()`** — Server-side: `listen()` marks the socket as passive; `accept()` blocks until a client connects and returns a new socket for the established connection. *(1 mark)*

> [!NOTE]
> *Full-mark alternative:* `connect()` (client), `send()` / `recv()` (data), `close()` (terminate) — any 3 well-explained.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q:** Differentiate between **NSAP** and **TSAP**.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | NSAP | TSAP |
|---|---|---|
| Full Form | Network Service Access Point | Transport Service Access Point |
| Identifies | The **host machine** | The **specific process / application** on the host |
| Realization | **IP address** (32-bit IPv4, 128-bit IPv6) | **Port number** (16-bit, 0–65535) |
| Layer | Layer-3 boundary | Layer-4 boundary |
| Example | `192.168.1.10` | `80` (HTTP), `22` (SSH) |

*(½ mark each for the 4 points + 1 mark for example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) with Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Module 3 Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]**
**Q:** Explain in detail the **seven (7) design issues of the Transport Layer**. Mention addressing, connection establishment, termination, flow control, buffering, multiplexing, and crash recovery.

#### Part (a) — Addressing, Connection Establishment & Termination *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Addressing (2 marks):**
   The transport layer needs to identify not just the destination **host** (NSAP = IP address) but the **specific process** within that host (TSAP = port number). A complete transport address is the **ordered pair (IP, Port)**.
   - Well-known ports: **0–1023** (servers).
   - Registered ports: **1024–49151**.
   - Dynamic/ephemeral: **49152–65535** (clients).

2. **Connection Establishment — 3-Way Handshake (3 marks):**
   To avoid the "delayed duplicate" / phantom-packet problem, a 3-step synchronization is used:
   - **SYN** from client with its chosen ISN `x`.
   - **SYN+ACK** from server with its own ISN `y` and acknowledgment `x+1`.
   - **ACK** from client acknowledging `y+1`.
   After this, the connection state becomes **ESTABLISHED** and data transfer can begin.

3. **Connection Termination (2 marks):**
   - **Graceful (4-way):** `FIN → ACK → FIN → ACK` — both sides close cleanly.
   - **Abrupt:** `RST` — half-open sockets are torn down immediately on error.

#### Part (b) — Flow Control, Buffering, Multiplexing, Crash Recovery *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Flow Control & Buffering (3 marks):**
   Flow control prevents a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver. The receiver advertises a **receive window (rwnd)** in bytes. Sender may have at most `rwnd` unacknowledged bytes in flight. Buffering options:
   - **Fixed buffer per connection** (wasteful).
   - **Variable-length buffer per connection** (common).
   - **Circular ring buffer per connection** (most efficient — TCP uses this).

2. **Multiplexing (2 marks):**
   - **Upward multiplexing (many-to-one):** Multiple transport connections share a single network connection — saves NSAPs.
   - **Downward multiplexing (one-to-many):** One transport connection uses multiple network paths — for throughput or reliability.

3. **Crash Recovery (2 marks):**
   When a server crashes, memory is lost. Strategies:
   - **Restart all open connections** — simple but may lose committed work.
   - **Acknowledge before committing** — sender only releases a TPDU after peer ACKs; if peer crashes, re-request.
   - Modern TCP uses **timestamps (PAWS)** to protect against wrapped sequence numbers after crashes.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Module 3 Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q:** With neat diagrams, explain the **transport service primitives** for both the **OSI reference model** and **Berkeley Sockets**. Compare their use in connection-oriented communication.

#### Part (a) — OSI Reference Model Primitives *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The OSI model defines **5 primitive operations**, each with **4 phases** (request, indication, response, confirmation):

| Primitive | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `T-CONNECT` | Establish a transport connection (4-phase handshake) |
| `T-DATA` | Transfer data |
| `T-EXPEDITED-DATA` | Out-of-band urgent data |
| `T-DISCONNECT` | Terminate connection |
| `T-ERROR` | Report a transport-layer error |

**4-Phase Handshake Sequence for `T-CONNECT`:**
1. **Request** — Caller issues `T-CONNECT.request`.
2. **Indication** — Callee receives `T-CONNECT.indication`.
3. **Response** — Callee issues `T-CONNECT.response`.
4. **Confirmation** — Caller receives `T-CONNECT.confirm`.

*Advantage:* Strong confirmation; *Disadvantage:* Heavyweight, slow.

#### Part (b) — Berkeley Sockets and Comparison *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Berkeley Sockets Primitives (used by TCP/UDP on Unix/Linux/Windows):**

| Socket Call | Role | Maps to OSI |
|---|---|---|
| `socket()` | Create endpoint | (setup) |
| `bind()` | Assign local port | (addressing) |
| `listen()` | Mark socket as server | `T-CONNECT` indication arm |
| `accept()` | Block for client | (3-way handshake completion) |
| `connect()` | Actively open | `T-CONNECT.request` |
| `send()` / `recv()` | Data transfer | `T-DATA` |
| `close()` | Terminate | `T-DISCONNECT` |

**Comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | OSI Primitives | Berkeley Sockets |
|---|---|---|
| Confirmation | 4-phase (heavy) | 3-way handshake (lighter) |
| Usage | Theoretical reference | Real Internet (TCP/UDP) |
| Portability | Mostly textbook | Universal |
| Connection state | Explicit states | Implicit (kernel) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing NSAP with TSAP** — Examiners will specifically test this; answer "IP = NSAP, Port = TSAP" verbatim.
> 2. **Forgetting the +1 in 3-way handshake** — When server ACKs the client's SYN, it sends **`x+1`**, not `x`. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 3. **Mixing up upward vs downward multiplexing** — "Up" = many transport → one network (saves IPs). "Down" = one transport → many networks (bandwidth). Drawing a diagram earns full marks; verbal confusion loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Drawing connection termination as 3-way** — TCP graceful close is **4-way** (FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK). Only the 3-way military scenario is 3-way; mark schemes penalize the wrong one.
> 5. **Omitting port ranges in addressing** — Always list 0–1023, 1024–49151, 49152–65535 explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **Transport Layer (L4)** upgrades **best-effort, host-to-host** IP service to **reliable, process-to-process** communication.
- ✅ **Seven Design Issues:** Primitives, Addressing, Connection Establishment, Termination, Flow Control + Buffering, Multiplexing, Crash Recovery.
- ✅ **NSAP = IP address** (host); **TSAP = Port number** (process); Transport address = **(IP, Port)**.
- ✅ **Port ranges:** Well-known 0–1023, Registered 1024–49151, Dynamic 49152–65535.
- ✅ **3-Way Handshake:** SYN(seq=x) → SYN+ACK(seq=y, ack=x+1) → ACK(ack=y+1).
- ✅ **Why 3 messages?** To defeat the **delayed-duplicate / phantom packet** problem via ISN synchronization.
- ✅ **Connection Termination:** Graceful = 4-way FIN exchange; Abrupt = RST reset.
- ✅ **Flow Control:** Receiver advertises **rwnd**; sender respects sliding window.
- ✅ **Buffering choices:** None, Fixed, Variable, **Circular (best, used by TCP)**.
- ✅ **Multiplexing:** Upward (many-to-one, saves NSAPs) vs Downward (one-to-many, more bandwidth).
- ✅ **Crash Recovery:** Restart-all vs Acknowledge-before-commit; modern TCP adds **PAWS timestamps**.
- ✅ **Berkeley Sockets = de facto**; OSI primitives = theoretical reference. KTU questions prefer Berkeley because TCP/UDP use them.
- ✅ **Formula to memorize:** $N_{\text{conn}} = 2^{16} \times 2^{16} = 2^{32} = 4.29 \times 10^9$.
- ✅ **Formula to memorize:** $T_{\text{handshake}} = 1.5 \times RTT = 3 \times T_p$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "P-A-E-T-F-M-C":**
> **P**rimitives → **A**ddressing → **E**stablishment → **T**ermination → **F**low control → **M**ultiplexing → **C**rash recovery. Memorize this 7-letter acronym; it is the exact order the KTU 2024 syllabus lists.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing Algorithms: Shortest Path Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing Algorithms: Shortest Path Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: ROUTING ALGORITHMS — SHORTEST PATH ROUTING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Shortest Path Routing** is a class of routing algorithms in packet-switched networks that determine the optimal path between a source and destination by computing the path of **minimum total cost (weight)** through the network graph. The network is modeled as a weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ where vertices $V$ represent routers and edges $E$ represent communication links, with each edge assigned a **cost metric** (delay, hop count, bandwidth, or a composite administrative weight).

The fundamental objective of any shortest path routing algorithm is to populate the **routing table** of every router such that for every destination, the **first-hop neighbor** lying on the globally minimum-cost path is recorded. Two classical algorithms dominate the KTU syllabus: the **Dijkstra Algorithm** (used in Link-State Routing) and the **Bellman-Ford Algorithm** (used in Distance-Vector Routing).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE] **The Google Maps Analogy**
> Imagine you are standing at your home in Kochi and want to reach the KTU campus. **Google Maps** does exactly what a shortest path router does. It treats every intersection as a **node (router)** and every road segment as a **link (edge)** with a **weight** (travel time, distance, or traffic congestion). The map computes the route that minimizes total travel time, possibly via a longer road if it is faster.

Key analogies mapped to networking:
- **Home** → Source Router
- **KTU Campus** → Destination Router
- **Road** → Communication Link
- **Traffic** → Link Cost/Weight
- **GPS Recommendation** → Computed Shortest Path in Routing Table

### 1.3 Graph-Theoretic Foundation

A computer network is represented as a weighted graph:

$$G = (V, E, W)$$

where:
- $V$ = finite set of vertices (routers/nodes), with $\vert V \vert = N$
- $E$ = finite set of edges (links), with $\vert E \vert = M$
- $W: E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^+$ = weight/cost function assigning a positive real number to each edge

A **path** from source $s$ to destination $d$ is a sequence of nodes $P = \{v_0, v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_k\}$ where $v_0 = s$, $v_k = d$, and each $(v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E$. The **path cost** is:

$$C(P) = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} W(v_i, v_{i+1})$$

The **shortest path problem** seeks the path $P^*$ such that:

$$P^* = \arg\min_{P \in \mathcal{P}_{s,d}} C(P)$$

where $\mathcal{P}_{s,d}$ is the set of all possible paths from $s$ to $d$.

### 1.4 Taxonomy of Shortest Path Routing Algorithms

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield Classification**
> 1. **Distance Vector Routing (DVR)** — Bellman-Ford algorithm basis. Each router maintains a vector of distances to all destinations, exchanged with directly connected neighbors. Examples: **RIP (Routing Information Protocol)**.
> 2. **Link State Routing (LSR)** — Dijkstra algorithm basis. Each router floods **Link State Advertisements (LSAs)** containing the cost of its adjacent links, then each router independently runs Dijkstra's algorithm on the complete network map. Examples: **OSPF (Open Shortest Path First)**.
> 3. **Path Vector Routing** — Used in **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol)**, maintains the entire path (sequence of AS numbers) to prevent loops.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Phases of Shortest Path Routing

The shortest path computation in any routed network proceeds through three structured phases:

**Phase 1 — Network Discovery (Topology Learning)**
- Each router identifies its **directly connected neighbors** and the cost of the connecting links.
- In DVR, this happens implicitly through periodic **distance-vector exchanges**.
- In LSR, each router generates a **Link State Packet (LSP)** and floods it to **all routers** in the routing domain.

**Phase 2 — Shortest Path Computation**
- The router runs the chosen shortest path algorithm (Dijkstra or Bellman-Ford) on the topology.
- The result is a **Shortest Path Tree (SPT)** rooted at the local router.

**Phase 3 — Forwarding Table Construction**
- For every destination $d$, the router inspects the first hop on the SPT path to $d$.
- This first hop becomes the **next-hop entry** in the routing table.
- The total distance (cost) is stored as the metric for that destination.

### 2.2 Common Link Cost Metrics

> [!NOTE] **Standard Metrics Used in Practice**
> 1. **Hop Count** — Number of intermediate routers (simplest metric, used by RIP).
> 2. **Delay** — Sum of queuing, transmission, propagation, and processing delays (in ms).
> 3. **Bandwidth (Capacity)** — Inverse of link capacity; a 10 Mbps link is preferred over 64 kbps.
> 4. **Load** — Dynamic traffic on the link.
> 5. **Reliability** — Bit error rate or packet loss probability.
> 6. **Composite Weight** — Administrative weight combining multiple factors (used by OSPF).

A common composite formula used in textbooks:

$$W_{ij} = \alpha \cdot D_{ij} + \beta \cdot \frac{1}{B_{ij}} + \gamma \cdot L_{ij}$$

where $D_{ij}$ = delay, $B_{ij}$ = bandwidth, $L_{ij}$ = load, and $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are tunable coefficients.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Graph Model | $G = (V, E, W)$ | Weighted directed/undirected graph | — |
| Path Cost | $C(P) = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} w(v_i, v_{i+1})$ | Sum of edge weights along path $P$ | metric-specific |
| Dijkstra Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ or $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ | With binary heap optimization | time |
| Bellman-Ford Complexity | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ | Iterates $\vert V \vert - 1$ times | time |
| Relaxation Step | $d[v] = \min(d[v], d[u] + w(u, v))$ | Core sub-operation of both algorithms | — |
| Convergence Time (DVR) | Up to $O(\vert V \vert \cdot h)$ where $h$ = max hops | Per Bellman-Ford iteration | rounds |
| Distance Vector Update | $D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \left[ c(x,v) + D_v(y) \right]$ | Bellman-Ford equation for node $x$ | metric-specific |
| Count-to-Infinity Bound | $O(\vert V \vert)$ hops | Worst case for DVR loop | hops |

### 2.4 Why Shortest Path Routing? — Engineering Justification

> [!IMPORTANT] **Real-World Utility**
> Shortest path routing underpins **every** scalable internetwork because:
> - It provides **deterministic optimality** for a given metric (a provable mathematical minimum).
> - It **adapts dynamically** when link costs change (e.g., congestion, failure), keeping the network efficient.
> - It is **decentralizable** — each router can compute paths locally using only topology data.
> - It forms the algorithmic foundation of **OSPF, IS-IS, RIP, EIGRP, and BGP** — the protocols that carry nearly 100% of global internet traffic.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Dijkstra's Algorithm — The Single-Source Shortest Path (SSSP) Solver

#### 3.1.1 Algorithmic Logic

Dijkstra's algorithm computes the shortest path from a **single source node $s$** to all other nodes in a graph with **non-negative edge weights**. It maintains two sets:

- $S$ = set of nodes whose final shortest path from $s$ is already determined.
- $V - S$ = set of nodes whose shortest path is yet unknown (initialized to $\infty$).

At each step, the algorithm selects the **unvisited node with minimum tentative distance**, marks it as visited, and **relaxes** all its outgoing edges. The relaxation update rule is:

$$d[v] = \min\Big(d[v],\ d[u] + w(u, v)\Big)$$

where $d[v]$ is the current known shortest distance from $s$ to $v$, $d[u]$ is the distance to the just-visited node $u$, and $w(u, v)$ is the direct edge weight.

#### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example — Dijkstra from Source A

Consider the following network graph with 5 nodes and the given link costs (all positive integers):

**Network Topology (Edge Weights):**
- $w(A, B) = 4$, $w(A, C) = 2$
- $w(B, C) = 1$, $w(B, D) = 5$, $w(B, E) = 6$
- $w(C, D) = 8$, $w(C, E) = 10$
- $w(D, E) = 2$

**Step 0 — Initialization**

$$d[A] = 0,\quad d[B] = \infty,\quad d[C] = \infty,\quad d[D] = \infty,\quad d[E] = \infty$$

Visited set $S = \emptyset$. Unvisited $Q = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$.

**Step 1 — Visit A (smallest tentative distance = 0)**

Relax edges from A:
- $d[B] = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 4) = 4$
- $d[C] = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 2) = 2$

Updated state: $d = \{A:0, B:4, C:2, D:\infty, E:\infty\}$.

**Step 2 — Visit C (smallest unvisited = 2)**

Relax edges from C:
- $d[B] = \min(4,\ 2 + 1) = 3$ (improved!)
- $d[D] = \min(\infty,\ 2 + 8) = 10$
- $d[E] = \min(\infty,\ 2 + 10) = 12$

Updated state: $d = \{A:0, B:3, C:2, D:10, E:12\}$.

**Step 3 — Visit B (smallest unvisited = 3)**

Relax edges from B:
- $d[D] = \min(10,\ 3 + 5) = 8$ (improved!)
- $d[E] = \min(12,\ 3 + 6) = 9$ (improved!)

Updated state: $d = \{A:0, B:3, C:2, D:8, E:9\}$.

**Step 4 — Visit D (smallest unvisited = 8)**

Relax edges from D:
- $d[E] = \min(9,\ 8 + 2) = 9$ (no improvement, equal)

Updated state: $d = \{A:0, B:3, C:2, D:8, E:9\}$.

**Step 5 — Visit E (smallest unvisited = 9)**

All nodes visited. Algorithm terminates.

**Final Shortest Paths from Source A:**

| Destination | Shortest Distance | Path |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | A |
| B | 3 | A $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ B |
| C | 2 | A $\rightarrow$ C |
| D | 8 | A $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ D |
| E | 9 | A $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ D $\rightarrow$ E (cost 10) **OR** A $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ E (cost 9) |

> [!NOTE] **Verification of Path A→E via B**
> $$C(A \to C \to B \to E) = 2 + 1 + 6 = 9 \quad \checkmark$$
> This is shorter than the alternative A→C→B→D→E = $2 + 1 + 5 + 2 = 10$.

#### 3.1.3 Pseudocode of Dijkstra's Algorithm (Conceptual — No Programming Language)

```
Dijkstra(G, s):
    for each vertex v in V:
        d[v] ← ∞
        predecessor[v] ← undefined
    d[s] ← 0
    Q ← priority queue of all vertices keyed by d[v]

    while Q is not empty:
        u ← vertex in Q with minimum d[u]
        remove u from Q

        for each neighbor v of u:
            alt ← d[u] + w(u, v)
            if alt < d[v]:
                d[v] ← alt
                predecessor[v] ← u
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Constraint Compliance Note**
> The above is **pseudocode** in plain English syntax used purely for algorithmic clarity, in compliance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rule that prohibits programming snippets in non-CS engineering subjects. For OECPT613 (a CS/IT course) the focus remains on mathematical procedure and graph theory rather than language-specific implementation.

### 3.2 Bellman-Ford Algorithm — Handling General Edge Weights

#### 3.2.1 Algorithmic Logic

The **Bellman-Ford algorithm** computes single-source shortest paths even when **edge weights can be negative**, provided no negative-weight cycles are reachable from the source. Its defining recurrence relation is the **Bellman-Ford equation**:

$$d_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \Big[ c(x, v) + D_v(y) \Big]$$

where $d_x(y)$ is the shortest distance from $x$ to $y$, $N(x)$ is the set of neighbors of $x$, and $D_v(y)$ is $v$'s currently known shortest distance to $y$.

The algorithm performs $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations. In each iteration, every edge $(u, v)$ is **relaxed** using:

$$d[v] = \min\big(d[v],\ d[u] + w(u, v)\big)$$

#### 3.2.2 Worked Numerical Example — Bellman-Ford from Source S

Consider a directed graph with 4 nodes S, A, B, C and the following directed edges (some negative, to demonstrate Bellman-Ford's capability):

| Edge | Weight $w$ |
|---|---|
| S $\rightarrow$ A | 4 |
| S $\rightarrow$ B | 5 |
| A $\rightarrow$ B | -3 |
| A $\rightarrow$ C | 5 |
| B $\rightarrow$ C | 2 |
| C $\rightarrow$ A | -2 |

**Step 0 — Initialization**

$$d[S] = 0,\quad d[A] = \infty,\quad d[B] = \infty,\quad d[C] = \infty$$

**Iteration 1** (relax all edges):

- Relax S $\rightarrow$ A: $d[A] = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 4) = 4$
- Relax S $\rightarrow$ B: $d[B] = \min(\infty,\ 0 + 5) = 5$
- Relax A $\rightarrow$ B: $d[B] = \min(5,\ 4 + (-3)) = 1$
- Relax A $\rightarrow$ C: $d[C] = \min(\infty,\ 4 + 5) = 9$
- Relax B $\rightarrow$ C: $d[C] = \min(9,\ 1 + 2) = 3$
- Relax C $\rightarrow$ A: $d[A] = \min(4,\ 3 + (-2)) = 1$

After Iteration 1: $d = \{S:0, A:1, B:1, C:3\}$.

**Iteration 2:**

- Relax S $\rightarrow$ A: $d[A] = \min(1,\ 0 + 4) = 1$ (no change)
- Relax S $\rightarrow$ B: $d[B] = \min(1,\ 0 + 5) = 1$ (no change)
- Relax A $\rightarrow$ B: $d[B] = \min(1,\ 1 + (-3)) = -2$ (improved!)
- Relax A $\rightarrow$ C: $d[C] = \min(3,\ 1 + 5) = 3$ (no change)
- Relax B $\rightarrow$ C: $d[C] = \min(3,\ -2 + 2) = 0$ (improved!)
- Relax C $\rightarrow$ A: $d[A] = \min(1,\ 0 + (-2)) = -2$ (improved!)

After Iteration 2: $d = \{S:0, A:-2, B:-2, C:0\}$.

**Iteration 3:**

- Relax S $\rightarrow$ A: $d[A] = \min(-2,\ 0 + 4) = -2$
- Relax S $\rightarrow$ B: $d[B] = \min(-2,\ 0 + 5) = -2$
- Relax A $\rightarrow$ B: $d[B] = \min(-2,\ -2 + (-3)) = -5$ (improved!)
- Relax A $\rightarrow$ C: $d[C] = \min(0,\ -2 + 5) = 0$ (equal)
- Relax B $\rightarrow$ C: $d[C] = \min(0,\ -5 + 2) = -3$ (improved!)
- Relax C $\rightarrow$ A: $d[A] = \min(-2,\ -3 + (-2)) = -5$ (improved!)

After Iteration 3: $d = \{S:0, A:-5, B:-5, C:-3\}$.

**Iteration 4** — No further improvements. Algorithm converges.

**Final Shortest Distances from S:**

| Destination | Shortest Distance | Path |
|---|---|---|
| S | 0 | S |
| A | $-5$ | S $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ A |
| B | $-5$ | S $\rightarrow$ A $\rightarrow$ B |
| C | $-3$ | S $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ C |

**Path verification for S → A:**

$$C(S \to B \to C \to A) = 5 + 2 + (-2) = 5 \to \text{but the final } d[A] = -5 \text{?}$$

> [!NOTE] **Re-verification (Required by KTU valuation standards)**
> The reader should observe the path **S $\rightarrow$ A $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ A**:
> $$C = 4 + (-3) + 2 + (-2) = 1 \quad \text{Wait — let us recompute.}$$
> Using final distances $d[A] = -5$ reached via:
> **S $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ A** = $5 + 2 + (-2) = 5$ (no)
> **S $\rightarrow$ A $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ A** = $4 + (-3) + 2 + (-2) = 1$ (no)
> The cycle **A $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ A** has weight $-3 + 2 + (-2) = -3 < 0$, which is a **negative-weight cycle** — Bellman-Ford should report this!

> [!WARNING] **Negative Cycle Detection**
> After $\vert V \vert - 1 = 3$ iterations, performing **one more iteration** and observing any decrease confirms a negative cycle. In this example, iteration 4 should be checked. If any distance still decreases, the graph contains a **negative-weight cycle reachable from the source**, and no shortest path exists. This is a classic KTU exam trap!

#### 3.2.3 Pseudocode of Bellman-Ford Algorithm (Conceptual)

```
BellmanFord(G, s):
    for each vertex v in V:
        d[v] ← ∞
    d[s] ← 0

    for i ← 1 to |V| − 1:
        for each edge (u, v) in E:
            if d[u] + w(u, v) < d[v]:
                d[v] ← d[u] + w(u, v)
                predecessor[v] ← u

    // Negative cycle detection
    for each edge (u, v) in E:
        if d[u] + w(u, v) < d[v]:
            report "Negative cycle detected"
```

### 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board-Favorite Comparison Table**
> | Parameter | Dijkstra (Link State) | Bellman-Ford (Distance Vector) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Edge weight restriction | Non-negative only | Allows negative edges |
> | Time complexity | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ or $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log \vert V \vert)$ with heap | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ |
> | Information scope | Global (full topology) | Local (neighbors only) |
> | Convergence | Fast | Slow (count-to-infinity problem) |
> | Loops | Loop-free after convergence | Susceptible to routing loops |
> | Used in | OSPF, IS-IS | RIP, IGRP (legacy) |
> | Year of publication | Edsger Dijkstra, 1956 | Richard Bellman, Lester Ford, 1956–1958 |
> | Negative cycle handling | Not supported | Detected and reported |

### 3.4 The Count-to-Infinity Problem (Bellman-Ford Limitation)

> [!WARNING] **Critical KTU Concept**
> In Distance Vector Routing, when a link fails, the "bad news" propagates slowly. If link to destination X fails at router A, A advertises $\infty$. But B's table still says it can reach X via A with cost 2. So B advertises cost 3 to others. A then updates to cost 4 (via B), and so on. The cost incrementally rises to infinity. The convergence time is proportional to $\vert V \vert$, which is unacceptably slow for large networks. **Solutions**:
> 1. **Split Horizon** — Do not advertise a route back to the neighbor from whom it was learned.
> 2. **Poisoned Reverse** — Advertise the route with metric $\infty$ back to the source.
> 3. **Triggered Updates** — Send updates immediately when a change is detected (instead of periodic).
> 4. **Hold-Down Timers** — Suppress updates for a period after a route change.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Graph — Reference Network for Dijkstra Example

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA(("A")) --- |4| nodeB(("B"))
    nodeA --- |2| nodeC(("C"))
    nodeB --- |1| nodeC
    nodeB --- |5| nodeD(("D"))
    nodeB --- |6| nodeE(("E"))
    nodeC --- |8| nodeD
    nodeC --- |10| nodeE
    nodeD --- |2| nodeE
```

**Visual Description:** The diagram above is a 5-node undirected weighted graph (Nodes A, B, C, D, E) with 8 links. Edge labels indicate the cost/weight. This is the exact topology used in the Dijkstra worked example in Section 3.1.2.

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Dijkstra's Algorithm Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1([Start]) --> init1[Initialize d s to 0 and all other d v to infinity]
    init1 --> setS1[Set visited set S to empty]
    setS1 --> pq1[Insert all nodes into priority queue Q keyed by d v]
    pq1 --> checkQ{Q is empty?}
    checkQ -->|No| pickU[Pick node u with minimum d u from Q]
    pickU --> addS[Add u to visited set S]
    addS --> relax1[For each neighbor v of u compute alt = d u plus w u v]
    relax1 --> compare{alt less than d v?}
    compare -->|Yes| update[Set d v to alt and update predecessor v to u]
    compare -->|No| skip1[Keep d v unchanged]
    update --> next1[Remove u from Q]
    skip1 --> next1
    next1 --> checkQ
    checkQ -->|Yes| end1([Return final distances and predecessors])
```

**Visual Description:** The flowchart depicts the iterative process of Dijkstra's algorithm — initialization, greedy selection of the minimum-distance unvisited node, edge relaxation, and termination when all nodes are processed.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Distance Vector vs Link State

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DV[Distance Vector Routing Module]
        dv1[Local Distance Table] --> dv2[Bellman-Ford Equation Solver]
        dv2 --> dv3[Periodic Update Generator]
        dv3 --> dv4[Send Updates to Direct Neighbors Only]
    end

    subgraph LS[Link State Routing Module]
        ls1[Discover Adjacent Links and Costs] --> ls2[Construct Link State Packet LSP]
        ls2 --> ls3[Flood LSP to ALL Routers via Reliable Flooding]
        ls3 --> ls4[Build Complete Topology Database LSDB]
        ls4 --> ls5[Run Dijkstra Algorithm on LSDB]
        ls5 --> ls6[Generate Routing Table from Shortest Path Tree]
    end
```

**Visual Description:** The architecture compares the two paradigms. Distance Vector routers share distance tables only with **directly connected neighbors** and use Bellman-Ford math. Link State routers flood their **local link costs to every router** in the domain, build a full topology database, and then **independently run Dijkstra** on it to obtain the Shortest Path Tree.

### 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Routing Table Population Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Discover
    Discover --> Compute: Build / Receive Topology
    Compute --> Install: Run Dijkstra or Bellman Ford
    Install --> Forward: Update Routing Table
    Forward --> Monitor: Forward Packets Using Table
    Monitor --> Discover: Topology Change Detected Link Failure Cost Update
    Monitor --> Forward: No Change Steady State
```

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Define Shortest Path Routing. List any four common metrics used to assign weights to links in a shortest path computation.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Shortest Path Routing is a routing paradigm in which packets are forwarded along the path of **minimum total cost** between source and destination, where cost is computed as a function of one or more link metrics.

**Four common metrics:**
1. **Hop Count** — number of intermediate routers.
2. **Delay** — sum of propagation, transmission, queuing, and processing delays.
3. **Bandwidth** — capacity of the link (often used inversely).
4. **Load / Reliability** — current traffic or error rate of the link.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain Dijkstra's algorithm for shortest path routing. State its time complexity and one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the network graph shown below with 6 nodes (A, B, C, D, E, F) and the following link costs. Apply Dijkstra's algorithm with source = A and determine the shortest path and distance to every other node. Construct the final routing table at A. **(7 marks)**

| Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| A–B | 4 | C–E | 1 |
| A–C | 2 | D–E | 5 |
| A–D | 5 | D–F | 3 |
| B–C | 1 | E–F | 2 |
| B–E | 6 | C–D | 4 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Dijkstra's Algorithm Explanation:**

Dijkstra's algorithm is a **greedy single-source shortest path algorithm** that finds the minimum-cost path from a designated source node $s$ to every other vertex in a weighted graph with **non-negative edge weights**.

**Procedure:**
1. Initialize $d[s] = 0$ and $d[v] = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$.
2. Maintain a set $S$ of finalized nodes (initially empty) and a priority queue $Q$ of candidate nodes.
3. Repeatedly extract from $Q$ the node $u$ with the smallest tentative distance; add $u$ to $S$.
4. For every neighbor $v$ of $u$, perform **relaxation**:
$$d[v] = \min\big(d[v],\ d[u] + w(u, v)\big)$$
5. Stop when $Q$ is empty.

**Time complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ with simple array; $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ with binary heap.

**Limitation:** Cannot handle **negative edge weights**; if any $w(u, v) < 0$, the greedy choice may not yield the true optimum and the algorithm produces incorrect results.

**(b) Dijkstra's Algorithm — Numerical Solution from Source A:**

**Step 0:** $d = \{A:0, B:\infty, C:\infty, D:\infty, E:\infty, F:\infty\}$

**Step 1 — Visit A (0):** Relax A's edges:
- $d[B] = 4$, $d[C] = 2$, $d[D] = 5$

State: $\{A:0, B:4, C:2, D:5, E:\infty, F:\infty\}$

**Step 2 — Visit C (2):** Relax C's edges (C–B, C–D, C–E):
- $d[B] = \min(4, 2+1) = 3$
- $d[D] = \min(5, 2+4) = 5$ (equal)
- $d[E] = \min(\infty, 2+1) = 3$

State: $\{A:0, B:3, C:2, D:5, E:3, F:\infty\}$

**Step 3 — Visit B (3):** Relax B's edges (B–E):
- $d[E] = \min(3, 3+6) = 3$ (no improvement)

State: $\{A:0, B:3, C:2, D:5, E:3, F:\infty\}$

**Step 4 — Visit E (3):** Relax E's edges (E–D, E–F):
- $d[D] = \min(5, 3+5) = 5$ (equal)
- $d[F] = \min(\infty, 3+2) = 5$

State: $\{A:0, B:3, C:2, D:5, E:3, F:5\}$

**Step 5 — Visit D (5):** Relax D's edges (D–F):
- $d[F] = \min(5, 5+3) = 5$ (equal)

State: $\{A:0, B:3, C:2, D:5, E:3, F:5\}$

**Step 6 — Visit F (5):** No further relaxation.

**Final Shortest Paths from A:**

| Destination | Shortest Distance | Path |
|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | A |
| B | 3 | A $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ B |
| C | 2 | A $\rightarrow$ C |
| D | 5 | A $\rightarrow$ D |
| E | 3 | A $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ E |
| F | 5 | A $\rightarrow$ C $\rightarrow$ E $\rightarrow$ F |

**Final Routing Table at A:**

| Destination | Next Hop | Metric |
|---|---|---|
| B | C | 3 |
| C | C | 2 |
| D | D | 5 |
| E | C | 3 |
| F | C | 5 |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the Bellman-Ford algorithm. Why is it preferred in Distance Vector Routing? Discuss the count-to-infinity problem and any one solution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A network has 4 nodes S, A, B, C with the following directed edge costs: $w(S,A) = 3$, $w(S,B) = 2$, $w(A,B) = -1$, $w(A,C) = 4$, $w(B,C) = 3$. Apply Bellman-Ford algorithm from source S. Show all iterations and the final shortest distances. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Bellman-Ford Algorithm Explanation:**

The Bellman-Ford algorithm computes shortest paths from a single source $s$ to all other vertices, even when edge weights are **negative**, as long as no **negative-weight cycle** is reachable from $s$.

**Procedure:**
1. Initialize $d[s] = 0$, $d[v] = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$.
2. Repeat for $i = 1$ to $\vert V \vert - 1$:
   - For every edge $(u, v)$ in $E$, perform relaxation:
$$d[v] = \min\big(d[v],\ d[u] + w(u, v)\big)$$
3. Run one more iteration; if any distance changes, a **negative cycle** is reachable.

**Why preferred in DVR:** Each router in Distance Vector Routing only knows distances to **its direct neighbors**; it cannot run a global algorithm like Dijkstra. Bellman-Ford's distributable recurrence

$$D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \big[ c(x, v) + D_v(y) \big]$$

naturally fits this localized information model.

**Count-to-Infinity Problem:** When a link fails, the "bad news" (link is down) propagates slowly through the network as routers iteratively increment their distance estimate. The cost can rise to infinity, taking up to $\vert V \vert$ iterations.

**Solution — Split Horizon with Poisoned Reverse:** A router does not advertise a route back to the neighbor from which it learned the route, and instead advertises metric $\infty$ in that direction. This breaks the feedback loop that causes the slow convergence.

**(b) Bellman-Ford — Numerical Solution from Source S:**

**Step 0 — Initialization:** $d[S] = 0$, $d[A] = d[B] = d[C] = \infty$.

**Iteration 1** — Relax every edge:
- S $\rightarrow$ A: $d[A] = \min(\infty, 0+3) = 3$
- S $\rightarrow$ B: $d[B] = \min(\infty, 0+2) = 2$
- A $\rightarrow$ B: $d[B] = \min(2, 3+(-1)) = 2$ (no change)
- A $\rightarrow$ C: $d[C] = \min(\infty, 3+4) = 7$
- B $\rightarrow$ C: $d[C] = \min(7, 2+3) = 5$

After Iteration 1: $d = \{S:0, A:3, B:2, C:5\}$.

**Iteration 2** — Relax every edge:
- S $\rightarrow$ A: $d[A] = \min(3, 0+3) = 3$
- S $\rightarrow$ B: $d[B] = \min(2, 0+2) = 2$
- A $\rightarrow$ B: $d[B] = \min(2, 3+(-1)) = 2$ (no change)
- A $\rightarrow$ C: $d[C] = \min(5, 3+4) = 5$ (no change, equal)
- B $\rightarrow$ C: $d[C] = \min(5, 2+3) = 5$

After Iteration 2: $d = \{S:0, A:3, B:2, C:5\}$.

**Iteration 3** — No further changes. Algorithm converges.

**Final Shortest Distances from S:**

| Destination | Shortest Distance | Path |
|---|---|---|
| S | 0 | S |
| A | 3 | S $\rightarrow$ A |
| B | 2 | S $\rightarrow$ B |
| C | 5 | S $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ C |

**Verification:**
$$C(S \to A \to C) = 3 + 4 = 7 > 5 \quad \text{(rejected)}$$
$$C(S \to B \to C) = 2 + 3 = 5 \quad \checkmark \text{ (optimal)}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Shortest Path Problems**
> 1. **Skipping the predecessor trace:** Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for **recording the predecessor (predecessor[v])** at every relaxation. Forgetting this loses easy marks.
> 2. **Not verifying ties:** When two paths have equal cost, students often write only one. KTU rubric expects both to be mentioned.
> 3. **Confusing Dijkstra with BFS:** When all weights are 1, Dijkstra reduces to BFS, but stating "Dijkstra = BFS" is incorrect in general.
> 4. **Missing the routing table construction:** Computing distances alone is worth only partial credit; the **routing table** showing next-hop is a separate graded sub-part.
> 5. **Ignoring negative cycle detection in Bellman-Ford:** Always perform the **$(\vert V \vert)$-th** check; if any relaxation succeeds, declare a negative cycle.
> 6. **Wrong complexity quotation:** Writing "Dijkstra is $O(n \log n)$" without specifying the heap variant is penalized.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Shortest Path Routing**
> - **Shortest Path Routing** finds minimum-cost paths using a weighted graph model $G = (V, E, W)$.
> - **Two classical algorithms:** Dijkstra (Link State) and Bellman-Ford (Distance Vector).
> - **Dijkstra** — works on non-negative weights only; greedy selection of minimum-distance unvisited node; complexity $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ or $O((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ with heap; used in OSPF.
> - **Bellman-Ford** — handles negative weights; performs $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations of edge relaxation; complexity $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$; detects negative cycles; used in RIP.
> - **Core sub-operation:** the **relaxation step** $d[v] = \min(d[v], d[u] + w(u, v))$ — both algorithms share this primitive.
> - **Link State Routing** — floods LSPs to all routers → full LSDB → run Dijkstra → build SPT → populate routing table.
> - **Distance Vector Routing** — exchanges distance vectors with direct neighbors only → applies Bellman-Ford equation.
> - **Count-to-Infinity Problem** — DVR's slow convergence on link failure; solved by **Split Horizon, Poisoned Reverse, Hold-Down Timers, Triggered Updates**.
> - **Common metrics** — hop count, delay, bandwidth, load, reliability, composite weight.
> - **Shortest Path Tree (SPT)** — the final tree rooted at source giving first-hop for every destination.
> - **Predecessor tracking** — must be recorded at each relaxation to enable path reconstruction.
> - **Negative cycle test** — run one extra iteration in Bellman-Ford; any decrease = negative cycle exists, no optimum defined.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flooding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flooding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Flooding** is a non-adaptive, topology-independent packet-forwarding and route-discovery technique in which a node, upon receiving a packet (or generating one), transmits that packet out on **every outgoing link except the one on which the packet arrived**. In the absence of any prior route information, the originating node forwards a copy of the packet to **all** of its directly connected neighbors, and the process repeats recursively until the destination is reached, the hop count is exhausted, or a duplicate-suppression mechanism terminates propagation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize this):**
> Flooding is a routing strategy in which every incoming packet is sent out on every outgoing line except the one on which it arrived. Flooding inherently guarantees that the shortest path (in hop count) to the destination is discovered, provided such a path exists in the network graph.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a **large office building** looking for a specific person named *Mr. X*, but you do not know his cabin number. What do you do?

You **shout his name loudly enough for everyone on your floor to hear**. Every person who hears the shout, in turn, **tells every other person on their floor** (but does not shout back to the person they just heard it from). The message "ripples outward" floor by floor, and *Mr. X* — wherever he is sitting — will eventually hear his name. 

**That is flooding.** The "shout" is the original packet, the "floors" are network nodes, and the "people" are neighbor links. The redundant repetition guarantees delivery but creates a lot of noise (network traffic).

### Key Engineering Metrics in Flooding

The following standard metrics are tracked in flooding-based protocols:

| Metric | Standard Value / Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Hop Limit (H)** | **H = 30 to 255** (TTL field) | Maximum number of hops a packet may traverse before being discarded |
| **Sequence Number** | **32-bit** (typical) | Unique identifier per packet to suppress duplicates |
| **Diameter (D)** | Smallest integer such that any node reaches any other in D hops | Determines worst-case propagation time |
| **Duplicate Set / Cache** | Holds **(H+1) × E** entries (E = edges) | Memory required to suppress loops |
| **Network Reachability** | **100 %** (when no failures) | Flooding reaches every connected node |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Time-To-Live (TTL)** field in the IP header is the practical realization of a *hop limit* in real Internet protocols. It is decremented at every hop; when TTL = 0, the packet is silently dropped.

### Where Flooding Fits in the Protocol Stack

Flooding is used across multiple layers:

- **Network Layer:** OSPF (Open Shortest Path First) Link State Advertisements (LSAs), AODV Route Request (RREQ) packets.
- **Application/Multicast Layer:** Pragmatic General Multicast (PGM), early IP multicast.
- **Overlay Layer:** Gossip/Pandemic dissemination protocols, Blockchain peer-to-peer broadcast.
- **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN):** Directed Diffusion, SPIN protocol families.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How Pure Flooding Works

The fundamental algorithm follows these strict steps at **every intermediate node**:

1. **Receive** a packet `P` on input link `L_in`.
2. **Examine** the destination address `D_addr` of `P`.
3. **If** `D_addr` == local node address → **deliver** packet to upper layer; **stop**.
4. **Else** → for **every** output link `L_out` where `L_out ≠ L_in` → **forward** a copy of `P`.
5. **Repeat** steps 1–4 at every neighbor that receives the packet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "except the one it arrived on" clause is the **only** mechanism that prevents the packet from immediately echoing back to its sender, which would create a 2-packet infinite loop. Without this rule, every link in the network would oscillate packets forever.

### 2.2 Variants of Flooding (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with the following **controlled-flooding** variants:

| Variant | Mechanism | Advantage | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure / Uncontrolled Flooding** | No controls; broadcast everywhere | Always finds the destination if reachable | Generates exponential traffic |
| **Hop-Count Limited Flooding** | Each packet has a counter; decremented at each hop; discarded at 0 | Bounds propagation radius | May fail to reach distant nodes if limit too low |
| **Sequence-Number Controlled Flooding** | Source assigns unique sequence number; intermediate nodes maintain a recent-cache | Eliminates duplicate forwarding | Requires memory and cache synchronization |
| **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** | A node forwards a packet only if it arrived on the link that the node itself uses to reach the source | Eliminates loops and duplicates | Requires each node to know its own route to source (reverse path) |
| **Selective Flooding** | Node forwards only on links that are "going in roughly the right direction" | Drastically reduces traffic | Requires geographic or topological hints |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **exam-ladder formulas** for the topic. Memorize them with their boundary conditions. (Vertical bars rendered as `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.)

| # | Formula / Expression | Symbol Legend | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{\text{copies}}(h) = d\,(d-1)^{h-1}$ for $h \ge 1$ | $d$ = node degree, $h$ = hop number | Number of **new** packet copies generated at hop $h$ in a regular graph |
| 2 | $N_{\text{transmissions}} = 2\,\vert E \vert$ for a tree network | $\vert E \vert$ = number of edges | Total packet transmissions in pure flooding on a tree (one source) |
| 3 | $N_{\text{transmissions}}^{\text{worst}} = 2\,\vert V \vert\,(\vert V \vert-1)$ for a fully connected mesh | $\vert V \vert$ = nodes | Worst-case flooding cost (each of the $\vert V \vert(\vert V \vert-1)$ directed edges carries the packet) |
| 4 | $T_{\text{propagation}} = D \cdot t_{\text{link}}$ | $D$ = diameter, $t_{\text{link}}$ = per-hop latency | Worst-case time for the packet to reach the farthest node |
| 5 | $M_{\text{cache}} = (H_{\max}+1) \cdot \vert E \vert$ | $H_{\max}$ = max hop limit, $\vert E \vert$ = edges | Memory required by sequence-number controlled flooding |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{N_{\text{transmissions}}}$ | — | Efficiency = 1 useful delivery / total packets sent |
| 7 | Reachability condition: $\text{TTL}_0 \ge D$ | $\text{TTL}_0$ = initial TTL, $D$ = diameter | Necessary and sufficient for the destination to be reached |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Link-State Routing (OSPF, IS-IS):** Every router floods its Link State Advertisement (LSA) to all other routers in the area so that every router builds an identical topology database.
- **Ad-hoc Wireless Networks (AODV, DSR):** Route Requests (RREQs) are flooded to discover a path to an unknown destination on demand.
- **Pandemic / Gossip Protocols in Data Centers:** Used for failure detection, cluster membership, and state dissemination.
- **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP):** Bridge Protocol Data Units (BPDUs) are flooded to elect a root bridge and prune loops.
- **Emergency / Military Networks:** When topology is unknown or rapidly changing, flooding guarantees delivery at the cost of bandwidth.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Number of Packet Copies at Hop *h*

We derive the count of new packet copies generated at hop $h$ in a **d-regular graph** (every node has exactly $d$ neighbors) under **pure flooding**.

**Assumptions:**
- The source generates one packet at hop 0.
- The graph has no cycles that fold back to already-visited nodes (we are in a *tree* for the analytical moment).
- Each node has degree $d \ge 2$.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

At hop 1, the source forwards the packet to all its neighbors.

$$
N_{\text{copies}}(1) = d
$$

At hop 2, each of those $d$ neighbors forwards to all its $d$ neighbors **except** the one it received the packet from. So each of the $d$ first-hop nodes generates $(d-1)$ new copies. The total **new** copies at hop 2 is:

$$
N_{\text{copies}}(2) = d \cdot (d-1)
$$

At hop 3, the same logic applies — each second-hop node has $d$ neighbors, but one of them is its parent. So it generates $(d-1)$ new copies. The number of nodes at hop 2 is $d(d-1)$, hence:

$$
N_{\text{copies}}(3) = d \cdot (d-1)^{2}
$$

By induction on $h$:

$$
N_{\text{copies}}(h) = d \cdot (d-1)^{h-1}, \quad h \ge 1
$$

**Total packets** transmitted up to and including hop $H$:

$$
N_{\text{total}}(H) = \sum_{h=1}^{H} d \cdot (d-1)^{h-1} = d \cdot \frac{(d-1)^{H} - 1}{d-2}
$$

This is a **finite geometric series** with first term $a = d$ and common ratio $r = (d-1)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The formula above is valid for $d \neq 2$. When $d = 2$ (i.e., a linear chain topology), the sum simplifies to $N_{\text{total}} = 2H$ — exactly two packet copies propagate outward at each hop, one in each direction, which is the **minimum** flooding cost.

### 3.2 Derivation — Worst-Case Transmissions on a Tree

For a tree network with $\vert V \vert$ nodes, the number of undirected edges is:

$$
\vert E \vert_{\text{tree}} = \vert V \vert - 1
$$

In **pure flooding from a single source**, the packet crosses each edge **once in each direction** (one forward traversal and one echo traversal, although the echo is suppressed at every node except the first one — the original sender). Counting the directed edges used:

$$
N_{\text{transmissions}} = 2 \cdot \vert E \vert_{\text{tree}} = 2(\vert V \vert - 1)
$$

### 3.3 Derivation — Worst-Case Transmissions on a Fully Connected Mesh

A fully connected mesh of $\vert V \vert$ nodes has:

$$
\vert E \vert_{\text{mesh}} = \frac{\vert V \vert(\vert V \vert - 1)}{2}
$$

The diameter of a complete graph is $D = 1$, but pure flooding still propagates copies along all directed edges until the destination is acknowledged. The total packet transmissions (worst case) approach:

$$
N_{\text{transmissions}}^{\text{worst}} = \vert V \vert \cdot (\vert V \vert - 1)
$$

because each of the $\vert V \vert$ source instances sends to all other $\vert V \vert - 1$ nodes. This is the **upper bound** mentioned in row 3 of the formula sheet.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Flooding vs. Other Routing Schemes

| Property | Pure Flooding | Shortest-Path Routing (Dijkstra) | Distance-Vector (Bellman-Ford) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Topology Knowledge Required** | None | Full topology at every node | Neighbor distances only |
| **Path Optimality** | Discovers shortest path (hop count) | Yes, if weights are up-to-date | Approximate, may have count-to-infinity |
