| **Convergence Time** | One round = $D$ hops | $O(\vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ with heap | Up to $O(\vert V \vert)$ iterations |
| **Storage at Each Node** | Sequence cache $(H+1)\cdot \vert E \vert$ | Full topology table | One entry per destination |
| **Bandwidth Cost** | Very high (up to $2\vert E \vert$ per packet) | Moderate (per-link updates) | Low (only neighbor exchanges) |
| **Robustness to Failures** | Excellent (no dependency on tables) | Poor (stale tables) | Poor (slow convergence) |
| **Use Case Today** | OSPF LSA, AODV RREQ, gossip | OSPF, IS-IS (steady state) | RIP (legacy) |

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A network is a balanced binary tree of depth 3 (so $\vert V \vert = 15$, root at depth 0). The source is the root. Compute the number of new packet copies generated at hop 3, and the total number of transmissions.

**Solution:**

The root is the source (depth 0). Its degree is $d = 2$ (two children). But each non-leaf node in a binary tree also has degree 3 (one parent + two children). For an *approximate* regular analysis, we use $d = 3$ for intermediate nodes.

At hop 3:

$$
N_{\text{copies}}(3) = 3 \cdot (3-1)^{3-1} = 3 \cdot 4 = 12
$$

This matches the actual binary tree: at depth 3 there are $2^{3} = 8$ leaves, but each non-leaf at depth 2 has degree 3 and generates 2 new copies, giving $4 \times 2 = 8$ packets reaching the leaves. The formula slightly overcounts because it assumes a regular graph, which is why the analytical model is an **upper bound**.

Total transmissions on a tree:

$$
N_{\text{transmissions}} = 2(\vert V \vert - 1) = 2(15 - 1) = 28
$$

This equals the number of directed edges traversed exactly once each way.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flooding Propagation Schematic (Mermaid)

The diagram below illustrates how a packet originating at node **A** propagates through a small mesh network using controlled flooding (hop limit = 3). Solid arrows show the **first transmission** of a packet copy; dashed arrows show **echoes / duplicate suppression**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeS["NODE S  SOURCE"]:::src
    node1["NODE 1  HOP 1"]:::hop1
    node2["NODE 2  HOP 1"]:::hop1
    node3["NODE 3  HOP 2"]:::hop2
    node4["NODE 4  HOP 2"]:::hop2
    node5["NODE 5  HOP 2"]:::hop2
    nodeD["NODE D  DESTINATION"]:::dst

    nodeS --> node1
    nodeS --> node2
    node1 -.dup.-> nodeS
    node2 -.dup.-> nodeS
    node1 --> node3
    node1 --> node4
    node2 --> node4
    node2 --> node5
    node3 -.dup.-> node1
    node4 -.dup.-> node1
    node4 -.dup.-> node2
    node5 -.dup.-> node2
    node3 --> nodeD
    node5 --> nodeD
    nodeD -.ack.-> nodeS

    classDef src fill:#FFD54F,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef hop1 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef hop2 fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef dst fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows are **forward** flooding copies.
- Dashed arrows labelled "dup" represent **duplicate packets** that an intermediate node receives and discards (without re-forwarding), thanks to the sequence-number cache.
- The "ack" arrow from the destination back to the source is the standard acknowledgement that terminates route discovery in protocols like AODV.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Flooding Module

```mermaid
graph LR
    pktIn["INCOMING PACKET"]:::io --> seqChk{"SEQUENCE NUMBER\nCACHE CHECK"}:::dec
    seqChk -- DUPLICATE --> drop["DROP PACKET"]:::drop
    seqChk -- NEW PACKET --> hopChk{"HOP COUNT\nOR TTL ZERO"}:::dec
    hopChk -- YES --> ttl["DROP PACKET"]:::drop
    hopChk -- NO --> ttlDec["DECREMENT TTL\nOR HOP COUNT"]:::proc
    ttlDec --> addCache["ADD SEQ NUMBER\nTO CACHE"]:::proc
    addCache --> fwdAll["FORWARD PACKET\nON ALL OUTGOING LINKS\nEXCEPT INCOMING ONE"]:::fwd
    fwdAll --> pktOut["OUTGOING COPIES"]:::io

    classDef io fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef fwd fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    classDef drop fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```

**Interpretation of the architecture:**
- The **Sequence Number Cache Check** is the heart of *controlled flooding*. It guarantees each packet is processed once.
- The **Hop Count / TTL Check** enforces the propagation radius and prevents the packet from circling the network forever.
- The **Forward-All-Except-Incoming** block is the canonical definition of flooding at each node.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define flooding. Mention any two of its disadvantages.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Flooding is a non-adaptive routing technique in which a packet received by an intermediate node is forwarded on **every outgoing link except the one on which the packet arrived**. The process continues recursively until the packet reaches the destination or its hop count is exhausted.

**Two disadvantages (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Enormous traffic generation:** In a network with $n$ nodes and $e$ edges, pure flooding can generate up to $2e$ packet transmissions per source packet, leading to congestion and packet loss.
2. **Duplicate packets and possible looping:** Without proper duplicate suppression (sequence numbers) or a hop-count limit, the same packet may circulate indefinitely, wasting bandwidth and node processing power.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*Differentiate between pure flooding and selective flooding.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Pure Flooding | Selective Flooding |
|---|---|---|
| **Forwarding rule** | Packet is sent on **all** outgoing links | Packet is sent only on selected links deemed "useful" |
| **Topology knowledge** | **None** required | Requires **directional / geographic / topological hints** |
| **Traffic volume** | Very high (close to $2e$) | Drastically lower |
| **Reliability** | Maximum (reaches all nodes) | May fail if "useful" direction is misjudged |

Selective flooding is a controlled variant of pure flooding where the node uses an intelligent criterion (e.g., geography, distance, or neighbor table) to forward only on a subset of links.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Describe the **flooding** routing technique in detail. Explain the **three main variations** of flooding with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A network has **8 nodes** arranged in a **balanced binary tree of depth 3**, with the root as the source. Calculate the **number of packet copies generated at hop 2** and the **total number of packet transmissions** under pure flooding. State the **two assumptions** made. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (1 mark):** Flooding is a non-adaptive routing strategy in which every incoming packet is transmitted on every outgoing link except the one on which it arrived.

**Three Main Variations (2 marks each):**

**1. Pure (Uncontrolled) Flooding:**
- No duplicate suppression, no hop count, no sequence number.
- Every node forwards to all neighbors except the one it heard the packet from.
- *Disadvantage:* Generates exponential traffic; possible infinite looping.

**2. Hop-Count Limited Flooding:**
- Each packet carries a hop counter (initially set to a maximum value, e.g., $H$).
- Every node decrements the counter before forwarding. If counter reaches zero, the packet is discarded.
- *Advantage:* Bounds the propagation radius and prevents looping.
- *Limitation:* If the limit is set below the network diameter, some destinations will be unreachable.

**3. Sequence-Number Controlled Flooding:**
- Source assigns a unique sequence number to each packet.
- Every intermediate node maintains a cache of recently seen sequence numbers.
- If a packet's sequence number is already in the cache, the node drops it; otherwise, it forwards the packet and adds the sequence number to its cache.
- *Advantage:* Eliminates duplicate forwarding and ensures a packet is processed only once per node.
- *Requirement:* Cache memory of size $(H+1) \times \vert E \vert$ in the worst case.

**Diagram (additional 1 mark for any one variation):**

Refer to the schematic in Section 4.1 of these notes.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Given:** Balanced binary tree, $\vert V \vert = 8$, depth = 3, source = root.

**Step 1 — Identify the degree.** 
The root has degree 2 (two children). Each non-leaf internal node has degree 3 (one parent + two children). For analytical simplicity, we model the **intermediate hop-1 nodes** as having effective degree $d = 2$ (their only new forward links are to their two children).

**Step 2 — Number of new packet copies at hop 2.**
At hop 1, the source forwards to 2 nodes. Each of those nodes forwards to 2 new children at hop 2.

$$
N_{\text{copies}}(2) = 2 \times 2 = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Total number of transmissions.**
A tree with $\vert V \vert$ nodes has $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges. Total transmissions under pure flooding = $2(\vert V \vert - 1)$.

$$
N_{\text{transmissions}} = 2(8 - 1) = 14
$$

**Step 4 — State the two assumptions (1 mark each):**
1. The network is treated as a **tree** (no cycles), so each undirected edge is traversed exactly twice (once in each direction).
2. The **sequence number cache** is assumed disabled for this calculation, so every node re-broadcasts the packet on every forward link (pure, uncontrolled flooding).

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Alternative Internal Choice]**
**(a)** Compare **flooding** with **distance-vector routing**. List **four advantages and two disadvantages** of flooding. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain **Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF)** as a controlled variant of flooding. State **one real-world protocol** that uses RPF. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Flooding | Distance-Vector |
|---|---|---|
| Routing type | Non-adaptive | Adaptive |
| Topology knowledge | Not required | Each node knows only neighbor distances |
| Optimal path | Yes (always finds shortest hop-count path) | Approximate, may have count-to-infinity |
| Convergence | One round (D hops) | Up to O(V) iterations |
| Bandwidth | Very high | Low |

**Four Advantages of Flooding (1 mark each, 4 marks total):**
1. **Simplicity:** No routing tables or topology information required at any node.
2. **Robustness:** Even if multiple links or nodes fail, as long as a path exists, the packet will reach the destination.
3. **Optimality in hop count:** The first arrival of a packet to the destination corresponds to the shortest path (in terms of hops).
4. **Always up-to-date:** Since no routing table is consulted, the packet propagation reflects the current network state at the moment of transmission.

**Two Disadvantages (1.5 marks total):**
1. **Bandwidth wastage:** A single source packet can generate up to $2 \vert E \vert$ transmissions, leading to severe congestion in large networks.
2. **Duplicate packets and processing overhead:** Even with sequence-number caching, intermediate nodes must inspect and decide on every incoming packet, increasing CPU and memory load.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) — Explanation (4 marks):**

In RPF, a node forwards a flood packet **only if the packet arrived on the same link that the node itself would use to reach the source**. If a packet arrives on any other link, the node treats it as a potential duplicate and discards (or selectively forwards) it.

This works because, in a properly routed network, the **best path back to the source** is unique (assuming symmetric routing). Therefore, only one copy of the packet — the one that arrived on the reverse of the source's path — is forwarded further. All other copies arriving on "side" links are duplicates that can be suppressed.

**Step-by-step at an intermediate node N:**
1. Receive packet P from neighbor X. P contains source address S.
2. Look up the routing table of N: the link that N uses to reach S.
3. If that link == the link to X, forward P on all other links (except back to X).
4. Else, drop P as a duplicate.

**Diagram (2 marks):**

```
   S -------- A -------- B -------- D
              |          |
              |          |
              C -------- E

Suppose S floods a packet to A.
- A receives on link S-A. A's route to S is via S-A. ✓ → Forward on A-B and A-C.
- B receives on A-B. B's route to S is via B-A-S, first hop is A. ✓ → Forward on B-D and B-E.
- C receives on A-C. C's route to S is via C-A-S, first hop is A. ✓ → Forward on C-E.
- D receives on B-D. D's route to S is via D-B-A-S, first hop is B. ✓ → Forward on others if any.
- E receives from C and from B. E's route to S is via E-C-A-S, first hop is C. The packet from B is dropped.
```

**Real-world protocol using RPF (1 mark):** **DVMRP (Distance Vector Multicast Routing Protocol)** uses RPF to forward multicast packets efficiently across the Internet. RPF is also used in the multicast forwarding tables of PIM (Protocol Independent Multicast) in its Sparse Mode.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing flooding with broadcast:** Flooding reaches *every node in the network graph*; broadcast reaches *every node on the local physical segment only*. A 1-mark deduction is common for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting the "except incoming link" rule:** If a student writes "flooding sends a packet on all links including the incoming one," the answer is **incorrect** — that is a 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Mis-stating complexity as $O(V)$ instead of $O(E)$:** The correct flood cost is $2 \vert E \vert$ transmissions, not $2 \vert V \vert$. The number of nodes is $\vert V \vert$ but the number of *directed link traversals* is $2 \vert E \vert$.
> 4. **Forgetting the source edge in tree analysis:** In a tree of $\vert V \vert$ nodes, students often write $\vert E \vert = \vert V \vert$, which is wrong. The correct relation is $\vert E \vert = \vert V \vert - 1$ for any tree.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flooding is a non-adaptive, table-free forwarding strategy** in which every incoming packet is sent on every outgoing link *except* the one it arrived on.
- **Pure flooding** is simple but traffic-explosive; **controlled flooding** uses hop count, sequence numbers, or RPF to make it practical.
- **Hop-count limited flooding** bounds propagation via a counter; **sequence-number controlled flooding** bounds duplicates via a cache; **Reverse Path Forwarding** bounds duplicates via routing-table lookup.
- **Worst-case transmissions on a tree:** $2(\vert V \vert - 1)$.
- **Worst-case transmissions on a complete mesh:** $\vert V \vert(\vert V \vert - 1)$.
- **Reachability condition:** $\text{TTL}_0 \ge D$, where $D$ is the network diameter.
- **Cache size for sequence-number flooding:** $(H_{\max} + 1) \cdot \vert E \vert$ entries.
- **Used in:** OSPF LSAs, AODV RREQ, DVMRP multicast, STP BPDUs, gossip protocols.
- **Always finds the shortest hop-count path** when one exists.
- **Disadvantages:** very high bandwidth use, duplicate processing, no scalability beyond small / controlled networks.
- **Advantage:** maximum robustness and zero routing-table dependency.
- **Key formula to remember:** $N_{\text{copies}}(h) = d \cdot (d-1)^{h-1}$ in a $d$-regular graph.
- **Key insight for the exam:** Flooding's "except incoming link" rule is what prevents the trivial 2-packet loop; the rest of its design is about *controlling* this rule to avoid exponential growth.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hierarchical Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hierarchical Routing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hierarchical Routing** is a scalable routing strategy in which the entire network is partitioned into a logical multi-level tree of regions (also called clusters, domains, or areas), and routing is performed independently at each level of the hierarchy. A router maintains detailed routing information only for destinations within its own region (**intra-region routing**) and aggregated summary information for destinations in other regions (**inter-region routing**). In a 2-level hierarchy: - **Level-0 (Local):** Routers within a single region know complete paths to every other router in the same region. - **Level-1 (Global):** For every other region, the router stores only **one entry** that points to the gateway router which leads out of the local region. In an **L-level** hierarchy, this concept is extended recursively: a region at level *i* is itself a member of a region at level *i+1*, and so on, up to the topmost level (often the global Internet level). ### 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Postal System Imagine you want to send a letter from your home in Kottayam to a friend in Kasaragod: - **Step 1 (Local):** You don't memorize the full street map of India. You just know the route from your house to the **local post office**. - **Step 2 (State Level):** The Kottayam post office knows how to send mail to the **Kerala state hub**. - **Step 3 (National Level):** The Kerala hub routes it to the **Karnataka hub** (since Kasaragod is the northern-most district and mail routes through the north). - **Step 4 (Destination):** The Karnataka hub routes it to the Kasaragod local post office, which then delivers it to your friend's house. At every stage, the post office only knows the **next hop** at the appropriate level of detail — never the complete end-to-end path. This is exactly how **Hierarchical Routing** works in computer networks! > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology for KTU 2024:** > - **Region (Cluster):** A logical grouping of routers treated as a single unit at the next higher level. > - **Gateway Router:** A router that lies on the boundary of a region and is responsible for forwarding inter-region traffic. > - **Intra-region Routing:** Routing decisions made *within* a single region. > - **Inter-region Routing:** Routing decisions made *between* regions. > - **Aggregation:** The process of collapsing multiple detailed routes into a single summary route at a higher level. ### 1.3 Why Hierarchical Routing? — The Scalability Crisis In **flat routing** (non-hierarchical), every router must know about every other router in the network. | Network Type | Routers (N) | Flat Table Size | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Small Campus LAN | 10 | **10** entries | | Mid-size ISP | 1,000 | **1,000** entries | | Global Internet Backbone | ~1,000,000+ | **1,000,000+** entries | > [!IMPORTANT] > A single routing table entry typically requires **32 bytes** (IPv4) or **64 bytes** (IPv6). Storing **1 million** entries would require **32 MB** of memory per router — clearly infeasible. Hierarchical Routing solves this by reducing the table size from **O(N)** to **O(√N)** for a 2-level hierarchy, and to **O(k · N^(1/k))** for a *k*-level hierarchy. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown A packet traversing a hierarchically routed network goes through a **two-step forwarding process** at every router: 1. **Step 1 — Destination Match Check:** The router checks the destination IP address of the packet against its routing table. 2. **Step 2 — Level-Appropriate Forwarding:** - If the destination lies **within the same region** → the router uses its **detailed intra-region** table. - If the destination lies **outside the region** → the router forwards the packet to the **gateway router** of its region using the **inter-region** table entry. **Key Routing Rules (Memorize for KTU):** - A router has **detailed knowledge** of only the routers in its *own* region. - A router has **one aggregated entry per other region** at the highest hierarchy level. - The path chosen by inter-region routing is **not always the globally optimal (shortest) path** — this is called the **sub-optimality trade-off**. ### 2.2 Real-World Application: The Internet Architecture The Internet itself is the largest example of hierarchical routing: | Level | Entity | Routing Protocol | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 (Top) | Internet Backbone (Tier-1 ISPs) | **BGP** (Border Gateway Protocol) | | 2 (Mid) | Autonomous System (AS) | **OSPF**, **IS-IS**, **EIGRP** (intra-AS) | | 3 (Within AS) | OSPF Areas (Backbone, Stub, etc.) | **OSPF** with Area Border Routers (ABRs) | | 4 (Within Area) | Individual Subnet / LAN | **Static**, **RIP**, or direct routing | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Real-World Mapping:** The Border Gateway Router (BGP) between Autonomous Systems is exactly the **gateway router** of Hierarchical Routing theory. The OSPF Area Border Router (ABR) plays the same role between OSPF areas. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following table is the **definitive cheat sheet** for solving KTU numerical problems on Hierarchical Routing. All quantities are dimensionless integers. | Symbol | Meaning | Mathematical Expression | Constraint | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $N$ | Total number of routers in the network | $N \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ | Given | | $k$ | Number of hierarchy levels | $k \in \mathbb{Z}^+,\; k \geq 1$ | $k = 1$ implies flat routing | | $L_{flat}$ | Routing table size in flat routing | $L_{flat} = N$ | Baseline for comparison | | $L_{2}$ | Routing table size in 2-level hierarchy | $L_{2} = \dfrac{N}{K} + K - 1$ | $K$ = number of regions | | $L_{k}$ | Routing table size in $k$-level hierarchy | $L_{k} = k \cdot N^{1/k} - 1$ | Optimal balanced design | | $K_{opt}^{(2)}$ | Optimal region count (2-level) | $K_{opt}^{(2)} = \sqrt{N}$ | Minimizes $L_{2}$ | | $K_{opt}^{(2)\;size}$ | Optimal cluster size (2-level) | $K_{opt}^{(2)\;size} = \sqrt{N}$ | Equals $K_{opt}^{(2)}$ | | $L_{min}^{(2)}$ | Minimum table size (2-level) | $L_{min}^{(2)} = 2\sqrt{N} - 1$ | Achieved at $K = \sqrt{N}$ | | $k_{opt}$ | Optimal number of levels | $k_{opt} = \ln(N)$ | Minimizes $L_{k}$ | | $b_{opt}$ | Optimal branching factor | $b_{opt} = e \approx 3$ | Achieved at $k = \ln(N)$ | | $L_{min}^{(opt)}$ | Global minimum table size | $L_{min}^{(opt)} = e \cdot \ln(N) - 1$ | Theoretical lower bound | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** The optimal branching factor of **e ≈ 3** is the famous result. If a question asks for the "ideal" number of levels, just remember: **branching factor of 3** is the engineering optimum. This is why real-world protocols like **OSPF** use a 2-level (or 3-level) area design with reasonably small areas. ### 2.4 Engineering Trade-Offs (Must-Know for KTU Essays) | Aspect | Flat Routing | Hierarchical Routing | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Table Size | $O(N)$ — grows linearly | $O(\sqrt{N})$ to $O(\ln N)$ — sub-linear | | Optimality | **Always** finds the shortest path | **Sometimes** sub-optimal (inter-region detour) | | Scalability | Poor (fails for large N) | Excellent (handles millions of nodes) | | Administrative Overhead | Low | High (requires region design) | | Failure Localization | Hard | Easy (failure contained to a region) | | Memory/Processing | High | Low | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Region Count for 2-Level Hierarchical Routing > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement:** Given $N$ routers to be grouped into $K$ regions for a 2-level hierarchical routing scheme, find the value of $K$ that minimizes the routing table size per router, and the corresponding minimum table size. **Step 1 — Count Intra-Region Entries** Each region contains $N/K$ routers. For any given router, the number of *other* routers within the same region is: $$ \text{Intra-region entries} = \frac{N}{K} - 1 $$ **Step 2 — Count Inter-Region Entries** There are $K - 1$ other regions outside the current one. Since each region is represented by a single aggregated entry in the routing table: $$ \text{Inter-region entries} = K - 1 $$ **Step 3 — Total Routing Table Size** $$ L_{2}(K) = \left(\frac{N}{K} - 1\right) + (K - 1) = \frac{N}{K} + K - 2 + 1 $$ Adding the "+1" accounts for the default gateway/self-region entry. Simplifying: $$ L_{2}(K) = \frac{N}{K} + K - 1 $$ **Step 4 — Minimize the Function** Differentiate $L_{2}(K)$ with respect to $K$ and set equal to zero: $$ \frac{dL_{2}}{dK} = -\frac{N}{K^{2}} + 1 = 0 $$ Solving for $K$: $$ K^{2} = N \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = \sqrt{N} $$ **Step 5 — Verify the Minimum (Second Derivative Test)** $$ \frac{d^{2}L_{2}}{dK^{2}} = \frac{2N}{K^{3}} > 0 \;\; \text{for all } K > 0 $$ The second derivative is positive → confirmed **global minimum**. **Step 6 — Substitute Back to Get Minimum Table Size** $$ L_{min}^{(2)} = \frac{N}{\sqrt{N}} + \sqrt{N} - 1 = \sqrt{N} + \sqrt{N} - 1 $$ $$ \boxed{L_{min}^{(2)} = 2\sqrt{N} - 1} $$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Optimal Hierarchy Depth (k-Level Generalization) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** Generalize the above for $k$ hierarchy levels and find the optimal $k$ that minimizes the table size. **Step 1 — General Form of Table Size** For a $k$-level hierarchy with optimal balanced design where each level has a branching factor $b$: $$ N = b^{k} \quad \Rightarrow \quad b = N^{1/k} $$ The routing table contains entries for each level (intra-group entries at the bottom level, plus one entry per group at each of the $k - 1$ higher levels): $$ L_{k}(b) = (b - 1) + (b - 1) + \cdots + (b - 1) = k(b - 1) = k\left(N^{1/k} - 1\right) $$ $$ \boxed{L_{k} \approx k \cdot N^{1/k} - k} $$ **Step 2 — Optimize the Number of Levels** To find the optimal $k$, treat $k$ as a continuous variable and differentiate $L(k) = k \cdot N^{1/k}$ with respect to $k$: $$ \frac{dL}{dk} = N^{1/k} + k \cdot N^{1/k} \cdot \ln(N) \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{k^{2}}\right) $$ $$ \frac{dL}{dk} = N^{1/k} \left[1 - \frac{\ln(N)}{k}\right] $$ Setting $\dfrac{dL}{dk} = 0$: $$ 1 - \frac{\ln(N)}{k} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_{opt} = \ln(N) $$ **Step 3 — Optimal Branching Factor** Substituting $k_{opt} = \ln(N)$ back into $b = N^{1/k}$: $$ b_{opt} = N^{1/\ln(N)} = e^{\ln(N)/\ln(N)} = e $$ $$ \boxed{b_{opt} = e \approx 2.718 \approx 3} $$ **Step 4 — Global Minimum Table Size** $$ L_{min}^{(opt)} = e \cdot \ln(N) - 1 $$ For example, if $N = 100{,}000$ routers: - $L_{flat} = 100{,}000$ entries - $L_{min}^{(2)} = 2\sqrt{100{,}000} - 1 \approx 632$ entries - $L_{min}^{(opt)} = e \cdot \ln(100{,}000) - 1 \approx 2.718 \cdot 11.51 - 1 \approx 30$ entries This is a **3,300× reduction** in table size — the power of hierarchical routing. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Question:** Consider a network with $N = 2025$ routers using 2-level hierarchical routing. **(a) Find the optimal number of regions and cluster size.** $$ K_{opt} = \sqrt{N} = \sqrt{2025} = 45 $$ Each region contains $N/K = 2025/45 = 45$ routers. **Symmetric result** — the cluster size equals the number of regions. **(b) Calculate the minimum routing table size per router.** $$ L_{min}^{(2)} = 2\sqrt{N} - 1 = 2(45) - 1 = 89 \text{ entries} $$ **Comparison with flat routing:** $L_{flat} = 2025$ entries → **22.7× reduction**. **(c) If we now use 3-level hierarchy, what is the new table size?** Since $N = 2025 = 45^2 = (3^4)(5^2) = 3^4 \cdot 5^2$, and $b_{opt} = e$, the nearest integer branching is $b = 3$. So $3^4 = 81 \neq 2025$. We instead use a non-uniform tree; assume $b_1 = b_2 = b_3 = 3^{4/3} \approx 4.3$. Using the formula: $L_3 = 3 \cdot N^{1/3} - 3 = 3 \cdot \sqrt[3]{2025} - 3 = 3(12.65) - 3 = 35$ entries. **Comparison:** 3-level gives **35 entries** vs. 2-level's **89 entries** — a further **2.5× reduction** by adding one more level. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for KTU Problems ``` START: Given N routers and hierarchy level k | |---> Compute b = N^(1/k) [branching factor] | |---> Compute L_k = k * (b - 1) [table size] | |---> Is L_k minimal? | |---> YES → Optimal hierarchy found | |---> NO → Try k' = k ± 1 and recompute | |---> Verify with k_opt = ln(N), b_opt = e | END: Report N, k, b, L_k ``` ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Two-Level Hierarchical Routing Architecture The following diagram illustrates a 2-level hierarchical routing topology with **3 regions (A, B, C)**, each containing a gateway router and several internal routers. Inter-region traffic is exchanged only between gateway routers. ```mermaid graph TB InternetCloud[Global Internet Backbone Level 1] subgraph RegionA[REGION A - Local Level 0] R1A[Router R1 Gateway of A] R2A[Router R2] R3A[Router R3] R4A[Router R4] end subgraph RegionB[REGION B - Local Level 0] R1B[Router R5 Gateway of B] R2B[Router R6] R3B[Router R7] R4B[Router R8] end subgraph RegionC[REGION C - Local Level 0] R1C[Router R9 Gateway of C] R2C[Router R10] R3C[Router R11] R4C[Router R12] end InternetCloud --- R1A InternetCloud --- R1B InternetCloud --- R1C R1A --- R2A R1A --- R3A R1A --- R4A R1A -. Inter Region Link .-> R1B R1B -. Inter Region Link .-> R1C R1B --- R2B R1B --- R3B R1B --- R4B R1C --- R2C R1C --- R3C R1C --- R4C ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Solid lines** (e.g., `R1A --- R2A`) represent **intra-region** links — these routers know each other's detailed paths. - **Dotted lines** (e.g., `R1A -.-> R1B`) represent **inter-region gateway links** — R1A, R1B, and R1C each store only **one aggregated entry** for the other regions. - The **Internet Cloud** represents the next higher hierarchy level (e.g., the Tier-1 ISP backbone). --- ### 4.2 Routing Table Structure for a Router in Region A The following block diagram shows the logical layout of the routing table inside **R1A** (the gateway of Region A). ```mermaid graph LR RT[R1A Routing Table] --> Intra[Intra-Region Block] RT --> Inter[Inter-Region Block] Intra --> Entry1[Entry 1: R2A via direct] Intra --> Entry2[Entry 2: R3A via direct] Intra --> Entry3[Entry 3: R4A via direct] Inter --> InterB[Entry 4: Region B via R1A to R1B] Inter --> InterC[Entry 5: Region C via R1A to R1B to R1C] Inter --> InterW[Entry 6: Outside World via Tier 1 ISP] ``` **Key Observation:** Only **3 detailed entries** for Region A's internal routers, but only **3 aggregated entries** for all external destinations. Without hierarchical routing, R1A would have needed **11 entries** (one for every other router). Hierarchical routing reduces this to **6 entries** — a **45% reduction** for this small example, scaling dramatically for larger networks. --- ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Hierarchical Packet Forwarding This schematic shows the *decision-making flow* inside a router when it receives a packet in a hierarchical routing scheme. ```mermaid graph TD Pkt[Incoming Packet with Destination IP] --> Lookup{Routing Table Lookup} Lookup --> Match{Destination IP matches
an entry?} Match --> NoMatch[Packet Dropped
ICMP Destination Unreachable] Match --> YesMatch{Is destination in
same region?} YesMatch --> IntraPath[Use Intra-Region Entry
Direct Shortest Path] YesMatch --> InterPath[Use Inter-Region Entry
Forward to Gateway] IntraPath --> Forward1[Forward to Next Hop
within Region] InterPath --> Gateway[Send to Local Gateway Router] Gateway --> Transit[Gateway forwards packet
up the hierarchy tree] Transit --> DestRegion[Packet reaches destination region] DestRegion --> FinalHop[Final intra-region delivery] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Functional Insight:** The router performs a **two-stage lookup** in hierarchical routing. This is why modern routers use **TCAM (Ternary Content Addressable Memory)** for fast longest-prefix-match lookups, ensuring that the two-stage decision is completed in microseconds even with millions of routes. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** > **Define Hierarchical Routing. Why is it preferred over flat routing in large networks?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Hierarchical Routing is a routing strategy in which routers are organized into regions, and routing is performed at multiple levels of the hierarchy. Each router maintains detailed routing information for routers within its own region and aggregated summary information for routers in other regions. It is preferred over flat routing in large networks because flat routing requires every router to store an entry for every other router, leading to routing tables of size **O(N)**. In contrast, hierarchical routing reduces the table size to **O(√N)** for 2-level or **O(ln N)** for optimal design, providing excellent scalability, faster lookups, and lower memory consumption. *(3 marks — Full marks for clear mention of O(N) vs O(√N) reduction.)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** > **Distinguish between intra-region and inter-region routing.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Intra-Region Routing | Inter-Region Routing | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Scope | Within a single region | Between different regions | | Table Granularity | Detailed — one entry per destination router | Aggregated — one entry per remote region | | Path Optimality | Always shortest within the region | May be sub-optimal (regional aggregation forces detours) | | Example | OSPF within an OSPF Area | BGP between two Autonomous Systems | *(3 marks — awarded for clear distinction, ideally with a real-world example.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one** of the two questions below. Each question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **Hierarchical Routing** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its **advantages and disadvantages** over flat routing. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Derive the expression for the **optimal number of regions** and the **minimum routing table size** in a 2-level hierarchical routing scheme for a network with $N$ routers. *(7 marks)* --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Concept of Hierarchical Routing** *(7 marks — distribution: 2 marks concept, 2 marks diagram, 3 marks advantages/disadvantages)* Hierarchical routing is a scalable routing strategy where the network is logically divided into regions. Each router maintains: - **Detailed intra-region** routing table for routers within its own region. - **Aggregated inter-region** routing table with one entry per remote region. A typical 2-level hierarchy consists of a *local level* (intra-region) and a *global level* (inter-region), with **gateway routers** at the boundary of each region. **Block Diagram:** ``` ┌─────────── Level 1 (Global) ───────────┐ │ [Region A] [Region B] [Region C] │ └─────▲───────────▲───────────▲───────────┘ │ │ │ ┌─────▼─────┐ ┌───▼─────┐ ┌───▼─────┐ │ Gateway A │ │Gateway B│ │Gateway C│ Level 0 │ R1, R2, │ │ R5, R6, │ │ R9, R10,│ (Local) │ R3, R4 │ │ R7, R8 │ │ R11,R12 │ └───────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ ``` **Advantages:** 1. **Scalability:** Table size grows as **O(√N)** instead of O(N) — handles millions of routers. 2. **Reduced Memory and CPU Load:** Fewer entries → faster lookups, less processing. 3. **Failure Isolation:** A failure in one region does not require global routing updates. 4. **Administrative Autonomy:** Each region can run its own internal routing protocol. **Disadvantages:** 1. **Sub-optimal Paths:** Aggregation forces packets to take detours, e.g., a packet from Region A → Region C may go via Region B's gateway even if a direct A → C link exists. 2. **Complex Design:** Requires careful region planning and gateway placement. 3. **Slower Convergence:** Hierarchical protocols may converge slower than flat protocols like OSPF. --- **(b) Derivation of Optimal Region Count** *(7 marks — distribution: 2 marks setup, 2 marks differentiation, 1 mark verification, 2 marks final result)* For $N$ routers divided into $K$ regions of size $N/K$ each: $$ L_{2}(K) = \underbrace{\left(\frac{N}{K} - 1\right)}_{\text{intra-region}} + \underbrace{(K - 1)}_{\text{inter-region}} $$ $$ L_{2}(K) = \frac{N}{K} + K - 2 $$ Differentiating and setting to zero: $$ \frac{dL_{2}}{dK} = -\frac{N}{K^{2}} + 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = \sqrt{N} $$ Second derivative check: $$ \frac{d^{2}L_{2}}{dK^{2}} = \frac{2N}{K^{3}} > 0 \;\; (\text{confirms minimum}) $$ Minimum routing table size: $$ L_{min}^{(2)} = \frac{N}{\sqrt{N}} + \sqrt{N} - 2 = 2\sqrt{N} - 2 $$ $$ \boxed{K_{opt} = \sqrt{N}, \quad L_{min}^{(2)} = 2\sqrt{N} - 2} $$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Option** > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 3]** **(a)** Compare **flat routing and hierarchical routing**. Explain **intra-region and inter-region routing** with a suitable real-world example. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A network has $N = 640$ routers. Using **hierarchical routing**, calculate: *(7 marks)* 1. The **optimal cluster size** and **minimum routing table size** for a 2-level hierarchy. 2. The **optimal routing table size** for a 3-level hierarchy. 3. The **global optimum** number of levels and the corresponding minimum table size. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison and Real-World Example** *(7 marks — distribution: 3 marks comparison, 2 marks intra/inter-region, 2 marks example)* | Parameter | Flat Routing | Hierarchical Routing | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Table size | $O(N)$ | $O(\sqrt{N})$ to $O(\ln N)$ | | Scalability | Limited | Excellent | | Optimality | Always shortest path | Sometimes sub-optimal | | Memory | High | Low | | Update overhead | Global updates | Regional updates | **Real-World Example — The Internet:** The Internet uses a 3-level hierarchical structure: - **Level 1 (Tier-1 ISPs):** Routers at the global backbone level exchange aggregated routes using **BGP**. - **Level 2 (Autonomous Systems / OSPF Areas):** Each ISP runs **OSPF** internally, with each AS divided into OSPF areas. **Area Border Routers (ABRs)** act as gateway routers. - **Level 3 (Within an Area):** Individual routers and subnets, with detailed intra-area routing tables. **Intra-region routing** happens within an OSPF area — routers know complete LSAs (Link-State Advertisements) for their area. **Inter-region routing** happens between areas — only summary LSA Type-3 routes are exchanged via ABRs. --- **(b) Numerical Solution for N = 640** *(7 marks — distribution: 2 + 2 + 3 marks for the three sub-parts)* **Sub-part (i): 2-Level Hierarchy** $$ K_{opt} = \sqrt{N} = \sqrt{640} \approx 25.30 $$ Take $K = 25$ regions, each containing $640/25 = 25.6 \approx 25$ routers. $$ L_{min}^{(2)} = \frac{N}{K} + K - 2 = 25 + 25 - 2 = 48 \text{ entries} $$ *(Equivalently, using the exact formula: $2\sqrt{640} - 2 \approx 48.6$ → **48 entries** after rounding.)* **Sub-part (ii): 3-Level Hierarchy** For 3-level hierarchy, the optimal branching factor is $b = N^{1/3}$: $$ b = \sqrt[3]{640} \approx 8.6 $$ Choosing a balanced design with $b = 8$ at the bottom and middle levels (since $8 \times 8 \times 10 = 640$ — one level has 10 groups): $$ L_{3} = 3 \cdot N^{1/3} - 3 = 3(8.6) - 3 \approx 22.8 \rightarrow 23 \text{ entries} $$ **Sub-part (iii): Global Optimum** $$ k_{opt} = \ln(N) = \ln(640) \approx 6.46 $$ So the optimal number of levels is **6 or 7** (round to integer). With $k = 6$, branching factor $b = 640^{1/6} \approx 2.94 \approx 3$. $$ L_{min}^{(opt)} = 6 \cdot 3 - 1 = 17 \text{ entries} $$ **Comparison Table for N = 640:** | Hierarchy | $k$ | $b$ | $L_k$ entries | Reduction vs. Flat | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Flat | 1 | — | 640 | 1× | | 2-Level | 2 | 25 | 48 | 13.3× | | 3-Level | 3 | 8 | 23 | 27.8× | | **6-Level (Optimal)** | **6** | **3** | **17** | **37.6×** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Lose 1–2 marks here):** > 1. **Forgetting the "−1" or "−2" correction term** in $L_{2}(K) = N/K + K - 1$ — this off-by-one error is the most common mistake. > 2. **Confusing $K$ (number of regions) with $N/K$ (cluster size)** — they are equal only at the optimum $K = \sqrt{N}$, but students often write the same value for both without checking. > 3. **Not verifying the second derivative** in derivation questions — KTU examiners explicitly allocate marks for the *verification step*. > 4. **Ignoring the sub-optimality trade-off** in essay questions — always mention that inter-region paths may not be globally shortest. > 5. **Rounding errors** with $e \approx 3$ — show both the exact value and the integer approximation in your answer. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hierarchical Routing (Module 3)** - [x] **Core Idea:** Group routers into regions; detailed routing within regions, aggregated routing between regions. - [x] **Key Formula (2-level):** $L_{2} = \dfrac{N}{K} + K - 1$ - [x] **Optimal 2-level design:** $K_{opt} = \sqrt{N}$, giving $L_{min}^{(2)} = 2\sqrt{N} - 1$ - [x] **General k-level formula:** $L_{k} \approx k \cdot N^{1/k}$ - [x] **Global optimum levels:** $k_{opt} = \ln(N)$ - [x] **Optimal branching factor:** $b_{opt} = e \approx 3$ (this is the famous engineering result!) - [x] **Gateway Router:** Border router of a region; routes inter-region traffic. - [x] **Two-Stage Lookup:** First check intra-region, then inter-region. - [x] **Real-World Mapping:** Internet (BGP) ↔ OSPF Areas (ABR) ↔ Subnets — a 3-level hierarchy. - [x] **Major Trade-off:** Scalability vs. Path Optimality — hierarchical routing scales beautifully but may not always use the globally shortest path. - [x] **Memory Trick:** "The Three is the Magic Number" — the optimal tree has a branching factor of approximately 3. - [x] **Common Exam Trap:** Flat routing is $O(N)$, not $O(1)$. Be precise with big-O notation in derivations.

topics.title: Broadcast
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Broadcast # Module 3: Broadcast — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Broadcast** is a one-to-*all* communication method in which a single source node transmits a single data packet that is **delivered to every node** within a defined logical boundary called a **Broadcast Domain**, without the need for unicast replication at the application layer. In the TCP/IP protocol suite, broadcast is implemented at two distinct layers of the OSI model: | Layer | Broadcast Mechanism | Address Used | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** | Ethernet MAC broadcast | `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` | | **Network Layer (Layer 3)** | IP broadcast | `255.255.255.255` (limited) or network-specific directed broadcast | A **Broadcast Domain** is the set of all devices that will receive a broadcast frame originating from **any** device within that set. By default in IPv4, **routers separate broadcast domains**, while switches, hubs, and bridges extend them. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** A *unicast* targets **one** device, a *multicast* targets a **subscribed group**, and a *broadcast* targets **everyone** in the domain. This "audience" difference is the most heavily tested KTU concept in this module. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Think of broadcast like a **loudspeaker announcement in a school assembly hall**: - The principal (source) speaks once into the microphone. - Every student, teacher, and staff member in the hall hears the message. - People in a *different* building (separated by a thick wall — acting like a router) **do not** hear the announcement, even though it was broadcast in the original building. - Even students who don't care about the announcement still receive it; they simply choose to ignore it (just as a network interface discards frames not addressed to its unicast or joined multicast address). This analogy maps perfectly to networking: - **Hall** → Broadcast Domain - **Thick wall** → Router - **Microphone signal** → Broadcast frame/packet - **Students who ignore** → NIC filtering at MAC layer ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Ethernet Broadcast MAC Address:** `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` (48 bits, all ones) - **IPv4 Limited Broadcast:** `255.255.255.255` - **IPv4 Directed Broadcast:** `.<11111111.11111111.11111111>` (host portion all ones) - **IEEE 802.3 Minimum Frame Size:** **64 bytes** (relevant for collision/broadcast integrity) - **IEEE 802.3 Maximum Frame Size:** **1518 bytes** (standard Ethernet MTU) - **IPv6:** **No broadcast exists** — replaced entirely by multicast (e.g., `ff02::1` = all-nodes link-local) > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently test that **IPv6 eliminates broadcast** and uses multicast (`ff02::1`) for one-to-all communication. This is a guaranteed 2-3 mark question in Part A. # Module 3: Broadcast — Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Broadcast (Hierarchical Breakdown) ### 2.1.1 Layer 2 (MAC) Broadcast - Address: `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` - Forwarded by: **Switches, Hubs, Bridges** - **NOT forwarded by: Routers** - Used by protocols: ARP, DHCP Discovery, NetBIOS ### 2.1.2 Layer 3 (IP) Broadcast — Three Sub-Types | Type | Address | Scope | Routed? | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Limited Broadcast** | `255.255.255.255` | Local physical subnet only | **No** (never forwarded) | | **Directed Broadcast (Net-directed)** | `.255` | All hosts in a specific remote network | **Yes** (by default disabled on modern routers) | | **Subnet-directed Broadcast** | `.255` | All hosts in a specific subnet | **Yes** (subnet-aware routers) | ## 2.2 Why Broadcast Exists — The Engineering Rationale Broadcast solves three critical problems in network bootstrapping: 1. **Address Discovery (ARP):** *"Who has IP 192.168.1.5? Tell 192.168.1.10"* — must reach all hosts to find the right MAC. 2. **Service Advertisement (DHCP):** New device joins the network, has no IP, needs to find a DHCP server — sends `DHCPDISCOVER` to `255.255.255.255`. 3. **Group Notifications (Routing Protocols):** OSPF uses `224.0.0.5` (multicast, conceptually similar), and legacy protocols used broadcast hellos. ## 2.3 Broadcast Domain vs. Collision Domain This is the **most repeated KTU comparison table** for this topic: | Property | Collision Domain | Broadcast Domain | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition** | Set of devices whose frames can collide | Set of devices that receive each other's broadcasts | | **Separated by** | Switch, Bridge, Router | **Router only** | | **Extended by** | Hub, Repeater | Hub, Bridge, Switch | | **Impact of extension** | More collisions, lower throughput | More broadcast traffic, risk of storms | | **Modern relevance** | Largely eliminated by full-duplex switches | Still critical for VLAN design | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Golden Rule:** A **Layer 2 switch creates a separate collision domain per port** but **a single broadcast domain across all ports** — unless VLANs are configured. This distinction is heavily tested in Part B (14-mark) questions. ## 2.4 Broadcast Storm — Failure Mode Analysis A **Broadcast Storm** is a catastrophic network condition where broadcast frames multiply exponentially due to switching loops or malfunctioning NICs. **The mathematics of broadcast storms (relevant for KTU problem-solving):** If a single broadcast packet is received by $N$ switches in a loop, and each switch forwards it out $K$ ports (excluding ingress), the number of copies after $t$ iterations is: $$B(t) = N \cdot K^{t}$$ > **Example:** A 10-switch ring topology where each switch has 2 forwarding ports: > $$B(5) = 10 \cdot 2^{5} = 320 \text{ copies after 5 iterations}$$ This **exponential growth** consumes 100% of link bandwidth within milliseconds, causing complete network failure. The solution is **Spanning Tree Protocol (STP / IEEE 802.1D)**, which logically blocks redundant paths. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Rule | Expression | Engineering Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Number of usable hosts per subnet | $H = 2^{h} - 2$ | Where $h$ = host bits | | Directed broadcast address | $D = \text{Network ID} + (2^{h}-1)$ | Last address in subnet | | Subnet mask for broadcast detection | All host bits = 1 | Identifies broadcast address | | Broadcast frame copies in storm | $B(t) = N \cdot K^{t}$ | Network capacity planning | | ARP broadcast frequency | $f_{ARP} = \text{per new destination}$ | Caching reduces this | | Bandwidth consumed by storm | $\text{MTU} \times B(t) / \Delta t$ | Estimating collapse time | | Switch broadcast forwarding | $F_{broadcast} = 1 \text{ inbound} \rightarrow N_{ports} - 1 \text{ outbound}$ | Flooding behavior | | IPv6 replacement | $\text{Broadcast} \rightarrow \text{Multicast } ff02::1$ | All-nodes link-local | > **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** In KTU answer sheets, use `\vert` instead of `$\vert x \vert$` vertical bars when typing modular expressions, and ensure no raw `|` characters appear inside markdown tables. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Enterprise Network Design:** VLANs are designed specifically to limit broadcast domain size, typically keeping broadcast traffic below **2% of total link bandwidth** (Cisco best practice). - **Data Center Architecture:** Modern leaf-spine designs use **VXLAN** to create overlay broadcast domains over an underlay that natively handles only unicast. - **IoT Networks:** Low-power protocols like **Zigbee** use their own broadcast (`0xFFFC` APS broadcast address) with limited TTL to prevent mesh-wide flooding. - **5G Core:** Network function discovery uses **SBI (Service-Based Interface)** broadcast/multicast via NRF (Network Repository Function). # Module 3: Broadcast — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Computing a Directed Broadcast Address **Given (Standard KTU Problem Statement):** An IPv4 network uses the address block `192.168.10.0` with subnet mask `255.255.255.240`. Find: (a) The network address (b) The directed broadcast address (c) The number of usable host addresses (d) Whether `192.168.10.15` is a broadcast address ### Step 1 — Convert subnet mask to binary The mask `255.255.255.240` in binary is: ``` 11111111.11111111.11111111.11110000 ``` Count the ones: $8 + 8 + 8 + 4 = 28$ ones → **/28 prefix** Therefore, host bits $h = 32 - 28 = 4$ bits. ### Step 2 — Compute the Network Address (logical AND of IP and mask) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Network} &= \text{IP} \;\text{AND}\; \text{Mask} \\ &= 192.168.10.0 \;\text{AND}\; 255.255.255.240 \\ &= 192.168.10.0 \end{aligned} $$ This is straightforward because the IP and mask align on a subnet boundary. ### Step 3 — Compute the Directed Broadcast Address The directed broadcast address sets **all host bits to 1** while keeping network bits intact: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Broadcast} &= \text{Network} \;\text{OR}\; (\text{all host bits} = 1) \\ &= 192.168.10.0000\;\text{(network)} \;\text{OR}\; 0000.1111 \\ &= 192.168.10.0000\;\text{OR}\; 0000.1111 \\ &= 192.168.10.\;\underbrace{00001111}_{= 15} \\ &= 192.168.10.15 \end{aligned} $$ > **Answer (a):** Network Address = `192.168.10.0` > **Answer (b):** Directed Broadcast = `192.168.10.15` > **Answer (d):** Yes, `192.168.10.15` is the directed broadcast address. ### Step 4 — Number of Usable Host Addresses $$ \begin{aligned} H_{\text{total}} &= 2^{h} = 2^{4} = 16 \\ H_{\text{usable}} &= 2^{h} - 2 = 16 - 2 = 14 \end{aligned} $$ The two reserved addresses are: - **Network address:** `192.168.10.0` (host bits = 0) - **Broadcast address:** `192.168.10.15` (host bits = 1) > **Answer (c):** 14 usable host addresses. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Broadcast Scenarios | Scenario | Source Action | Destination Address | Routed? | Reaches | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Local ARP request | Host sends ARP | `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` (L2) / `255.255.255.255` (L3) | No | All in local subnet | | DHCP Discover | Client has no IP | `255.255.255.255` | No (relay can forward) | Local subnet only | | Ping to subnet broadcast | Admin testing | `.255` | Depends on router config | All in that subnet | | OSPF Hello | Router adj. setup | `224.0.0.5` (multicast) | Limited TTL=1 | OSPF-speaking routers | | IPv6 Neighbor Solicitation | Address resolution | `ff02::1:ffXX:XXXX` (solicited-node multicast) | No | Local link only | | IPv6 All-Nodes | Router advertisement | `ff02::1` | No (link-local) | All IPv6 nodes on link | ## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough: Broadcast Storm Math **Problem (KTU 14-mark style):** A network has 5 switches connected in a physical ring. Each switch has 3 ports used in the ring (uplink to 2 neighbors, 1 unused). A single broadcast frame enters Switch 1 at $t = 0$. Assuming no STP is running, calculate the number of broadcast frames in the network after 8 propagation iterations. **Step 1 — Identify parameters** - Number of switches in the loop: $N = 5$ - Each switch forwards out: $K = 2$ ports (ring neighbors, excluding ingress) - Time iterations: $t = 8$ **Step 2 — Apply the storm growth formula** $$ \begin{aligned} B(t) &= N \cdot K^{t} \\ B(8) &= 5 \cdot 2^{8} \\ &= 5 \cdot 256 \\ &= 1280 \text{ broadcast frames} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Compute bandwidth consumption** If each frame is the standard Ethernet maximum **1518 bytes**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total bytes} &= 1280 \times 1518 = 1{,}943{,}040 \text{ bytes} \\ &= \frac{1{,}943{,}040 \times 8}{10^{6}} \text{ Mbit} \\ &\approx 15.54 \text{ Mbit of broadcast data} \end{aligned} $$ On a 100 Mbps link, this single broadcast event would consume $\frac{15.54}{100} = 15.5\%$ of the link capacity in just 8 iterations — illustrating why STP is **mandatory** in switched networks. > [!NOTE] > This is a high-yield derivation type. KTU examiners love asking students to compute broadcast amplification in looped topologies. ## 3.4 Broadcast Address Recognition Rule (For Fast Problem Solving) **Algorithm a student can apply mentally during the exam:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{IsBroadcast}(IP) &= \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if } (IP \;\text{AND}\; \overline{Mask}) = \overline{Mask} \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \end{aligned} $$ **Worked example:** Is `10.0.5.63` broadcast on subnet `10.0.5.0/26`? - Mask `/26` = `255.255.255.192` = `...11\;000000` (6 host bits) - Inverse mask (host bits) = `0.0.0.63` - Test: `10.0.5.63 AND 0.0.0.63 = 0.0.0.63` ✓ - **Therefore, `10.0.5.63` IS the directed broadcast.** # Module 3: Broadcast — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Broadcast Domain Architecture (Single Domain) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BD1[BROADCAST DOMAIN 1 — Subnet 192.168.1.0/24] direction TB H1[Host A
192.168.1.10]:::host H2[Host B
192.168.1.11]:::host H3[Host C
192.168.1.12]:::host H4[Host D
192.168.1.13]:::host SW1[Layer 2 Switch
Floods Broadcasts]:::switch end H1 --- SW1 H2 --- SW1 H3 --- SW1 H4 --- SW1 R1[Router
SEPARATES domains]:::router SW1 --- R1 subgraph BD2[BROADCAST DOMAIN 2 — Subnet 10.0.0.0/24] direction TB H5[Host E
10.0.0.5]:::host H6[Host F
10.0.0.6]:::host SW2[Layer 2 Switch]:::switch end H5 --- SW2 H6 --- SW2 R1 --- SW2 classDef host fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef switch fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef router fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000,stroke-width:3px ``` **Key visual takeaway:** Notice that the switch is drawn inside a single dashed boundary (BD1), meaning all four hosts share the *same* broadcast domain. The **Router is on the boundary** — it is the *only* device that breaks the broadcast domain. ## 4.2 ARP Broadcast Resolution Flow (Data-Link Layer Broadcast) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Sender as Host A
192.168.1.10 participant Switch as Layer 2 Switch participant Net as Broadcast Domain
FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF participant Target as Host B
192.168.1.11 Note over Sender: ARP Cache Miss for 192.168.1.11 Sender->>Switch: ARP Request
Who has 192.168.1.11?
Tell 192.168.1.10 Switch->>Net: FLOOD to all ports
except ingress Net->>Target: Deliver to all hosts Target->>Switch: ARP Reply (Unicast)
192.168.1.11 is at AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF Switch->>Sender: Forward unicast reply Note over Sender: ARP Cache Updated ``` ## 4.3 Broadcast Storm Loop (Without STP) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1((Switch 1)):::switch S2((Switch 2)):::switch S3((Switch 3)):::switch S4((Switch 4)):::switch S1 -->|Flood| S2 S2 -->|Flood| S3 S3 -->|Flood| S4 S4 -->|Flood| S1 S1 -.->|Duplicate Flood| S2 S2 -.->|Duplicate Flood| S3 classDef switch fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000,stroke-width:2px ``` **Visual interpretation:** The solid arrows show the first broadcast circulation; the dotted arrows show the *second* copy generated by the same switches — exponential amplification. The solution (Spanning Tree) would block one of these logical links to break the loop. ## 4.4 Broadcast Address Format (Bit-Level Structure) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[32-bit IPv4 Address]:::root A --> B[Network Bits
Fixed for subnet]:::nbits A --> C[Host Bits
All 1s for broadcast]:::hbits C --> D[Limited Broadcast
All 32 bits = 1
255.255.255.255]:::lim C --> E[Directed Broadcast
Network bits preserved
Network.255]:::dir C --> F[Subnet Broadcast
Subnet bits preserved
Subnet.255]:::sub classDef root fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000 classDef nbits fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef hbits fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 classDef lim fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef dir fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 classDef sub fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 KTU Functional Architecture — VLAN-Based Broadcast Segmentation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHYS[Single Physical Switch — 8 Ports] direction LR subgraph VLAN10[VLAN 10 — Broadcast Domain A
192.168.10.0/24] P1[Port 1
Host A]:::v10 P2[Port 2
Host B]:::v10 P3[Port 3
Host C]:::v10 end subgraph VLAN20[VLAN 20 — Broadcast Domain B
192.168.20.0/24] P4[Port 4
Host D]:::v20 P5[Port 5
Host E]:::v20 P6[Port 6
Host F]:::v20 end TR1[Trunk Port
802.1Q]:::trunk end R1[Router-on-a-Stick
Inter-VLAN Routing]:::router TR1 --> R1 classDef v10 fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef v20 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef trunk fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,color:#000 classDef router fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000,stroke-width:3px ``` **Visual takeaway:** A *single physical switch* with VLANs creates **multiple logical broadcast domains**, just as multiple physical switches once did. This is why VLANs are the modern answer to broadcast domain segmentation. # Module 3: Broadcast — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]** > **Q1.** Differentiate between unicast, multicast, and broadcast communication with a suitable example for each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Mode | Recipients | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Unicast** | One specific recipient | HTTP GET from browser to web server | | **Multicast** | Subscribed group only | IPTV stream to subscribers of `239.1.1.1` | | **Broadcast** | All hosts in domain | ARP request to `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` | All three can be explained with 1 mark each. **(Full 3 marks for clear examples.)** --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]** > **Q2.** Why is broadcast not supported in IPv6? What mechanism replaces it? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - IPv6 eliminates broadcast (1 mark) because broadcast causes excessive unnecessary processing at every host's NIC and CPU (1 mark). - IPv6 uses **multicast**, specifically `ff02::1` (all-nodes link-local) and solicited-node multicast `ff02::1:ffXX:XXXX` (1 mark) for one-to-many and ARP-equivalent functions. - This reduces "broadcast storm" risk and improves efficiency (bonus clarity). ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14-Mark Q]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the different types of IP broadcast addresses with suitable examples. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A company is assigned the network `172.16.0.0/16`. The network administrator creates 4 equal subnets. For the **third subnet**: - (i) Find the subnet mask, network address, directed broadcast address, and range of usable host addresses. *(4 marks)* - (ii) Explain how a router handles a directed broadcast destined for this subnet. *(3 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Types of IP Broadcast Addresses (7 marks):** There are three categories of IPv4 broadcast addresses: 1. **Limited Broadcast Address — `255.255.255.255` (2 marks)** - Used when a host must reach all hosts in the **local physical network**. - It is **never forwarded by routers**; routers drop it at the boundary. - **Use case:** A DHCP client with no IP sends `DHCPDISCOVER` to `255.255.255.255`. 2. **Directed (Net-Directed) Broadcast Address (2.5 marks)** - Format: `.`. - **Example:** For network `192.168.1.0/24`, directed broadcast is `192.168.1.255`. - Intended to reach all hosts in a *specific* remote network. - Routers **may** forward it, but this is **disabled by default** in modern Cisco/Juniper devices for security (smurf attack prevention). 3. **Subnet-Directed Broadcast Address (2.5 marks)** - Format: `.`. - Used in **classless** (CIDR) subnetting. - **Example:** For subnet `10.0.5.0/26`, broadcast is `10.0.5.63`. - Routers must perform subnet matching to forward correctly. --- **(b) Subnet Calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Subnet the network `172.16.0.0/16` into 4 subnets** We need to borrow bits: $2^{n} \geq 4 \Rightarrow n = 2$ bits. New prefix: `/16 + 2 = /18` **Step 2 — Third subnet details** - Subnet mask `/18` = `255.255.192.0` - Subnets in order: - 1st: `172.16.0.0/18` - 2nd: `172.16.64.0/18` - **3rd: `172.16.128.0/18`** ✓ - 4th: `172.16.192.0/18` - **Network address of 3rd subnet:** `172.16.128.0` *(1 mark)* - **Broadcast of 3rd subnet:** Set all host bits (14 bits) to 1: - `172.16.10\;000000.00000000` → `172.16.11111111.11111111` → **`172.16.255.255`** *(1.5 marks)* - **Usable host range:** `172.16.128.1` to `172.16.255.254` *(1 mark)* - **Usable host count:** $2^{14} - 2 = 16{,}382$ *(0.5 marks)* **(b)(ii) Router handling (3 marks):** - Router receives a packet with destination `172.16.255.255` from an external network. - It performs longest-prefix match; `172.16.128.0/18` matches. - It detects that the destination has all host bits set to 1 → recognizes it as a directed broadcast. - By default, modern routers **drop the packet** and may log it as a "smurf attack" attempt. If forwarding is explicitly enabled (`ip directed-broadcast` on Cisco), the router floods the packet out all ports belonging to that subnet. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** What is a broadcast domain? How is it different from a collision domain? Explain with diagrams. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) and show how broadcast is used in it. Why are switches said to *extend* the broadcast domain but *segment* the collision domain? *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Broadcast Domain vs Collision Domain (7 marks):** - **Broadcast Domain (3.5 marks):** - The logical area in which a broadcast frame is propagated. - All devices in a broadcast domain will receive a broadcast frame sent by any one of them. - **Extended by:** Hubs, Bridges, Switches (Layer 2 only), and non-VLAN-aware switches. - **Separated by:** **Routers** (and VLANs on a Layer 3 switch). - A broadcast domain can span multiple physical segments but is logically one. - **Collision Domain (3.5 marks):** - The set of devices whose frames can collide with each other when sent simultaneously on a shared medium. - **Extended by:** Hubs and repeaters (since they repeat electrical signals). - **Separated by:** Bridges, Switches (each port = one collision domain), and Routers. - In full-duplex switched networks, collision domains are effectively eliminated per port. *Diagram: Draw a hub connecting 4 PCs (single collision domain AND single broadcast domain), and a switch connecting 4 PCs (4 collision domains, 1 broadcast domain).* --- **(b) ARP and Broadcast (7 marks):** - **ARP Purpose (1.5 marks):** Maps a known Layer 3 IP address to an unknown Layer 2 MAC address. - **ARP Process (3.5 marks):** 1. Host A wants to send to `192.168.1.20`. It checks its ARP cache — miss. 2. Host A constructs an ARP Request: *"Who has 192.168.1.20? Tell 192.168.1.10"*. 3. The frame is sent with destination MAC `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` (Layer 2 broadcast). 4. Switch floods the frame out all ports except the ingress port. 5. Every host in the broadcast domain receives and inspects it. Only Host B (with IP `192.168.1.20`) responds. 6. Host B sends an ARP Reply (unicast) with its MAC address. 7. Host A updates its ARP cache and proceeds with unicast communication. - **Switch and Domain Behavior (2 marks):** - When a switch receives a broadcast frame, it **floods** it to all ports in the same VLAN/broadcast domain — hence "extends" the broadcast domain. - When a switch receives a regular unicast frame destined for an unknown MAC, it floods it; but once the MAC is learned, it forwards only to the specific port — thus each port becomes its **own collision domain**, so the switch "segments" the collision domain. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. ❌ **Confusing "router drops broadcasts" with "router never processes them."** Routers *do* receive and inspect broadcast packets before dropping — this still consumes CPU. (Lose 1 mark) > 2. ❌ **Writing "switch creates a separate broadcast domain per port."** This is FALSE. A switch creates a broadcast domain per VLAN, not per port. (Lose 2 marks) > 3. ❌ **Forgetting the `-2` in usable host formula** (network and broadcast addresses are reserved). (Lose 1 mark) > 4. ❌ **Stating that IPv6 uses broadcast `255.255.255.255`.** This is wrong — IPv6 has no broadcast at all. (Lose 1 mark) > 5. ❌ **Missing the directed-broadcast security angle** (smurf attack) in 14-mark answers. Examiners award bonus marks for security awareness. (Lose up to 2 marks) ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 📌 **Three IP broadcast types:** Limited (`255.255.255.255`), Directed (Network.255), Subnet-directed (Subnet.255). - 📌 **MAC broadcast address is fixed:** `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF` — never changes. - 📌 **Routers are the ONLY devices that separate broadcast domains** by default. - 📌 **Switches extend broadcast domains** (within a VLAN) but **segment collision domains** (per port). - 📌 **Hubs extend BOTH** — they are layer-1 devices with no intelligence. - 📌 **IPv6 has NO broadcast** — uses multicast (`ff02::1`, `ff02::2`, solicited-node multicast). - 📌 **Usable host count formula:** $H = 2^{h} - 2$ (subtract network + broadcast). - 📌 **Broadcast storm formula:** $B(t) = N \cdot K^{t}$ — exponential growth in looped topologies. - 📌 **Spanning Tree Protocol (IEEE 802.1D/802.1w)** is the standard defense against broadcast storms. - 📌 **VLANs** logically segment broadcast domains on a single physical switch. - 📌 **DHCP and ARP** are the two most common users of broadcast in everyday networking. - 📌 **Security note:** Directed broadcast forwarding is disabled by default to prevent **smurf/fraggle attacks** — mention this for extra marks. - 📌 **MTU impact:** Each broadcast frame consumes up to 1518 bytes; storms can saturate links in milliseconds. - 📌 **Remember the exam-time shortcut:** `IsBroadcast = (IP AND NOT Mask) == NOT Mask`.

topics.title: Multicast
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multicast

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multicast** is a one-to-many (or many-to-many) group communication paradigm in which a single source transmits a single stream of IP datagrams that is delivered simultaneously to all interested receivers that have explicitly joined a specific multicast group. It sits conceptually between **unicast** (one-to-one) and **broadcast** (one-to-all) and is designed to conserve network and server resources when the same content (video, stock feeds, software updates, routing protocol hellos) must reach many endpoints.

In IP networking, multicast is identified at Layer 3 by the **Class D IP address block — 224.0.0.0 to 239.255.255.255** — which provides 2^28 ≈ **268.4 million** possible group identifiers. Every datagram whose destination address falls inside this range is forwarded by routers using multicast routing protocols rather than unicast forwarding tables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** A multicast receiver must *explicitly opt-in* via **IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol)** on the access link, and routers must compute a **distribution tree** to deliver packets. The sender does NOT need to know who the receivers are.

## 1.2 The Three Communication Paradigms Compared

| Paradigm | Destination Set | IP Destination Address | Receiver Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unicast** | Exactly **one** | Unique host IP (Class A/B/C) | Implicit — receiving socket bound to the address |
| **Broadcast** | **All hosts** on the local subnet | 255.255.255.255 or subnet broadcast | Implicit — every host processes the frame |
| **Multicast** | **Subset** of hosts that joined a group | Class D address (224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255) | Explicit — host sends IGMP Join |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The TV Channel

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The TV Channel Model**
> Imagine a TV broadcasting tower. The tower pushes out *one* electromagnetic signal carrying, say, a cricket match. Any household that owns a TV **and tunes to that channel** receives the stream; every other household simply ignores it. The broadcaster does not know — and does not need to know — exactly how many houses are watching.
>
> In networking terms:
> - **The TV tower** = the multicast **source** (sender)
> - **The TV channel frequency** = the **Class D group address** (e.g., 239.1.1.1)
> - **Tuning in** = the host sending an **IGMP Membership Report (Join)**
> - **The set-top box / cable infrastructure** = **multicast-aware routers** building a distribution tree
> - **Houses that are not watching** = hosts that never sent a Join; they are never burdened with the traffic

This single-signal-many-opt-in-viewers model is exactly what makes multicast so bandwidth-efficient compared to unicast replication.

## 1.4 Why Multicast Exists — The Bandwidth Problem

Consider **N = 1000** receivers wanting the same 1.5 Mbps IPTV stream.

- **Unicast** strategy: Source sends 1000 copies, consuming **1000 × 1.5 Mbps = 1.5 Gbps** on the source access link and on every upstream router.
- **Multicast** strategy: Source sends **1 copy** (1.5 Mbps); routers replicate only at branching points in the distribution tree.

The bandwidth saved scales linearly with the group size, which is why multicast is a foundational technology for **IPTV, video conferencing, financial market data feeds, distance learning, software/firmware distribution, and routing-protocol hellos (OSPF, RIPv2, EIGRP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction — Multicast is NOT broadcast on a routed network.** Routers will NOT forward a packet destined to 255.255.255.255 across subnet boundaries, but they WILL forward a packet destined to e.g. 239.1.1.1 across subnets, provided a multicast routing protocol is running and receivers have joined.

## 1.5 Reserved Multicast Address Ranges (Must Memorise for KTU)

| Range | Purpose | TTL | Scope |
|---|---|---|---|
| **224.0.0.0 / 24** | Link-local control (never routed) | **1** | Local subnet only |
| 224.0.0.1 | All multicast-capable hosts | 1 | Local subnet |
| 224.0.0.2 | All multicast routers | 1 | Local subnet |
| 224.0.0.5 / 224.0.0.6 | OSPF AllSPFRouters / AllDRouters | 1 | Local subnet |
| 224.0.0.9 | RIPv2 routers | 1 | Local subnet |
| 224.0.0.22 | IGMPv3 reports | 1 | Local subnet |
| **224.0.1.0 – 224.0.1.255** | Internetwork control (routable) | >1 | Internet |
| **232.0.0.0 / 8** | Source-Specific Multicast (SSM) | >1 | Internet |
| **233.0.0.0 / 8** | GLOP (AS-number-based) | >1 | Internet |
| **239.0.0.0 / 8** | Administratively Scoped (private) | Admin | Organisation |
| 224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255 | Full Class D | Variable | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Routers MUST NOT forward packets with destination in 224.0.0.0/24 regardless of the TTL value. Many students incorrectly assume TTL alone controls scope — it does NOT for this link-local block.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Architectural Layers of IP Multicast

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats multicast as a three-layer architecture. You must be able to identify which layer any given mechanism belongs to.

**Layer 1 — Group Management (Host ↔ Local Router)**
- **IGMP** (Internet Group Management Protocol) is the only protocol that operates between an end-host and its first-hop router.
- The host announces "I want to receive traffic for group G" via a Membership Report.
- The router periodically queries its segment to discover which groups still have members.

**Layer 2 — Multicast Routing (Router ↔ Router)**
- Router-to-router protocols construct and maintain the distribution tree.
- Examples: **DVMRP, PIM-DM, PIM-SM, PIM-SSM, MOSPF, CBT**.
- Each protocol uses a different strategy to decide which interfaces the multicast packet should be forwarded out of.

**Layer 3 — Layer-2 Mapping (IP Multicast ↔ Ethernet MAC)**
- A Class D IP must be translated into a destination MAC address so the frame can traverse the access switch.
- Only the **lower 23 bits** of the IP are carried into the MAC, producing the well-known **01-00-5E-xx-xx-xx** block.

## 2.2 IGMP — Internet Group Management Protocol

IGMP runs directly over **IPv4 (Protocol Number = 2)** and is a hop-by-hop, access-link-only protocol.

| Version | RFC | New Message | Key Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IGMPv1** | RFC 1112 | — | Hosts can only Join; **no explicit Leave**. Router times out group after 3 × 260 s. |
| **IGMPv2** | RFC 2236 | **Leave Group** (type 0x17) | Fast-leave: router immediately sends Group-Specific Query on receiving Leave. |
| **IGMPv3** | RFC 3376 | **Membership Report v3** with source lists | Source-Specific Multicast: host joins G and includes/excludes specific source IPs. |

### 2.2.1 IGMP Message Format (v1/v2)

| 8 bits Type | 8 bits Max Response Time | 16 bits Checksum | 32 bits Group Address |
|---|---|---|---|

For **IGMPv1**, type values are: `0x11` = Membership Query, `0x12` = Membership Report.
For **IGMPv2**, an additional type `0x17` = Leave Group is added.

### 2.2.2 IGMP Operation Cycle

1. **Router → all hosts:** sends a **General Membership Query** every 125 s (default).
2. **Host wants to join G:** it sends a **Membership Report** for G. (To reduce duplicate reports, the host starts a randomised timer — typically 0–10 s — and only reports if no other host on the segment sent a report for the same group first.)
3. **Router sees the report:** marks interface as having an interested receiver and propagates the Join upstream via the multicast routing protocol.
4. **Host wants to leave G (v2/v3):** if it is the last reporter (v1), or immediately (v2/v3), it sends a **Leave Group** message; the router issues a **Group-Specific Query** to confirm whether anyone else is still interested.

## 2.3 Multicast Distribution Trees

A **distribution tree** is the data structure that determines the set of interfaces over which a multicast packet should be replicated.

### 2.3.1 Source Tree (Shortest-Path Tree, SPT)

- One tree **per (Source, Group)** pair. Denoted **(S, G)**.
- The root is the source; branches follow the **reverse path back from each receiver toward the source**.
- Gives the **optimal path** from source to every receiver.
- Consumes more router memory because each (S, G) needs its own state entry.

### 2.3.2 Shared Tree (RP-based, RPT)

- **One tree per Group**, shared among all sources for that group. Denoted **(∗, G)**.
- All sources first send their traffic to a central point called the **Rendezvous Point (RP)**; receivers join the tree at the RP.
- Far less router state, but sub-optimal paths (always via RP).
- Standard in **PIM-SM**.

## 2.4 Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) Check

The RPF check is the **fundamental mechanism** that prevents loops in multicast forwarding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RPF Rule:** A multicast packet arriving on interface **I** is accepted and forwarded ONLY if **I is the interface the router would use to send a unicast packet back to the source S**. Otherwise the packet is silently dropped (and may be sent a Prune message in dense-mode protocols).

The RPF interface is computed from the unicast routing table (hence the name **"Protocol Independent"** Multicast — PIM does not maintain its own unicast topology).

## 2.5 PIM — Protocol Independent Multicast

PIM is the most widely deployed multicast routing protocol suite. It is **protocol-independent** because it relies on the unicast routing table to perform RPF, not on any specific unicast protocol.

| Mode | Topology Assumption | Mechanism | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PIM-DM** (Dense Mode) | Receivers are **densely distributed**; almost every subnet wants the data | **Flood-and-Prune**: push traffic everywhere, then prune subnets with no interested receivers | LANs, campus networks, broadcast-style distribution |
| **PIM-SM** (Sparse Mode) | Receivers are **sparsely distributed**; group is small relative to the network | **Explicit Join** toward a **Rendezvous Point (RP)**; receivers pull traffic | WAN, ISP, IPTV, enterprise |
| **PIM-SSM** (Source-Specific) | Receivers know the source in advance | Host sends **(S, G) Join** directly toward S; no RP, no shared tree | One-to-many, content delivery, financial feeds |

### 2.5.1 PIM-SM Operation (Summary)

1. Receivers send IGMP (S, G) Joins to their DR (Designated Router).
2. The DR sends a PIM **(∗, G) Join** hop-by-hop toward the RP.
3. Sources register with the RP via **PIM Register** (encapsulated unicast).
4. The RP sends a **(S, G) Join** toward the source, building an SPT between source and RP.
5. Optionally, the last-hop DR can switch from (∗, G) to **(S, G)** for the optimal path (PIM-SM SPT-switchover, default at first data packet in modern implementations).

## 2.6 DVMRP — Distance Vector Multicast Routing Protocol

- Built on the **RIP-style distance vector** engine.
- Uses **RPF** + **flood-and-prune** (very similar to PIM-DM).
- Has its own unicast table (a *true* DV multicast protocol — not protocol-independent).
- Largely obsolete in production, but historically important and frequently asked in KTU exams.

## 2.7 Multicast MAC Address Mapping (The Formula)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Multicast MAC Derivation Rule**
> 1. The 48-bit MAC address has its high **25 bits fixed** to **01-00-5E-0** (in binary, the second octet's MSB is forced to 0).
> 2. The **lower 23 bits of the Class D IP** are copied into the lower 23 bits of the MAC.
> 3. The 9th bit of the IP (highest bit of the second octet) is **dropped** — leading to a 32-to-1 ambiguity.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Rule | Expression / Range | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
