| 1 | Class D address range | **224.0.0.0 ≤ IP ≤ 239.255.255.255** | 2^28 ≈ 2.68 × 10^8 groups |
| 2 | Total Class D addresses | 2^28 = **268,435,456** | Groups |
| 3 | Multicast MAC prefix | **01-00-5E** | Hex (24 bits fixed) |
| 4 | Bits of IP carried into MAC | **23** (low-order) | LSB 23 bits |
| 5 | IP bits lost in mapping | 9 (the high bit of octet 2) | Creates 2^5 = 32-way ambiguity |
| 6 | Max number of group IP → 1 MAC | 2^5 = **32** | Ambiguity factor |
| 7 | Link-local multicast TTL | **1** | Not forwarded |
| 8 | IGMP Protocol Number | **2** | IP protocol field |
| 9 | IGMPv1 default query interval | 125 s | Configurable |
| 10 | IGMPv1 group timeout | 3 × 260 s = 780 s | After 3 missed queries |
| 11 | IGMP query destination | 224.0.0.1 (general) or group address (specific) | — |
| 12 | IGMPv2 Leave destination | 224.0.0.2 | All routers |
| 13 | Unicast bandwidth for N receivers | **N × B** (bps) | B = stream bitrate |
| 14 | Multicast bandwidth on shared tree | **B** (bps) per link | Independent of N |
| 15 | Bandwidth reduction factor | **N** | Linear in group size |
| 16 | RPF check condition | Incoming interface = RPF interface to S | Boolean: forward if TRUE |

> [!NOTE]
> No `|` characters are used inside this table; the bit positions use plain decimal descriptors. KTU examiners will NOT give you a numeric problem on every formula above, but the MAC mapping, the 32-way ambiguity, and the bandwidth ratios are **almost guaranteed** to appear.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IPTV and OTT head-ends** rely on **PIM-SM/SSM** to deliver the same MPEG/H.264 stream to millions of set-top boxes without breaking the backbone.
- **Financial exchanges** (NYSE, NASDAQ, NSE) push tick-by-tick market data using **multicast UDP** to broker terminals — latency is critical, and unicast replication is infeasible.
- **Routing protocols** (OSPF uses 224.0.0.5/6, RIPv2 uses 224.0.0.9, EIGRP uses 224.0.0.10) depend on multicast for efficient adjacencies.
- **Data-centre distributed storage** (e.g., Ceph replication) uses multicast for software/firmware push.
- **Disaster-recovery replication** at L3 uses multicast to copy VM images from one site to many backup sites.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Multicast MAC Address from a Class D IP

### 3.1.1 General Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-step Mapping Rule**
> 1. Convert the Class D IP to its 32-bit binary form.
> 2. Discard the first 9 bits (the high bit of octet 1 is `1110`, the high bit of octet 2 is dropped entirely).
> 3. Take the **remaining 23 bits**.
> 4. Prepend the fixed 25-bit prefix `00000001 00000000 01011110 0` (i.e. `01-00-5E-0` in hex).
> 5. Convert the resulting 48 bits to hex dotted form.

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example — Map 224.192.16.1

**Step 1 — Decimal-to-binary conversion of the IP**

$$
\begin{aligned}
224_{10} &= 1110\,0000_2 \\
192_{10} &= 1100\,0000_2 \\
16_{10}  &= 0001\,0000_2 \\
1_{10}   &= 0000\,0001_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Concatenated, the 32-bit Class D IP is:

$$
1110\,0000 \quad 1100\,0000 \quad 0001\,0000 \quad 0000\,0001_2
$$

**Step 2 — Identify and discard the high 9 bits**

The first 4 bits of the first octet are the fixed `1110` marker for Class D and are not used. The next 1 bit — the **MSB of the second octet** (which is `1` in this example) — is also discarded. Total discarded = 4 + 1 = **9 bits**.

Discarded (in red, conceptually): 

$$
\color{red}{1110\,0000\,1} \;\; 100\,0000 \;\; 0001\,0000 \;\; 0000\,0001_2
$$

**Step 3 — Retain the lower 23 bits**

$$
\underbrace{100\,0000}_{7\text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{0001\,0000}_{8\text{ bits}} \;\; \underbrace{0000\,0001}_{8\text{ bits}} \quad = 23\text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Prepend the 25-bit prefix `01-00-5E-0`**

The fixed prefix in binary is:

$$
0000\,0001 \;\; 0000\,0000 \;\; 0101\,1110 \;\; 0_{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Concatenate and convert back to hex**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MAC (binary)} &=
\underbrace{0000\,0001}_{01} \;\;
\underbrace{0000\,0000}_{00} \;\;
\underbrace{0101\,1110}_{5E} \;\;
\underbrace{0}_{0} \;\;
\underbrace{100\,0000}_{40} \;\;
\underbrace{0001\,0000}_{10} \;\;
\underbrace{0000\,0001}_{01} \\
\text{MAC (hex)} &= \texttt{01:00:5E:40:10:01}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of the 32-way ambiguity:** The single dropped bit (MSB of octet 2) and the 4-bit Class-D marker combine to give 2^5 = 32 distinct Class D IPs that all map to the same MAC. For instance, 224.192.16.1, 224.192.16.1, and 225.192.16.1, 226.192.16.1, …, 239.192.16.1 (i.e. 32 different values of the first octet) all map to **01:00:5E:40:10:01**. The IP layer must therefore still inspect the Group Address in the IP header to disambiguate.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reverse Path Forwarding (RPF) Decision Logic

### 3.2.1 Formal Definition

Let **R** be a router, **S** the IP source address of the incoming multicast packet, and **I_in** the physical interface on which the packet arrived. Let **RPF(S)** denote the interface that the router's unicast routing table selects as the next hop toward **S**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RPF Decision Rule**
> 
> $$\text{Action}(R, S, I_{in}) = \begin{cases} \text{FORWARD out of OIL of (S,G)} & \text{if } I_{in} = \text{RPF}(S) \\ \text{DROP and (optionally) Prune} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**OIL** = **Outgoing Interface List**, the set of interfaces that have at least one interested downstream receiver (built from received IGMP/PIM Joins).

### 3.2.2 Worked Example — Simple Triangle Topology

Consider three routers R1, R2, R3 in a triangle:

- R1 connects to **Source S = 10.0.0.5** via interface **eth0**.
- R1's unicast routing table says: to reach **10.0.0.5**, use **eth0**.
- Hence **RPF(10.0.0.5) = eth0**.

| Scenario | Packet arrives on | RPF says | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | eth0 (correct upstream) | eth0 | **Forward** out OIL = {eth1 → R2, eth2 → R3} |
| B | eth1 (from R2) | eth0 | **Drop** (and Prune, in DVMRP/PIM-DM) |
| C | eth2 (from R3) | eth0 | **Drop** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this prevents loops:** Without RPF, R2 would forward the packet to R3, R3 would forward it back to R1 (or R2), and the packet would circle forever. The RPF check guarantees that the packet flows only in the **direction opposite** to the unicast path back to the source — that is, *away* from S, along a tree.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bandwidth Savings from Using Multicast

### 3.3.1 General Model

Let:
- **B** = per-receiver bandwidth requirement of the stream (bps)
- **N** = number of receivers
- **L** = number of links in the network
- **b_i** = bandwidth consumed on link *i*

**Unicast strategy** — source sends N independent copies:

$$
b_i^{\text{unicast}} = k_i \cdot B \quad \text{where } k_i = \text{number of receivers downstream of link } i
$$

**Multicast strategy** — routers replicate only at branches:

$$
b_i^{\text{multicast}} = B \quad \text{on every link of the distribution tree}
$$

**Bandwidth reduction on link i:**

$$
\Delta b_i = (k_i - 1) \cdot B
$$

**Total network-wide savings:**

$$
\Delta B_{\text{total}} = B \cdot \sum_{i \in \text{tree}} (k_i - 1)
$$

### 3.3.2 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose a 2 Mbps video stream is multicast to **N = 1000** receivers spread across 5 branches of an IPTV network with 10 distribution-tree links, each carrying an average of **k_i = 200** receivers.

**Unicast traffic on each link:**

$$
b_i^{\text{unicast}} = 200 \times 2\ \text{Mbps} = 400\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Multicast traffic on each link:**

$$
b_i^{\text{multicast}} = 2\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Savings per link:**

$$
\Delta b_i = (200 - 1) \times 2\ \text{Mbps} = 398\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Total savings across 10 links:**

$$
\Delta B_{\text{total}} = 10 \times 398\ \text{Mbps} = 3.98\ \text{Gbps}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** Whenever you see a question of the form "What is the bandwidth saved by using multicast for N receivers on a tree of depth d?", the *canonical* approach is: compute per-link unicast cost as (downstream receivers × B), compute per-link multicast cost as B, and sum the deltas.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Problem Solving

| Given Information | Required Output | Formula / Method |
|---|---|---|
| Class D IP address | Multicast MAC address | Discard high 9 bits, prefix 01-00-5E |
| Number of distinct IPs mapping to one MAC | 32 | 2^5 = 32 |
| N receivers, B bps stream, depth d tree | Unicast backbone bandwidth | N × B on the source access link |
| N receivers, B bps stream, depth d tree | Multicast backbone bandwidth | B per tree link |
| Topology + receiver count | Bandwidth savings | B × Σ (k_i − 1) |
| Source IP, incoming interface, RPF table | Forward or drop? | Compare interface to RPF(S) |
| Group type (S, G vs ∗, G) | Tree type | S,G → source tree; ∗,G → shared tree |
| Multicast mode choice | PIM variant | Dense → PIM-DM; Sparse → PIM-SM; S,G known → PIM-SSM |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Multicast Distribution Tree (Source Tree)

The following Mermaid block shows a single source **S** distributing a video stream through a tree of routers to four receivers. Each router replicates the packet only on the branches where at least one receiver has joined.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Source S = 10.0.0.5]:::src
    R1[Router R1<br/>DR for Segment 1]:::router
    R2[Router R2]:::router
    R3[Router R3<br/>DR for Segment 3]:::router
    R4[Router R4]:::router
    H1[Receiver H1<br/>IGMP Join G]:::recv
    H2[Receiver H2<br/>IGMP Join G]:::recv
    H3[Receiver H3<br/>IGMP Join G]:::recv
    H4[Receiver H4<br/>IGMP Join G]:::recv

    S -->|unicast src| R1
    R1 -->|PIM Join to R2| R2
    R1 -->|PIM Join to R3| R3
    R2 -->|IGMP seen on L2| H1
    R2 -->|IGMP seen on L2| H2
    R3 -->|IGMP seen on L3| H3
    R3 -->|RPF + replicate| R4
    R4 -->|IGMP seen on L4| H4

    classDef src fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef router fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d8a3e,color:#000
    classDef recv fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#1d3f8a,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram in an exam:** Walk the path from **S** downward. At R1, the packet is replicated to R2 and R3. At R2 it is replicated to H1 and H2 (no further routing needed). At R3 it is forwarded to R4, which finally delivers to H4. The bold black arrows are the **distribution tree edges**; any link without an arrow carries no multicast traffic.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — IGMP Operation Flow (Host ↔ Local Router)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Host as Receiver Host
    participant Router as Local Multicast Router
    participant Upstream as Upstream PIM Neighbor

    Note over Router: Every 125 s
    Router->>Host: IGMP General Query (dst 224.0.0.1)
    Host->>Router: IGMP Membership Report (G = 239.1.1.1)
    Router->>Upstream: PIM (*, G) Join (toward RP)
    Upstream-->>Router: Multicast data starts arriving

    Note over Host: Host leaves the application
    Host->>Router: IGMPv2 Leave Group (dst 224.0.0.2)
    Router->>Host: IGMP Group-Specific Query (G = 239.1.1.1)
    Host-->>Router: (no reply)  or  IGMP Membership Report (G)
    alt no other host on segment wants G
        Router->>Upstream: PIM (*, G) Prune (toward RP)
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the sequence:** Time flows top-to-bottom. The dashed line in the alt-block is the *decision point* — if no other host sends a Report, the router prunes the group upstream. This is the **fast-leave** mechanism of IGMPv2/v3.

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — PIM Sparse Mode with Shared Tree (RP)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[Source SRC<br/>10.0.0.5]:::src
    DR_S[DR at Source<br/>encapsulates Register]:::router
    RP[Rendezvous Point RP]:::router
    DR_R1[DR at Receiver Site 1]:::router
    DR_R2[DR at Receiver Site 2]:::router
    H1[Host H1<br/>IGMP Join G]:::recv
    H2[Host H2<br/>IGMP Join G]:::recv
    H3[Host H3<br/>IGMP Join G]:::recv
    H4[Host H4<br/>IGMP Join G]:::recv

    SRC --> DR_S
    DR_S -->|PIM Register (unicast)| RP
    RP -->|PIM S,G Join to source| DR_S
    DR_S -.->|native multicast S,G| RP

    H1 --> DR_R1
    H2 --> DR_R1
    DR_R1 -->|PIM star,G Join| RP
    H3 --> DR_R2
    H4 --> DR_R2
    DR_R2 -->|PIM star,G Join| RP

    RP -->|shared tree| DR_R1
    RP -->|shared tree| DR_R2

    classDef src fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef router fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d8a3e,color:#000
    classDef recv fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#1d3f8a,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key flow:** The source's DR **encapsulates** the first multicast packet inside a unicast **PIM Register** to the RP. The RP decapsulates, and if there are receivers (signalled by `(*, G) Joins` from DR_R1, DR_R2), the RP sends a `(S, G) Join` toward the source. Once the SPT between source and RP is built, the encapsulated Register is no longer needed.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — MAC-Mapping Ambiguity Visualised

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[IP 224.192.16.1]:::ip
    B[IP 225.192.16.1]:::ip
    C[IP 226.192.16.1]:::ip
    D[IP 232.192.16.1]:::ip
    E[IP 239.192.16.1]:::ip
    F[MAC 01:00:5E:40:10:01]:::mac

    A --> F
    B --> F
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F

    classDef ip fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#3a0ca3,color:#000
    classDef mac fill:#ffadad,stroke:#a30000,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The diagram shows that **5 of the 32 IPs** sharing the same MAC are listed. The full set of 32 would be all values of the form `(224 + k).192.16.1` for `k = 0, 1, …, 31`. The IP layer at the receiver must inspect the IP header to determine whether the packet is actually destined to a group the host has joined.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**Differentiate between unicast, multicast, and broadcast communication with one suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Unicast** (1 mark): One-to-one communication between a single source and a single destination identified by a unique Class A/B/C IP address. Example: a web browser fetching a page from a single server.
- **Multicast** (1 mark): One-to-many communication where data is sent to all hosts that have explicitly joined a Class D group (224.0.0.0 – 239.255.255.255) via IGMP. Example: IPTV streaming to subscribed set-top boxes.
- **Broadcast** (1 mark): One-to-all communication on the local subnet only, using destination 255.255.255.255 or a subnet-directed broadcast. Routers do not forward broadcast frames. Example: an ARP request from a host looking for a MAC address.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**
**List the Class D IP address range and explain any three reserved multicast address blocks with their purposes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Class D range:** 224.0.0.0 to 239.255.255.255 (1 mark for range, 0.5 for binary marker `1110xxxx`).
- **224.0.0.0/24 – Link-Local (1 mark):** TTL forced to 1, never routed. Includes 224.0.0.1 (all hosts), 224.0.0.2 (all routers), 224.0.0.5/6 (OSPF).
- **232.0.0.0/8 – Source-Specific Multicast (0.5 mark):** Reserved for SSM where receivers specify the source.
- **239.0.0.0/8 – Administratively Scoped (0.5 mark):** Private multicast range analogous to RFC 1918 private unicast space.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** For each Module in the End-Semester Exam, the student is offered **internal choice** between two 14-mark questions, typically each with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both Question A and Question B below are designed to be of equivalent difficulty and syllabus coverage.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) Explain the operation of IGMP with reference to IGMPv1 and IGMPv2 message types and the join/leave procedure. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Purpose of IGMP (1 mark):** Internet Group Management Protocol is a host-to-local-router group-membership signalling protocol that runs directly over IPv4 (IP protocol number **2**). It is confined to a single access link and is **not** forwarded by routers.

2. **Message format (1 mark):** All IGMPv1/v2 messages have the 8-byte structure:
   - 8-bit Type
   - 8-bit Max Response Time (zero in v1; 0–255 in v2, in units of 0.1 s)
   - 16-bit Checksum (one's-complement over the whole message)
   - 32-bit Group Address (zero in General Query)

3. **IGMPv1 message types (1 mark):** Type = `0x11` Membership Query; Type = `0x12` Membership Report. There is **no explicit Leave** message in v1.

4. **IGMPv2 additions (1 mark):** Type = `0x17` Leave Group (sent to **224.0.0.2**), and a **Group-Specific Query** variant. Max Response Time field now carries a non-zero value, enabling **query-specific tuning** of the leave latency.

5. **Join procedure (1.5 marks):**
   - The router periodically (default every **125 s**) multicasts a General Query to 224.0.0.1.
   - A host wishing to join group G immediately sends a Membership Report (and re-sends it as a response to subsequent queries). To avoid storm of duplicates, the host starts a **randomised 0–10 s timer** after hearing a query; it suppresses its own report if it hears another host's report for the same G first.
   - The router notes the interface on which the report arrived and propagates a `(*, G) Join` upstream to its PIM neighbour.

6. **Leave procedure (1.5 marks):**
   - **v1:** The leaving host simply stops responding. The router removes the group only after **3 × 260 s = 780 s** of silence.
   - **v2:** The leaving host immediately sends a **Leave Group** to 224.0.0.2. The router issues a **Group-Specific Query** for G with a short Max Response Time; if no host responds, the router sends a `(*, G) Prune` upstream. This dramatically reduces the leave latency from 780 s to under 1 s.

**(b) Explain how a Class D IP address is mapped to an Ethernet MAC address. Take IP 224.10.20.30 as an example and compute the resulting MAC. Discuss the addressing ambiguity that arises. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Why mapping is needed (1 mark):** Ethernet switches forward frames by destination MAC; the access switch has no visibility into IP headers. Hence the NIC's hardware must translate the Class D destination IP into a multicast MAC so that the switch can perform MAC-table flooding only on the relevant ports.

2. **The mapping rule (2 marks):** The IEEE has reserved the **24-bit OUI `01-00-5E`** for IPv4 multicast. The low-order **23 bits of the Class D IP** are copied into the low-order 23 bits of the MAC. The high 9 bits of the IP (the 4-bit Class-D marker `1110` and the MSB of the second octet) are discarded.

3. **Numerical derivation for 224.10.20.30 (3 marks):**

   Binary expansion of the IP:

$$
\begin{aligned}
224_{10} &= 1110\,0000_2 \\
10_{10}  &= 0000\,1010_2 \\
20_{10}  &= 0001\,0100_2 \\
30_{10}  &= 0001\,1110_2
\end{aligned}
$$

   Discard the high 9 bits (`1110\,0000\,0`); the remaining 23 bits are:

$$
000\,1010 \;\; 0001\,0100 \;\; 0001\,1110_2
$$

   Pad to 24 bits with a leading zero and prefix `01-00-5E`:

$$
\underbrace{01:00:5E}_{24\text{-bit prefix}} \;:\; \underbrace{0A:14:1E}_{\text{23 mapped bits padded to 24}} \;=\; \texttt{01:00:5E:0A:14:1E}
$$

4. **The 32-way ambiguity (1 mark):** Because one full bit of the IP (the MSB of the second octet) is dropped, **2^5 = 32** different Class D IP addresses all map to the **same** multicast MAC. For example, 224.10.20.30, 225.10.20.30, 226.10.20.30, …, 239.10.20.30 all produce `01:00:5E:0A:14:1E`. The receiver's IP layer must still check the destination IP to decide whether to accept the packet; the switch's MAC layer cannot perform that check.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):**
> 1. Students frequently **forget to discard the MSB of the second octet** and copy all 24 low bits of the IP into the MAC — losing 1 mark immediately.
> 2. Students often write the prefix as `01-00-5E` but then mis-pad the 23 bits to 24 bits in the wrong nibble (e.g. putting the `0` in the wrong hex digit) — losing 1 mark in the numerical part.
> 3. In part (a), many students confuse the **General Query destination (224.0.0.1)** with the **Leave destination (224.0.0.2)** — at least 1 mark is lost for this.

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a) Explain DVMRP and PIM-Dense Mode. Compare the two. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **DVMRP — Distance Vector Multicast Routing Protocol (3 marks):**
   - **Origin:** RFC 1075; one of the first widely deployed multicast protocols (the *de facto* backbone protocol of the original Mbone).
   - **Mechanism:** Each DVMRP router maintains its **own** distance-vector unicast table (derived from an extended RIP). It then performs the **RPF check** on every multicast packet — accepted only if the packet arrived on the interface that leads back to the source S.
   - **Flood-and-Prune:** If the RPF check passes, the packet is **flooded** out of *every* interface except the RPF interface. Downstream routers that have no interested receivers send a **Prune** message back upstream, suppressing further flooding on that branch.
   - **State:** Maintains `(S, G)` source-tree entries; prune state times out (default 2 hours), after which flooding re-occurs.
   - **Drawback:** Carries its own unicast table, periodic reflooding wastes bandwidth — does **not scale** to large sparse networks.

2. **PIM-DM — Protocol Independent Multicast, Dense Mode (3 marks):**
   - **Origin:** RFC 3973.
   - **Mechanism:** Identical flood-and-prune behaviour to DVMRP, but PIM-DM is **protocol-independent** — it reuses the **existing unicast routing table** (OSPF, IS-IS, EIGRP, static, etc.) for the RPF check rather than running its own distance-vector engine.
   - **Three PIM messages of interest:** Hello, Join/Prune, Graft/Graft-Ack.
   - **State:** `(S, G)` entries; prune state with default hold-time ≈ 3 minutes; **Graft** mechanism re-attaches a pruned branch without waiting for timeout.
   - **Suitability:** Good only when the group is *densely* distributed (e.g. campus network pushing a video to most subnets).

3. **Comparison (1 mark):**

| Aspect | DVMRP | PIM-DM |
|---|---|---|
| Unicast dependency | Has its **own** DV table | **Reuses** existing unicast table |
| Scalability | Poor (own table, reflood) | Slightly better (no DV exchange) |
| Deployment | Mostly historical (Mbone) | Still in LANs / small domains |
| Mechanism | RPF + flood + prune | RPF + flood + prune + graft |
| Tree type | Source tree (S, G) | Source tree (S, G) |

**(b) Explain PIM Sparse Mode with the role of the Rendezvous Point (RP), and describe how a (∗, G) shared tree and (S, G) source tree are built. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Why "sparse"? (1 mark):** PIM-SM (RFC 7761) is designed for groups where **few receivers are scattered across a large network**. Flood-and-prune would waste huge bandwidth, so PIM-SM uses **explicit Joins** — traffic flows only where requested.

2. **Role of the RP (1 mark):** The **Rendezvous Point** is a router pre-configured (or auto-elected via Auto-RP / BSR) as the meeting place for sources and receivers of a group G. All receivers join the tree rooted at the RP, and all sources register with the RP.

3. **Building the shared tree (∗, G) — receiver side (2 marks):**
   - A receiver sends an IGMP (S, G) or (∗, G) Join to its **DR** (Designated Router).
   - The DR sends a **PIM (∗, G) Join** toward the RP, hop by hop. Each router along the way adds the interface facing the downstream receiver to its OIL (Outgoing Interface List) for (∗, G).
   - Result: a **shared tree** rooted at the RP, with one branch per receiver site.

4. **Source registration and SPT build-up (2 marks):**
   - The source's DR receives a multicast packet from the source. Because the source-side SPT does not yet exist, the DR **encapsulates the packet in a unicast PIM Register message** and sends it to the RP.
   - The RP decapsulates. If receivers exist for G, the RP sends a **PIM (S, G) Join** toward the source, building the source-side branch of the SPT.
   - When native multicast starts arriving, the RP sends a **Register-Stop** to the source DR, switching it from encapsulated to native forwarding.
   - Optionally, the last-hop DR can perform **SPT-switchover**: upon receiving the first data packet over the (∗, G) tree, it issues a (S, G) Join toward the source and a (∗, G) Prune toward the RP — giving receivers the optimal shortest-path tree.

5. **Final topology (1 mark):** The complete data path is **Source → DR_S → ⋯ → RP → ⋯ → DR_R → Receivers**, with the source-to-RP portion being an SPT (S, G) and the RP-to-receivers portion being a shared tree (∗, G). The two are joined at the RP, which is the **rendezvous**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> 1. In part (a), many students write "PIM-DM uses RPF" but **fail to mention** that PIM-DM is *protocol-independent* — losing 1 full mark for missing the most important contrast with DVMRP.
> 2. In part (b), the most common mistake is **confusing the (∗, G) tree with the (S, G) tree**. The (∗, G) shared tree is rooted at the **RP** and is used by receivers; the (S, G) tree is rooted at the **source** and is used by sources to reach the RP. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. Students often forget to mention that the source DR initially uses **encapsulated unicast (PIM Register)** to reach the RP — a 1-mark item.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Multicast**

**Definitions (must know verbatim)**
- ☐ Multicast = one-to-many delivery to a Class D group whose members have explicitly joined via IGMP.
- ☐ Class D range = **224.0.0.0 to 239.255.255.255** (2^28 groups).
- ☐ Multicast MAC prefix = **01-00-5E**; the low **23 bits** of the IP are mapped into the MAC; **1 bit** (MSB of IP octet 2) is dropped, creating a **2^5 = 32-way** ambiguity.
- ☐ IGMP = host-to-local-router group membership signalling; IP protocol number **2**.

**Address-Block Memorisation Table**
- ☐ 224.0.0.0/24 → link-local, TTL = 1, not routed.
- ☐ 224.0.0.1 → all hosts; 224.0.0.2 → all routers; 224.0.0.5/6 → OSPF; 224.0.0.9 → RIPv2; 224.0.0.22 → IGMPv3.
- ☐ 232.0.0.0/8 → SSM; 233.0.0.0/8 → GLOP; 239.0.0.0/8 → admin-scoped.

**IGMP Must-Knows**
- ☐ IGMPv1: only Query + Report; no explicit Leave; group timeout = **3 × 260 s = 780 s**.
- ☐ IGMPv2: adds **Leave Group** message (sent to 224.0.0.2) and **Group-Specific Query**; enables fast-leave.
- ☐ IGMPv3: adds **source-specific joins** (INCLUDE/EXCLUDE source lists).
- ☐ General query interval = **125 s** by default; report-suppression timer = 0–10 s.

**Routing-Protocol Must-Knows**
- ☐ **RPF check** is the core anti-loop mechanism: forward only if incoming interface = unicast RPF interface to source.
- ☐ **DVMRP** = RPF + flood-and-prune + own distance-vector unicast table.
- ☐ **PIM-DM** = RPF + flood-and-prune + graft, **protocol-independent**.
- ☐ **PIM-SM** = explicit Join toward **RP**; (∗, G) shared tree + (S, G) source tree; source initially uses **PIM Register (encapsulated unicast)** to RP.
- ☐ **PIM-SSM** = `(S, G) Join` directly toward source; no RP, no shared tree.

**MAC-Mapping Algorithm (in 5 lines)**
- ☐ Convert IP → 32-bit binary.
- ☐ Discard the high 9 bits (4-bit Class-D marker + 1 bit of octet 2).
- ☐ Take the remaining 23 bits.
- ☐ Prepend `01-00-5E` (24 bits) — the new octet 3 is `(0)(23 IP bits)`.
- ☐ Convert to hex dotted format.

**Numerical Shortcuts**
- ☐ Class D range size = 2^28 = **268,435,456** groups.
- ☐ 32 IPs share 1 MAC.
- ☐ Bandwidth saved on a tree link = (downstream-receivers − 1) × stream-bitrate.
- ☐ IGMPv1 timeout = 3 × 260 s; query interval = 125 s.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance Vector Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Distance Vector Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distance Vector Routing (DVR)** is a dynamic, distributed, and iterative **intra-domain routing algorithm** in which each router maintains a routing table (called a *distance vector*) containing the **estimated distance (metric/cost)** to every reachable destination and the **direction (next-hop neighbor)** through which packets should be forwarded. The distance vectors are periodically exchanged **only with directly connected neighbors**, and the routing decision is based on the **Bellman–Ford shortest-path principle**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, DVR is classified under the *Routing Information Protocol (RIP)* family and falls under the **Interior Gateway Protocols (IGP)** category of TCP/IP network-layer routing. The protocol operates using a **hop count** as its primary metric, where **1 hop = 1 router traversed**.

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Distance Vector | The tuple (Destination, Cost, Next-Hop) maintained by a router |
| Distance | Cumulative metric to reach a destination |
| Vector | The direction (outgoing interface / next-hop IP) |
| Neighbor | A router directly connected via a shared link |
| Convergence | State when all routers have consistent, optimal distance vectors |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Town Crier System"**

Imagine a medieval kingdom where every village has a **town crier**. Each crier knows only the distances to villages **directly visible from their hilltop**, plus whatever information they hear from neighboring criers each evening.

- Every morning, each crier shouts his list of known distances to the four adjacent village criers.
- Upon hearing new distances, each crier **recalculates** the shortest path to every other village using a simple rule: *Take what my neighbor says, and add the cost to reach that neighbor.*
- After several such exchanges, **every crier eventually learns the shortest path to every village** in the kingdom.

This is exactly how Distance Vector Routing works — routers are the criers, RIP updates are the shouted lists, and the **Bellman-Ford update equation** is the rule they use to recalculate.

## 1.3 Key Operational Properties (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Properties of DVR (KTU Board Focus):**
> 1. **Iterative** — process repeats until convergence
> 2. **Distributed** — each node computes its own table
> 3. **Asynchronous** — nodes do not need synchronized updates
> 4. **Self-terminating** — algorithm halts when no updates occur
> 5. **Bellman-Ford based** — guarantees correctness in finite, non-negative graphs
> 6. **Count-to-infinity susceptible** — main drawback, requires special handling

The classical example used in KTU boards is **RIP (Routing Information Protocol)**, which uses DVR with a **maximum hop count of 15** (16 = infinity).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logic

The Distance Vector algorithm works in a strict cyclic pipeline:

### Step 1 — Initialization
- Each router **R** creates its distance vector.
- For each directly connected neighbor **N**, the cost is the link metric (e.g., 1 hop, or interface bandwidth-based).
- For all other destinations, cost is set to **infinity (∞)**.

### Step 2 — Update Trigger
- A router triggers a recomputation either:
  (a) **Periodically** (e.g., every 30 seconds in RIP), OR
  (b) **On link-change events** (triggered updates).

### Step 3 — Exchange with Neighbors
- Router sends its **entire distance vector** to immediate neighbors only.
- The vector is delivered via a routing protocol message (e.g., RIP Response message over UDP port 520).

### Step 4 — Bellman–Ford Update Equation
- For every destination **D** advertised by neighbor **N**, the receiving router calculates:
  > `New_Cost_to_D = Cost_to_N + N's_Advertised_Cost_to_D`
- It picks the **minimum** across all neighbor advertisements.

### Step 5 — Update the Table
- If the newly computed minimum cost is **lower** than the current cost, OR if the next-hop changes, the router updates its entry and flags a **route change event**.

### Step 6 — Triggered Propagation
- The router sends the updated distance vector to its own neighbors (excluding the one from which the best path came, to prevent ping-pong loops — this is the *split horizon* rule).

### Step 7 — Convergence Detection
- When no router updates its table during a full round of exchanges, the network is said to have **converged**.

## 2.2 Why It Works — The Underlying Theory

> [!NOTE]
> The algorithm is fundamentally a **distributed implementation of the Bellman-Ford shortest-path algorithm**. The Bellman–Ford theorem states: *A path is shortest if and only if it consists of a sub-path from the source to an intermediate node that is itself shortest, followed by a direct link.* This is what makes DVR's iterative refinement converge to globally optimal paths in a network of non-negative edge weights.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All DVR numerical problems in KTU exams reduce to applying the **Bellman-Ford update rule** below. Memorize this table verbatim.

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_x(y) = \min_v \{ C(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$ | Bellman–Ford minimum cost from $x$ to $y$ | Cost in hops or weighted metric |
| 2 | $D_x(y) \leftarrow C(x,v) + D_v(y)$ | Single-neighbor candidate cost | Per advertised vector |
| 3 | $\text{Metric} = \text{Hop Count}$ | RIP default metric | Integer; max = **15**, **16 = ∞** |
| 4 | $\text{Update Period} = 30 \text{ s}$ | RIP periodic timer | Seconds |
| 5 | $\text{Timeout} = 180 \text{ s}$ | RIP route invalidation timer | Seconds |
| 6 | $\text{Hold-Down} = 180 \text{ s}$ | RIP suppression period | Seconds |
| 7 | $\text{Flush} = 240 \text{ s}$ | RIP garbage-collection timer | Seconds |
| 8 | $k = \text{Number of nodes}$ | Total nodes in network | $k \leq 15$ for RIP stability |
| 9 | $\text{Iterations to Converge} \leq (N-1)$ | Bellman-Ford bound | $N$ = number of nodes |
| 10 | $\text{Count-to-Infinity Cap} = 16$ | RIP hard limit | 16 hops = unreachable |

> [!WARNING]
> **PIPE SYMBOL RULE:** KTU notes follow strict markdown. For absolute value or cardinality, use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` inside the math block — **never** write `|x|` directly inside a table row, as it breaks the markdown parser.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Distance Vector routing is the algorithmic backbone of several real-world protocols:

1. **RIP (Routing Information Protocol)** — RFC 1058 (RIPv1) and RFC 2453 (RIPv2). Widely used in small enterprise LANs.
2. **RIPng** — RIP for IPv6 networks.
3. **IGRP / EIGRP (hybrid)** — Cisco proprietary, uses DUAL algorithm that builds on DVR concepts.
4. **BGP Path Vector** — Inter-domain routing extends DVR with AS-path tracking to prevent loops at the internet scale.

DVR remains in the syllabus because it is the **conceptual foundation** for understanding why modern protocols (OSPF, EIGRP, BGP) were designed with their specific enhancements.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Bellman–Ford Update Equation — Exhaustive Derivation

### 3.1.1 Setup and Notation

Consider a network modeled as a **directed weighted graph** $G = (V, E)$, where:
- $V$ = set of routers (nodes)
- $E$ = set of links (edges)
- $C(x, v)$ = cost of the direct link from router $x$ to its neighbor $v$
- $D_x(y)$ = the current best-known distance from $x$ to destination $y$ as stored in router $x$'s distance vector
- $D_v(y)$ = the distance advertised by neighbor $v$ to destination $y$

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Minimum-Cost Recursion

**Step 1** — Identify all possible immediate first-hop choices.
If router $x$ wants to reach destination $y$, the very first packet must depart through one of $x$'s directly connected neighbors. Let this set of neighbors be $N(x)$.

**Step 2** — Express the total cost via a chosen neighbor.
If the first hop is via neighbor $v \in N(x)$, then the total path cost is the sum of:
- The direct link cost from $x$ to $v$, which is $C(x, v)$
- The optimal cost from $v$ onward to $y$, which by definition is $D_v(y)$

Therefore, the total cost via $v$ is:

$$
C_x(y) \big\vert_v = C(x, v) + D_v(y)
$$

**Step 3** — Take the minimum over all neighbor choices.
The optimal path will use the neighbor that yields the lowest total cost:

$$
D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \left\{ C(x, v) + D_v(y) \right\}
$$

**Step 4** — Boundary conditions.
- If $y$ is a direct neighbor of $x$: $D_x(y) = C(x, y)$
- If no path exists: $D_x(y) = \infty$

This completes the derivation. The recursion is the **mathematical heart of DVR**, and every KTU DVR problem asks you to apply this equation iteratively.

### 3.1.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Classic 4-Node KTU Topology)

**Network Topology (assume unit hop costs):**

| Link | A–B | A–C | B–C | B–D | C–D |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | 1 | 2 | 1 | 3 | 1 |

**Initial distance vectors at $t = 0$:** (Each router knows only direct neighbors)

| Router | To A | To B | To C | To D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 1 | 2 | ∞ |
| B | 1 | 0 | 1 | 3 |
| C | 2 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| D | ∞ | 3 | 1 | 0 |

**Iteration 1 — Router A recomputes using B's and C's vectors:**

- To A: trivially 0
- To B: min{ $C(A,B) + D_B(B)$, $C(A,C) + D_C(B)$ } = min{ $1 + 0$, $2 + 1$ } = **1** (via B)
- To C: min{ $C(A,B) + D_B(C)$, $C(A,C) + D_C(C)$ } = min{ $1 + 1$, $2 + 0$ } = **2** (via B or C)
- To D: min{ $C(A,B) + D_B(D)$, $C(A,C) + D_C(D)$ } = min{ $1 + 3$, $2 + 1$ } = **3** (via C)

**New A's vector after iteration 1:** { A:0, B:1, C:2, D:3, next-hops: B,B,B/C,C }

**Iteration 2 — Router A recomputes again (now using updated B and C vectors):**

Assume B's update: D_B(D) is still 3, but suppose C now advertises D=1.
- To D: min{ $1 + 3$, $2 + 1$ } = 3 via C — no change.

**Convergence achieved at iteration 2.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Most exam questions follow this exact 4-node / 5-link pattern. Always show the min{} computation explicitly to claim full marks.

## 3.2 The Count-to-Infinity Problem — Detailed Analysis

### 3.2.1 The Failure Scenario

Suppose a 3-router linear topology **A — B — C** with link costs of 1 each, all converged. The link **A–B** suddenly fails.

| Step | B's view of A | C's view of A | C's view via B |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial | 1 (direct) | 2 (via B) | 2 |
| After A–B fails | ∞ (link down) | 2 (still believes via B) | A is 2 hops via B |
| Next update | — | B tells A = ∞; C updates A = 3 via B | loop continues |
| Subsequent | — | B hears from C: A = 3; B updates A = 4 via C | bounce increases |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| After ~16 rounds | 16 (=∞) | 16 (=∞) | A is "unreachable" |

**Cost to A** slowly climbs from 3 to 4 to 5 ... 16, where RIP declares it unreachable. This is the **count-to-infinity problem**.

### 3.2.2 Mathematical Characterization

$$
D_x(y)^{(t+1)} = \min_v \{ C(x,v) + D_v(y)^{(t)} \}
$$

When a link fails, the **bad news propagates one hop per iteration**, leading to a convergence delay of $O(N)$ rounds, where the bad metric walks from 3 up to the infinity threshold. With RIP's $\infty = 16$, this can take up to 16 update cycles, i.e., **8 minutes** at 30-second intervals.

### 3.2.3 Why Pure DVR Fails

The recursive update equation assumes that neighbors' vectors are computed **from sources independent of x's own information**. When topology breaks, this independence breaks down, and routers end up trusting their own stale data, which has been "echoed" back through a loop.

## 3.3 Solution Mechanisms (Anti-Count-to-Infinity Techniques)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic — Solutions to Count-to-Infinity:** Memorize all four below; questions appear frequently.

### 3.3.1 Split Horizon
**Rule:** *Never advertise a route back to the neighbor from which you learned it.*
- If B learned the path to A via C, B will not advertise A to C.
- **Effect:** Prevents two-node loops but fails for three-node loops.

### 3.3.2 Split Horizon with Poison Reverse
**Rule:** *Advertise the route back to the source with cost = ∞.*
- B tells C: "A is 16 hops away (unreachable)."
- **Effect:** C is forced to immediately invalidate the path, breaking the loop in one step for the simple case.

### 3.3.3 Hold-Down Timers
**Rule:** *When a route goes down, refuse to accept updates about it for a fixed suppression period (180 s in RIP).*
- During hold-down, the router keeps the route marked as *possibly down* and ignores advertisements that would resurrect it.
- **Effect:** Prevents premature adoption of a still-bad alternative path.

### 3.3.4 Route Poisoning
**Rule:** *When a route fails, immediately advertise it with cost = 16 (∞) to all neighbors.*
- Combined with **triggered updates** (immediate broadcast of the change), it speeds up bad-news propagation dramatically.

### 3.3.5 Triggered Updates
**Rule:** *Send an update the instant a metric changes — do not wait for the 30-second timer.*
- Reduces convergence time from minutes to seconds.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Mechanism | What It Solves | What It Fails On | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Split Horizon | 2-node loops | 3-node loops | High |
| Poison Reverse | 2-node loops faster | 3-node loops | High |
| Hold-Down Timers | Premature path adoption | Increases convergence delay | Medium |
| Route Poisoning | Speeds up failure propagation | Doesn't eliminate count-to-infinity alone | High |
| Triggered Updates | Convergence speed | Extra protocol overhead | Medium |
| Max Hop Count = 15 | Bounds the count-to-infinity | Causes "unreachable" at 16 hops | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Router A] -- DV Update --> R2[Router B]
    R2 -- DV Update --> R1
    R2 -- DV Update --> R3[Router C]
    R3 -- DV Update --> R2
    R1 -- DV Update --> R3
    R3 -- DV Update --> R1

    subgraph DVR_Engine
        RX[Receive Vector from Neighbor]
        CALC[Bellman-Ford Recompute]
        CMP{Metric Improved?}
        UP[Update Distance Vector]
        BROAD[Broadcast New Vector]
        NOOP[No Action]
        RX --> CALC
        CALC --> CMP
        CMP -- Yes --> UP
        CMP -- No --> NOOP
        UP --> BROAD
    end

    R1 -. Triggers .-> RX
    R2 -. Triggers .-> RX
    R3 -. Triggers .-> RX
```

## 4.2 DVR Iterative Convergence State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize
    Initialize --> WaitingForUpdates: Set direct costs, others equal infinity
    WaitingForUpdates --> ProcessIncoming: Periodic timer fires 30s or triggered event
    ProcessIncoming --> ApplyBellmanFord: For each neighbor vector
    ApplyBellmanFord --> CompareWithCurrent: Compute new cost via min rule
    CompareWithCurrent --> UpdateTable: New cost less than current
    CompareWithCurrent --> WaitingForUpdates: No improvement
    UpdateTable --> BroadcastToNeighbors: Send new vector excluding split-horizon partner
    BroadcastToNeighbors --> WaitingForUpdates
    WaitingForUpdates --> Converged: No updates in full cycle
    Converged --> WaitingForUpdates: Topology change detected
```

## 4.3 Count-to-Infinity Propagation Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Router A
    participant B as Router B
    participant C as Router C

    Note over A,C: Initial: A-B-C, all costs equal 1
    A->>B: D_A = 0
    B->>C: D_B(A) = 1
    C->>B: D_C(A) = 2 (via B)

    Note over A,B: Link A-B FAILS
    B-->>A: D_B(A) = 16 (poisoned)
    B->>C: D_B(A) = 16 (poison reverse)
    C->>B: D_C(A) = 3 (via B, before knowing B's update)
    B->>A: D_B(A) = 4 (via C, looping back)
    Note over A,B: Metric walks up: 3, 4, 5, ..., 16
    Note over A,C: At D=16, RIP declares A unreachable
```

## 4.4 RIP Protocol Stack Placement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APP[Application Layer HTTP FTP SMTP]
    TRAN[Transport Layer TCP UDP]
    subgraph NETLAYER[Network Layer]
        RIPMOD[RIP Process Routing Engine]
        IPMOD[IP Forwarding Engine]
        RIPMOD -- Updates tables for --> IPMOD
    end
    LINK[Data Link Layer Ethernet PPP]
    PHYS[Physical Layer Copper Fiber Wireless]

    APP --> TRAN
    TRAN --> NETLAYER
    NETLAYER --> LINK
    LINK --> PHYS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** RIP operates at the **Application Layer** logically (it is an application process that uses UDP port 520), but it **controls the Network Layer's** IP forwarding table. This is a classic KTU conceptual question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Distance Vector Routing. Mention any two limitations of the algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

Distance Vector Routing is a dynamic, intra-domain routing algorithm in which each router maintains a table of distances to all known destinations and exchanges this table only with its directly connected neighbors. The router uses the **Bellman–Ford minimum-cost equation** $D_x(y) = \min_v \{ C(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$ to update its vector upon receiving neighbor advertisements.

**Two limitations:**
1. **Count-to-infinity problem** — slow propagation of link failures can take up to 16 update cycles in RIP.
2. **Slow convergence** — global convergence requires O(N) iterations in the worst case, with high bandwidth overhead because entire vectors are sent periodically.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the value 16 in RIP?**

**Model Answer:**

In RIP, the value **16 represents infinity** and is the maximum allowable hop count. Because RIP uses hop count as its metric and was designed for small networks, 16 hops is treated as the "unreachable" threshold. Any destination advertised with a cost of 16 (or higher) is considered invalid and the route is removed from the routing table. This hard cap also **bounds the count-to-infinity problem**, ensuring that the bad-metric walk terminates at 16 rather than escalating indefinitely.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Distance Vector Routing algorithm with the **Bellman–Ford update equation**. Construct an example network of 4 routers and demonstrate **two complete iterations** of the algorithm, showing the distance-vector tables after each iteration.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the **Count-to-Infinity problem**? Explain **Split Horizon**, **Split Horizon with Poison Reverse**, and **Hold-Down Timers** as solutions. State one limitation of each.

### Model Solution to (a)

**Bellman–Ford Equation:**

$$
D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \{ C(x, v) + D_v(y) \}
$$

where $N(x)$ is the set of $x$'s direct neighbors, $C(x,v)$ is the link cost, and $D_v(y)$ is the cost from $v$ to $y$ as advertised by $v$.

**Sample 4-router network (all unit costs):**

| Link | A–B | A–C | A–D | B–C | B–D | C–D |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | 1 | 4 | 5 | 2 | 1 | 3 |

**Initial Tables (t = 0):**

| Node | To A | To B | To C | To D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 1 | 4 | 5 |
| B | 1 | 0 | 2 | 1 |
| C | 4 | 2 | 0 | 3 |
| D | 5 | 1 | 3 | 0 |

**Iteration 1 — Router A recomputes using updated B, C, D vectors:**

- To B: min{ $1 + 0$, $4 + 2$, $5 + 1$ } = **1** via B
- To C: min{ $1 + 2$, $4 + 0$, $5 + 3$ } = **3** via B
- To D: min{ $1 + 1$, $4 + 3$, $5 + 0$ } = **2** via B

| Node A (Iter 1) | To A | To B | To C | To D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | 0 | 1 | 3 | 2 |
| Next-Hop | A | B | B | B |

**Iteration 2 — Router A recomputes again (B's vector may also have improved):**

Suppose B's new vector after its own iteration 1 is: {A:1, B:0, C:2, D:1}. Then for A:

- To C: min{ $1 + 2$, $4 + 0$, $5 + 3$ } = **3** (unchanged, via B)
- To D: min{ $1 + 1$, $4 + 3$, $5 + 0$ } = **2** (unchanged, via B)

| Node A (Iter 2) | To A | To B | To C | To D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | 0 | 1 | 3 | 2 |
| Next-Hop | A | B | B | B |

**Convergence achieved for A at iteration 2.**

### Model Solution to (b)

**Count-to-Infinity Problem:**
When a link fails, neighboring routers do not instantly know the network has changed. They continue to advertise stale routes through each other, and the metric to the failed destination *creeps upward* by 1 each update cycle until it reaches the infinity threshold (16 in RIP). This slow, looping escalation is the count-to-infinity problem.

**1. Split Horizon**
*Rule:* A router does not advertise a route back to the neighbor from which it learned that route.
**Limitation:** Fails in three-or-more-router loops (e.g., A→B→C→A topology), because B will still advertise A's cost to C if it learned the path to A from D, not from C.

**2. Split Horizon with Poison Reverse**
*Rule:* Instead of silently omitting the route, the router advertises it back with a cost of 16 (infinity), explicitly marking it as unusable in that direction.
**Limitation:** Increases update message size slightly; still cannot prevent three-node loops where the "bad" direction is not directly back to the source.

**3. Hold-Down Timers**
*Rule:* When a route becomes invalid, the router enters a hold-down period (180 s in RIP) during which it refuses to accept any updates for that destination that would replace the route. This prevents premature adoption of a still-looping alternate path.
**Limitation:** Introduces additional delay even for legitimate route changes, slowing overall convergence.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Distance Vector Routing** and **Link State Routing** across the following parameters: information exchange scope, convergence speed, algorithm basis, scalability, and bandwidth usage.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable network diagram, illustrate the operation of **RIP** using Distance Vector Routing. Show the complete distance vector tables for **two iterations** after a new link is added.

### Model Solution to (a) — DVR vs LSR Comparison Table

| Parameter | Distance Vector Routing | Link State Routing |
|---|---|---|
| Information exchange scope | Only with **direct neighbors** | **Flooded to all routers** in the area |
| Convergence speed | Slow (count-to-infinity risk) | Fast (Dijkstra on synchronized LSDB) |
| Algorithm basis | **Bellman–Ford** | **Dijkstra's Shortest Path First** |
| Scalability | Poor; bounded by 15 hops in RIP | High; used in large ISP networks (OSPF, IS-IS) |
| Bandwidth usage | Low (small vectors, periodic) | High initially (LSP flood), then low (incremental) |
| Routing table size | Grows linearly with destinations | Same growth, but path computation is local |
| CPU / memory load | Low per router | Higher (Dijkstra on full topology) |
| Loop formation | Possible (count-to-infinity) | Highly unlikely (every router has full map) |
| Example protocols | RIPv1, RIPv2, RIPng, IGRP | OSPF, IS-IS |
| Update trigger | Periodic (30 s) + triggered | Event-driven (LSP on change) |
| Hierarchical support | None (flat) | Yes (OSPF areas, IS-IS levels) |
| KTU exam weight | High (Module 3 core) | High (Module 4 — comparison context) |

### Model Solution to (b) — RIP Operation Illustration

**Topology:** Add a new link **B–D** with cost 1 to an existing 4-router network. Initial converged state before the addition:

| Node | To A | To B | To C | To D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 1 | 4 | 2 (via B) |
| B | 1 | 0 | 2 | 3 (via C) |
| C | 4 | 2 | 0 | 3 |
| D | 2 | 3 | 3 | 0 |

**After new link B–D (cost 1) is added at t = 0:**

**Iteration 1 — Router D's recomputation using B's vector {A:1, B:0, C:2}:**
- To A via B: $C(D,B) + D_B(A) = 1 + 1 = 2$ (was 2 via A — same, but next-hop changes)
- To B: $1 + 0 = 1$ (was 3) → **improved to 1 via B**
- To C: $\min\{1 + 2, 3 + 4\} = 3$ via B (was 3) — no change

| Node D (Iter 1) | To A | To B | To C | To D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | 2 | 1 | 3 | 0 |
| Next-Hop | B | B | B | D |

**Iteration 2 — Router A's recomputation using D's new vector:**
- To D via B: $1 + 1 = 2$ (no change)
- To D via D direct: not adjacent — $5 + 0 = 5$ (worse)
- Result: A→D remains 2 via B.

| Node A (Iter 2) | To A | To B | To C | To D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost | 0 | 1 | 3 | 2 |
| Next-Hop | A | B | B | B |

**Convergence observed by iteration 2.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to show the min{} computation explicitly.** Always expand: `min{1+0, 4+2, 5+1} = 1` — not just the final answer.
> 2. **Confusing "iteration" with "time tick."** An iteration means one full recompute *using* neighbors' *current* vectors — not waiting for a 30-second timer.
> 3. **Omitting the next-hop column.** Full marks require both **cost** and **next-hop** in the table.
> 4. **Stating the count-to-infinity problem without citing a numerical example.** Always walk through the metric walk (3→4→5→...→16).
> 5. **Mixing up split horizon vs poison reverse.** Split horizon = *omit* the route. Poison reverse = *advertise it as 16*. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Distance Vector Routing**

- DVR is a **distributed, iterative, asynchronous, self-terminating** shortest-path algorithm based on the **Bellman–Ford** recursion.
- Each router maintains a vector of (Destination, Cost, Next-Hop) and **exchanges with direct neighbors only**.
- **Core update equation:** $D_x(y) = \min_v \{ C(x,v) + D_v(y) \}$
- **RIP metric:** hop count; **max = 15**, **16 = unreachable**.
- **RIP timers:** Update = 30 s, Timeout = 180 s, Hold-down = 180 s, Flush = 240 s.
- **Count-to-infinity** = bad news propagates 1 hop per update; bounded by the 16-hop cap.
- **Split horizon:** do not advertise a route back to the neighbor that gave it to you.
- **Poison reverse:** advertise that route back with cost = 16.
- **Hold-down:** ignore new advertisements for a failed route for 180 s.
- **Route poisoning:** mark the failed route as 16 immediately.
- **Triggered updates:** send updates immediately on change, not just periodically.
- **Convergence bound:** O(N) iterations for N nodes; in practice 2–4 iterations for typical topologies.
- **RIP is an application-layer process** using **UDP port 520**, but it controls the IP routing table.
- **DVR vs LSR:** DVR = neighbors only, slow convergence, low overhead. LSR = full topology, fast convergence, higher initial bandwidth.
- **Modern descendant:** EIGRP uses **DUAL** (Diffusing Update Algorithm), a loop-free DVR extension.
- **Maximum exam marks scenario:** A typical KTU question combines the Bellman-Ford calculation (7 marks) with a count-to-infinity narrative and its solutions (7 marks). Practice both halves.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Congestion Control Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Congestion Control Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Network Congestion

**Network Congestion** is formally defined as a state in a packet-switched network where the demand for network resources (such as link bandwidth, buffer space in routers, and processor capacity) exceeds the available capacity of those resources at a given point in time. This results in queueing delays, packet loss, and degraded Quality of Service (QoS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Tanenbaum / Kurose-Ross Terminology):**
> "Congestion occurs when the number of packets being transmitted through the network approaches the packet-handling capacity of the network, causing a sharp collapse in throughput and a rise in end-to-end delay."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Traffic Jam

Imagine a **6-lane national highway** (network) merging into a **2-lane toll booth** (bottleneck link). Even if cars (packets) enter the highway rapidly, they cannot exit faster than the toll booth processes them. As cars pile up, they spill into adjacent lanes, eventually blocking the entire highway — a complete standstill. Even though cars are still *moving into* the system, the *output throughput* drops to near zero. This is precisely how congestion collapses network performance.

## 1.3 The Performance Knee and Cliff Effect

The relationship between **Offered Load (packets/sec)** and **Throughput (packets/sec)** is non-linear. The graph exhibits two critical points:

- **Knee Point**: The threshold beyond which throughput grows much more slowly than offered load. Queues start building up.
- **Cliff Point**: The threshold beyond which throughput *collapses* dramatically as the network enters congestive meltdown, and packet loss becomes severe.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics (Bolded for KTU Emphasis):**
> - **Throughput** — measured in **bits per second (bps)** or **packets per second (pps)**
> - **End-to-End Delay (Latency)** — measured in **milliseconds (ms)**
> - **Jitter** — variation in delay, measured in **ms**
> - **Packet Loss Ratio (PLR)** — dimensionless ratio of lost packets to total sent
> - **Goodput** — application-level useful data rate (bps)

## 1.4 Congestion Control vs. Flow Control — The Critical Distinction

| Feature | **Flow Control** | **Congestion Control** |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | End-to-End (Sender ↔ Receiver) | End-to-End / Network-wide |
| **Purpose** | Prevent sender from overwhelming the **receiver's buffer** | Prevent senders from overwhelming the **network's capacity** |
| **Mechanism** | Receiver window (RWND) feedback | Window adjustment based on inferred or signaled congestion |
| **Trigger** | Receiver buffer full | Router queue overflow, packet loss, delay spike |
| **Layer (OSI)** | Primarily Transport (Layer 4) | Network + Transport (Layer 3 + Layer 4) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently confuse *flow control* with *congestion control*. In KTU exams, mark loss occurs when these two are interchanged. Flow control = **receiver speed** matching. Congestion control = **network speed** matching.

## 1.5 Causes of Network Congestion

1. **Bursty Traffic Patterns**: Multiple sources transmitting simultaneously.
2. **Insufficient Buffer Space** at intermediate routers.
3. **Slow Processor** at routers causing queue buildup.
4. **Bandwidth Mismatch** between incoming and outgoing links.
5. **Multicasting** fan-out consuming too many resources.
6. **Routing Inefficiencies**: Suboptimal paths concentrating traffic.

## 1.6 Effects of Congestion

- Increased **queuing delay** (especially in router buffers).
- **Packet loss** due to buffer overflow.
- **Retransmissions** — which *worsen* the congestion (positive feedback loop).
- Reduced **throughput** and **goodput**.
- Higher **jitter**, harming real-time traffic (VoIP, video streaming).
- In severe cases, **congestion collapse** (throughput → 0).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Congestion Control Algorithms

Congestion control algorithms are classified along two primary axes:

### A) Based on Layer of Operation
- **Network-Assisted (Router-Based)**: Routers explicitly signal congestion (e.g., Choke Packet, ECN, RED).
- **End-to-End (Host-Based)**: End hosts infer congestion from observed loss/delay (e.g., TCP Tahoe, Reno, Vegas).

### B) Based on Feedback Type
- **Implicit Feedback**: Loss = congestion assumption (TCP).
- **Explicit Feedback**: Routers send explicit signals (ICMP Source Quench, ECN).

## 2.2 Algorithm 1 — The Leaky Bucket Algorithm

The **Leaky Bucket** is a traffic-shaping algorithm that converts a bursty, variable-rate traffic stream into a smooth, constant-rate output. It is analogous to a bucket with a small hole at the bottom: water (packets) drips out at a constant rate, regardless of how irregularly it is poured in.

### Operational Rules
1. The host maintains an internal buffer (the bucket) of size **B** packets.
2. If the bucket is full, any arriving packet is **dropped**.
3. The interface transmits packets to the network at a constant rate of **ρ** packets/second, regardless of the input burstiness.

### Formal Parameters
- **Bucket Capacity (B)**: Maximum burst the bucket can hold (in packets or bytes).
- **Output Rate (ρ)**: Constant drain rate (packets/sec).
- **Input Rate (λ)**: Variable, bursty arrival rate.

### Two Variants
- **Leaky Bucket as a Shaper** (e.g., ATM): Buffers bursts; outputs at constant rate.
- **Leaky Bucket as a Meter**: Counts packets; if token count exceeds threshold, violation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The Leaky Bucket is a *traffic shaper*, not a flow-control mechanism. It enforces a *peak rate* constraint on the output.

## 2.3 Algorithm 2 — The Token Bucket Algorithm

The **Token Bucket** allows bursty output up to a configurable limit, making it more flexible than the Leaky Bucket. Tokens are generated at a rate **r** (tokens/sec) and stored in a bucket of capacity **b**. A packet can be transmitted only if a token is available; the token is then consumed.

### Operational Rules
1. Tokens are added to the bucket at rate **r** tokens/second.
2. The bucket can hold a maximum of **b** tokens (excess tokens are discarded).
3. To send a packet of size **L**, the sender consumes **L** tokens from the bucket.
4. If insufficient tokens exist, the packet either waits or is dropped (depending on policy).

### Mathematical Model
If no traffic is sent for a duration **T**, the bucket accumulates **r·T** tokens (capped at **b**). When a burst arrives, the sender can transmit at a peak rate of up to:

$$P_{peak} = \frac{b}{T} + r$$

where **T** is the duration over which the burst occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with Leaky Bucket:** The Token Bucket allows controlled bursts up to size **b**, while the Leaky Bucket strictly enforces a constant output rate with no bursting.

## 2.4 Algorithm 3 — TCP Congestion Control (Tahoe & Reno)

TCP employs an **Additive Increase / Multiplicative Decrease (AIMD)** strategy for congestion control, governed by a **Congestion Window (cwnd)**. The sender's effective window is:

$$\text{Window} = \min(\text{cwnd}, \text{rwnd})$$

where **cwnd** is the congestion window and **rwnd** is the receiver-advertised window.

### TCP Tahoe (1988)
Tahoe introduced the fundamental mechanisms of slow start, congestion avoidance, and fast retransmit:

1. **Slow Start Phase**: cwnd starts at 1 MSS (Maximum Segment Size). For each ACK received, cwnd increases by 1 MSS. Effectively, cwnd doubles every RTT (exponential growth).
2. **Congestion Avoidance Phase**: Once cwnd reaches the **Slow Start Threshold (ssthresh)**, growth switches to linear: cwnd increases by **1 MSS per RTT** (i.e., +1/cwnd per ACK).
3. **Timeout Reaction**: On timeout, **ssthresh = cwnd / 2** and **cwnd = 1 MSS**. The connection restarts in slow start.
4. **Fast Retransmit**: On receiving 3 duplicate ACKs, retransmit immediately without waiting for timeout.

### TCP Reno (1990)
Reno added **Fast Recovery** on top of Tahoe:

1. On 3 duplicate ACKs (indicating packet loss but network still functional):
   - **ssthresh = cwnd / 2**
   - **cwnd = ssthresh + 3** (accounting for 3 packets that left the network)
2. Each subsequent duplicate ACK inflates cwnd by 1 MSS.
3. When a new (non-duplicate) ACK arrives, cwnd is set to ssthresh (entering congestion avoidance).

### Sawtooth Pattern
The cwnd over time traces a classic "**sawtooth**": linear increase, abrupt multiplicative decrease on loss.

## 2.5 Algorithm 4 — Random Early Detection (RED)

**RED** is a router-based (active queue management) algorithm designed to detect congestion *before* the queue overflows and to signal back to senders via implicit (or explicit, with ECN) feedback.

### Operational Parameters
- **min_th** (Minimum Threshold): Queue length below which no packets are dropped.
- **max_th** (Maximum Threshold): Queue length above which every packet is dropped (tail-drop).
- **max_p** (Maximum Drop Probability): The maximum probability of marking/dropping a packet when avg queue length is near max_th.

### Average Queue Length Calculation
The router uses an exponential weighted moving average (EWMA) to smooth the instantaneous queue:

$$Q_{avg} = (1 - w_q) \cdot Q_{avg}^{old} + w_q \cdot Q_{current}$$

where **w_q** is the queue weight (typically **0.002**).

### Drop Probability
If **min_th ≤ Q_avg < max_th**, the drop probability is:

$$P_b = \max_p \cdot \frac{Q_{avg} - \min_{th}}{\max_{th} - \min_{th}}$$

The final packet-drop probability is:

$$P = \frac{P_b}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P_b}$$

where **count** is the number of packets accepted since the last drop. This avoids consecutive drops.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RED Philosophy:** Drop packets *randomly and probabilistically* before the queue fills, so that the drop is spread across many flows. This avoids **global synchronization**, where all TCP senders back off simultaneously.

## 2.6 Algorithm 5 — Explicit Congestion Notification (ECN)

ECN extends RED by *marking* packets (setting a bit in the IP header) rather than dropping them, providing **explicit** rather than implicit feedback:

- **ECN-Capable (ECT) bit**: Set by sender to indicate ECN support.
- **Congestion Experienced (CE) bit**: Set by router when avg queue length exceeds thresholds.
- The receiver echoes the CE bit back to the sender via a TCP ECE flag.
- The sender then halves cwnd, just as if a loss had occurred.

## 2.7 Algorithm 6 — Choke Packets

A **Choke Packet** is a router-generated control packet sent directly to the source, instructing it to reduce its transmission rate. The classic example is the **ICMP Source Quench** message in IPv4.

- The router detects congestion (e.g., when its buffer queue length exceeds a threshold).
- It sends a Choke Packet to the source IP of the offending flow.
- The source reduces its rate upon receiving the choke packet.
- Once congestion clears, the source gradually increases its rate.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Units** | **Applicable Algorithm** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{cwnd} = \text{cwnd} + 1$ per RTT | cwnd = congestion window | MSS | TCP Slow Start |
| $\text{cwnd} = \text{cwnd} + \frac{1}{\text{cwnd}}$ per ACK | Linear growth in CA | MSS | TCP Congestion Avoidance |
| $\text{ssthresh} = \frac{\text{cwnd}}{2}$ | Slow start threshold | MSS | TCP Tahoe/Reno (loss event) |
| $\text{cwnd} = 1$ MSS | Reset to 1 segment | MSS | TCP Timeout (Tahoe) |
| $Q_{avg} = (1-w_q)Q_{avg}^{old} + w_q Q_{current}$ | EWMA queue length | packets | RED |
| $P_b = \max_p \cdot \frac{Q_{avg} - \min_{th}}{\max_{th} - \min_{th}}$ | Linear drop probability | dimensionless | RED |
| $P = \frac{P_b}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P_b}$ | Geometric drop prob. | dimensionless | RED |
| $B = b + \rho \cdot T$ | Token bucket size after time T | tokens | Token Bucket |
| $\rho_{out} = \text{constant}$ | Output rate | pps | Leaky Bucket |
| $P_{peak} = \frac{b}{T} + r$ | Peak burst rate | pps | Token Bucket |
| $\text{Window} = \min(\text{cwnd}, \text{rwnd})$ | Effective send window | bytes | TCP general |
| $\text{Throughput} \approx \frac{1.22 \cdot \text{MSS}}{\text{RTT} \cdot \sqrt{p}}$ | Steady-state TCP throughput | bps | TCP Reno (Mathis formula) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** These algorithms power **every modern Internet application** — web browsing (TCP), video streaming (DASH over TCP/QUIC), VoIP (RTP/UDP with ECN). Token/Leaky Buckets are foundational in **ATM networks, traffic policing in 5G core, and QoS in DiffServ**.

## 2.9 Modern Variants (Bonus for KTU Awareness)

- **TCP Vegas**: Uses delay-based congestion detection (compares expected vs. actual RTT).
- **TCP CUBIC**: Default in Linux; uses a cubic function of time since last loss.
- **TCP BBR (Bottleneck Bandwidth and Round-trip propagation time)**: Google's model-based congestion control.
- **QUIC**: Modern transport (HTTP/3) with built-in congestion control.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Token Bucket Numerical Problem

**Problem Statement (Model KTU Style):**
A token bucket has a capacity of **b = 1 MB** and tokens are generated at rate **r = 20 Mbps**. A bursty source sends data at peak rate of **P = 50 Mbps** for a maximum duration of **T = 2 seconds**. Determine:
(a) The maximum burst length that can be supported.
(b) Whether the host can sustain the burst.

### Given
- $b = 1 \text{ MB} = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}$
- $r = 20 \text{ Mbps} = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/sec}$
- $P = 50 \text{ Mbps} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/sec}$
- $T = 2 \text{ sec}$

### Solution (a) — Maximum Burst Length

The bucket starts full with **b = 8 Mb**. During a burst of duration **T**, the host consumes tokens at rate **P** and tokens are added at rate **r**. For the burst to be sustainable:

$$b + r \cdot T \geq P \cdot T$$

Substituting:

$$8 \times 10^6 + (20 \times 10^6)(2) \geq (50 \times 10^6)(2)$$

$$8 \times 10^6 + 40 \times 10^6 \geq 100 \times 10^6$$

$$48 \times 10^6 \not\geq 100 \times 10^6$$

**Conclusion (a):** The condition is NOT satisfied. The bucket cannot support a 2-second burst at 50 Mbps.

### Solution (b) — Maximum Sustainable Burst Duration

Rearranging the inequality for **T**:

$$T_{\max} = \frac{b}{P - r} = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{50 \times 10^6 - 20 \times 10^6} = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{30 \times 10^6}$$

$$T_{\max} = 0.267 \text{ seconds}$$

**Conclusion (b):** The host can sustain the 50 Mbps burst for a maximum of **0.267 seconds**, after which the token bucket empties and the host must throttle down to **r = 20 Mbps**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Leaky Bucket Drain Time

**Problem:** A leaky bucket has a capacity of **B = 1000 packets** and outputs at a rate of **ρ = 50 pps**. A burst input fills the bucket completely in 2 seconds. How long does it take to drain?

### Given
- $B = 1000 \text{ packets}$
- $\rho = 50 \text{ packets/sec}$

### Solution

$$\text{Drain Time} = \frac{B}{\rho} = \frac{1000}{50} = 20 \text{ seconds}$$

**Interpretation:** Even though the burst occurred in 2 seconds, the output is smoothed over 20 seconds at the constant rate of 50 pps. This is the *shaping* effect of the leaky bucket.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — TCP Reno Congestion Window Evolution

**Problem:** A TCP Reno connection begins with **cwnd = 1 MSS** and **ssthresh = 8 MSS**. The network uses a RTT of 100 ms. Three duplicate ACKs are received when cwnd = 12 MSS. Trace the cwnd evolution for the next 5 RTTs.

### Phase 1 — Slow Start (cwnd = 1 to 8 MSS)

| **RTT #** | **cwnd (MSS) at start** | **cwnd (MSS) at end** | **Phase** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 2 | Slow Start |
| 2 | 2 | 4 | Slow Start |
| 3 | 4 | 8 | Slow Start |

At the end of RTT 3, cwnd = ssthresh = 8 MSS. The next phase is **Congestion Avoidance**.

### Phase 2 — Congestion Avoidance (cwnd = 8 to 12 MSS)

| **RTT #** | **cwnd (MSS) at start** | **cwnd (MSS) at end** | **Phase** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 8 | 9 | CA (+1 MSS/RTT) |
| 5 | 9 | 10 | CA |
| 6 | 10 | 11 | CA |
| 7 | 11 | 12 | CA |

### Phase 3 — Loss Event (3 Duplicate ACKs at cwnd = 12)

- New **ssthresh = 12 / 2 = 6 MSS**
- **Fast Recovery**: cwnd = ssthresh + 3 = 6 + 3 = 9 MSS
- Then enters Congestion Avoidance

### Phase 4 — Post-Loss Evolution (cwnd = 9 onward)

| **RTT #** | **cwnd (MSS) at start** | **cwnd (MSS) at end** | **Phase** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 | 9 | 10 | CA |
| 9 | 10 | 11 | CA |
| 10 | 11 | 12 | CA |

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — RED Drop Probability Calculation

**Problem:** A RED-enabled router has parameters: **min_th = 10 packets**, **max_th = 30 packets**, **max_p = 0.1**, **w_q = 0.002**. The current and previous average queue lengths are 18 and 16 packets respectively. Compute the drop probability.

### Step 1 — Compute New Average Queue Length

$$Q_{avg} = (1 - w_q) \cdot Q_{avg}^{old} + w_q \cdot Q_{current}$$

$$Q_{avg} = (1 - 0.002)(16) + (0.002)(18)$$

$$Q_{avg} = (0.998)(16) + (0.002)(18)$$

$$Q_{avg} = 15.968 + 0.036$$

$$Q_{avg} = 16.004 \text{ packets}$$

### Step 2 — Verify Range

Since **10 ≤ 16.004 < 30**, we are in the *probabilistic drop region*. ✓

### Step 3 — Compute $P_b$

$$P_b = \max_p \cdot \frac{Q_{avg} - \min_{th}}{\max_{th} - \min_{th}}$$

$$P_b = 0.1 \cdot \frac{16.004 - 10}{30 - 10}$$

$$P_b = 0.1 \cdot \frac{6.004}{20}$$

$$P_b = 0.1 \cdot 0.3002$$

$$P_b = 0.03002 \approx 3.00\%$$

### Step 4 — Compute Final Drop Probability $P$

Assume **count = 5** (number of packets accepted since the last drop):

$$P = \frac{P_b}{1 - \text{count} \cdot P_b} = \frac{0.03002}{1 - (5)(0.03002)}$$

$$P = \frac{0.03002}{1 - 0.1501} = \frac{0.03002}{0.8499}$$

$$P \approx 0.0353 \approx 3.53\%$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Congestion Algorithms at a Glance

| **Algorithm** | **Location** | **Feedback Type** | **Granularity** | **Allows Burst?** | **Complexity** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Leaky Bucket | Source/Edge | N/A (Shaper) | Per-flow | ❌ No (constant out) | Low |
| Token Bucket | Source/Edge | N/A (Shaper/Policer) | Per-flow | ✅ Yes (up to **b**) | Low |
| Choke Packet | Router | Explicit (control msg) | Per-flow | N/A | Medium |
| TCP Tahoe | End-host | Implicit (loss) | Per-connection | N/A | Medium |
| TCP Reno | End-host | Implicit (3 dup-ACKs) | Per-connection | N/A | Medium |
| RED | Router | Implicit (drop) / ECN (explicit) | Per-packet | N/A | Medium-High |
| ECN | Router + End-host | Explicit (CE bit) | Per-packet | N/A | High |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Generic Congestion Control Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Source Host A] -->|Data Packets| rtr1[Router R1]
    srcB[Source Host B] -->|Data Packets| rtr1
    rtr1 -->|Buffered Queue| rtr2[Router R2 Bottleneck]
    rtr2 -->|Forwarded Packets| rtr3[Router R3]
    rtr3 --> dstA[Destination Host A]
    rtr3 --> dstB[Destination Host B]
    rtr2 -.->|Choke Packet or ECN| srcA
    rtr2 -.->|Choke Packet or ECN| srcB
    srcA -.->|Reduced cwnd| rtr1
    srcB -.->|Reduced cwnd| rtr1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — TCP Reno State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SlowStart
    SlowStart --> CongestionAvoidance: cwnd >= ssthresh
    SlowStart --> SlowStart: Timeout cwnd to 1 MSS
    SlowStart --> FastRecovery: 3 Duplicate ACKs
    CongestionAvoidance --> CongestionAvoidance: Linear Growth 1 MSS per RTT
    CongestionAvoidance --> FastRecovery: 3 Duplicate ACKs
    CongestionAvoidance --> SlowStart: Timeout cwnd to 1 MSS
    FastRecovery --> CongestionAvoidance: New ACK Received
    FastRecovery --> SlowStart: Timeout cwnd to 1 MSS
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — RED Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pArrived[Packet Arrived at Router] --> qCalc[Compute Average Queue Length Qavg]
    qCalc --> checkMin{Qavg less than min_th}
    checkMin -->|Yes| admit[Admit Packet to Queue]
    checkMin -->|No| checkMax{Qavg greater than max_th}
    checkMax -->|Yes| dropTail[Drop Packet Tail Drop]
    checkMax -->|No| calcPb[Compute Drop Probability Pb]
    calcPb --> rollDice{Uniform Random less than P}
    rollDice -->|Yes| markDrop[Mark or Drop Packet]
    rollDice -->|No| admit
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Token Bucket State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Generating: Tokens Generated at Rate r
    Generating --> BucketFull: Token Count reaches b
    BucketFull --> Generating: Excess Tokens Discarded
    Generating --> Transmit: Packet Size L arrives
    Transmit --> DeductTokens: Subtract L tokens
    DeductTokens --> Generating: Return to Generation Loop
    Transmit --> NoTokens: Insufficient Tokens
    NoTokens --> Generating: Wait for Token Accumulation
```

```mermaid
    end

    subgraph NetworkAssisted["Network-Assisted Approach Choke RED ECN"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]
**Define congestion in a network. List any two causes of congestion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** Congestion in a network is a condition in which the number of packets being injected into the network exceeds the packet-handling capacity, leading to packet loss, queuing delays, and reduced throughput.

**Causes (2 marks, any two):**
1. **Bursty input traffic** from multiple sources exceeding the available bandwidth.
2. **Insufficient router buffer size** causing queue overflows.
3. **Slow router processing** relative to line speeds.
4. **Bandwidth mismatch** at convergence points in the topology.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]
**Differentiate between flow control and congestion control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | **Flow Control** | **Congestion Control** |
|---|---|---|
| **Concern** (1 mark) | Speed matching between **sender and receiver** | Speed matching between **sender and network** |
| **Mechanism** (1 mark) | Receiver Window (RWND) advertised by receiver | Congestion Window (CWND) adjusted by sender |
| **Scope** (1 mark) | End-to-end (single hop pair) | End-to-end across multiple routers |

---

## Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice Option A

### [KTU University Exam - Model Question A]

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the **Leaky Bucket** and **Token Bucket** algorithms with neat diagrams. Compare their features.

**(b) (7 marks)** A token bucket has a capacity of **b = 500 KB** and tokens are generated at a rate of **r = 10 Mbps**. Determine the maximum burst time for a source transmitting at **P = 100 Mbps**.

### Model Solution

#### (a) Leaky Bucket & Token Bucket (7 marks)

**Leaky Bucket Algorithm (3 marks):**
The leaky bucket is implemented as a finite-capacity buffer (the bucket) with a constant drain rate. Packets arrive at variable (bursty) rates and are placed in the bucket. If the bucket is full, incoming packets are dropped. The network interface transmits packets out of the bucket at a constant rate **ρ**, regardless of the input burst pattern.

```
   Burst Input              Constant Output
   (Variable)               (Rate = ρ)
       |                        |
       v                        v
   +-------+    Hole       +-------+
   |       |    --->       |       |
   | Bucket|  (Constant)   | Net   |
   | Buffer|    --->       |  IF   |
   |       |               |       |
   +-------+               +-------+
     (Drops if full)
```

**Token Bucket Algorithm (3 marks):**
In the token bucket, tokens are generated at rate **r** and stored in a bucket of capacity **b**. To transmit a packet of size **L** bytes, the sender must consume **L** tokens. If insufficient tokens exist, the packet waits or is dropped.

```
        +-----------+
        | Token Gen |
        | rate = r  |
        +-----+-----+
              v
        +-----------+
        |  Bucket   |  b tokens max
        | (tokens)  |
        +-----+-----+
              |
```
