### Electronic Mail (E-mail)

E-mail is one of the oldest and most-used application-layer services. Three core protocols are involved:

| Protocol | Function | Default Port | Mode |
|----------|----------|--------------|------|
| **SMTP** (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) | Sending mail (push) | TCP/25 (or 587) | Push |
| **POP3** (Post Office Protocol v3) | Retrieving mail (download & delete) | TCP/110 | Pull |
| **IMAP** (Internet Message Access Protocol) | Retrieving mail (server-side) | TCP/143 | Pull |

---

## 1.4 The Three Core "Elements" of Transport Protocols (Forouzerian Framework)

The KTU 2024 syllabus lists three key elements. Memorize these — they appear every year:

1. **Addressing (TSAPs & NSAPs):** Identifying end-points.
   - **NSAP** (Network Service Access Point) — IP address (network-layer endpoint).
   - **TSAP** (Transport Service Access Point) — Port number (transport-layer endpoint).
2. **Connection Management:** Establishing, maintaining, and tearing down connections (3-way handshake, 4-way teardown).
3. **Flow Control & Buffering:** Ensuring a fast sender does not overrun a slow receiver (using sliding windows, credit allocation, buffers).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Transport Service Access Points (TSAPs) and Port Numbers

A **TSAP** is the abstract endpoint at which a transport service is exposed to a process. In the TCP/IP world, TSAPs are implemented as **16-bit port numbers** (range: **0 to 65535**).

| Port Range | Category | Examples |
|------------|----------|----------|
| 0 – 1023 | **Well-Known Ports** (privileged) | HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, FTP=21, SSH=22, SMTP=25, DNS=53 |
| 1024 – 49151 | **Registered Ports** | MySQL=3306, RDP=3389 |
| 49152 – 65535 | **Dynamic / Ephemeral Ports** | Assigned by OS to client sockets |

A **socket** is the full endpoint tuple:

$$ \text{Socket} = (\text{IP\_address}, \text{Port\_number}) $$

A **connection** is a pair of sockets:

$$ \text{Connection} = ( \text{Client\_Socket}, \text{Server\_Socket} ) = ( (\text{IP}_c, P_c),\ (\text{IP}_s, P_s) ) $$

---

## 2.2 Elements of Transport Protocols — The KTU Three-Element Model

### A) Addressing (Multiplexing / Demultiplexing)

The transport layer must deliver each segment to the **correct application process** on the host. This is **demultiplexing**. Sending multiple streams into the same lower layer is **multiplexing**.

- **UDP demultiplexing** uses the 2-tuple: `(destination IP, destination port)`.
- **TCP demultiplexing** uses the 4-tuple: `(source IP, source port, destination IP, destination port)` — this is why a single server can support thousands of simultaneous clients.

### B) Connection Management — The TCP State Machine (Core KTU Topic)

TCP connections traverse a state machine. The three critical phases are:

**Phase 1 — Establishment (3-Way Handshake):**

$$ \text{C} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN}, seq=x} \text{S} $$
$$ \text{S} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN+ACK}, seq=y, ack=x+1} \text{C} $$
$$ \text{C} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK}, ack=y+1} \text{S} $$

**Phase 2 — Data Transfer** (reliable, in-order, using sliding windows).

**Phase 3 — Termination (4-Way Handshake):**

$$ \text{A} \xrightarrow{\text{FIN}} \text{B} $$
$$ \text{B} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK}} \text{A} $$
$$ \text{B} \xrightarrow{\text{FIN}} \text{A} $$
$$ \text{A} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK}} \text{B} $$

### C) Flow Control & Error Control

- **Stop-and-Wait ARQ** — Sender waits for ACK after every frame. *Efficiency* = $\frac{1}{1+2a}$ where $a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}}$.
- **Go-Back-N ARQ** — Sender can have up to $N$ unacknowledged frames. *Window size* $W \le 2^n - 1$ where $n$ is the sequence number bits.
- **Selective Repeat ARQ** — Receiver buffers out-of-order frames. *Window size* $W \le 2^{n-1}$.

---

## 2.3 UDP — The Connectionless Workhorse

**User Datagram Protocol (UDP)** is defined in **RFC 768**. Header is a **fixed 8 bytes**.

| Field | Size (bits) | Purpose |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| Source Port | 16 | Sender's port (optional) |
| Destination Port | 16 | Receiver's port |
| Length | 16 | UDP header + data length |
| Checksum | 16 | Optional error detection (mandatory in IPv6) |

> [!NOTE]
> UDP provides **no** reliability, **no** ordering, **no** flow control, **no** congestion control. It is a thin wrapper over IP with the addition of a checksum and port demultiplexing.

**UDP Pseudo-Header (for Checksum):** The checksum is computed over a 12-byte pseudo-header + UDP header + data.

$$ \text{Pseudo-header} = \{ \text{Source IP (32)} + \text{Dest IP (32)} + \text{Zero (8)} + \text{Protocol (8)} + \text{UDP Length (16)} \} $$

### UDP Checksum Algorithm (One's Complement Sum)

The KTU board expects this 16-bit one's complement sum algorithm:

$$ \text{Checksum} = \overline{ \left( \sum_{i} \text{16-bit word}_i \right) }_{\text{1's complement}} $$

> [!TIP]
> If the data length is odd, the sender pads a single zero byte to make an even number of 16-bit words before summation.

---

## 2.4 TCP — The Reliable, Connection-Oriented Backbone

**Transmission Control Protocol (TCP)** is defined in **RFC 793** (and updated by RFCs 7323, 7414, etc.). It is a **byte-stream**, **connection-oriented**, **full-duplex**, **reliable** protocol.

### TCP Header Format (20 bytes minimum, up to 60 bytes with options)

| Field | Size (bits) |
|-------|-------------|
| Source Port | 16 |
| Destination Port | 16 |
| Sequence Number | 32 |
| Acknowledgment Number | 32 |
| Data Offset (header length) | 4 |
| Reserved | 6 |
| URG / ACK / PSH / RST / SYN / FIN flags | 6 (1 each) |
| Window Size | 16 |
| Checksum | 16 |
| Urgent Pointer | 16 |
| Options + Padding | 0–320 |

### TCP Maximum Segment Size (MSS)

The MSS is the largest amount of TCP payload (data only, excluding headers) that can be sent in a single segment:

$$ \text{MSS} = \text{MTU}_{interface} - \text{IP\_header} - \text{TCP\_header} = 1500 - 20 - 20 = 1460 \text{ bytes} $$

This is the **standard Ethernet MSS**.

### TCP Windowing & Flow Control

TCP uses a **credit-based sliding window**. The receiver advertises a window:

$$ \text{AdvertisedWindow} = \text{rwnd} = \text{Buffer\_size} - \text{Bytes\_currently\_held} $$

The sender must not have more than `rwnd` unacknowledged bytes in flight.

### TCP RTT and Timeout Formulas (KTU High-Yield!)

$$ \text{SRTT}_{n+1} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot \text{SRTT}_n + \alpha \cdot \text{RTT\_sample}_n $$

$$ \text{RTTVAR}_{n+1} = (1 - \beta) \cdot \text{RTTVAR}_n + \beta \cdot \vert \text{RTT\_sample}_n - \text{SRTT}_n \vert $$

$$ \text{RTO}_{n+1} = \text{SRTT}_{n+1} + \max(G, 4 \cdot \text{RTTVAR}_{n+1}) $$

**Standard RFC 6298 constants:** $\alpha = \frac{1}{8}$, $\beta = \frac{1}{4}$, $G$ (clock granularity) = **0.1 seconds**, $K = 4$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Karn's Algorithm** rule: do **not** sample RTT for retransmitted segments. Use the exponential backoff on timeout: $\text{RTO} \leftarrow 2 \cdot \text{RTO}$ on each timeout, up to a cap (typically 60–240 seconds).

---

## 2.5 DNS — Deeper Architecture

**DNS** uses a **distributed, hierarchical database** structured as an inverted tree with the **root** at the top.

### Domain Namespace Hierarchy

| Level | Example | Managed By |
|-------|---------|------------|
| Root | `.` | 13 root-server clusters (A–M) |
| Top-Level Domain (TLD) | `.in`, `.com`, `.edu` | IANA / TLD operators |
| Second-Level | `ktu` | Domain registrar |
| Sub-domain | `www`, `mail` | Domain owner |

### DNS Resource Records (RRs)

| Type | Code | Function |
|------|------|----------|
| **A** | 1 | Hostname → IPv4 address |
| **AAAA** | 28 | Hostname → IPv6 address |
| **CNAME** | 5 | Alias to another name |
| **MX** | 15 | Mail exchange server |
| **NS** | 2 | Authoritative name server |
| **PTR** | 12 | IP → Hostname (reverse DNS) |
| **SOA** | 6 | Start of Authority (zone info) |
| **TXT** | 16 | Free-form text |

### DNS Message Format

A DNS message has 5 sections:

$$ \text{Message} = \{ \text{Header (12B)}, \text{Question}, \text{Answer}, \text{Authority}, \text{Additional} \} $$

**Header fields:** ID (16), QR (1), Opcode (4), AA (1), TC (1), RD (1), RA (1), Z (3), RCODE (4), QDCOUNT (16), ANCOUNT (16), NSCOUNT (16), ARCOUNT (16).

> [!NOTE]
> **Iterative vs Recursive Query:** In a *recursive* query, the server takes full responsibility for resolving. In an *iterative* query, the server returns the *best referral* it has. KTU questions often ask you to compare these two.

---

## 2.6 SNMP — Architecture

SNMPv2/v3 components:

- **Managed Devices** (routers, switches, hosts) — each runs an **SNMP agent** (UDP/161).
- **Network Management Station (NMS)** — runs the **manager** (UDP/162 for traps).
- **Management Information Base (MIB)** — the database of variables.
- **Structure of Management Information (SMI)** — the language for defining MIB variables (uses **ASN.1**).

### SNMP Protocol Operations (PDU types)

| PDU Type | Direction | Purpose |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| `GetRequest` | Manager → Agent | Retrieve value |
| `GetNextRequest` | Manager → Agent | Walk MIB tree |
| `SetRequest` | Manager → Agent | Modify value |
| `Response` | Agent → Manager | Reply to request |
| `Trap` | Agent → Manager (async) | Unsolicited alarm (UDP/162) |
| `InformRequest` | Manager → Manager (v2) | Acknowledged trap |

---

## 2.7 Electronic Mail — Architecture

### MIME (Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions)

MIME extends the old 7-bit ASCII-only SMTP to support binary attachments, non-Latin scripts, and multimedia.

**Five MIME header fields:**

$$ \text{MIME-Version},\ \text{Content-Type},\ \text{Content-Transfer-Encoding},\ \text{Content-Id},\ \text{Content-Description} $$

### Final Delivery Formula

$$ \text{User Agent} \xrightarrow{\text{SMTP push}} \text{Mail Server A} \xrightarrow{\text{SMTP push}} \text{Mail Server B} \xrightarrow{\text{POP3/IMAP pull}} \text{Recipient UA} $$

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule |
|---|---------|----------------|
| 1 | UDP Header | **8 bytes** fixed |
| 2 | TCP Header | **20–60 bytes** |
| 3 | TCP MSS (Ethernet) | MTU − 40 = **1460 bytes** |
| 4 | Socket tuple | $(\text{IP}, \text{Port})$ |
| 5 | Go-Back-N window | $W \le 2^n - 1$ |
| 6 | Selective Repeat window | $W \le 2^{n-1}$ |
| 7 | Stop-and-Wait efficiency | $\eta = \frac{1}{1+2a},\ a = t_p/t_f$ |
| 8 | Smoothed RTT | $\text{SRTT} = (1-\alpha)\text{SRTT} + \alpha R$ |
| 9 | RTTVAR | $(1-\beta)\text{RTTVAR} + \beta \vert R - \text{SRTT} \vert$ |
| 10 | RTO | $\text{SRTT} + 4 \cdot \text{RTTVAR}$ |
| 11 | Checksum (UDP/TCP) | 1's complement of 16-bit one's-complement sum |
| 12 | Well-known ports | 0 – 1023 |
| 13 | DNS port | UDP/53, TCP/53 (zone transfer) |
| 14 | SNMP ports | UDP/161 (agent), UDP/162 (manager) |
| 15 | SMTP / POP3 / IMAP | TCP/25, TCP/110, TCP/143 |
| 16 | DNS ID field size | **16 bits** (max 65536 simultaneous) |

> [!TIP]
> The single most-tested formula in KTU Module 4 is the **UDP Checksum calculation**. Practice computing one's-complement sum manually. We do it in full in Section 3.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — UDP Checksum Calculation

**Problem:** Compute the UDP checksum for a UDP segment with the following 4-byte payload: `0xA1 0x2B 0x3C 0x4D`. The pseudo-header values are: Source IP = `0xC0A80101`, Dest IP = `0xC0A80102`, Protocol = 17, UDP Length = 12 (8 header + 4 data).

### Step 1 — Build the pseudo-header (12 bytes)

| Word (hex) | Meaning |
|------------|---------|
| `C0A8` | Source IP high |
| `0101` | Source IP low |
| `C0A8` | Dest IP high |
| `0102` | Dest IP low |
| `0011` | Zero (8 bits) + Protocol 17 (8 bits) |
| `000C` | UDP Length = 12 |

### Step 2 — Append the UDP header (with checksum = 0 initially)

The UDP header is: `Source Port (2B) + Dest Port (2B) + Length (2B) + Checksum (2B)`.

Let us assume Source Port = `0x1A0B`, Dest Port = `0x0035` (DNS), Length = `0x000C`, Checksum = `0x0000` (set to 0 during computation).

So UDP header words: `1A0B`, `0035`, `000C`, `0000`.

### Step 3 — Append the data

Data = `A1 2B 3C 4D` ⇒ words: `A12B`, `3C4D`.

### Step 4 — Perform 16-bit one's-complement addition of every word

Let us list all 8 words to be summed:

$$ S = \{ \text{C0A8},\ \text{0101},\ \text{C0A8},\ \text{0102},\ \text{0011},\ \text{000C},\ \text{1A0B},\ \text{0035},\ \text{000C},\ \text{0000},\ \text{A12B},\ \text{3C4D} \} $$

We perform binary addition in hexadecimal:

**First batch — sum 8 words:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C0A8} + \text{0101} &= \text{C1A9} \\
\text{C1A9} + \text{C0A8} &= \text{18251}_{\text{hex}} \Rightarrow \text{low 16} = 8251,\ \text{carry} = 1 \\
\text{8251} + 1 &= 8252 \\
\text{8252} + \text{0102} &= 8354 \\
\text{8354} + \text{0011} &= 8365 \\
\text{8365} + \text{000C} &= 8371 \\
\text{8371} + \text{1A0B} &= \text{9D7C} \\
\text{9D7C} + \text{0035} &= \text{9DB1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Second batch — add remaining words with carry-fold:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{9DB1} + \text{000C} &= \text{9DBD} \\
\text{9DBD} + \text{0000} &= \text{9DBD} \\
\text{9DBD} + \text{A12B} &= \text{13EE8}_{\text{hex}} \Rightarrow \text{low 16} = 3EE8,\ \text{carry} = 1 \\
\text{3EE8} + 1 &= 3EE9 \\
\text{3EE9} + \text{3C4D} &= \text{7B36}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Take the one's complement

$$ \text{Checksum} = \overline{\text{7B36}}_{16} = \text{84C9} $$

**Verification of the 1's complement rule:** No carry remains (the final sum `7B36` is a 16-bit value, so no end-around carry is needed).

### Step 6 — Encapsulation

The sender now inserts `0x84C9` into the UDP checksum field. The receiver repeats the same computation *over the now-valid checksum*; the result **must equal `0xFFFF` (all ones)** for an error-free segment.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiners' favorite question:** *"Why does the receiver expect 0xFFFF and not 0x0000?"* Answer: Because the sender stored the **one's complement** of the sum. So when the receiver adds the checksum back into the sum, the total becomes the one's complement of zero, which is all 1s.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — TCP 3-Way Handshake Step-by-Step

Let us trace a TCP connection between Client C and Server S with initial sequence numbers ISN_c = 100, ISN_s = 300.

| Step | Direction | Flag | Seq | Ack | Segment | Client State | Server State |
|------|-----------|------|-----|-----|---------|--------------|--------------|
| 1 | C → S | SYN | 100 | – | (100, SYN) | SYN_SENT | LISTEN |
| 2 | S → C | SYN+ACK | 300 | 101 | (300, SYN, ack=100+1) | SYN_SENT | SYN_RCVD |
| 3 | C → S | ACK | 101 | 301 | (101, ack=300+1) | ESTABLISHED | ESTABLISHED |

**Key insight (often asked in exams):**

$$ \text{Ack number acknowledges the next expected byte} = \text{received\_seq} + 1 + \text{data\_bytes} $$

This is why the **SYN consumes one sequence number** even though it carries no data — it is "consumed" so that the ack can be `ISN_c + 1`.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — TCP 4-Way Termination

| Step | Direction | Flag | Action |
|------|-----------|------|--------|
| 1 | A → B | FIN | A enters FIN_WAIT_1, B enters CLOSE_WAIT |
| 2 | B → A | ACK | A enters FIN_WAIT_2, B's app finishes → LAST_ACK |
| 3 | B → A | FIN | A receives, enters TIME_WAIT |
| 4 | A → B | ACK | A holds TIME_WAIT for 2×MSL ≈ **240 s** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **TIME_WAIT** state (also called 2MSL wait) exists so that any delayed final ACK or duplicate FIN can be properly absorbed. After 2 × Maximum Segment Lifetime, A definitively closes the connection.

---

## 3.4 Stop-and-Wait ARQ Efficiency — Full Derivation

Let the frame transmission time be $t_f$ and the propagation time be $t_p$. Define:

$$ a = \frac{t_p}{t_f} $$

**Channel utilization** (fraction of time the channel carries useful data):

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{\text{time carrying new frame}}{\text{total cycle time}} \\
U &= \frac{t_f}{t_f + 2 t_p + t_{ack}} \quad \text{(idealized, ignoring ACK time)} \\
U &= \frac{1}{1 + 2a}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Efficiency (when a retransmission occurs with probability $p$):**

$$ \eta = \frac{1 - p}{1 + 2a} $$

**Numerical example:** If $t_f = 10\,\text{ms}$, $t_p = 200\,\text{ms}$, then $a = 20$, and $\eta = \frac{1}{1+40} = 0.0244 = 2.44\%$. This dramatic result is **why** stop-and-wait is unusable on long-fat networks, and **why** sliding window protocols (Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat) exist.

---

## 3.5 DNS Resolution — Iterative Query Trace

Suppose a host `H` wants to resolve `mail.example.com`. The trace of an *iterative* query from H to its local resolver R:

| Step | H → R | R → Root (.) | Root → R | R → TLD (`.com`) | TLD → R | R → Authoritative | Authoritative → R | R → H |
|------|-------|-------------|----------|------------------|---------|-------------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Query `mail.example.com` | | | | | | | |
| 2 | | | Referral: ask `.com` TLD | | | | | |
| 3 | | | | Query | | | | |
| 4 | | | | | Referral: ask `example.com` auth NS | | | |
| 5 | | | | | | Query | | |
| 6 | | | | | | | A record: `203.0.113.5` | |
| 7 | | | | | | | | A record returned to H |

**Total queries generated by H:** 1 (recursive to R). **Total queries across the internet:** 3 (iterative referrals).

---

## 3.6 SNMP Message Exchange — Worked Example

Scenario: An NMS polls a router's interface byte counter.

| Step | Direction | PDU | OID | VarBind |
|------|-----------|-----|-----|---------|
| 1 | NMS → Agent | `GetRequest` | 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10.1 (ifInOctets) | `{ifInOctets.1, NULL}` |
| 2 | Agent → NMS | `Response` | 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10.1 | `{ifInOctets.1, 12345678}` |
| 3 | NMS → Agent | `SetRequest` | ifAdminStatus | `{ifAdminStatus.1, down(2)}` |
| 4 | Agent → NMS | `Response` | ifAdminStatus | `{ifAdminStatus.1, down(2)}` |
| 5 | Agent → NMS (async) | `Trap` | 1.3.6.1.6.3.1.1.5.3 (linkDown) | `{ifIndex.1, 1}` |

> [!TIP]
> **Trap** vs **InformRequest:** A trap is a fire-and-forget UDP message; an InformRequest is a trap with an acknowledgment requirement (added in SNMPv2). KTU expects you to know that traps are **unreliable**.

---

## 3.7 SMTP Session — Exhaustive Step Trace

Let `alice@ktu.edu` send mail to `bob@example.com`.

```
S: 220 mail.ktu.edu ESMTP Postfix
C: EHLO client.ktu.edu
S: 250-mail.ktu.edu Hello client.ktu.edu
S: 250-SIZE 10240000
S: 250 PIPELINING
C: MAIL FROM:<alice@ktu.edu>
S: 250 Ok
C: RCPT TO:<bob@example.com>
S: 250 Ok
C: DATA
S: 354 End data with <CR><LF>.<CR><LF>
C: From: Alice <alice@ktu.edu>
C: To: Bob <bob@example.com>
C: Subject: KTU Exam
C:
C: Hello Bob, see you at the exam.
C: .
S: 250 Ok: queued as 4A2B7C
C: QUIT
S: 221 Bye
```

**Critical SMTP commands (KTU expects these):** `HELO/EHLO`, `MAIL FROM`, `RCPT TO`, `DATA`, `RSET`, `NOOP`, `QUIT`.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 TCP State Transition Diagram (Complete)

The TCP state machine has 11 states. Below is the full Mermaid block-level flow:

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CLOSED
    CLOSED --> LISTEN : passive open (LISTEN)
    CLOSED --> SYN_SENT : active open (CONNECT)
    LISTEN --> SYN_RCVD : receive SYN / send SYN+ACK
    SYN_SENT --> SYN_RCVD : receive SYN / send SYN+ACK
    SYN_SENT --> ESTABLISHED : receive SYN+ACK / send ACK
    SYN_RCVD --> ESTABLISHED : receive ACK
    ESTABLISHED --> FIN_WAIT_1 : close / send FIN
    ESTABLISHED --> CLOSE_WAIT : receive FIN / send ACK
    FIN_WAIT_1 --> FIN_WAIT_2 : receive ACK
    FIN_WAIT_1 --> CLOSING : receive FIN
    FIN_WAIT_2 --> TIME_WAIT : receive FIN / send ACK
    CLOSE_WAIT --> LAST_ACK : close / send FIN
    CLOSING --> TIME_WAIT : receive ACK
    LAST_ACK --> CLOSED : receive ACK
    TIME_WAIT --> CLOSED : timer 2MSL expires
```

**Reading the diagram:** Any path from `CLOSED` to `ESTABLISHED` requires 3 messages (3-way handshake). Any path from `ESTABLISHED` back to `CLOSED` requires 4 messages (4-way teardown) **plus** the 2MSL wait.

---

## 4.2 TCP Header Format — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Source Port 16 bits"]
    B["Destination Port 16 bits"]
    C["Sequence Number 32 bits"]
    D["Acknowledgment Number 32 bits"]
    E["Data Offset 4 bits"]
    F["Reserved 6 bits"]
    G["URG ACK PSH RST SYN FIN 6 bits"]
    H["Window Size 16 bits"]
    I["Checksum 16 bits"]
    J["Urgent Pointer 16 bits"]
    K["Options and Padding 0 to 320 bits"]
    L["Data Payload Variable Length"]
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    E --> F --> G --> H --> I --> J --> K --> L
```

```mermaid
    SP --> DP --> LN --> CK --> DT
```

## 4.5 SNMP Architecture — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    NMS["Network Management Station NMS Manager"]
    AGENT1["Agent on Router UDP 161"]
    AGENT2["Agent on Switch UDP 161"]
    AGENT3["Agent on Server UDP 161"]
    MIB1["MIB Router vars"]
    MIB2["MIB Switch vars"]
    MIB3["MIB Server vars"]
    TRAP["Trap Channel UDP 162"]
    NMS --> AGENT1
    NMS --> AGENT2
    NMS --> AGENT3
    AGENT1 --- MIB1
    AGENT2 --- MIB2
    AGENT3 --- MIB3
    AGENT1 -.-> TRAP -.-> NMS
    AGENT2 -.-> TRAP
    AGENT3 -.-> TRAP
```

---

## 4.6 Electronic Mail Architecture — End-to-End Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    UA1["User Agent Alice MUA"]
    MSA1["Message Submission Agent ktu.edu MSA"]
    MDA1["Mail Storage ktu.edu"]
    MTA1["Mail Transfer Agent ktu.edu"]
    MTA2["Mail Transfer Agent example.com"]
    MDA2["Mail Storage example.com"]
    POP3["POP3 Server example.com UDP 110"]
    IMAP["IMAP Server example.com UDP 143"]
    UA2["User Agent Bob MUA"]
    UA1 --> MSA1 --> MDA1 --> MTA1
    MTA1 -- SMTP push --> MTA2
    MTA2 --> MDA2
    MDA2 --> POP3
    MDA2 --> IMAP
    POP3 --> UA2
    IMAP --> UA2
```

---

## 4.7 TCP Sliding Window Mechanism — Conceptual View

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["Sent and ACKed"]
    S2["Sent and Not Yet ACKed"]
    S3["Can Be Sent Window"]
    S4["Cannot Be Sent"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
```

**Block view interpretation:** The sender's window slides forward each time an ACK is received, expanding the "Can Be Sent" region.

---

## 4.8 UDP Pseudo-Header (for Checksum) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    PH1["Source IP Address 32 bits"]
    PH2["Destination IP Address 32 bits"]
    PH3["Zero 8 bits"]
    PH4["Protocol 17 for UDP 8 bits"]
    PH5["UDP Length 16 bits"]
    UDPH["UDP Header including checksum set to 0"]
    DATA["Data Payload padded to even bytes"]
    PH1 --> PH2 --> PH3 --> PH4 --> PH5 --> UDPH --> DATA
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"List and briefly explain the three elements of transport protocols." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three elements of transport protocols as specified in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **Addressing (TSAPs / Ports):** The transport layer identifies the source and destination application processes using **Transport Service Access Points (TSAPs)**. In the TCP/IP model these are realized as **16-bit port numbers**, combined with the IP address to form a **socket**.
2. **Connection Management:** This governs the establishment, maintenance, and graceful release of a logical session. TCP uses a **3-way handshake** (SYN, SYN+ACK, ACK) to establish and a **4-way handshake** (FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK) to terminate.
3. **Flow Control & Buffering:** Ensures a fast sender does not overrun a slow receiver. TCP uses a **sliding-window protocol with a 16-bit advertised window** that tells the sender the exact number of bytes the receiver can accept.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**"Differentiate between connection-oriented and connectionless service. Give one example protocol for each." (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Connection-Oriented | Connectionless |
|-----------|--------------------|----------------|
| Setup Phase | Required (handshake) | None |
| Reliability | Guaranteed (acks, retransmission) | No guarantee |
| State | Connection state maintained at both ends | Stateless |
| Ordering | Maintained | Not maintained |
| Resource cost | Higher (state, buffers) | Lower |
| Example | **TCP** | **UDP** |

**Examples:** TCP is used by HTTP, FTP, SSH. UDP is used by DNS, VoIP, live video streaming.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one full question** (14 marks) and may choose between two alternatives.

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) Explain the TCP header format with a neat diagram. List and explain the function of each flag bit. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The TCP header (RFC 793) is a **20-byte minimum**, variable up to 60 bytes. Fields:

1. **Source Port (16 bits)** — Port of the sending application.
2. **Destination Port (16 bits)** — Port of the receiving application.
3. **Sequence Number (32 bits)** — Byte-stream offset of the first byte in this segment.
4. **Acknowledgment Number (32 bits)** — Next expected byte from the other side; cumulative ack.
5. **Data Offset (4 bits)** — Header length in 32-bit words (5 to 15).
6. **Reserved (6 bits)** — Set to 0.
7. **Flags (6 bits):**
   - **URG** — Urgent pointer field is valid.
   - **ACK** — Acknowledgment field is valid (always set after handshake).
   - **PSH** — Push: deliver data to the application immediately, do not buffer.
   - **RST** — Reset: abort the connection on error.
   - **SYN** — Synchronize: used during the 3-way handshake to establish initial sequence numbers.
   - **FIN** — Finish: graceful close, no more data from this side.
8. **Window Size (16 bits)** — Receiver-advertised flow-control credit (bytes).
9. **Checksum (16 bits)** — Mandatory 1's-complement checksum over pseudo-header + segment.
10. **Urgent Pointer (16 bits)** — Offset from the sequence number of the last urgent byte (valid only if URG=1).
11. **Options (variable)** — MSS, Window Scaling, Timestamps, Selective Acknowledgment (SACK).

**Diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid block in Section 4.2.

---

**(b) With neat timing diagrams, explain the TCP 3-way handshake and 4-way termination procedures. Why does TCP use a TIME_WAIT state? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**3-Way Handshake (Connection Establishment):**

```
       Client                              Server
         |   ------ SYN, seq=x -------->    |
         |   <---- SYN+ACK, seq=y, ack=x+1 |
         |   ------ ACK, ack=y+1 ------>   |
      ESTABLISHED                       ESTABLISHED
```

1. **SYN:** Client picks an initial sequence number ISN_c = x and sends a SYN segment.
2. **SYN+ACK:** Server chooses ISN_s = y, acknowledges the client (ack = x+1) and sends its own SYN.
3. **ACK:** Client acknowledges the server (ack = y+1). Connection is now ESTABLISHED on both sides.

**4-Way Termination (Graceful Close):**

```
         A  ------ FIN --------------->  B
         A  <----- ACK ---------------   B
         A  <----- FIN ---------------   B
         A  ------ ACK -------------->   B
       TIME_WAIT                       CLOSED
```

1. **FIN (A→B):** A has no more data; enters FIN_WAIT_1.
2. **ACK (B→A):** B acknowledges; enters CLOSE_WAIT.
3. **FIN (B→A):** B finishes; enters LAST_ACK.
4. **ACK (A→B):** A acknowledges; enters TIME_WAIT.

**Why TIME_WAIT?** The TIME_WAIT state (duration = 2 × MSL, typically **240 s**) exists for two reasons:

- **Absorb delayed final ACK or duplicate FIN packets** from the network so that they do not corrupt a *future* connection using the same 4-tuple.
- **Ensure the remote end has time to receive the final ACK** before A's port is fully released.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) Explain the Domain Name System (DNS) architecture. With a diagram, describe how an iterative DNS query is resolved. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**DNS Architecture:**
DNS is a **distributed, hierarchical database** structured as an inverted tree:

- **Root Zone** (`.`) — 13 root-server clusters.
- **TLD Servers** (`.com`, `.org`, `.in`, country codes).
- **Authoritative Name Servers** (per domain).
- **Local/Recursive Resolvers** (operated by ISPs and enterprises).

**Domain Namespace:** Levels — Root → TLD → Second-Level → Sub-domains.

**Resource Records:** A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, NS, PTR, SOA, TXT.

**Iterative Query Resolution Diagram:**

```
   Client            Local Resolver         Root             TLD             Authoritative
     |   recursive       |                    |                |                    |
     |---query--------->|                    |                |                    |
     |                  |----query--------->  |                |                    |
     |                  |                     |  Referral:    |                    |
     |                  |                     |  ask TLD  .com|                    |
     |                  |<---referral---------|                |                    |
     |                  |                                     |                    |
     |                  |----query--------------------------->|                    |
     |                  |                                     | Referral:          |
     |                  |                                     | ask example.com NS|
     |                  |<---referral------------------------|                    |
     |                  |                                                          |
     |                  |----query----------------------------------------------->|
     |                  |                                                          |
     |                  |<---A record 203.0.113.5---------------------------------|
     |<--answer---------|                                                          |
```

In iterative resolution, the **client's resolver does the chasing**; the root and TLD servers only return *referrals* (the address of the next NS to ask).

---

**(b) Explain the Simple Network Management Protocol (SNMP) architecture. List and explain the SNMP operations (PDU types). (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**SNMP Architecture (4 components):**

1. **Managed Devices** — Routers, switches, servers, printers. Each runs an **SNMP agent** (UDP/161).
2. **Network Management Station (NMS)** — The controlling host running the manager.
3. **Management Information Base (MIB)** — A hierarchical, ASN.1-encoded collection of variables.
4. **Network Management Protocol (SNMP itself)** — The message format exchanged.

**SMIs:** ASN.1 BER encoding is used. OIDs (Object Identifiers) uniquely name each variable, e.g., `1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2.1.10.1` is `ifInOctets.1`.

**SNMP Operations (PDU Types):**

| PDU | Direction | Function |
|-----|-----------|----------|
| `GetRequest` | Manager → Agent | Read one or more variables |
| `GetNextRequest` | Manager → Agent | Walk MIB (used by `snmpwalk`) |
| `SetRequest` | Manager → Agent | Write/modify variables |
| `Response` | Agent → Manager | Reply to any of the above |
| `Trap` | Agent → Manager (UDP/162) | Unsolicited, async alarm |
| `InformRequest` | Manager → Manager (v2) | Acknowledged trap |

**Versions:**

- **SNMPv1** — Original, plain-text community strings.
- **SNMPv2c** — Adds `GetBulkRequest`, `InformRequest`; still uses community strings.
- **SNMPv3** — Adds authentication (HMAC-MD5/SHA) and encryption (DES/AES); the version used in production.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Confusing **port 161** (agent listens, manager requests) with **port 162** (manager listens for traps).
> 2. Forgetting that **Trap is the only unsolicited PDU** — the other operations are request-response.
> 3. Saying SNMP uses TCP — it does not; it uses UDP for low overhead.
> 4. Forgetting to mention the **MIB** when asked for the SNMP architecture (4 components, not 3).

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### Transport Layer
- [ ] Two protocols: **TCP (reliable, connection-oriented, byte-stream)** and **UDP (unreliable, connectionless, datagram)**.
- [ ] TCP header = **20 bytes minimum**, UDP header = **8 bytes fixed**.
- [ ] TCP 3-way handshake = **SYN, SYN+ACK, ACK**.
- [ ] TCP 4-way termination = **FIN, ACK, FIN, ACK**, plus 2MSL wait in TIME_WAIT.
- [ ] MSS (Ethernet) = **1460 bytes** = MTU (1500) − 40.
- [ ] Well-known ports = **0–1023**; registered = **1024–49151**; dynamic = **49152–65535**.
- [ ] SRTT update: $\text{SRTT} = (1-\alpha)\text{SRTT} + \alpha R$, with $\alpha = 1/8$.
- [ ] RTO: $\text{RTO} = \text{SRTT} + 4 \cdot \text{RTTVAR}$.
- [ ] **Karn's Algorithm:** Do not sample RTT for retransmitted segments.

### Flow & Error Control
- [ ] Stop-and-Wait efficiency = $\eta = \frac{1-p}{1+2a}$.
- [ ] Go-Back-N window = $2^n - 1$.
- [ ] Selective Repeat window = $2^{n-1}$.

### UDP Checksum
- [ ] 16-bit **one's-complement sum** of pseudo-header + UDP header (checksum=0) + data.
- [ ] Receiver expects result = **0xFFFF**.
- [ ] Pseudo-header includes source IP, dest IP, 0, protocol (17), UDP length.

### DNS
- [ ] **UDP/53** (queries), **TCP/53** (zone transfers >512 bytes).
- [ ] RRs: A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, NS, PTR, SOA, TXT.
- [ ] Iterative = chase referrals; Recursive = server takes full responsibility.
- [ ] Message has 5 sections: Header, Question, Answer, Authority, Additional.

### SNMP
- [ ] **UDP/161** (agent), **UDP/162** (manager / traps).
- [ ] 6 PDU types: Get, GetNext, Set, Response, Trap, Inform.
- [ ] Components: NMS, Agent, MIB, Network (the protocol itself).
- [ ] Versions: v1, v2c, v3 (only v3 is secure).

### Electronic Mail
- [ ] **SMTP** (TCP/25) for sending; **POP3** (TCP/110) and **IMAP** (TCP/143) for retrieval.
- [ ] SMTP uses 7-bit ASCII; **MIME** extends it for binary and Unicode.
- [ ] SMTP commands: `HELO/EHLO, MAIL FROM, RCPT TO, DATA, QUIT`.
- [ ] POP3 typically **deletes after download**; IMAP keeps mail on the server.

### Common KTU Exam Traps
- [ ] Mixing up **TSAP vs NSAP** (TSAP = port, NSAP = IP).
- [ ] Forgetting that **SYN consumes a sequence number** (no data, but still counted).
- [ ] Saying **TIME_WAIT = 2 RTT** (it is 2 × MSL, where MSL is the maximum segment lifetime, not the round-trip time).
- [ ] Saying **HTTP = TCP/80 = UDP/80** — HTTP is TCP only.
- [ ] Confusing **MX record** (mail server) with **A record** (host IP).
- [ ] Writing **0x0000** instead of **0xFFFF** as the expected receiver checksum result.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as **(i) Theory + Diagram (5–6 marks) + (ii) Sub-question answer (4–5 marks) + (iii) Numerical/Working example (3–4 marks)**. Examiners allocate marks per *expected point*, not per paragraph. Bullet your answer; do not write essays.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The World Wide Web, HTTP, Streaming Audio and Video
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT613 - NETWORKING AND COMMUNICATION PROTOCOLS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** The World Wide Web, HTTP, Streaming Audio and Video

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 — The World Wide Web, HTTP, Streaming Audio and Video

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The World Wide Web (WWW)

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The World Wide Web is a distributed, **hypermedia** information system based on the **client-server** model, in which a client running a web browser requests resources from a web server using the **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)**, and the resources themselves are formatted using **HyperText Markup Language (HTML)** and identified by **Uniform Resource Locators (URLs)**.

- **Invented by:** Sir Tim Berners-Lee at **CERN (European Organization for Nuclear Research)** in **1989**.
- **Foundation Year:** The first web page went live in **1991**.
- **Distinguishing feature:** Uses **hypertext** — text containing embedded links that, when clicked, jump to another resource anywhere on the internet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WWW ≠ Internet.** The Internet is the *physical/global network infrastructure* (routers, links, protocols like TCP/IP). The WWW is just *one of many applications* running on top of the Internet. Email, FTP, and BitTorrent also use the Internet but are NOT part of the Web.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Library of Everything 📚

Think of the WWW as a **giant, planet-wide digital library**:

- Every **web page** is like a **book** on a shelf.
- Each book has a unique **call number** — that is the **URL** (e.g., `https://www.ktu.edu.in/syllabus`).
- The **bookshelves and reading rooms** are the **web servers** that physically store the books.
- **You, the reader**, are the **client (browser)** — you ask the librarian for a book by its call number.
- The **librarian who fetches the book and brings it to your desk** is the **HTTP protocol**.
- **Hyperlinks** inside books are like *cross-references* — click one, and the librarian instantly fetches a completely different book, possibly from a different branch library across the world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core WWW Components (KTU Board Favorite):**
> 1. **HTML / XHTML / HTML5** — formatting language
> 2. **URL / URI** — addressing scheme
> 3. **HTTP / HTTPS** — transfer protocol
> 4. **Web Browser** — client software
> 5. **Web Server (Apache, Nginx, IIS)** — server software

---

### 1.2 HTTP — HyperText Transfer Protocol

**Definition:**
> HTTP is an **application-layer**, **request-response**, **stateless** protocol used in the WWW for transferring hypermedia documents (HTML, images, video, etc.) between clients and servers. It operates by default over **TCP port 80** (HTTPS uses **port 443**).

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Waiter 🍽️

HTTP works exactly like a waiter in a restaurant:

1. You (the **client/browser**) sit at a table and write your **order on a paper slip** (the **HTTP request** — *"I want one Margherita pizza"* = `GET /pizza HTTP/1.1`).
2. The **waiter (HTTP)** takes the slip to the **kitchen (web server)**.
3. The kitchen prepares the dish and the waiter returns with the **prepared food on a plate** (the **HTTP response** — pizza + a bill).
4. The waiter **forgets you the moment you pay** — HTTP is **stateless**. Every new order must repeat your name, table number, and preferences from scratch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Stateless" means the server retains NO memory of previous requests from the same client.** State is added externally using **Cookies**, **Sessions**, or **Tokens (JWT)**.

---

### 1.3 Streaming Audio and Video

**Definition:**
> **Streaming** is the continuous transmission of audio/video data from a server to a client such that the media can be **played back in real time** as it is being received, without requiring the entire file to be downloaded first.

- **Pre-recorded streaming:** Stored media (YouTube, Netflix, Spotify) — client can pause, rewind.
- **Live streaming:** Real-time capture and broadcast (Twitch, live news, IPL cricket).
- **Progressive download** is NOT true streaming — the file is fully downloaded but the player starts playback early.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The Tap vs. The Bucket 🚰

- **Downloading a video** = filling a **bucket** with water, then pouring it into a glass. You must finish filling the bucket before you can drink.
- **Streaming a video** = drinking water **directly from a tap** as it flows. A small **buffer** (a small cup) holds a few seconds of water in case the flow hiccups.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Major Streaming Categories (KTU Syllabus):**
> 1. **Real-Time Streaming (RTSP/RTP)** — protocol-driven, used in IPTV, video conferencing, surveillance.
> 2. **HTTP-Based Adaptive Streaming (DASH / HLS)** — uses ordinary HTTP, used by YouTube, Netflix, Hotstar.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deep Analysis — World Wide Web Architecture

The WWW follows a **strict 3-tier logical architecture**:

#### Tier 1 — The Client (Browser)
- Examples: Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge.
- Functions: sends HTTP requests, renders HTML/CSS, executes JavaScript, decodes media.
- Contains the **rendering engine** (Blink, Gecko, WebKit) and **JavaScript engine** (V8, SpiderMonkey).

#### Tier 2 — The Network (Internet + HTTP/TCP/IP)
- The transport substrate.
- Uses **DNS** to translate hostnames to IP addresses.
- HTTP rides on top of **TCP** (and in HTTP/3, on **QUIC/UDP**).

#### Tier 3 — The Server
- Hardware + web server software (Apache, Nginx, Microsoft IIS) + backend (Node.js, Django, Spring).
- Hosts the actual **HTML pages, CSS, JS, images, and media files**.

**Why "Hypermedia" and not "Hypertext"?**
Because modern WWW resources are not just text — they include images, audio, video, animations, and interactive forms. The correct modern term is **hypermedia**.

---

### 2.2 Deep Analysis — HTTP Operational Mechanics

#### Step 1: URL Resolution
The browser parses the URL:
`https://www.example.com:443/path/page.html?query=1#section2`

- **Scheme:** `https`
- **Host:** `www.example.com`
- **Port:** `443`
- **Path:** `/path/page.html`
- **Query:** `query=1`
- **Fragment:** `section2` (handled client-side, not sent to server)

#### Step 2: DNS Lookup
The browser asks the DNS resolver to translate `www.example.com` → an **IPv4 (32-bit) or IPv6 (128-bit) address**.

#### Step 3: TCP Connection
A **3-way handshake** is performed: `SYN` → `SYN-ACK` → `ACK`. For HTTP/3, this is replaced by QUIC.

#### Step 4: HTTP Request/Response Exchange
The browser sends the request, the server responds, the page (and its embedded sub-resources) is fetched.

#### Step 5: Connection Termination
`FIN` → `ACK` → `FIN` → `ACK` (4-way close for non-persistent HTTP/1.0) or kept alive for **persistent connections** in HTTP/1.1.

---

### 2.3 HTTP Versions — KTU Comparison Table

| Version | Year | Connection Model | Transport | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **HTTP/0.9** | 1991 | One request per TCP connection | TCP | Plain HTML only, GET method |
| **HTTP/1.0** | 1996 | Non-persistent (one object per TCP) | TCP | Added headers, status codes, methods (POST, HEAD) |
| **HTTP/1.1** | 1997 | Persistent + Pipelining | TCP | Host header, chunked transfer, caching, keep-alive |
| **HTTP/2** | 2015 | Multiplexed, binary | TCP | HPACK header compression, server push, single connection |
| **HTTP/3** | 2022 | QUIC-based multiplexed | UDP | 0-RTT handshake, no head-of-line blocking, encrypted by default |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HTTP/1.1's "HOL Blocking"** — Head-of-Line Blocking — is the critical flaw solved by HTTP/2 and HTTP/3. In HTTP/1.1, a slow response behind a large response blocks every subsequent request on the same connection.

---

### 2.4 HTTP Methods (Verbs) — Must-Know for KTU

| Method | Purpose | Idempotent? | Safe? |
|---|---|---|---|
| `GET` | Retrieve a resource | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| `POST` | Submit data / create resource | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| `PUT` | Replace a resource entirely | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| `PATCH` | Partial modification | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| `DELETE` | Remove a resource | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| `HEAD` | Same as GET but no body | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| `OPTIONS` | Discover supported methods | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |

**Idempotent** = calling it N times produces the same final state.
**Safe** = does not modify server state.

---

### 2.5 HTTP Status Codes — The Five Classes

| Class | Range | Meaning | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1xx Informational** | 100–199 | Request received, continue | 100 Continue |
| **2xx Success** | 200–299 | Request accepted | 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content |
| **3xx Redirection** | 300–399 | Further action needed | 301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified |
| **4xx Client Error** | 400–499 | Bad request from client | 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found |
| **5xx Server Error** | 500–599 | Server failed | 500 Internal Server Error, 502 Bad Gateway, 503 Service Unavailable |

---

### 2.6 HTTP Message Format — Formal Structure

**Request Message:**
```
REQUEST-LINE  :  Method SP Request-URI SP HTTP-Version CRLF
HEADERS       :  Header-Name: Header-Value CRLF
              :  ...
              :  CRLF
BODY          :  <entity body, optional>
```

**Response Message:**
```
STATUS-LINE   :  HTTP-Version SP Status-Code SP Reason-Phrase CRLF
HEADERS       :  Header-Name: Header-Value CRLF
              :  ...
              :  CRLF
BODY          :  <entity body>
```

> [!NOTE]
> **CRLF** = Carriage Return (\r) + Line Feed (\n). This is the line terminator in HTTP.

---

### 2.7 Non-Persistent vs Persistent Connections — Performance Trade-off

**Non-Persistent (HTTP/1.0 default):** Each object requires a fresh TCP connection. 3-way handshake overhead dominates.

**Persistent (HTTP/1.1 default):** Single TCP connection serves multiple objects. Combined with **pipelining**, multiple requests are sent without waiting for responses.

---

### 2.8 Cookies — Adding State to Stateless HTTP

A **cookie** is a small key-value pair that the server sends in the `Set-Cookie` header; the browser stores it and replays it in the `Cookie` header of subsequent requests to the same server.

Components of a cookie: **Name**, **Value**, **Domain**, **Path**, **Expires/Max-Age**, **HttpOnly**, **Secure**, **SameSite**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cookies are how HTTP simulates state** for sessions, shopping carts, and authentication. Without cookies, every request would be brand new.

---

### 2.9 Web Caching (Proxy Server)

A **web cache / proxy** stores recently fetched objects. When a request arrives, the cache is checked first — this is called a **HIT** (served from cache) vs **MISS** (fetched from origin server).

**Benefits:**
- Reduces **response time** for clients.
- Reduces **traffic** at the origin server.
- Improves **availability** when the origin is down.

---

### 2.10 Streaming Audio and Video — Theoretical Depth

#### 2.10.1 Streaming vs. Downloading

- **Downloading:** Entire file saved locally before playback. High storage, no buffering needed once complete.
- **Streaming:** Playback begins within ~1 second of reception. Small client-side buffer (~ few seconds), media discarded after playback.

#### 2.10.2 Real-Time Streaming Protocol Stack (Pre-HTTP Era)

| Protocol | Role | Transport |
|---|---|---|
| **RTSP** (Real-Time Streaming Protocol) | Session control (play, pause, record) | TCP (typically) |
| **RTP** (Real-Time Transport Protocol) | End-to-end delivery of media packets with timestamps & sequence numbers | UDP |
| **RTCP** (RTP Control Protocol) | QoS feedback, jitter reports, sync between streams | UDP |
| **SIP** (Session Initiation Protocol) | Session setup, often for VoIP | TCP/UDP |

> [!NOTE]
> **RTP does NOT guarantee delivery** (since it runs on UDP). It includes **sequence numbers** and **timestamps** so the client can reorder packets, detect losses, and synchronize audio/video.

#### 2.10.3 DASH — Dynamic Adaptive Streaming over HTTP (Modern Standard)

**DASH** (also called **MPEG-DASH**) is the **ISO/IEC 23009-1** standard for HTTP-based adaptive streaming. It is the dominant technique used by **YouTube, Netflix, Hotstar, Amazon Prime**.

**How DASH Works:**
1. The server stores the video in **multiple versions** (renditions) at different **bitrates** and **resolutions** (e.g., 240p/500 kbps, 720p/2500 kbps, 1080p/5000 kbps).
2. Each version is chopped into small **segments** (chunks), typically **2 to 10 seconds** long.
3. A **manifest file** (MPD — Media Presentation Description) tells the client which segments are available at what bitrates.
4. The client measures **available bandwidth** continuously and switches to a higher or lower bitrate segment — this is called **ABR (Adaptive Bitrate) selection**.

#### 2.10.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Bitrate** | $R = \frac{F \times b}{T}$ | $R$ in bps, $F$ = file size bits, $b$ = compression factor, $T$ = duration |
| **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = R \times d$ | $R$ in bps, $d$ (one-way delay) in seconds; gives bits "in flight" |
| **Packet Transmission Time** | $t_{tx} = \frac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) |
| **End-to-End Delay** | $d_{e2e} = d_{proc} + d_{queue} + d_{tx} + d_{prop}$ | Four components: processing, queuing, transmission, propagation |
| **Jitter** | $J = \vert d_{i} - d_{i-1} \vert$ (averaged) | Variation in inter-packet arrival time |
| **Video Compression** | $\text{PSNR} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{MAX^{2}}{MSE}\right)$ | dB; quality metric for compressed video |
| **Buffer Size Required** | $B = R \times t_{rebuffer}$ | $R$ in bps, $t$ in seconds; minimum bytes buffered |
| **Throughput** | $Throughput = \frac{\text{Useful data received}}{\text{Total time}}$ | bps, accounting for retransmissions |
| **Non-Persistent HTTP Response Time** | $T_{np} = N \times (2RTT + t_{file})$ | $N$ objects, $RTT$ = round-trip time, $t_{file}$ = file transmission time |
| **Persistent HTTP Response Time** | $T_{p} = (N+1) \times RTT + N \times t_{file}$ | One TCP handshake, pipelined |
| **HTTP/2 Multiplexed** | $T_{mux} \approx RTT + \max(t_{file_i})$ | All requests sent in parallel over 1 connection |
| **CDN Hit Ratio** | $H = \frac{\text{Requests served by CDN}}{\text{Total requests}}$ | Fraction of traffic served by edge |
| **Average Latency with CDN** | $d_{cdn} = H \cdot d_{edge} + (1-H) \cdot d_{origin}$ | Weighted between edge and origin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **non-persistent HTTP with parallel connections** (browsers open 6 parallel TCP connections by default), the response time is:
> $$T_{parallel} = (2RTT + t_{base}) + \left(\frac{N}{6}\right)(2RTT + t_{object})$$
> where $t_{base}$ is the base HTML object and $N$ is the number of referenced objects. **This equation is a KTU board favorite.**

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation — Non-Persistent vs Persistent vs Parallel HTTP

**Problem (Standard KTU Numericals Style):**
> A web page references **8 small objects** of equal size. Each object's transmission time is **2 RTT**, and the base HTML file's transmission time is **2 RTT**. One RTT = **50 ms**. Compute the total response time under:
> 1. Non-persistent HTTP (no parallelism)
> 2. Non-persistent HTTP with 6 parallel connections
> 3. Persistent HTTP (pipelined)

#### Notation

Let:
- $N = 8$ (number of referenced objects)
- $RTT = 50$ ms
- $t_{file} = 2 \cdot RTT$ (transmission time of each object)
- $t_{base} = 2 \cdot RTT$ (transmission time of base HTML)

**General formula for non-persistent, no parallelism:**
$$T_{np} = t_{base} + 2RTT + N \times (2RTT + t_{file})$$

**Step 1: Plug in values**
$$T_{np} = 2RTT + 2RTT + 8 \times (2RTT + 2RTT)$$

**Step 2: Simplify inside parentheses**
$$2RTT + 2RTT = 4RTT$$

**Step 3: Multiply by N**
$$8 \times 4RTT = 32RTT$$

**Step 4: Sum the terms**
$$T_{np} = 2RTT + 2RTT + 32RTT = 36RTT$$

**Step 5: Convert to ms**
$$T_{np} = 36 \times 50 = 1800 \text{ ms} = 1.8 \text{ seconds}$$

---

#### Case 2: Non-Persistent with 6 Parallel Connections

The 8 objects are split across 6 parallel TCP connections:
- Connections 1–6 fetch **1 object each** (6 objects done in 1 round).
- Connections 7–8 fetch **1 object each** in the 2nd round (2 objects done).

**Step 1: Compute rounds needed**
$$\text{Rounds} = \left\lceil \frac{N}{6} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{8}{6} \right\rceil = 2 \text{ rounds}$$

**Step 2: Apply the formula**
$$T_{p6} = (2RTT + t_{base}) + \text{Rounds} \times (2RTT + t_{file})$$

**Step 3: Substitute**
$$T_{p6} = (2RTT + 2RTT) + 2 \times (2RTT + 2RTT)$$

**Step 4: Simplify**
$$T_{p6} = 4RTT + 2 \times 4RTT = 4RTT + 8RTT = 12RTT$$

**Step 5: Convert to ms**
$$T_{p6} = 12 \times 50 = 600 \text{ ms} = 0.6 \text{ seconds}$$

---

#### Case 3: Persistent HTTP with Pipelining

**Step 1: One TCP handshake, then all objects in series over the same connection**
$$T_{persistent} = 2RTT + t_{base} + N \times t_{file}$$

**Step 2: Substitute**
$$T_{persistent} = 2RTT + 2RTT + 8 \times 2RTT$$

**Step 3: Simplify**
$$T_{persistent} = 4RTT + 16RTT = 20RTT$$

**Step 4: Convert to ms**
$$T_{persistent} = 20 \times 50 = 1000 \text{ ms} = 1.0 \text{ second}$$

---

#### Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary

| Strategy | Total RTTs | Total Time (ms) | Speedup vs Non-Persistent | TCP Handshakes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-Persistent, No Parallelism** | 36 | 1800 | 1.0× (baseline) | 9 (1 base + 8 objects) |
| **Non-Persistent, 6 Parallel** | 12 | 600 | 3.0× | 9 (still 9 handshakes) |
| **Persistent, Pipelined** | 20 | 1000 | 1.8× | 1 (single connection) |
| **HTTP/2 Multiplexed** | ~6 | ~300 | 6.0× | 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Parallelism saves time but at the cost of many TCP handshakes. Pipelining saves handshakes but cannot exploit parallelism. HTTP/2 multiplexing achieves *both* — single connection, full parallelism.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — DASH Bitrate Adaptation Mathematics

**Problem:**
> A DASH client has a playback buffer of 30 seconds. The video segments are 4 seconds long. Available renditions are 500 kbps, 1000 kbps, 2500 kbps, and 5000 kbps. The client starts at 1000 kbps. If the measured throughput drops to 1500 kbps for two consecutive segments, what bitrate should the client select? Justify with the bandwidth-residency formula.

**Step 1: Define the residence condition**

For smooth playback, the segment bitrate must be **less than or equal to the average available throughput**, leaving headroom for fluctuations:

$$R_{selected} \leq Throughput \times (1 - \text{safety margin})$$

Using a 20% safety margin:
$$R_{selected} \leq 1500 \times 0.8 = 1200 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2: Select the highest available rendition ≤ 1200 kbps**

Available renditions: 500, 1000, 2500, 5000 kbps.
The largest that satisfies the inequality is **1000 kbps**.

**Step 3: Verify the buffer condition**

Buffer is 30 s, segments are 4 s → 7.5 segments worth of buffer exists.
At 1000 kbps × 4 s = 4000 kb = 500 KB per segment. The client can absorb 7 such segments even at zero throughput, so the buffer is **safe**.

**Step 4: Decision**

$$R_{selected} = 1000 \text{ kbps}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **safety margin**. A naive "pick the largest ≤ throughput" rule causes **rebuffering** whenever throughput fluctuates. The 10–20% margin is what distinguishes a good ABR algorithm from a bad one.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation — Bandwidth-Delay Product for Streaming Buffer

**Problem:**
> A video stream is sent at 4 Mbps with one-way propagation delay of 40 ms. Compute the bandwidth-delay product and the minimum buffer size to prevent underrun.

**Step 1: Compute BDP**
$$BDP = R \times d_{prop}$$

**Step 2: Substitute**
$$BDP = 4 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} \times 40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Multiply**
$$BDP = 1.6 \times 10^{5} \text{ bits} = 160000 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4: Convert to kilobytes**
$$BDP = \frac{160000}{8 \times 1024} \approx 19.53 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 5: Add safety buffer (typically 2× to 5× BDP)**
$$B_{min} = 3 \times BDP = 3 \times 19.53 \approx 58.6 \text{ KB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 3× BDP?** Because network jitter can cause bursts of delay up to 2–3× the average. The buffer must "absorb" these bursts to prevent the decoder from running out of data.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 WWW — Client-Server Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Types URL in Browser] --> B[Browser Performs DNS Lookup]
    B --> C[DNS Resolver Returns IP Address]
    C --> D[TCP 3-Way Handshake SYN SYN-ACK ACK]
    D --> E[Browser Sends HTTP GET Request]
    E --> F[Web Server Processes Request]
    F --> G[Server Sends HTTP Response with HTML]
    G --> H[Browser Parses HTML]
    H --> I{Need Sub Resources}
    I -->|Yes| J[Send More HTTP Requests for CSS JS Images]
    J --> F
    I -->|No| K[Render Final Web Page to User]
```

### 4.2 HTTP Request-Response Cycle (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ClientSide["CLIENT SIDE"]
        C1[Browser Constructs HTTP Request]
        C2[Request Line: Method URI Version]
        C3[Headers: Host User-Agent Accept]
        C4[Optional Body: Form Data JSON]
        C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
    end

    subgraph Network["TRANSPORT NETWORK"]
        N1[TCP Connection Established]
        N2[Request Bytes Serialized on Wire]
        N3[Server Receives and Parses]
    end

    subgraph ServerSide["SERVER SIDE"]
        S1[Application Logic Executes]
        S2[Status Line: Version Code Phrase]
        S3[Response Headers: Content-Type Content-Length]
        S4[Response Body: HTML JSON Binary]
        S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
    end

    C4 --> N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> S1
    S4 --> ClientSide
```

### 4.3 Streaming Media — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Source["SOURCE LAYER"]
        V1[Raw Video File]
        V2[Audio File]
        V3[Live Camera Feed]
    end

    subgraph Encode["ENCODING LAYER"]
        E1[Transcoder: H.264 H.265 AV1]
        E2[Multiple Bitrate Renditions 240p to 4K]
        E3[Segmenter: Chunks of 2 to 10 seconds]
    end

    subgraph Delivery["DELIVERY LAYER"]
        D1[Origin Server]
        D2[CDN Edge Node 1]
        D3[CDN Edge Node 2]
        D4[CDN Edge Node 3]
    end

    subgraph Client["CLIENT LAYER"]
        CL1[Player App: YouTube Netflix VLC]
        CL2[ABR Logic: Measures Throughput]
        CL3[Buffer: Stores 10 to 30 seconds]
        CL4[Decoder: Renders Frames to Display]
    end

    V1 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> D1
    V2 --> E1
    V3 --> E1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D1 --> D4
    D2 --> CL1
    D3 --> CL1
    D4 --> CL1
    CL1 --> CL2 --> CL3 --> CL4
```

### 4.4 DASH Adaptive Bitrate Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client Receives MPD Manifest] --> B[Player Starts at Lowest Bitrate]
    B --> C[Download Next Segment]
    C --> D[Measure Download Throughput]
    D --> E{Throughput Greater Than Current Bitrate}
    E -->|Yes| F{Higher Bitrate Available}
    E -->|No| G{Lower Bitrate Available}
    F -->|Yes| H[Switch to Next Higher Bitrate]
    F -->|No| I[Stay at Current Bitrate]
    G -->|Yes| J[Switch to Next Lower Bitrate]
    G -->|No| I
    H --> K[Append Segment to Buffer]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Buffer Level Safe}
    L -->|Yes| C
    L -->|No| M[Pause and Rebuffer]
    M --> C
```

### 4.5 HTTP Versions Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[HTTP 0.9 1991] -->|Added Headers| A1[HTTP 1.0 1996]
    A1 -->|Persistent Connections| A2[HTTP 1.1 1997]
    A2 -->|Multiplexing Binary| A3[HTTP 2 2015]
    A3 -->|QUIC over UDP| A4[HTTP 3 2022]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architecture Insight:** The transition from HTTP/1.1 to HTTP/2 was driven by the realization that **TCP's head-of-line blocking** could not be solved at the application layer. Only **HTTP/3's move to QUIC (UDP-based)** truly eliminated HOL blocking at the transport layer.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the World Wide Web. List any four components of the WWW.**

**Model Answer:**

> The World Wide Web is a distributed hypermedia information system that runs over the Internet and enables users to access linked documents and resources using a browser. It was invented by **Tim Berners-Lee** at **CERN** in **1989**.
>
> **Four Components of WWW:**
> 1. **Uniform Resource Locator (URL)** — Addressing scheme for resources.
> 2. **HyperText Transfer Protocol (HTTP)** — Application-layer transfer protocol.
> 3. **HyperText Markup Language (HTML)** — Document formatting language.
> 4. **Web Browser** — Client software that fetches and renders resources (e.g., Chrome, Firefox).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by HTTP being a "stateless" protocol? How is state added using cookies?**

**Model Answer:**

> HTTP is called **stateless** because the server retains **no memory** of previous client requests. Each HTTP request is independent; the server does not know that two requests came from the same client unless the client explicitly identifies itself.
>
> **State is added using Cookies** as follows:
> 1. The client sends an HTTP request to the server (e.g., login form).
> 2. The server generates a unique **session ID** and responds with a `Set-Cookie: SID=abc123` header.
> 3. The browser stores this cookie locally and **automatically includes it** in the `Cookie: SID=abc123` header of all subsequent requests to the same domain.
> 4. The server recognizes the session ID and treats the request as part of an ongoing session.
>
> Cookies therefore provide **session continuity**, **user authentication**, and **personalization** on top of an inherently stateless protocol.

---

### Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Explain the HTTP message format in detail with a sample request and response. Differentiate between persistent and non-persistent HTTP connections.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**HTTP Message Format — Two Types:**

HTTP messages are plain text (in HTTP/1.x) consisting of a **start line**, **header fields**, an **empty line (CRLF)**, and an **optional body**.

**Sample HTTP Request:**
```
GET /index.html HTTP/1.1
Host: www.example.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: text/html
Connection: keep-alive

(empty body for GET)
```

- **Request Line:** `GET` is the method, `/index.html` is the URI, `HTTP/1.1` is the version.
- **Headers:** Provide metadata about the request.
- **Body:** Empty for GET; present for POST.

**Sample HTTP Response:**
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Date: Mon, 14 Oct 2024 12:00:00 GMT
Content-Type: text/html
Content-Length: 1024
Connection: keep-alive

<html><body>Hello, World!</body></html>
```

- **Status Line:** `200 OK` means success.
- **Headers:** Describe the response body.
- **Body:** The actual HTML.

**Persistent vs Non-Persistent Connections:**

| Aspect | Non-Persistent (HTTP/1.0 default) | Persistent (HTTP/1.1 default) |
|---|---|---|
| **TCP Connections** | One per object | One for multiple objects |
| **Handshakes** | Repeated 3-way handshakes | Single handshake |
| **Latency** | High (RTT × N) | Low (one RTT shared) |
| **Server Load** | High | Low |
| **Pipelining** | Not supported | Supported in HTTP/1.1 |

**Conclusion:** Persistent connections drastically reduce latency, especially for web pages with many embedded objects, and are the standard in modern web traffic.

---

**(b) Describe the architecture of the World Wide Web with a neat diagram. Differentiate between WWW and the Internet.** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**WWW Architecture — 3 Tiers:**

The WWW follows a **client-server** architecture built on top of the Internet:

1. **Client Tier (Browser):** Sends HTTP requests and renders responses. Examples: Chrome, Firefox.
2. **Network Tier (Internet + TCP/IP):** Provides the transport infrastructure, including **DNS** for name resolution and **TCP** for reliable delivery.
3. **Server Tier:** Web server software (Apache, Nginx, IIS) and backend application logic, serving HTML pages, images, and media.

**Diagram (block form):**
```
[Browser] ---HTTP---> [Internet + TCP/IP + DNS] ---HTTP---> [Web Server] ---> [Database / Files]
   (Client)               (Network Tier)                       (Server Tier)
```

**Differences between WWW and the Internet:**

| Aspect | Internet | World Wide Web |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Global network of networks | Service running on the Internet |
| **Layer** | Network infrastructure | Application layer |
| **Protocols** | TCP, IP, UDP, ICMP | HTTP, HTML, URL |
| **Components** | Routers, links, servers | Browsers, web servers, web pages |
| **Year of Birth** | 1969 (ARPANET) | 1989 (CERN) |
| **Scope** | Email, FTP, VoIP, WWW, etc. | Just hypermedia documents |

**Conclusion:** The Internet is the *substrate*; the WWW is *one service* running on it. The Internet can exist without the WWW, but the WWW cannot exist without the Internet.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Alternate]**

**(a) Explain the DASH (Dynamic Adaptive Streaming over HTTP) protocol. How does it achieve adaptive bitrate streaming?** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**DASH — Definition:**

> DASH (Dynamic Adaptive Streaming over HTTP), standardized as **ISO/IEC 23009-1**, is a technique that delivers pre-recorded media over HTTP by **chopping the media into small segments** and **encoding them at multiple bitrates**, allowing the client to dynamically switch between renditions based on available bandwidth.

**Architecture:**

1. **Server side:** The original video is **transcoded** into N renditions (e.g., 360p, 720p, 1080p, 4K) at different bitrates. Each rendition is **segmented** into chunks of 2–10 seconds (typically 4 s).
2. **Manifest file (MPD):** A separate XML file describes the available renditions, segment URLs, durations, and codec info.
3. **Client side:** The player's **ABR algorithm** continuously measures **throughput** and **buffer occupancy** to decide which rendition to request for the next segment.

**Adaptive Bitrate Decision Process:**

- **Step 1:** Client measures download speed for the latest segment.
- **Step 2:** Compares with current bitrate.
- **Step 3:** If throughput > current × 1.2 (with safety margin), upgrade to the next higher rendition.
- **Step 4:** If throughput < current × 0.8, downgrade.
- **Step 5:** If buffer falls below a critical threshold (~5 s), aggressively downgrade to prevent rebuffering.

**Key Algorithms Used:** BOLA, MPC, Throughput-based heuristics.

**Advantages of DASH:**

- Uses **standard HTTP servers and CDNs** — no special streaming infrastructure.
- Works through **firewalls and NATs** easily.
- **Codec-agnostic** — supports H.264, H.265, AV1, etc.
- **Scalable** — millions of concurrent users (just like YouTube).

**Conclusion:** DASH has become the de-facto standard for HTTP-based streaming, replacing older RTSP-based solutions due to its simplicity, scalability, and CDN-friendliness.

---

**(b) Explain the working of RTSP, RTP, and RTCP protocols used in real-time streaming. Why is RTP preferred over TCP for real-time media?** **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**RTSP (Real-Time Streaming Protocol):**
- Defined in **RFC 2326**.
- Acts as a **"network remote control"** for media — supports commands like `PLAY`, `PAUSE`, `RECORD`, `TEARDOWN`.
- Runs typically over **TCP port 554**.
- Does **not transport** the media itself; it only sets up and controls the session.

**RTP (Real-Time Transport Protocol):**
- Defined in **RFC 3550**.
- Carries the **actual audio/video payload** in UDP packets.
- Each RTP packet header contains:
  - **Sequence number** (for reordering and loss detection)
  - **Timestamp** (for playout timing and A/V sync)
  - **Payload type** (codec identifier, e.g., 96 for H.264)
  - **SSRC** (Synchronization Source identifier)

**RTCP (RTP Control Protocol):**
- Works alongside RTP to provide **out-of-band control** information.
- Periodic **Sender Reports (SR)** and **Receiver Reports (RR)** carry statistics such as:
  - Packets sent / received
  - **Jitter** (variation in packet arrival)
  - **Packet loss rate**
  - Round-trip time
- Used by the server to **adjust encoding bitrate** and by the client to **synchronize** multiple streams.

**Why RTP is preferred over TCP for real-time media:**

| Aspect | TCP | RTP (over UDP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Retransmission** | Yes — delays playback | No — drops on time |
| **Head-of-Line Blocking** | Yes — late packet blocks all later ones | No — independent packets |
| **Congestion Control** | Aggressive, can cause video to stall | None, but custom-friendly |
| **Header Overhead** | 20+ bytes | 12 bytes (RTP header) |
| **Use Case** | File transfer, email, web | Live audio/video, VoIP |

> [!NOTE]
> Real-time media values **timeliness over reliability**. A dropped video frame is acceptable; a late one causes a **stall**. RTP over UDP aligns perfectly with this trade-off.

**Conclusion:** The RTSP/RTP/RTCP trio was the dominant real-time streaming stack before HTTP-based DASH. It remains relevant in **VoIP (SIP/RTP)**, **video conferencing (WebRTC uses SRTP)**, and **IPTV**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing WWW with Internet** — examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this. Always say "WWW is a service running on the Internet."
> 2. **Forgetting port numbers** — HTTP = 80, HTTPS = 443, RTSP = 554, RTP = dynamic UDP ports. Examiners specifically test these.
> 3. **HTTP message format** — students often forget the **empty CRLF line** between headers and body. This is a 1-mark question waiting to happen.
> 4. **Stateless confusion** — don't just write "no memory." Always explain **what** has no memory and **how cookies fix it**.
> 5. **DASH numerics** — students forget the **safety margin** in bitrate selection, which is the key ABR concept.
> 6. **RTP vs RTSP roles** — examiners often ask "which protocol carries the media?" Many wrongly answer RTSP. The correct answer is **RTP**; **RTSP only controls** the session.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **WWW** = distributed hypermedia system, **invented by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN in 1989**.
- ✅ **WWW ≠ Internet** — Internet is infrastructure; WWW is one application on it.
- ✅ **HTTP** = application-layer, **request-response, stateless** protocol, port **80** (HTTPS = **443**).
- ✅ **Stateless** = no memory; **state added via Cookies**.
- ✅ **HTTP/1.0** = non-persistent; **HTTP/1.1** = persistent + pipelining; **HTTP/2** = multiplexed binary; **HTTP/3** = QUIC/UDP.
- ✅ **Head-of-Line Blocking** is the key flaw solved by HTTP/2 (mildly) and HTTP/3 (fully).
- ✅ **HTTP Methods** must know: `GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS`.
- ✅ **Status Codes**: 1xx informational, 2xx success, 3xx redirect, 4xx client error, 5xx server error.
- ✅ **HTTP Message Format** = Start Line + Headers + Empty CRLF + Optional Body.
- ✅ **Persistent vs Non-Persistent**: persistent saves handshakes; HTTP/1.1 defaults to persistent.
- ✅ **Cookies** = `Set-Cookie` header from server, `Cookie` header from client; carry session ID.
- ✅ **Web Caching** uses `If-Modified-Since` and `304 Not Modified` for conditional GET.
- ✅ **Streaming** = continuous playback during reception; buffer of few seconds; **DASH** is the modern standard.
- ✅ **DASH** uses **HTTP**, **chunks of 2–10 s**, **multiple bitrates**, and an **MPD manifest**.
- ✅ **ABR (Adaptive Bitrate)** algorithm uses throughput + buffer occupancy; **safety margin of 10–20%** is essential.
- ✅ **RTSP** = session control (port 554); **RTP** = media transport (UDP); **RTCP** = QoS feedback.
- ✅ **RTP over UDP** because real-time media prefers **timeliness over reliability**; **HOL blocking** of TCP is fatal for video.
- ✅ **CDN** = Content Distribution Network; brings content closer to user; reduces latency and origin load.
- ✅ **BDP** = $R \times d_{prop}$ — bits "in flight" on a link; key for sizing streaming buffers.
- ✅ **Formula for non-persistent parallel HTTP** with 6 connections:
  $$T = (2RTT + t_{base}) + \left\lceil \frac{N}{6} \right\rceil (2RTT + t_{object})$$
- ✅ **HTTP/3** uses **QUIC** over **UDP** to eliminate transport-layer HOL blocking.

---
